comment_text: From: Jean Naples <noreply@adv.actionnetwork.org> To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 1/30/2026 7:56:40 PM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 ---------------------------------------------------- Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. The Environmental Protection Agency, I am writing as a physician and public health/endangered animal protection advocate, that strongly supports continued full protection for our Wilderness areas to ensure access to clean potable water under the Clean Water Act and humane care and conservation for all endangered animals which require access to clean supplies of water to survive. As an environmental protection advocate, I can document that continued federal protection for our small, seasonal streams which form the headwaters of our more well-known, iconic waters is essential to protect our safe drinking water supplies. Water from these healthy streams is cleaner, costs less to treat and provides important nursery habitat for spawning fish and deliver cool, clean water downstream. As a physician and public health protection advocate, I am reminding the EPA that all life on Earth needs access to water to survive and our wetlands are among the most productive and biodiverse water sources in the world. The ability to access these wetlands is critical to the survival of all species because this access is essential to over 40% of animals and plants in our food web, including deer, birds, fish, turtles, dragonflies, and amphibians. At this time, I strongly oppose the proposed changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. For 50 years, this provision has provided essential protection for our nation's waterways by giving states and authorized tribes the ability to review federally permitted projects that could affect their water quality. I support this action because the proposed rule would significantly limit this authority by restricting reviews to only "direct discharges" into waterways, rather than allowing comprehensive assessments of broader environmental impacts. As a public health and environmental protection advocate, I can document that this change will weaken the ability for local communities to protect drinking water sources, fisheries, wetlands, and cultural resources. Please be aware that the proposed strict deadlines and limited the ability to conduct a thorough review of the proposed rule changes. The lack of provision of adequate review time will greatly increase approvals for dangerous public projects. I am very concerned because these projects will endanger community public health and result in environmental damage because these projects were approved without ensuring a thorough review to ensure adequate environmental protection. This comes at a particularly concerning time, following recent judicial decisions that have already reduced federal water protections. Please remember that access to clean water supplies are a shared resource that sustains communities, ecosystems, and economies. Once any community clean water source is contaminated, restoration can take generations. ‘ I strongly support preserving enforcement of the current Section 401 authority because it provides essential safeguards that allow local communities to protect their water resources for current and future generations. I strongly urge the EPA to please completely withdraw this proposed rule and maintain the current authority because it allows states and tribes to conduct meaningful water quality reviews. All the waterways in our country deserve comprehensive protection, not weakened standards that prioritize expedited project approvals over environmental and public health protection stewardship. The Supreme Court decision in Sackett v. EPA already drastically reduced the number of wetlands and streams protected by federal law, and the Clean Water Act was updated to conform with this decision. Therefore, this proposed rule is unnecessary. Despite the fact that the Supreme Court’s 2023 decision in Sackett v. EPA already dramatically reduced the scope of waters protected by the Clean Water Act, I am very concerned because this new proposal is much worse and significantly reduces federal protection for nearly all freshwater wetlands and millions of miles of streams The current federal protections of our streams and wetlands ensure full protection of all families and our wildlife for the ability to access clean, potable water supplies. As an environment protection advocate, I can document that protection for our wetlands and streams is paramount because these water bodies are valuable resources that provide essential habitat for fish and wildlife, store carbon, filter out harmful pollution, protect access to clean drinking water, and prevent flooding. Please remember that our wetlands provide real economic benefits to Americans because one cre of inland wetlands prevents $745 in residential flooding every year. Please realize that recent research by the Union of Concerned Scientists documented that in the Upper Midwest alone, 30 million acres of wetlands provide nearly $23 billion in annual residential flood protection. As a ocean wildlife protection advocate, I can report that in the United States, more than half of commercially harvested fish and shellfish live in wetlands for at least part of their lifecycle. This wetlands life requirement verifies that making wetlands protection absolutely fundamental to the $5.6 billion US commercial seafood industry. Please understand that across the United States, the ability for our fish to have full access to wetlands contribute more than $7.7 trillion per year to fishing, recreation, water quality, and flood control, according to the US Fish & Wildlife Service. At this time, I thank you for your consideration of my letter, my significant public health and environment protection concerns and my recommendations. As a physician and public health/environmental protection advocate, I am loudly reminding the EPA that all Americans deserve a safe and federally protected access to clean, safe water. To ensure that this fe4deral mandate is always fully honored, I strongly urge the EPA to please completely continue to authorize full protection for our waters to the greatest extent allowed within the confines of the Sackett decision. I strongly urge the EPA to please completely withdraw this proposed rule and maintain the current authority because it allows states and tribes to conduct meaningful water quality reviews. All the waterways in our country deserve comprehensive protection, not weakened standards that prioritize expedited project approvals over environmental and public health protection stewardship. Sincerely, Jean Marie Naples, MD-Ph.D. Jean New York

rationales.relevance: The comment clearly addresses EPA water protections, Section 401 of the Clean Water Act, wetlands, streams, and the Sackett decision, which are plausibly related to the docket topic. It is broadly on-topic, though it mixes several themes and does not tightly focus on the specific requested issues throughout.
rationales.reasoning: The core argument is understandable: weakening Section 401 would reduce state and tribal water-quality review and harm public health and ecosystems. However, the letter repeats points, contains some awkward phrasing, and occasionally makes broad leaps without fully connecting claims to the proposed rule.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes several concrete figures and references, such as the $745 flooding estimate, the Upper Midwest wetlands figure, and the $5.6 billion seafood industry claim. That said, citations are not provided, and some statistics are presented without context or verification, reducing evidentiary strength.
rationales.impact: The letter describes plausible impacts on drinking water, fisheries, wetlands, flooding, and public health, and explains why these matter. The impacts are generally specific, though some claims are broad or overstated and not always tied directly to the rule’s mechanics.
rationales.actionability: The commenter gives a clear request: withdraw the proposed rule and maintain current Section 401 authority, or preserve full protection to the extent allowed after Sackett. These are feasible regulatory asks, even if the letter does not offer more detailed alternative language or implementation suggestions.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is readable enough to follow, but it is highly repetitive, contains grammatical errors and typos, and lacks clean organization. It reads more like an unedited advocacy statement than a polished public comment.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and clearly oppositional public comment with a strong policy position and some concrete impact claims, but it is weakened by repetition, limited sourcing, and uneven presentation. It is effective as a general advocacy letter, though not especially rigorous or polished.

overall_summary: The comment is clearly on-topic and strongly urges EPA to withdraw the proposal, with plausible concerns about water quality, wetlands, and public health. Its main weaknesses are repetition, lack of citations, and uneven writing quality, which keep it from being a stronger, more persuasive technical comment.

comment_text: February 17, 2026 Lauren Kasparek Oceans, Wetlands, and Communities Division Office of Water U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue, NW, Washington, DC 20460 Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 - Comments on Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Dear Ms. Kasparek, The American Public Works Association (APWA) represents 32,000 public works professionals across North America who serve in both the public and private sectors providing expertise at the local, state and federal government levels. Working in the public interest, our members plan, design, build, operate, and maintain, America’s vast infrastructure network that is so fundamental to our economy, environment, public health, and safety. APWA appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed rule updating the water quality certification regulations. Under Clean Water Act (CWA) section 401, States and Tribes with “treatment in a similar manner as a State” (TAS) perform an essential function in establishing and enforcing water quality standards pursuant to CWA Sections 301, 302, 303, 306, and 307 and relevant state law when a project also requires federal permits. The public works professionals APWA represents work in large and small, urban and rural, water supply, wastewater treatment, stormwater management, drainage and flood control systems. Clear application requirements and transparent metrics for the review of section 401 certifications are crucial to ensuring critical infrastructure projects important to the communities they serve can be completed on time and on budget. With this in mind, we would like to offer the following recommendations on the proposed updates to the Water Quality Certification Regulations. 1. Maintaining state authority and the tradition of cooperative federalism in section 401 certifications. The CWA is built on a foundation of cooperative federalism wherein Congress recognized that it is the “rights of States to prevent, reduce, and eliminate pollution, to plan the development and use of land and water resources, and to consult with the [EPA] Administrator in the exercise of his authority under this chapter”. Section 401 provides a bridge between state and TAS water quality standards and federal permitting and licensing programs. This collaborative approach ensures that the technical expertise and specialized knowledge states and TAS have is central in water resource protections, such as the effluent limitations and standards of performance for new and existing discharge sources, water quality standards and implementation plans, and toxic pretreatment effluent standards. Loper Bright v. Raimondo established a statutory interpretation which must be based on the agency’s “best reading” of the statute. It is clear that Congress intended states and TAS to be the primary leaders in establishing water quality protections while also supporting the federal government in their authorizing activities. APWA urges EPA, in its final rule, to protect this successful example of cooperative federalism and the importance of the certifying authority’s role in setting water quality standards. 2. Preventing a piecemeal approach to certification requests APWA appreciates the EPA’s proposal to standardize the content of a “request for certification” to establish clear expectations for applicants. Doing this will create more certainty around the request for certification process and establish a bright line approach to the timeline for application review. However, we are concerned that this effort to expedite the process will unintentionally slow it down. Certifying authorities’ water quality standards differ. Therefore, the information they need to sufficiently evaluate a request for certification also varies. The most effective way to ensure a project will go smoothly and comply with water quality requirements is by making sure that the certifying agency reviewing the application has all relevant information before commencing the process. While certifying authorities and applicants can leverage pre-filing meetings or other communications to discuss an application, this does not guarantee that, if the regulatory requirement to trigger a request for certification happens prior to this process are limited, the completed application will result in a project that complies with all legal water quality requirements. This may lead to an increase in projects being denied or given conditional approval or have the certifying authority request application modifications. In all cases, these requests will cause unnecessary delays and increasing project cost. Therefore, APWA recommends that, the final rule explicitly allows certifying authorities to request specific additional, readily available, information and that the timeline for reviewing a request for certification does not begin until both federal and certifying authority information requirements have been submitted. 3. Importance of modifications APWA supports establishing a collaborative modification process and including the applicant as part of that agreement. APWA would also like to highlight that any additional information requested as part of a modification should be readily available, as defined in Section (V)(A)(2) in the preamble, and include supporting legal or technical rationale for such a request. 4. Considerations in a “may affect” determination An important part of protecting communities and water quality is the section 401(a)(2) process under which neighboring jurisdictions can be notified and have the opportunity to provide input about potential water quality implications for proposed projects is. Not only does unintentional discharge create a public health risk, it puts a burden on the ratepayers who would pay for additional remediation. This process is also especially important for bordering communities whose neighboring jurisdiction may have different water quality standards, and the additional discharge could put them out of compliance with their own discharge permits. Because of this, it is important that neighboring jurisdictions are notified. APWA supports a final rule that retains the 2023 Rule’s list of factors that EPA should consider when judging whether a discharge from a project “may impact” a neighboring jurisdiction and incorporates the additional parameters EPA uses in their current practices as outlined in Section (V)(F) to the preamble. While we recognize that every project is unique and not all parameters may be relevant in every circumstance, establishing a rule that specifies which factors should be taken into account will provide clear direction to the federal agencies and certifying authorities making the determination and transparency for applicants and neighboring jurisdiction regarding how and why a decision was made. It is important that neighboring jurisdictions are aware of potential risk to their water quality, but it is also important that the agency balances their legal requirement to make this determination, with ensuring certification applications are processed within a reasonable period of time, as required under section 401(a)(1). Therefore, APWA recommends retaining the 2023 Rule’s assertion that the “may affect” determination should not be a high analytical bar and should not consume considerable federal administrative resources or time. Conclusion On behalf of public works professionals across North America, we thank you for the opportunity to provide comments on this important topic. We are committed to working with the EPA on our common goal of clean water. If you have any questions, please contact APWA’s Government Affairs Manager, Leah Harnish at lharnish@apwa.org or 202-218- 6727. Sincerely, Scott D. Grayson, CAE Chief Executive Officer

rationales.relevance: The letter directly addresses the docket topic: updating water quality certification regulations under CWA section 401, and it responds to several specific issues in the proposal.
rationales.reasoning: The arguments are generally coherent and logically connected, especially around cooperative federalism, application completeness, modifications, and the 'may affect' determination. Some points are broad or somewhat repetitive, but the overall logic is clear.
rationales.evidence: The comment relies mostly on policy assertions and general statements rather than data, case-specific examples, or citations. It mentions Loper Bright and statutory provisions, but provides limited concrete support.
rationales.impact: The letter explains plausible impacts such as delays, increased costs, denials, and burdens on ratepayers, but these effects are described in general terms without quantification or detailed examples.
rationales.actionability: It offers clear recommendations, such as preserving state authority, allowing certifying authorities to request additional readily available information, and retaining the 2023 rule's factors and low analytical bar for 'may affect' determinations.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is well organized with a formal salutation, introduction, numbered sections, and a conclusion. It is readable and professionally formatted, though some sentences are awkward or contain minor grammatical issues.
rationales.overall: This is a solid, policy-relevant trade association comment letter with clear recommendations and a coherent structure. Its main weakness is the limited use of evidence and concrete support for its claims.

overall_summary: A relevant and well-structured comment letter that clearly engages the proposed rule and offers actionable recommendations, but it would be stronger with more factual support, examples, or data to substantiate its claims.

comment_text: Hi I’m a owner operator and I have concerns about the brokers making us sign a waiver giving up our rights to see exactly what the loads pay and load details.This has cost me to lose money and get blacklisted from companies such as Ryan transportation and pls logistics us carriers have no fight and when we seek smart or are very knowledgeable of how this trucking goes we are told they cant work with us.Brokers are looking for the cheapest guy and are stealing the fuel surcharges and are real arrogant and cocky I ask that you guys please level the playing field and help us out we have heavy regulations on us and it’s a free for all for brokers they are defrauding us and making massive profits off the work we put in and they have no risks I pray that you guys would discontinue the clause they have that forces us to waive our rights in order to book a load.I feel if brokers are being fair what’s their to hide

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses broker practices, waivers, load transparency, and the ability of owner-operators to see load details, which appears closely related to trucking/broker regulation issues.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is understandable and generally coherent, but it relies on broad assertions and emotional framing rather than a tightly developed line of reasoning.
rationales.evidence: No concrete data, citations, examples with specifics, or documented incidents are provided beyond named companies and general claims of harm.
rationales.impact: The commenter describes financial loss, blacklisting, and unequal bargaining power, but the magnitude and causation are not substantiated in a detailed or measurable way.
rationales.actionability: The request to discontinue clauses that force waivers of rights is clear and feasible, though it could be more specific about the exact regulatory change sought.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is a single informal paragraph with minimal punctuation and no clear organization, which reduces readability and professionalism.
rationales.overall: A relevant and understandable comment that raises a concrete policy concern, but it is weakened by lack of evidence, informal presentation, and limited specificity.

overall_summary: This is a relevant public comment focused on broker transparency and waiver clauses affecting owner-operators. It clearly expresses a requested policy change, but the submission is informal and largely unsupported by evidence or detailed examples.

comment_text: Steel Manufacturers Association Stands for American Steel. 1150 Connecticut Avenue, Suite 201 Washington, DC 20036 Brandon Farris Executive Vice President February 17, 2026 The Honorable Jessica Kramer Assistant Administrator, Office of Water Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue, NW Washington, DC 20460 Re: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929) Dear Assistant Administrator Kramer: The Steel Manufacturers Association appreciates the opportunity to provide comments in response to the Environmental Protection Agency Office of Water’s notice of proposed rulemaking, “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations.” SMA supports EPA’s proposed procedural revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 certification as improving regulatory certainty for capital-intensive, federally permitted facilities. SMA stands for American steel. Our members operate in communities across the country and range from the nation’s largest publicly traded steel producers to single-facility, family-owned businesses. Steel manufacturers generate $149.4 billion in annual economic impact, employing 87,266 men and women in community-sustaining jobs at an average annual wage of $142,730. They create the essential material that underpins American industry and national security. Steel manufacturers are investing in America’s communities, deploying $25 billion in capital here in the U.S. to modernize, expand, and construct projects. Environmental permitting is necessary for the realization of these investments, and improvements to the permitting process will reduce unnecessary project delays. Clear timelines and defined review criteria are critical for steel mills and raw material manufacturing and handling facilities that rely on predictable federal permitting schedules. The Section 401 certification process is one such example where improvements could be made to enhance certainty for permittees while ensuring environmental protection. Steel manufacturers have noted difficulties in securing authorizations under the Clean Water Act, and recommended modifications to Section 401 to focus state responsibility in water quality certification. 1 SMA therefore appreciates this proposed rulemaking by EPA, which promises to protect water quality and prevent overreach by state co-regulators. We respectfully submit several specific comments for consideration. Request for Certification SMA supports standardizing the contents of a request for water quality certification under 40 CFR 121.5 to improve efficiency, consistency, and predictability. Requiring submission of the federal permit or license application—or a draft of such—and readily available discharge-related water quality information provides sufficient information for certifying authorities to evaluate compliance with applicable water quality requirements. Furthermore, removing the ability of states and tribes to prescribe additional, specific content requirements will reduce inconsistency across jurisdictions and ensure adherence to statutory timelines, lowering administrative burden on permittees without diminishing water quality protection. SMA encourages EPA to clarify that the standardized list of required materials under 40 CFR 121.5 is intended to be sufficient for certification review and that completeness determinations may not be used to expand the scope of review or delay the start of the statutory review period. We request that EPA carefully review use of open-ended language such as “other relevant documentation” in 40 CFR 121.5(c)(4). Timeframe for Certification Analysis and Decision SMA supports EPA’s proposed changes to Section 401 certification timelines, which appropriately reinforce the statutory one-year maximum period for action on a certification request. Firm deadlines are particularly important for steel manufacturers and other permittees undertaking major capital projects subject to certification. Removing automatic extensions tied to public notice or force majeure events promotes timely decision-making. And prohibitions on certifying authority requests for withdrawal will preclude deadline circumvention schemes. Appropriate Scope for Section 401 Certification Review SMA supports proposed changes to the scope of Section 401 certification review in conformance with a plain reading of the Clean Water Act and recent court precedent, limiting such review to a discharge from a federally permitted activity into waters of the United States. Specifically, narrowing 1 America Builds: Clean Water Permitting and Project Delivery Hearing before Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment, 119th Cong. (February 11, 2025) (statement of Noah Hanners, Executive Vice President, Nucor Corporation), available at https://transportation.house.gov/uploadedfiles/02-11-2025_wre_hearing_-_noah_hanners_-_testimony.pdf. review to point-source discharges of an activity—rather than the activity as a whole—will provide clarity for permittees and regulators, and defining “discharge” as being a discharge into waters of the United States aligns with statutory authority and avoids federal overreach into non-jurisdictional waters. These proposed limitations promise to prevent Section 401 certification from being used to regulate aspects of a permitted activity unrelated to jurisdictional water quality impacts from a point source discharge. In response to specific request for comment from EPA regarding narrative water quality standards and the definition of “water quality requirements,” SMA takes the position that reliance on numeric water quality standards alone is more likely to enhance certainty for permittees and therefore would urge EPA to limit the definition accordingly. Similarly, in general SMA supports EPA interpreting the phrase “other appropriate requirement of State law” to constrict the ability of certifying authorities to go outside the bounds of enumerated provisions in the Clean Water Act. Contents of a Certification Decision SMA supports proposed revisions to certification decision documents. The EPA’s approach will promote consistent implementation across all jurisdictions. Clear linkage between conditions and regulatory standards helps permittees understand compliance obligations and avoid conflicting requirements. Requiring certification decisions to cite specific applicable water quality requirements—and removing those requirements pertaining to public notice procedures—will improve transparency and enforceability. Modifications SMA supports proposed revisions to 40 CFR 121.10 clarifying the procedures for modifying a grant of certification; in particular, preventing unilateral modification of grants of certification by certifying authorities. Providing a defined process for applicant involvement in certification modifications promotes transparency, fairness, and better coordination between applicants and certifying authorities. In response to EPA’s specific request for comment on applicant involvement in the development of modifications, SMA notes our support for enhancements to collaboration between applicants and certifying authorities as a general matter. Section 401(a)(2) Process SMA supports EPA’s proposed changes to the Section 401(a)(2) process. Establishing a 90-day deadline for federal agencies to hold a public hearing and make a determination on a state’s objection to issuance of a license or permit will reduce the risk of open-ended delays and allow for predictable resolution of such objections. Furthermore, SMA supports revisions requiring a state to include in an objection a citation to water quality requirements that would be violated by a discharge from a project. Treatment in a Similar Manner as a State SMA supports EPA’s proposed changes that remove the ability of Tribes to obtain treatment in a similar manner as a state for the purposes of Section 401 broadly, and for Section 401(a)(2) specifically. As the agency states, Tribes may obtain this status through existing means to administer water quality standards, a process by which those Tribes may develop rigorous standards that also enhance predictability for applicants in the certification process. Conclusion SMA appreciates the opportunity to provide these comments in response to EPA’s proposed rulemaking updating water quality certification regulations. We support the agency’s efforts to put forward commonsense regulations that encourage economic growth and environmental protection. Sincerely, Brandon Farris Executive Vice President Steel Manufacturers Association

rationales.relevance: The letter directly addresses the EPA rulemaking on water quality certification regulations and responds to multiple specific issues raised in the proposal, including request contents, timelines, scope, decision contents, modifications, Section 401(a)(2), and TAS treatment.
rationales.reasoning: The arguments are generally coherent and consistently tied to a central theme of regulatory certainty, statutory limits, and reduced delay. Some claims are asserted more than deeply analyzed, but the logic is clear and organized.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes a few concrete facts about the steel industry and one citation to congressional testimony, but it relies mostly on policy assertions and general statements rather than detailed data, legal citations, or empirical support.
rationales.impact: It explains likely impacts such as reduced administrative burden, fewer delays, and greater predictability for permittees, and these effects are plausible. However, the impacts are described at a high level and are not quantified or closely demonstrated.
rationales.actionability: The letter offers several clear requests to EPA, such as standardizing required materials, clarifying review scope, limiting open-ended language, and requiring specific citations in decisions. These recommendations are feasible and tied to the rule text.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is professionally formatted, with a formal letter structure, clear headings by issue, and a logical progression from introduction to conclusion. It is readable and easy to follow, though some sections are dense and repetitive.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, well-targeted industry comment letter that is highly relevant and reasonably persuasive, with clear recommendations and good organization. Its main weakness is limited evidentiary depth, but it effectively communicates the association’s policy position.

overall_summary: A focused and professionally written comment letter that strongly engages the proposed rule and offers clear, feasible regulatory recommendations. It is persuasive on policy grounds and well organized, though it would be stronger with more detailed evidence and analysis.

comment_text:
I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive and focuses on the requested issues of safety, transparency, and labeling.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with clear claims about natural labeling, synthetic biology, and allergenicity, though some assertions are stated more strongly than they are demonstrated.
rationales.evidence: The letter includes a few concrete technical references, such as Saccharomyces cerevisiae and specific genes, but it provides little empirical data, citations, or documented examples to support the safety and allergenicity concerns.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies plausible consumer transparency and allergen concerns, but the magnitude of the harms is not quantified and some claimed risks are presented without strong supporting detail.
rationales.actionability: It offers specific requests, including proteomic analysis, clearer labeling standards, and allergen warnings, which are feasible and directly tied to the concerns raised.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is well organized with numbered sections, clear headings, and a professional tone, making it easy to follow.
rationales.overall: This is a focused and well-structured comment with clear policy asks, but it would be stronger with more evidence and more substantiated claims about risk and impact.

overall_summary: A relevant and well-organized comment that clearly opposes synthetic-biology-derived labeling as natural and asks for stricter testing and disclosure. Its main weakness is limited evidence and reliance on asserted concerns rather than documented support.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the approval and labeling of the Phytolon beetroot red color additive, which is clearly on-topic for a docket about food additive regulation and labeling.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with a clear progression from labeling concerns to safety and allergenicity concerns. Some claims are asserted more strongly than they are demonstrated, but the overall logic is understandable.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes some concrete technical references, such as Saccharomyces cerevisiae, BvDODA1, and BvCYP76AD1, but it provides little actual data, citations, or independent evidence to support the safety and allergenicity claims.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible consumer impacts, especially deception and potential allergen exposure, but the magnitude of harm is not quantified and some claims are speculative rather than demonstrated.
rationales.actionability: The letter makes clear requests for proteomic analysis, labeling restrictions, and allergen warnings, which are specific and feasible in regulatory terms, though some requests are broad or absolute.

rationales.overall: This is a relevant and reasonably well-structured public comment that clearly opposes the additive and asks for specific labeling and safety measures. Its main weakness is limited evidentiary support for the more serious safety and allergenicity claims.
