Under both the 2023 and 2020 Rules, a “neighboring jurisdiction” can be a state or a Tribe with “Treatment in a Manner as a State” (TAS) under CWA §518(e). Amendments to the CWA enacted after section 401 provided that Tribes could seek TAS. As a result, when 401(a)(2) uses the word “state” or “states,” EPA has interpreted the words as including states as well as Tribes with TAS status for section 401. EPA also refers to Tribes with TAS for section 401 as “authorized tribes.” 91 CWA §401(a)(2), 33 U.S.C. §1341(a)(2); 40 CFR. §121.13. 27 Comments from the National Association of Wetland Managers February 17, 2026 The Proposed Rule requests recommendations for categories, thresholds, and documentation related to its review of potential cross-boundary effects of a discharge. NAWM believes that a “may affect” determination is inherently fact-dependent and must reflect situation-specific circumstances. Therefore, a detailed list of factors to be considered in all “may affect” determinations are not possible in a nationwide rule, but instead the Rule should be a non-exhaustive illustrative list of factors that EPA may consider. Factors will vary based on the type of discharge and the condition and characteristics of receiving waters, such as whether the receiving water body is TMDL-listed or has specific designated beneficial uses that must be met. Other illustrative factors for consideration could include, for example: • The extent, amount, type, chemical and physical parameters, and constituents of the discharge; • proximity of the discharges to WOTUS or other waters that could carry pollutants to WOTUS; • distance to the neighboring (often downstream, but could be upstream or adjacent to) state or Tribe; • conditions and characteristics of the trans-boundary water included associated wetlands, designated beneficial uses, existing impairments, TMDLs, or proximity of high quality resources; • monitoring results and other documentation related to the discharge and trans- boundary water, such as those related to chemical, physical, and biological integrity; • construction methods and equipment; • operational implementation and related management plans; and • plans for remedial actions for inadvertent discharge. The results of a section 401(a)(2) analysis and process would be more predictable and factually informed if neighboring jurisdictions have an opportunity to identify in advance of a 401(a)(2) action particular key information for EPA, certifying authorities, and other neighboring jurisdictions. Key information could involve watersheds, types and extents of discharges, and categories of activities about which neighboring jurisdictions are concerned. Such information would be particularly helpful to EPA for their “may affect” determinations. The Proposed Rule seeks comment on the appropriateness of categorical and case-specific “may affect” determinations. 92 The preamble notes that the proposal would distinguish “categorical determination” from an exclusion.” 93 A categorical “may affect” determination “refers to a standardized way of reviewing and acting upon notifications that meet a set of criteria for a ‘category’ of discharge types, project types, and/or projects in specific 92 91 Fed.Reg. 2008, 2034 (January 15, 2026). 93 91 Fed.Reg. 2008, 2034 (January 15, 2026). 28 Comments from the National Association of Wetland Managers February 17, 2026 locations.” 94 NAWM has serious concerns about excluding from an affirmative “may affect” determination any areas based upon a lack of hydrologic connection, if the same criteria for the connection is that proposed for wetlands and waters in the recent WOTUS proposal. 95 The CWA recognizes the importance of groundwater. 96 Groundwater connections as well as seasonal or periodic surface water connections should be included as hydrologic connections that may convey discharges to a neighboring jurisdiction. As discussed below, discharge through groundwater connections can serve as the “functional equivalent of a discharge” regulated under the CWA, consistent with the Supreme Court’s decision in the Maui case. 97 The proposal provides an example of a categorical “may affect” determination as where no hydrologic connection exists between the water which would receive discharge and a neighboring jurisdiction, “e.g. discharges into the ocean.” 98 The Proposed Rule’s use of “discharges into the ocean” illustrates the problem with categorical “may affect” determinations. Discharges into the ocean can and often do affect the water quality of neighboring jurisdictions when ocean currents carry pollutants along the coastline. In its 401(a)(2) regulations, EPA should avoid any suggestion that regional general permits, nationwide permits, or other categories of activities or discharges are not subject to section 401, including 401(a)(2). Recommendation: EPA should continue to distinguish “categorical determination” from an exclusion. Any “categorical determinations” should be agreed upon by states, Tribes with TAS, and the federal permitting or licensing agency, and EPA. 6(d) -- Timeframes for section 401(a)(2) Processes The statutory timeline for a “may affect” determination is 30 days for EPA and review and objection by states and Tribes with TAS is another 60 days. 99 These short timelines are appropriate only when all information needed by the neighboring jurisdiction is available up front prior to the commencement of the timeframe, along with a complete application for the permit or license. NAWM supports having a low analytical bar for EPA’s “may affect” decision, making it easier for the Agency to notify neighboring jurisdictions of the possibility of an effect, given the abbreviated timeframe. The Proposed Rule would provide that when objections are raised by neighboring states and Tribes with TAS, federal agencies would have 90 days to hold a public hearing and make a determination on the concerns and objections. NAWM disagrees that the 90-day 94 Id. 95 “Updated Deﬁnition of Waters of the United States” (Docket ID No. EPA–HQ–OW–2025–0322.), in 90 Fed.Reg. 52498 (November 20, 2025). 96 See, e.g., CWA §§102(a) and 304(a)(1). 97 County of Maui v. Hawaii Wildlife Fund, 590 U.S. 165, 183-84 (2020) (holding that the CWA requires a permit “when there is the functional equivalent of a direct discharge.”) 98 91 Fed.Reg. 2008, 2034 (January 15, 2026). 99 CWA §401(a)(2), 33 U.S.C. §1341(a)(2). 29 Comments from the National Association of Wetland Managers February 17, 2026 timeline would provide “enough time” to resolve the process. Public hearing and comment periods for complex projects alone may require a 60-day public notice period. Final decisions on permit or license requirements often require additional time as well. In addition, after the neighboring state or Tribe with TAS has made its objection, the timeframe for holding the public hearing should be of sufficient duration to allow project proponents, EPA, the federal licensing or permitting agency, and neighboring jurisdictions to prepare for testimony, arrange for subject matter experts, and organize data and evidence. The process could be streamlined when information specified by the neighboring jurisdiction is made available, and the neighboring jurisdiction was previously given the opportunity to pre-identify certain waters and discharges and associated conditions that would help ensure that water quality standards are not violated. The CWA does not establish a required timeframe for holding a hearing, so it is appropriate and consistent with the CWA for the hearing to be held only after the appropriate information and logistics can be arranged. This would not “unreasonably delay” projects, a concern noted in the preamble. 100 The Proposed Rule’s alternative approach to begin the 401(a)(2) process at the 6-month mark also fails to result in defensible decisions unless all needed information is provided at the beginning of the process. In some cases, there may be a need to collect data during specific timeframes, analyze the data, and prepare it for presentation at a hearing within a timeframe that may not coincide with the limited timeframes proposed. If there is not adequate time to acquire and analyze the information, NAWM predicts that certification for the license or permit will be denied due to lack of information. Recommendation: EPA should establish suggested, not mandatory, timeframes for hearings under 401(a)(2) that allow sufficient time for public notice, preparing for and holding the public hearing, analysis by the neighboring jurisdiction, and for the federal agency to make informed license or permit decisions that resolve the objections. A deadline of 90 days as a national standard would be too short, failing to accommodate the analytical challenges posed by complex projects. 6(e) -- Removal of EPA ability to request supplemental information and mandating use of an online notification portal The Proposed Rule indicates it would remove the entire section 121.12(c), which authorizes EPA to request supplemental information to inform its “may affect” determination. 101 If that section is removed, the ability of EPA to request supplemental information becomes much less clear, as under the Proposed Rule it is mentioned only in the preamble that an online portal will be used for submitting information. Additional new 100 91 Fed.Reg. 2008, 2035 (January 15, 2026). 101 91 Fed.Reg. 2008, 2032-2033 (January 15, 2026). 30 Comments from the National Association of Wetland Managers February 17, 2026 and existing data may need to be collected on the condition of existing interstate waters, as well as management plans for construction, operations and inadvertent discharges. The submittal of additional information is not prohibited under Section 401(a)(2). Lack of such information would result in less certainty for EPA in making “may affect” determinations. Detailed agreements, especially those developed with states and Tribes with TAS, would better address information needs, unique circumstances, requirements, and resources and ultimately result in a more efficient process. EPA is requesting comment on the proposed standardization of the 401(a)(2) notification process and procurement of any additional information through the use of the Agency’s new online notification portal. 102 NAWM recognizes the efficiencies in review that can be obtained through use of online tools. However, online tools and portals are subject to change over time and require maintenance and upkeep to function as users’ computer hardware and software changes. Online tools can suffer from outages that could cause delays in the already tight timelines built into the section 401(a)(2) process. Recommendation: EPA should retain section 121.12(c) in its current form, which explicitly authorizes EPA to request information in support of its “may affect” determination. Retaining the section will not extend the process since the “may affect” decisions are statutorily capped at 30 days. Due to the short time frame, the bar should remain low for EPA to make a “may affect” determination. EPA should allow, but not require, use of an online portal. 7. Treatment in a Similar Manner as a State (TAS) CWA section 518(e) authorizes EPA to treat eligible federally recognized Indian tribes “in a similar manner as a state” (TAS) for implementing and managing certain environmental programs. Indian tribes that successfully apply to receive TAS may play essentially the same role in Indian country that states do within state lands. Currently, over 80 Tribes have TAS for section 401. How Tribes receive TAS for section 401 has varied over time. Prior to 2023, a Tribe with TAS for the section 303(c) water quality standards program was automatically eligible for TAS for the section 401 water quality certification program unless the Tribe elected not to seek such eligibility. The 303(c) process has tended to be an onerous one that can take many years. The 2023 Certification Rule currently in effect established a procedure for Tribes to directly apply and receive TAS for section 401, as well as a more limited TAS to serve as a neighboring jurisdiction under CWA section 401(a)(2). Under the first option, a Tribe that receives 401 TAS becomes the 401 certifying authority for all federal licenses and permits within their jurisdiction and is considered a neighboring jurisdiction (“other State”) under the section 401(a)(2) process. The second option in the 2023 Rule provides 102 91 Fed.Reg. 2008, 2032-2033 (January 15, 2026). 31 Comments from the National Association of Wetland Managers February 17, 2026 Tribes with the ability to apply for TAS just for 401(a)(2) and be recognized by EPA as a neighboring jurisdiction (“other State”) and participate in the 402(a)(2) process. The 2023 certification regulations recognized the importance of providing streamlined approaches for Tribes to obtain TAS for section 401 and recognition as a neighboring jurisdiction under section 401(a)(2) that would be less labor intensive and take less time than receiving 401 TAS as part of the section 303(c) water quality standards TAS process. The Proposed Rule would repeal the current provisions providing an opportunity for Tribes to apply for TAS for the 401 certification program or for TAS as a neighboring jurisdiction only. Instead, under the proposal Tribes could only elect to receive TAS for 401 as part of receiving TAS for the water quality standards program. The Proposed Rule would remove provisions from the 2023 Certification Rule currently in effect that provides the two pathways for Tribes to apply for and receive TAS for section 401 and to become a neighboring jurisdiction. This proposal would return to TAS for 303(c) as the sole pathway for Tribes to receive TAS for section 401 and participate in the 401(a)(2) process. In other words, EPA would not coordinate with Tribes that lack TAS under 303(c) to determine if a federally licensed or permitted project that receives a 401 certification from another jurisdiction may affect tribal water quality. As discussed earlier, the Proposed Rule should clarify how waters within the jurisdiction of a Tribe without TAS for 401 will be protected. Currently, Tribes with TAS for 401 or for only 401(a)(2) can use certification as a tool to ensure their waters, water quality, and associated impacts to Traditional Resource Rights and Treaty Rights would be appropriately protected under federal licenses and permits. Removal of the option to apply directly for section 401 or section 401(a)(2) TAS significantly impedes the ability of Tribes to ensure protection for tribal aquatic resources, particularly when coupled with the Proposed Rule’s changes that (1) significantly narrow the scope of 401 analysis from the project as a whole to just point source discharges, (2) narrow the scope of information required to be submitted in 401 certification requests to just the footprint of the discharge - not the whole project, and (3) discuss policy options to limit the type of water quality requirements to either EPA-approved, or numeric criteria only, or both. Other EPA actions have even further limited Tribes’ ability to use section 401 as a water quality protection tool, including the recent proposal that would define WOTUS more narrowly, the withdrawal of a proposal that would provide baseline tribal water quality standards, and the frequent failure to consider the Federal Trust Responsibility when EPA acts as the certifying authority for tribal waters. These likely impacts on a Tribe’s ability to protect their waters and resource rights are of paramount concern to NAWM. EPA section 401 regulations must provide Tribes with reasonable access to TAS for section 401. Recommendation: EPA should retain the 2023 Rule’s section 121.11 and its two pathways for Tribes (receive either TAS for all of section 401 certification or TAS only as a 401(a)(2) neighboring jurisdiction ). At 32 Comments from the National Association of Wetland Managers February 17, 2026 present, of the 575 Federally recognized Tribes in the United States, 85 Tribes have TAS authorizing them to develop water quality standards under the 303(c) program. 103 Of those, only 52 Tribes have received EPA approval for their water quality standards and one Tribe has EPA-promulgated water quality standards. These statistics highlight inherent delays and challenges if EPA decides to allow only the section 303(c) TAS program as a Tribe’s sole access to 401 certification authority. 8. Enforceability of Certification Conditions The Clean Water Act’s cooperative federalism framework expressly preserves state and Tribal authority to adopt and enforce water quality requirements, including through CWA section 401. CWA section 510 establishes the right of any state to adopt or enforce any standard or discharge limitations so long as they are at least as stringent as federal requirements. 104 Nothing in the Act indicates that incorporation of certification conditions into a federal license or permit was intended to supersede or limit state or Tribal enforcement authority. NAWM is concerned that the Proposed Rule does not clearly address how enforcement of CWA section 401 certification conditions is intended to function under the revised framework, particularly with regard to the respective roles of federal agencies and state and Tribal certifying authorities. While the Proposed Rule discusses incorporation of certification conditions into federal licenses or permits, it does not adequately explain whether, or to what extent, states and Tribes retain independent authority to enforce those conditions under state or tribal law once they become conditions of a federal authorization. NAWM is further concerned that the Proposed Rule’s silence on this issue may be interpreted to suggest that enforcement of certification conditions rests solely with federal agencies once those conditions are incorporated into a federal license or permit. Such an interpretation would be inconsistent with CWA section 510, represent a significant departure from longstanding practice, and could undermine the ability of states and Tribes to effectively implement and enforce their water quality requirements. Many certifying authorities rely on state or tribal enforcement mechanisms to ensure compliance with certification conditions, particularly where federal agencies may lack the resources, jurisdictional focus, or enforcement priorities necessary to address site-specific or ongoing compliance issues. The absence of clear regulatory or preamble language addressing enforcement also creates uncertainty for regulated entities, certifying authorities, and federal agencies alike. Without clarity, disputes may arise regarding which entity has primary enforcement responsibility, 103 See https://www.epa.gov/tribal/tribes-approved-treatment-state-tas. 104 CWA §510, 33 U.S.C. §1370. 33 Comments from the National Association of Wetland Managers February 17, 2026 whether state or tribal enforcement actions are preempted by federal permitting, and how overlapping enforcement authorities are intended to operate in practice. Recommendation: EPA must clearly acknowledge that incorporation of section 401 certification conditions into a federal license or permit does not supersede or limit state or tribal authority to enforce those conditions under state or tribal law. EPA should also clarify that federal enforcement authority supplements, rather than replaces, state and tribal enforcement authority, consistent with the cooperative federalism framework of the CWA. 9. Other Clarifying and Confirming Revisions 9(a) -- Section 401 Addresses Both Direct and Indirect Discharges. The Proposed Rule would provide that a certification analysis should focus on a discharge from a point source, and that only discharges to WOTUS would be subject to certification. This policy approach must be interpreted and implemented in light of court decisions regarding indirect discharges into WOTUS, such as the 2020 U.S. Supreme Court decision in County of Maui. 105 Maui held that the CWA requires a permit for indirect discharges, such as a discharge via groundwater or other types of nonpoint source, when there is a functional equivalent of a direct discharge. As a result, were the Proposed Rule finalized with a focus on point source discharges, permits would be required for indirect discharges that are the functional equivalent of direct discharges and those permits would be subject to section 401 water quality certification. CWA section 401(a)(1) provides that “[a]ny applicant for a Federal license or permit to conduct any activity including, but not limited to, the construction or operation of facilities, which may result in any discharge into the navigable waters, shall provide the licensing or permitting agency a certification from the State in which the discharge originates or will originate…” This provision does not mandate that the triggering discharge be direct, only that a discharge may enter a WOTUS. Recommendation: EPA should better reflect the County of Maui v. Hawaii Wildlife Fund (2020) U.S. Supreme Court decision and indicate that indirect discharges which trigger a Federal permit also trigger 401 certification, and once 401 is triggered the scope of certification applies broadly to activities and any associated discharges (whether direct, indirect, point source, or nonpoint source). County of Maui v. Hawaii Wildlife Fund, 590 U.S. 165, 183-84 (2020) (holding that the statute requires a permit 105 “when there is the functional equivalent of a direct discharge.”) 34 Comments from the National Association of Wetland Managers February 17, 2026 9(b) -- Automatic Extensions to Address Public Notice Requirements and Force Majeure Events. NAWM is concerned that several additional revisions in the Proposed Rule, taken together, would reduce flexibility for certifying authorities and create new implementation challenges without clear corresponding benefits. For example, the Proposed Rule eliminates automatic extensions previously available to accommodate public notice requirements and force majeure events, and the increased reliance on formal memoranda of agreement (MOAs) to address timing and coordination issues. Many certifying authorities rely on limited extensions to account for circumstances beyond their control, such as public notice requirements, natural disasters, emergencies, or other force majeure events that temporarily disrupt review processes. Eliminating these mechanisms removes an important source of administrative flexibility and may increase the likelihood of denials or inadvertent waivers in circumstances unrelated to the merits of a proposed project. NAWM is concerned that the Proposed Rule does not adequately explain how certifying authorities are expected to manage these unavoidable disruptions within a rigid RPT framework. NAWM is also concerned that the Proposed Rule’s emphasis on formal MOAs as a means of addressing timing and coordination issues may be impractical for many states and Tribes. Developing, negotiating, and periodically revising MOAs can be time-consuming and resource-intensive, particularly given the frequency with which section 401 regulations have changed in recent years. Reliance on MOAs as a primary implementation tool may therefore reduce, rather than enhance, predictability and efficiency for certifying authorities and federal agencies alike. NAWM encourages EPA to provide clear examples in the regulatory text or preamble illustrating how certifying authorities are expected to address public notice requirements, force majeure events, and other unforeseen circumstances under the proposed framework. Without such examples, certifying authorities may face uncertainty regarding permissible approaches to managing review timelines and coordinating with federal agencies. Finally, NAWM notes that these procedural changes interact with other aspects of the Proposed Rule, including limitations on certifying authorities’ ability to define certification request contents. Taken together, these changes reduce certifying authorities’ ability to manage information needs and timing issues in a coordinated manner, increasing the risk of incomplete reviews, unnecessary denials, or inadvertent waivers. Recommendation: EPA should retain the 2023 Certification Rule’s automatic extensions to accommodate public notice requirements, force majeure events, and other circumstances beyond the control of certifying authorities. NAWM further recommends that EPA not rely on formal memoranda of agreement as the primary 35 Comments from the National Association of Wetland Managers February 17, 2026 mechanism for managing reasonable period of time issues, and instead preserve regulatory flexibility within the section 401 regulations themselves. Finally, EPA should provide clear examples in its final certification policy decisions of how to respond to unforeseen circumstances beyond the control of certifying authorities, such as notice requirements and force majeure events. 9(c) -- Adequate Provisions for Tribal Consultation and Input The Proposed Rule contains several provisions directly impacting a Tribe’s ability to use 401 certification as an effective water quality tool. As a result, in finalizing its certification policies, EPA should maximize opportunities for tribal input. The 30-day comment period for this overall proposed rulemaking should be extended by a minimum of 30 additional days, and formal government-to-government consultation letters should be sent to federally recognized Tribes. For each Tribe that requests consultation on these proposed rule changes, time should be made to ensure meaningful engagement and consultation. The July 2025 engagement that the EPA is calling consultation does not substitute for consultation on these specific proposed rule language changes and their potential impacts to Tribes. Tribes should be afforded sufficient time and opportunity to consult and no final certification policy decision should be made until meaningful tribal consultation on the Proposed Rule changes is completed. Recommendation: EPA should send tribal consultation offer letters to Tribes on these Proposed Rule changes and maximize opportunities for tribal input to inform any certification rulemaking decisions. Conclusions While this letter focuses on comments regarding this specific Proposed Rule, the proposal sets a concerning precedent of reducing state and tribal authority in cooperative federalism-based regulatory relationships. Cooperative federalism serves as the cornerstone for most of the nation’s environmental policies. Other non-environmental policies also rely on strong state and tribal roles. NAWM requests EPA to reconsider this Proposed Rule for many reasons, including the problematic move away from cooperative federalism and the precedent it sets for other rules and regulations that are critical to governance and the protection of public goods. The Proposed Rule repeatedly cites recent judicial developments, including Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo, as justification for narrowing state and tribal authority under CWA section 401. NAWM is concerned that this reliance is misplaced. While Loper Bright addresses the scope of judicial deference to agency interpretations, it does not compel the specific regulatory changes proposed here, nor does it require EPA to adopt interpretations that diminish long-recognized state and tribal roles under the CWA. Nothing in Loper Bright 36 Comments from the National Association of Wetland Managers February 17, 2026 alters the statutory text of section 401 or Congress’s express preservation of state and tribal authority within the CWA’s cooperative federalism framework. With its novel interpretation of the law and inconsistencies with U.S. Supreme Court precedent, the Proposed Rule will likely be stayed upon final rule promulgation. This creates tremendous confusion for states and Tribes who will be uncertain about what, how, and when to implement section 401. If the rule is not stayed, there will be new challenges filling the gaps in state and tribal law to support conditions that may leave resources poorly protected with negative impacts to water quality and valuable aquatic resources. In the end, this Proposed Rule limits the law more than the CWA currently allows and will create confusion, delays, and a potential increase in the number of certification denials. The cumulative proposed revisions to the section 401 rule fail to achieve the objectives of the CWA and will worsen water quality and harm all users of waters. NAWM asks EPA to also explore whether this erosion of state and tribal authority will increase costs to relevant industries and jurisdictions, such as commercial/industrial users of water, treatment and drinking water plants and the public. In summary, NAWM opposes the proposed changes to the rule as unwarranted, unproductive, and contrary to the text and intent of the CWA. If EPA proceeds with this rulemaking, as discussed in this letter numerous sections should be deleted or completely revised and re-proposed. The Proposed Rule is the latest in a series of efforts to weaken federal provisions for protecting and restoring waters, to the extent that even state and tribal authority is deemed undesirable. These efforts include multiple policy and regulatory changes, with the most impactful to wetlands and waterways being the reduction in federal jurisdictional waters in the recently proposed WOTUS rule and pending legislative proposals such as the PERMIT Act, 106 which, if enacted, would further alter federal permitting frameworks relevant to section 401. NAWM is concerned that proceeding with this rulemaking in parallel with ongoing statutory and policy changes increases regulatory instability and may necessitate additional rulemakings in the near future, imposing unnecessary administrative burdens on EPA, states, Tribes, and regulated entities alike. The proposed changes to the section 401 certification process, when combined with these other federal policy changes, present a significant and problematic reduction in state and tribal authority and protection of the Nation’s waters. EPA’s Proposed Rule should not be promulgated as it needs significant revision to maintain state and tribal authority and represents a move away from (not towards) cooperative federalism. For these reasons, NAWM requests that EPA withdraw the proposal. The Proposed Rule should not go 106 H.R. 3898, the “Promoting Eﬃcient Review for Modern Infrastructure Today Act or the PERMIT Act.” Available at: H.R.3898 - 119th Congress (2025-2026): PERMIT Act | Congress.gov | Library of Congress 37 Comments from the National Association of Wetland Managers February 17, 2026 forward to a final rule. Should EPA proceed with policy changes in any form involving section 401 certification, NAWM urges that policies be developed in collaboration with states and Tribes. NAWM appreciates the opportunity to comment on EPA’s proposal, “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations.” While these comments have been prepared by NAWM with input from the NAWM Board of Directors, they do not necessarily represent the individual views of all states and tribes; we therefore encourage your full consideration of the comments of individual states and tribes and other state and tribal associations. Please do not hesitate to contact me should you wish to discuss these comments. Sincerely, Portia Osborne Executive Director National Association of Wetland Managers portia@nawm.org (207) 892-3399 Cc: NAWM Board of Directors Jessica Kramer, EPA Assistant Administrator for Water Brian Frazer, Director, EPA Office of Wetlands, Oceans & Watersheds 38

rationales.relevance: The letter is directly on-topic, addressing the specific EPA section 401 certification rulemaking and many of the exact issues implicated by the docket.
rationales.reasoning: Arguments are generally coherent, legally grounded, and organized around discrete regulatory provisions with clear cause-and-effect explanations.
rationales.evidence: The comment is heavily supported by statutory citations, Federal Register references, Supreme Court cases, EPA guidance, and some program data.
rationales.impact: It explains likely impacts in concrete terms, such as delays, denials, waivers, reduced tribal authority, and implementation uncertainty, and these claims are plausible.
rationales.actionability: The letter repeatedly offers clear recommendations, including withdrawing the proposal, retaining the 2023 rule, preserving state/tribal authority, and holding consultation meetings.
rationales.structure_formatting: The submission is professionally formatted with headings, numbered sections, and recommendations, though it is very long and somewhat repetitive in places.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, substantive public comment letter with extensive legal and policy analysis, clear recommendations, and substantial support for its position.

overall_summary: A highly relevant and persuasive comment letter that is well supported by law and examples, with clear requests for EPA action. Its main weakness is length and some repetition, but overall it is a strong submission.

comment_text:
I hope this letter finds you well. I am writing to express my concerns regarding the lack of support for owner operators in enforcing their rights under Rule 49 CFR 371.3. As a stakeholder in the trucking industry, I believe it is crucial for the FMCSA to actively assist and advocate for the rights of owner operators. FMCSA has allowed broker fraud and non-compliance to run rampant and has yet to create the comprehensive enforcement program.

The lack of understanding by the FMCSA concerning this issue is astounding. It is imperative to comprehend that unlawful brokerage activities have far-reaching consequences. When brokers operate without proper oversight, it affects carriers' ability to maintain safe equipment, afford required insurance policies, and comply with licensing and permit requirements. These issues directly impact highway safety and the integrity of our nation's transportation infrastructure.

 Proper enforcement of rules deters unethical practices and enhances safety. The FMCSA must enforce regulations to protect the supply chain's integrity and the trucking industry's operational safety. The current process & lack of enforcement has led to high levels of fraud, predatory practices, and all stakeholders involved suffering. The industry is being exploited and is vulnerable as a result. 

The FMCSA has provided no way to report general freight brokers non-compliance. As a result the integrity & safety of the general freight industry is severely compromised. 

As the industry has evolved, so has the increase of identity theft, fraud, and market manipulation. This necessitates the dire need for regulation & oversight of load boards requirements, record keeping, privacy policies, data sharing, security, rate reporting, and data collection. At this time, there is zero oversight & enforcement upon general freight brokers. 

While we support strengthening the agency's teeth to better enforce the law and rules and regulations against brokers, we wish to point out that FMCSA already has enough teeth to immediately and diligently take a bite out of broker abuse with regards to CFR 371.3.  We respectfully request that they do so at this time.  

We urge Congress to support the FMCSA in these efforts and, if necessary, provide additional legislative clarity to empower the agency to carry out its duties effectively in these specific issues. Your attention to this matter is crucial in upholding the integrity and safety of our nation’s transportation system. 

Respectfully,

rationales.relevance: The comment clearly addresses FMCSA enforcement, broker compliance, and Rule 49 CFR 371.3, which are likely related to the docket topic. However, it is somewhat broad and does not tightly track specific requested issues.
rationales.reasoning: The letter presents a generally coherent argument that weak enforcement harms owner-operators and safety, but the logic is repetitive and sometimes jumps from broker misconduct to broader industry harms without fully connecting the steps.
rationales.evidence: The comment relies mostly on assertions and general claims about fraud, non-compliance, and safety impacts. It includes no citations, data, examples, or specific incidents to substantiate the concerns.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible harms such as fraud, predatory practices, and safety risks, but the impacts are stated in broad terms and lack concrete detail or measurable consequences.
rationales.actionability: The letter asks FMCSA to enforce existing rules and urges Congress to provide clarity if needed, which is actionable at a high level. Still, it does not specify particular enforcement steps, reporting mechanisms, or regulatory changes in detail.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is readable and organized into short paragraphs, with a respectful closing. That said, it is somewhat repetitive and could be more polished and focused for a formal public comment.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and understandable advocacy comment that raises a clear concern about broker enforcement, but it is weakened by limited evidence, broad assertions, and only moderately specific requests.

overall_summary: A relevant but largely unsupported comment that clearly advocates for stronger FMCSA enforcement of broker rules, yet offers limited evidence and only moderate specificity in its recommendations.

comment_text: Perez, JuanB From: Chapman, Eric, ENV <eric.chapman@env.nm.gov> Sent: Wednesday, January 21, 2026 6:46 PM To: OW-Docket; cwa401 Cc: Keyes, Christina, ENV; jonas.armstrong2@env.nm.gov; shelly.lemon@env.nm.gov Subject: Request for extension for Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Some people who received this message don't often get email from eric.chapman@env.nm.gov. Learn why this is important Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Good afternoon, The New Mexico Environment Department (NMED) respectfully requests a 30-day extension of the public comment period for the proposed rule, "Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations," published in the Federal Register on January 15, 2026. The current 30-day comment period, scheduled to close on February 17, 2026, does not provide sufficient time for a meaningful and thorough technical review of a rulemaking that fundamentally alters the landscape of Clean Water Act (CWA) implementation. As the state agency responsible for protecting New Mexico's water resources, NMED requires additional time to provide the EPA with the robust, data-driven feedback necessary for such a significant regulatory shift for the following reasons:  Complexity of Technical and Legal Revisions: This proposal represents a near-total reversal of the 2023 "Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule." Evaluating the impacts of shifting from an "activity as a whole" standard to a "discharge-only" standard requires coordination across multiple technical bureaus within NMED, including our Surface Water Quality, Ground Water Quality, and Legal divisions.  Unique Regional Impacts: New Mexico faces unique hydrological challenges, including a high percentage of ephemeral and intermittent waters. We must conduct a detailed assessment of how the narrowed "scope of review" will interact with recent changes to the definition of "Waters of the United States" (WOTUS). A 30-day window is inadequate to model the potential loss of state oversight for critical projects in our arid environment.  Inter-Agency and Tribal Coordination: NMED works closely with 23 sovereign tribes, nations, and pueblos within New Mexico. Many of these tribal partners are also "certifying authorities" under Section 401. A 30-day extension is necessary to facilitate meaningful consultation and to ensure that the state’s comments reflect a comprehensive understanding of the shared impacts on our water basins.  Public Participation: Given the high level of public interest in water security in the Southwest, NMED intends to gather input from local stakeholders and agricultural communities. The current deadline severely limits our ability to engage these constituents and incorporate their concerns into our official filing. 1 In light of the significant consequences this rule holds for state sovereignty and public health, we believe a 30-day extension is both warranted and necessary to uphold the CWA’s statutory obligation to provide a "reasonable period for participation." We thank you for your consideration of this request and look forward to your response. Respectfully, Eric Chapman, Ph.D. Science Coordinator - Office of Strategic Initiatives New Mexico Environment Department Harold Runnels Building 1190 S St. Francis Dr Santa Fe, NM 87505 Eric.Chapman@env.nm.gov (505) 629-3551 2

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the docket and the requested issue by asking for a 30-day extension to the public comment period on the proposed rule.
rationales.reasoning: The request is logically explained with several coherent reasons: complexity of the rule, regional impacts, inter-agency and tribal coordination, and public participation needs.
rationales.evidence: The letter includes some concrete details, such as the Federal Register date, the current deadline, and references to New Mexico's hydrology and tribal partners, but it provides little empirical data or citations.

rationales.overall: This is a strong, focused procedural comment that clearly requests a specific remedy and gives reasonable justification, but it would be stronger with more supporting evidence and tighter formatting.

overall_summary: A well-targeted and actionable request for more time, grounded in plausible administrative and regional concerns. Its main weakness is the limited use of data or citations to substantiate the need for the extension.

comment_text:
I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive and focuses on the requested issues of safety, transparency, and labeling.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with clear links between synthetic biology production, consumer expectations, and labeling concerns, though some claims are asserted more strongly than demonstrated.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes a few concrete technical references, such as specific genes and the yeast host, but it provides little empirical data, citations, or documented examples to support the safety and allergenicity claims.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible consumer and allergen concerns, but the magnitude and likelihood of the harms are not well quantified or substantiated with specific evidence.
rationales.actionability: The letter makes clear requests for proteomic analysis, labeling standards, and allergen warnings, which are feasible in principle and easy for the agency to understand.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is well organized with numbered sections, clear headings, and a professional tone, making it easy to follow.
rationales.overall: This is a focused and well-structured comment with clear policy asks, but it would be stronger with more evidence and more precise support for the safety and labeling concerns.

overall_summary: A relevant and well-organized comment that clearly opposes misleading labeling and asks for stricter safety review, but it relies more on concern and inference than on documented evidence or quantified impact.

comment_text:
My name is Jason Putney I represent a small motor carrier (18 trucks) called Downeast Shipping.  

TLDR: It is absurd to not legally have to let the service provider (carrier) know what is being paid for their services.  

Adding my small voice into the mix, I'd like to comment that this rulemaking has the potential to make a huge difference to the motor carrier community in general.  For many of us, it's not about having an effect on the rates themselves.  It's about the feeling of not knowing if it's a severely lopsided transaction every single time you haul a load.  It's the feeling of being lied to every single day.  Every motor carrier hears the lie all day every day - "sorry, that's all I've got in it".

Over time, this lie wears you down.  Or maybe it's not a lie!  But we'll never know!  And that's the thing.  Lies are an industry standard in logistics and to me this rule is at least a step in the right direction.  I've become worse and worse of a negotiator over time in these situations because of this feeling of being lied to.  The nature of the transaction typically goes like this...

Broker: "how much do you need to run it?"

Carrier: "this much"

Broker: "Sorry, this is all I have in it"

The carrier's answer to the brokers question "how much do you need to run it?" should only ever be "at least 90% of what the customer is paying"

Carriers should be getting AT LEAST 90% of what any customer is paying.  For shouldering 98% of the liability and expenses associated with servicing the customer.  For a middleman with SIGNIFICANTLY LESS liability and expense to extract 15%+ on any transaction is incredibly disheartening for the people actually involved in moving the shipments.  This industry is severely lopsided with motor carriers shouldering an enormous amount of the burden of inefficiencies involved.  

The freight brokerage industry is unique in the business world.  I haven't been able to find another industry where the middleman or person who works for commissions has full control of the money in the transaction, without having invested any of their own money into it, and can dole out the money in that transaction as they see fit. Every other industry that works for commissions typically has a set commission that is transparent to all parties.  The "commissions" that freight brokers work for do not fit the definition of the word commission at all.  It is absurd to not legally have to let the service provider (carrier) know what is being paid for their services.  

I would take this rule a step further and say that motor carriers deserve to know what customers are paying for their service every time! Not just at request, but as an industry standard.  That level of transparency would lift the hearts and minds of every motor carrier out here.  Knowing that the transaction is fair, knowing they are being paid a fair share of the total.

rationales.relevance: The comment clearly addresses broker transparency and carrier access to payment information, which appears directly related to the likely docket topic. It stays focused on the freight brokerage/motor carrier relationship throughout.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is understandable and generally coherent, but it relies heavily on emotional framing and broad assertions. Some claims are plausible, but the logic is not fully developed or carefully supported.
rationales.evidence: The comment provides almost no concrete evidence, data, citations, or specific examples beyond the author's own experience and general industry observations. Assertions about typical margins and industry practices are not substantiated.
rationales.impact: The commenter explains the perceived emotional and bargaining impacts on carriers, but the magnitude and broader effects are stated in general terms. The claimed benefits of transparency are plausible, though not quantified.
rationales.actionability: The comment makes a clear request for mandatory disclosure of customer payment information to carriers, ideally as an industry standard. However, it does not offer detailed implementation suggestions or address potential tradeoffs.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is readable and organized around a central theme, but it is informal, repetitive, and somewhat rambling. It lacks the structure and polish of a more professional public comment letter.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and understandable advocacy comment with a clear policy preference for greater transparency, but it is weakened by limited evidence, informal presentation, and broad unsupported claims.

overall_summary: A relevant and plainly stated comment that strongly advocates for broker-to-carrier payment transparency, but it relies mostly on personal frustration and general assertions rather than evidence or detailed policy analysis.

comment_text:
Personally, on one hand, I wouldn't be opposed to knowing what the broker is making on the load. But, I'm a realist that believes that everyone involved needs to make money. If I agree to haul for a certain number, that's on me. Although, a little more transparency might help the negotiation process.
On the other hand, in this free market society we live in, you'll always have both carriers & brokers that each think the other is making too much on the load. This may cause a problem with overzealous brokers or carriers. The brokers will always attempt to make the most they can, understandably. As well as the carrier, this is capitalism. I am a carrier, we don't use brokers a lot, but I look to make 25% over my expenses. The cost of everything has gone up substantially in the last few years, and we still need to budget for equipment replacement. Sometimes, 25% works out. Sometimes it doesn't. At the end of the day, I think it would be a good thing to have more transparency between brokers and carriers, for realists like me. Maybe not for carriers thinking of limiting the amount a broker can make, you agreed to the haul rate with the broker, which means it's good for your business in one way or another. If the rate negotiation with the broker can't get you where you need to be, look for something else.

rationales.relevance: The comment is generally on-topic because it discusses broker-carrier transparency and load pricing, but it does not clearly engage any specific docket questions or requested policy issues.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is understandable and mostly coherent, but it relies on broad assertions about free markets and negotiation rather than a tightly developed line of reasoning.
rationales.evidence: It includes one concrete personal benchmark about aiming for 25% over expenses, but otherwise offers no data, citations, or specific examples to support the claims.
rationales.impact: The comment suggests transparency could help negotiations, but it does not explain measurable effects or provide a detailed account of how the policy would affect carriers, brokers, or the market.
rationales.actionability: It offers a general preference for more transparency, but it does not propose a specific rule, threshold, disclosure requirement, or other feasible policy request.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is readable and organized into a simple two-part contrast, but it is informal and lacks the structure of a formal public comment letter.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant but fairly general opinion comment. It expresses a clear viewpoint on transparency in broker-carrier negotiations, but it lacks detailed evidence, specific policy requests, and strong impact analysis.

overall_summary: A moderately relevant and understandable comment that supports more broker-carrier transparency, but it is mostly opinion-based and does not provide strong evidence or concrete policy recommendations.

comment_text: It's about time that brokers are held accountable we has carriers and drivers should be able to the current rates. That way we can negotiate in good faith. And also why are at it. It should be mandatory that shipper and receivers have to use the double fork hi-lo to speed the load and unload process

rationales.relevance: The comment is somewhat relevant because it addresses broker accountability and freight rate transparency, which could relate to transportation policy. However, it also shifts to a separate operational request about loading/unloading equipment without clear connection to the docket topic.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is understandable but loosely organized and contains grammatical issues that make the logic harder to follow. It states a desired outcome, but does not clearly explain how the proposed changes would achieve it.
rationales.evidence: No facts, data, examples, or citations are provided to support the claims about broker accountability, rate visibility, or loading efficiency.
rationales.impact: The comment suggests that transparency would help carriers and drivers negotiate in good faith, but it does not explain the scale of the problem or provide concrete consequences. The loading/unloading request is also not supported with specifics about expected benefits.
rationales.actionability: It offers two general requests: make current rates available and require shippers/receivers to use a double fork hi-lo. These are somewhat actionable, but they are not framed as precise policy recommendations and lack implementation detail.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is very short and informal, with multiple grammatical errors and no clear separation of ideas. It is readable enough to understand the main points, but it is not professionally formatted.
rationales.overall: This is a brief, informal comment that raises a couple of potentially relevant concerns, but it lacks evidence, clarity, and specificity. It is more of a general complaint than a well-developed policy comment.

overall_summary: The comment touches on broker transparency and warehouse efficiency, but it is informal, underdeveloped, and unsupported by evidence. It communicates a basic policy preference without enough detail to be highly persuasive or actionable.

comment_text: UNITED STATES ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (40 C.F.R. Part 121) EXECUTIVE SUMMARY These comments request that EPA define the phrase “Federal license or permit” in 40 C.F.R. Part 121. Recent action by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) requires Clean Water Act §401 certification for hydropower exemptions from licensing (“license exemptions”) and license surrenders, proceedings that do not issue a license or permit and do not authorize a discharge. Historically, FERC interpreted §401—consistent with its early precedent referred to in Order No. 106—to recognize that certification was not required for exemption proceedings because no federal permit issued.1 In 2024, the Commission reinterpreted §401 to apply to any Commission authorization associated with potential water impacts.2 After the Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo (2024), agencies may not expand statutory scope based on 1 “In addition, if no license is issued, there is no applicable section 401 requirement.” Exemption From All or Part of Part I of the Federal Power Act of Small Hydroelectric Power Projects With an Installed Capacity of Five Megawatts or Less, Order No. 106, 45 Fed. Reg. 76115, 76120 (Nov. 18, 1980). See also, Philip Leber, Clean Water Act Permits and Hydroelectric Projects: What Are the Requirements?, Hydro Review, Winter 1983, at 18. 2 Establishment of Categorical Reasonable Period of Time for Action on Requests for Water Quality Certification Under Section 401(a)(1) of the Clean Water Act and Clarifying Types of Hydroelectric Project Proceedings That May Require Water Quality Certification, Order No. 2023, 189 FERC ¶ 61,130 (Nov. 21, 2024). 1 policy preferences or ambiguity. The statutory text of §401 ties certification to issuance of a federal license or permit for a discharge.3 A proceeding determining that a §401 certification of a license is unnecessary does not satisfy that condition. EPA should therefore clarify that §401 certification applies only to discharge-authorizing permits. Absent clarification, federal and state agencies may effectively nullify congressionally created hydropower exemptions under PURPA by imposing licensing-equivalent procedures. 3 Loper Bright Enters. v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369, 144 S. Ct. 2244, 2265–68 (2024)(holding courts must exercise independent judgment in interpreting statutes and may not defer to agency interpretations merely because a statute is ambiguous). 2 UNITED STATES ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (40 C.F.R. Part 121) Comments of Paul V. Nolan, Esq. I. Introduction These comments address a critical jurisdictional gap in the proposed Section 401 rule: the Environmental Protection Agency (“EPA” or “Agency”) has not defined what constitutes a “Federal license or permit” that triggers the need for Clean Water Act (“CWA”) §401 certification. The issue is now urgent. The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) recently adopted a policy requiring §401 certification for certain proceedings that are neither licenses nor permits, including hydropower exemptions from licensing and license surrender approvals. See Establishment of Categorical Reasonable Period of Time for Action on Requests for Water Quality Certification under Section 401(a)(1) of the Clean Water Act and Clarifying Types of Hydroelectric Project Proceedings that May Require Water Quality Certification, 189 FERC ¶ 61,130 (2024). Prior to the 2024 FERC policy, some state certifying agencies had likewise required certifications even while acknowledging that §401 does not require them. The Vermont Agency of Natural Resources’ website for hydro 401 certification, for example, states that FERC does not require a §401 certification before acting on a FERC exemption application, but the Secretary deems certification necessary to fulfill obligations in the FERC process as a fish and wildlife resource agency.4 4 “FERC hydropower license. FERC does not require a §401 certification before acting on a FERC exemption application, but the Secretary deems certification 3 The proposed rule must clarify the statutory trigger for certification. Without clarification, the rule will facilitate unlawful expansion of federal authority and create inconsistent national administration of §401. II. The Statutory Structure of Section 401 Section 401(a)(1) applies only when a Federal agency issues a license or permit for an activity that may result in a discharge into navigable waters. The trigger is therefore not federal involvement and not federal jurisdiction. It is a federal authorization for a discharge. Section 401 is a discharge-based statute. It ensures compliance with water quality standards before federal pollution-authorizing permits issue. It is not a general environmental review mechanism and does not attach to jurisdictional determinations. The proposed rule repeatedly references “licenses and permits” but never defines them. That omission now has concrete regulatory consequences. III. Congress Created Hydropower Exemptions to Reduce Federal Licensing In 1978 Congress enacted the Public Utility Regulatory Policies Act (“PURPA”), amending the Federal Power Act (“FPA”). Congress determined that the federal hydropower licensing program itself had become a barrier to small renewable energy development. Prior to PURPA, any non-federal hydropower project within federal necessary to fulfill the Secretary’s obligations in the FERC process as a fish and wildlife resource agency” -- https://dec.vermont.gov/watershed/business- support/water-quality-certification-section-401 (last visited February 16, 2026). 4 jurisdiction required a full FERC license regardless of size. Congress found that the licensing regime prevented development of small hydroelectric generation at existing dams and historic mill sites. Congress therefore reduced federal regulation. PURPA did not create a simplified license. Congress instead created an exemption from licensing. An exemption is a jurisdictional withdrawal and a statutory determination that a project shall not be licensed. IV. The Legal Meaning of an Exemption A hydropower exemption is not a permit, not a license, does not authorize a discharge, and does not substitute for a license. It is Congress’s directive that licensing authority does not apply. The Clean Water Act was enacted in 1972 — six years before Congress created hydropower exemptions. When Congress later created exemptions, it did not amend §401 to include them. If §401 applies to an exemption, a statutory removal of licensing becomes a licensing requirement, reversing the statutory structure. V. FERC’s New Certification Policy FERC now requires §401 certification for proceedings associated with hydropower exemptions and license surrenders. 189 FERC ¶ 61,130 (2024). The Commission states that authorizations potentially associated with water impacts require certification even where no license is issued. Requiring certification for an exemption converts the exemption process into a licensing process — the very result Congress enacted PURPA to avoid. 5 VI. The Requirement Is Contrary to PURPA’s Purpose Congress enacted hydropower exemptions to rapidly expand small renewable generation at existing dams. Conditioning an exemption on §401 certification restores the regulatory burden Congress intentionally removed and effectively nullifies the exemption statute. The proceeding designed to avoid licensing becomes functionally indistinguishable from licensing. This conflicts with national energy policy encouraging distributed renewable generation. VII. State Practice Demonstrates the Problem Some certifying agencies require certification despite acknowledging it is not required. Section 401 is thus used as a procedural intervention mechanism rather than a water-quality certification. Section 401 does not authorize states to impose certification requirements where no federal permit exists. VIII. Recommended Regulatory Clarification EPA should define the statutory trigger in 40 C.F.R. §121.1 as a federal authorization that itself authorizes a discharge of pollutants into waters of the United States. EPA should also clarify that §401 does not apply to hydropower licensing exemptions, license surrender approvals, jurisdictional determinations, or proceedings that do not authorize a discharge. 6 Additional Analysis: FERC’s Reinterpretation of Section 401 and Prior Practice -- Order No. 106 For decades prior to its 2024 certification policy, FERC’s practice recognized that §401 certification was not required for hydropower exemption proceedings because no federal license or permit issued. Contemporaneous commentary by a former attorney in the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission’s Office of General Counsel explained that Clean Water Act §401 applies only where a federal license is issued and therefore does not apply to hydropower licensing exemptions because an exemption proceeding determines that no license is required. Philip Leber, Clean Water Act Permits and Hydroelectric Projects: What Are the Requirements?, Hydro Review, Winter 1983, at 18. This interpretation was consistent with the Commission’s exemption rulemaking, which stated that “if no license is issued, there is no applicable section 401 requirement.” Exemption From All or Part of Part I of the Federal Power Act of Small Hydroelectric Power Projects With an Installed Capacity of Five Megawatts or Less, Order No. 106, 45 Fed. Reg. 76,115, 76,120 (Nov. 18, 1980). The exemption proceeding determined the absence of licensing authority rather than the issuance of a federal permit. In its 2024 rulemaking, 189 FERC ¶ 61,130, the Commission reinterpreted §401 to apply broadly to Commission ‘authorizations,’ including exemptions and license surrender approvals. This reinterpretation rests on an expanded reading of §401 rather than on any statutory amendment. The Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo requires courts and agencies to apply the best reading of statutory text. Section 401(a)(1) applies when a federal agency issues a license or permit for an activity that may result in a discharge. An exemption proceeding does not issue a license, does not issue a permit, and does not 7 authorize a discharge. The same is true for orders approving a license’s surrender. Accordingly, requiring certification for an exemption is inconsistent with the statutory text and converts a congressional withdrawal of licensing jurisdiction into a federal permitting requirement. EPA should clarify the meaning of ‘license or permit’ to avoid interagency conflict and ensure consistent national application of §401. Hydropower licensing exemptions were enacted to reduce federal licensing and encourage small renewable generation. Requiring §401 certification contradicts both the Clean Water Act and the Federal Power Act and undermines PURPA’s purpose. Section 401 applies only to discharge-authorizing permits, not to proceedings determining that no federal license is required. EPA should define “Federal license or permit” to ensure consistent national administration of §401. Respectfully submitted, ____________________________________ Paul V. Nolan, Esq. 5515 17th Street Arlington, VA 22205-2722 Email: pvnppvndiver@gmail.com 8

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the docket topic by urging EPA to define "Federal license or permit" in the Section 401 regulations and ties that issue to hydropower exemptions and license surrenders.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and follows a clear statutory theory: Section 401 should apply only to discharge-authorizing licenses or permits, and exemptions are not such authorizations. Some assertions are somewhat conclusory, but the logic is consistent.
rationales.evidence: The comment cites statutory history, FERC Order No. 106, the 2024 FERC order, Loper Bright, and a Vermont agency webpage. It uses concrete examples and citations, though it relies more on legal interpretation than empirical data.
rationales.impact: It explains plausible regulatory and practical impacts, such as inconsistent administration and potential erosion of hydropower exemptions, but the magnitude of those impacts is not quantified.
rationales.actionability: The comment offers clear recommendations: define the trigger in 40 C.F.R. § 121.1 and clarify that §401 does not apply to exemptions, surrender approvals, or jurisdictional determinations. These requests are specific and feasible.
rationales.structure_formatting: The submission is well organized with an executive summary, numbered sections, and a concluding recommendation. It is professional and readable, though somewhat repetitive in places.
rationales.overall: A strong, focused legal comment that is highly relevant and reasonably well supported. Its main weakness is limited empirical evidence and some overstatement of consequences, but it provides a clear regulatory ask grounded in statutory interpretation.

overall_summary: This is a well-targeted legal comment that squarely addresses the docket issue and proposes a concrete regulatory clarification. It is strongest on relevance and actionability, with solid legal reasoning and citations, though it relies more on doctrinal argument than factual evidence.

comment_text: February 17, 2026 Submitted via Federal eRulemaking Portal at regulations.gov Ms. Stacey Jensen Oceans, Wetlands and Communities Division Office of Water (4504T) U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue, N.W. Washington, D.C. 20460 Re: Joint Comments from APPA and NRECA on Proposed Rule “Updating the Water Quality Certiﬁcation Regulation,” Docket No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Dear Ms. Jensen, The American Public Power Association (APPA) and the National Rural Electric Cooperative Association (NRECA) (the Associations) respectfully submit these comments to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) on the proposed rule entitled “Updating the Water Quality Certiﬁcation Regulations” (the Proposal) which updates and clariﬁes important requirements for water quality certiﬁcation under Clean Water Act (CWA or Act) section 401. 1 APPA and NRECA are the national trade associations representing rural electric cooperatives and public power utilities nationwide. Together, we provide power to one in four Americans. The Associations represent not-for-proﬁt electric utilities that are actively working to address rising electricity demand by building and maintaining critical energy infrastructure, from power plants to substations. The Association's members also own or operate numerous hydroelectric facilities across the country which provide affordable, reliable electricity to their communities. APPA and NRECA have a signiﬁcant interest in the Proposal since the construction of new infrastructure and the re-licensing of existing infrastructure like hydroelectric facilities often requires certiﬁcation under CWA § 401, and the proper and efficient implementation of the CWA § 401 certiﬁcation process can have 1 91 Fed. Reg. 2008 (January 15, 2026). 1 signiﬁcant implications on a project, and especially on a project constructed and operated by rural and public power electric utilities such as those represented by APPA and NRECA. I. Executive Summary The CWA establishes a framework for shared responsibilities among the federal government, states, and tribes. APPA and NRECA recognize and support this balance, including the role of states and tribes to serve as certifying authorities under CWA § 401. However, while most states have acted in good faith to certify, waive certiﬁcation, or request conditions under CWA § 401, some bad actors have sought to weaponize the program by going beyond the parameters established by Congress to either block projects for concerns having little to nothing to do with water quality or weigh down projects with costly conditions that project proponents often have no choice but to accept if they want their project to proceed. Such actions can delay or block important projects that APPA and NRECA members are undertaking to respond to the country’s surging energy demand. Such actions can also add unnecessary costs that are ultimately passed down to consumers. EPA’s Proposal seeks to establish an appropriate scope for the factors that may be considered by states and tribes under the statute and makes other important changes to the CWA § 401 certiﬁcation process that will provide APPA and NRECA members with regulatory clarity and facilitate their continued goal of providing reliable and affordable energy. APPA and NRECA thus support the proposal in general and offer several recommendations to further improve the CWA § 401 certiﬁcation process as noted below. • Clarify exclusions from CWA § 401 certiﬁcation to conﬁrm that activities resulting only in incidental fallback do not constitute a discharge requiring certiﬁcation, consistent with existing Corps regulations, in order to prevent unnecessary delays, costs, and misapplication of CWA § 401 authority. • Establish an administrative appeals process allowing applicants to challenge improper CWA § 401 certiﬁcation decisions—such as overly broad scope determinations or misapplied water quality standards—through a limited, timely federal review before resorting to litigation, improving transparency, consistency, and regulatory certainty. • Maintain CWA § 401 certiﬁcation at the general permit level, particularly for Nationwide Permits, as upfront certiﬁcation is far more efficient than project-by-project review and is essential to preserving routine maintenance, repair, and reliability work critical to electric service, especially for public power utilities and rural electric cooperatives. II. Overview of America’s Electric Cooperatives and Public Power Utilities APPA and NRECA members are committed to providing affordable, reliable, and safe electricity while advancing energy innovation and integrating more renewable resources. 2 As not-for-profit utilities, they must balance environmental compliance and replacement power costs with limited financing options. These costs are ultimately borne by the communities they serve. The financial burdens imposed by the current rule place additional strain on small utilities with fewer resources, thereby extending project timelines. Together, APPA and NRECA members serve one in four of the nation’s electricity customers. We therefore share a strong interest in environmental policies that reflect small-entity considerations, provide feasible compliance timelines, support reliable electric service, and achieve cost-effective outcomes. A. About APPA APPA is a trade association composed of not-for-proﬁt, community-owned utilities that provide electricity to 2,000 towns and cities nationwide. APPA protects the interests of nearly 55 million people served by public power utilities and the 96,000 people they employ. APPA advocates and advises on electricity policy, technology, trends, training, and operations. APPA members strengthen their communities by providing superior service, engaging citizens, and instilling pride in community-owned power. APPA and our members have and continue to be dedicated to clean air in our communities and the protection of the environment. Many APPA members serve smaller communities. Approximately 1,300 of the nation’s 2,000 public power utilities have 10 or fewer employees and serve towns, villages, or counties with fewer than 10,000 people, and all but 144 of the nation’s public power utilities would be considered a “small governmental jurisdiction” under the Regulatory Flexibility Act, 5 U.S.C. §§ 601-612 (RFA). Public power utilities operate in 49 states (all but Hawaii) and in ﬁve U.S. territories (American Samoa, Guam, the Northern Mariana Islands, Puerto Rico, and the U.S. Virgin Islands). B. About NRECA NRECA is the national trade association representing nearly 900 not-for-proﬁt electric cooperatives and other rural electric utilities. America’s electric cooperatives are owned by the people that they serve and comprise a unique sector of the electric industry. NRECA’s member cooperatives include 64 generation and transmission (G&T) cooperatives and 830 distribution cooperatives. Electric cooperatives provide power to one in eight Americans and serve as engines of economic development for 42 million people across 56% of the nation’s landmass. They own and maintain 2.7 million miles, or 42%, of the nation’s electric distribution lines and serve large expanses of the United States that are primarily residential and typically sparsely populated. Those characteristics make it comparatively more expensive for rural electric cooperatives to operate than the rest of the electric sector, which tends to serve more compact, industrialized, and densely populated areas. 3 As not-for-proﬁt entities, electric cooperatives are unique in the way they are ﬁnanced. Cooperatives have no equity shareholders who can bear the costs of stranded generation assets, investment in new or alternative generation resources, or extensive upgrades to networks and systems. Cooperatives do not have a rate of return on equity as do investor- owned utilities. All costs are passed through directly to each cooperative’s consumer- members via increased electricity rates. Locally, cooperatives are focused on powering and empowering their communities. Nationally, electric cooperatives are focused on advocating for smart energy policy that keeps the lights on. This includes pressing for solutions to meet increasing energy demands at a cost local families and businesses can afford. III. Background In 2020, EPA promulgated the first rule to establish a standardized process for CWA § 401 certification, superseding practices that had been in place since before the enactment of the Clean Water Act.
