comment_text: From: annikafjelstad@everyactioncustom.com To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/9/2026 10:26:09 PM Subject: Docket ID# EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 — Opposition to Proposed 2026 Changes to Circumscribe Certification Authority of States and Tribes -------------------------------------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------- [You don't often get email from annikafjelstad@everyactioncustom.com. Learn why this is important at https://aka.ms/LearnAboutSenderIdentification ] Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Dear Administrator Lee Zeldin, I strongly oppose the EPA’s proposed 2026 rule changes that would circumscribe certification authority used by States and Tribes to protect clean water. The Clean Water Act was adopted by Congress to restore and maintain clean water and to ensure that States and Tribes could prevent violation of the water quality requirements they have adopted. The purpose of the EPA is to protect natural resources such as water, NOT to protect polluters. The people with the most at stake are the local states or tribal nations whose residents depend on the water for life. These people absolutely must maintain the ability to set standards for clean water. The EPA’s proposed 2026 certification rules are unlawful, unreasonable, and just plain wrong. Specifically: 1. ) I oppose the Rule’s elimination of current regulations facilitating Tribal certification authority and supporting neighboring/downstream Tribes in objecting to federal permits and preventing violations of Reservation water quality requirements. Shame on you for erasing Tribes from these regulations. 2. ) I oppose the Rule’s restrictions that would prevent any State or Tribe in the United States from setting conditions on Federal “general permits.” In Minnesota, we’ve used existing certification authority to prevent degradation and protect streams, Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters, and Outstanding Resource Value waters, like the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park. 3. ) I oppose all the Rule’s changes that tighten a noose around the scope of certification. They would prevent conditions or denial of Federal permits even if activities would violate State and Tribal standards and contaminate lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or seepage through groundwater into surface water. 4. ) I oppose the Rule’s forced waiver of State or Tribal certification after a year even if time is needed due to public process, acts of God, or because the project proposer concealed the truth about water impacts in its draft permit application. This rule would encourage polluters to hide the facts and do a bait-and-switch after time has run out for certification. 5. ) I oppose the Rule’s creation of new power in the EPA Administrator to determine without guardrails that an entire category of discharges will never affect downstream States or Tribes and deprive them of authority to object to violations of water quality requirements. What category would this EPA remove from certification review? All pipeline discharge? All mining waste? The EPA must reject or withdraw its 2026 proposed rule to undermine State and Tribal certification. Existing rules under the Clean Water Act do a much better job of protecting us and our children from contaminated water that they can’t safely drink, swim in, or fish. Thank you for considering my comments, Sincerely, Annika Fjelstad 4436 Aldrich Ave S Minneapolis, MN 55419-4822 annikafjelstad@gmail.com

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the docket topic: EPA’s proposed changes to state and tribal water quality certification authority under the Clean Water Act.
rationales.reasoning: The letter presents a clear opposition argument with several specific objections, and the logic is generally coherent, though some claims are more rhetorical than fully developed.
rationales.evidence: It includes a few concrete examples, such as Minnesota waters and places like the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park, but it does not provide citations, data, or detailed supporting facts.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies plausible harms, including weakened tribal authority, degraded water quality, and reduced public protections, but the impacts are stated broadly rather than quantified or deeply substantiated.
rationales.actionability: It clearly asks EPA to reject or withdraw the proposed rule and identifies several provisions the commenter opposes, making the requested action understandable and feasible.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is organized into a brief introduction, numbered points, and a closing, which aids readability, though the formatting is somewhat informal and includes repetitive or emotionally charged language.
rationales.overall: This is a strong public comment in terms of relevance and clear opposition, with some concrete examples and actionable requests, but it would be stronger with more evidence and a more formal, tightly reasoned presentation.

overall_summary: A relevant and clearly opposed comment that is easy to follow and includes specific requested outcomes, but it relies more on advocacy language than detailed evidence or technical support.

comment_text: I NDI ANA DE P ART ME NT O F E NV I RONME NT AL MANA G E ME NT 100 N. Senate Avenue • Indianapolis, IN 46204 (800) 451-6027 • (317) 232-8603 • Fax (317) 233-6647 • www.idem.IN.gov Mike Braun Clint Woods Governor Commissioner February 17, 2026 The Honorable Lee M. Zeldin Administrator U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Ave. NW, 1101A Washington, D.C. 20590 Submitted Electronically via Regulations.gov Re: Comments on Proposed Rulemaking “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations” by the Indiana Department of Environmental Management, Docket No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929, FRL-6976.2-01-OW Dear Administrator Zeldin: On January 15, 2026, the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) published in the Federal Register the proposed rule “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations” (91 Fed. Reg. 2008), to update and clarify several substantive and procedural requirements for water quality certification under the Clean Water Act (CWA). The Indiana Department of Environmental Management (IDEM), as the state agency responsible for the daily implementation of the CWA water quality programs and the certifying authority for Section 401 water quality certifications in Indiana, is pleased to provide these comments in support of the proposed rule. The proposed rule offers common sense updates and a significant course correction imposed under the final rule “Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule” issued on September 27, 2023, at 88 Fed. Reg. 66558 (the “2023 Rule”). Most notably, the proposed rule would revise several procedural and substantive aspects of the 2023 Rule. IDEM previously provided feedback on EPA’s July 7, 2025, request for input on implementation challenges or regulatory uncertainty associated with the 2023 Rule at 90 Fed. Reg. 29828. As noted in that feedback, IDEM has effectively implemented the 2023 Rule, but noted areas where additional clarity may be appropriate. IDEM supports the revision and amendment to § 121.3, the scope of certification, and § 121.7, certification decisions. With respect to the revision made to § 121.3, IDEM agrees that “[t]he plain language of CWA section 401(a) directs states to certify that any discharge resulting from the proposed Federally licensed or permitted activity will comply with the enumerated provisions of the CWA.” 91 Fed. Reg. at 2023. As EPA points out, this interpretation is further supported by the revisions made in 1972 to CWA section 401, changing “activity” to “discharge.” 91 Fed. Reg. at 2024-25. As such, IDEM agrees that EPA’s interpretation is the best reading of Section 401. See Loper Bright v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369, 400 (2024). Additionally, IDEM is supportive of EPA requiring certifying authorities to include specific contents in all certification decisions with the amendments to § 121.7. Currently, IDEM’s certification conditions are within the appropriate scope, the language of which was successfully coordinated with Indiana’s three Corps Districts. The language used to support each condition includes legal citations to support conditions that are included to protect water quality. IDEM seeks clarity on a scenario in which our federal partners were to disagree and exclude a condition. Regarding the proposed changes to the Section 401(a)(2) process, IDEM is supportive of the proposed changes and is confident in its ability to comply. For coordination with neighboring jurisdictions, IDEM has previously successfully worked with Kentucky and reviewed and supported the conditions Kentucky required on projects proposing discharge of dredge and fill to the Ohio River. So far, all the projects, when permitted with the conditions in Kentucky’s 401 water quality certifications, comply with Indiana’s water quality standards. IDEM appreciates the opportunity to review these projects and believes the requirement to coordinate is not overly burdensome and will help streamline certifications. EPA has requested comment on the proposed contents of a request for certification under § 121.5. IDEM believes the proposed rule would help provide clear and specific guidance on what is needed to fulfill the proposed certification request. IDEM appreciates the opportunity to comment on EPA’s proposed rule “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations.” IDEM supports EPA’s underlying rationale for reversing the 2023 Rule’s “activity as a whole” approach because Loper Bright requires that agencies adopt the “best reading” of the statute, rather than merely a “reasonable” reading under the Chevron deference doctrine. 91 Fed. Reg. at 2023. IDEM agrees with EPA that the 2023 Rule interpretation “does not reflect the best reading of the statute.” Id. In short, Loper Bright compels EPA to reevaluate whether its interpretation is the “best reading” of the statute, and the textual arguments set forth by EPA in the proposed rule support a discharge-focused approach. IDEM does not anticipate the revisions in the proposed rule to detract from its ability to maintain compliance. IDEM looks forward to continued collaboration on CWA implementation issues with EPA. Sincerely, Clint Woods Commissioner Indiana Department of Environmental Management

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the docketed rule on water quality certification regulations and responds to several specific provisions, including §§ 121.3, 121.5, 121.7, and the section 401(a)(2) process.
rationales.reasoning: The letter is generally coherent and logically organized, with clear support for EPA’s proposed changes and a consistent statutory interpretation argument. Some points are more conclusory than deeply developed.
rationales.evidence: It cites the Federal Register notice, the 2023 Rule, and Loper Bright, and includes a few concrete examples from Indiana’s coordination with Corps districts and Kentucky. However, it relies more on legal assertions than empirical evidence.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies practical implications for Indiana’s implementation and mentions that the rule would not be overly burdensome, but the claimed impacts are fairly general and not quantified or extensively demonstrated.
rationales.actionability: It supports the proposal and asks for clarity on a scenario involving disagreement with federal partners, but it offers limited specific recommendations or requested edits beyond general approval.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is professionally formatted, formally addressed, and readable, with a clear opening, body, and closing. It is somewhat dense and repetitive in places, but overall well organized.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, relevant agency comment letter that clearly supports the proposed rule and engages with key regulatory provisions. It is persuasive and professionally presented, though it would be stronger with more detailed evidence, sharper impact analysis, and more specific requested changes.

overall_summary: A relevant and well-written agency comment that closely tracks the proposed rule and provides solid legal support, but it is more supportive than directive and offers limited concrete evidence or detailed recommendations.

comment_text: February 17, 2026 Ms. Lauren Kasparek U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 Docket No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 [SUBMITTED ELECTRONICALLY] RE: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations The Edison Electric Institute (EEI) appreciates the opportunity to submit comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA or Agency) proposed rule, Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (Proposed Rule). 91 Fed. Reg. 2,008 (January 15, 2026). EEI generally supports the Proposed Rule, and in these comments offers modifications to further improve the certification process and facilitate the continued buildout of infrastructure required to meet American energy needs. EEI is the leading voice and advocate for investor-owned electric companies in the United States, which serve more than 250 million customers in 50 states and the District of Columbia. EEI member companies work every day to provide reliable energy to their customers at the lowest cost possible. EEI member companies also form the backbone of our nation’s electric power industry, which is essential to advancing our economic and national security priorities, including achieving global dominance in artificial intelligence (AI) and advanced computing technologies. Over the next five years, investor-owned electric companies will invest more than $1.1 trillion to enhance the electric grid, building critically needed new generation and transmission and distribution infrastructure required to power economic prosperity. EEI members own, operate, build, and maintain electric power generation, transmission, and distribution facilities requiring federal licenses or permits that deliver electric power throughout the U.S. These activities may result in discharges into navigable waters. EEI members are therefore directly impacted by the CWA Section 401 certification process and have a significant interest in its implementation. An efficient, consistent, and predictable certification process that simultaneously protects water quality is critical to EEI members’ goals of continuing to provide reliable energy at the lowest cost. Questions on these comments may be directed to Patrick McGuire (202-508-5167) or Daniel Whittle (202-508-5990). Sincerely, M. Patrick McGuire Associate General Counsel COMMENTS FROM THE EDISON ELECTRIC INSTITUTE ON THE U.S. ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION AGENCY’S PROPOSED RULE, UPDATING WATER QUALITY CERTIFICATION REGULATIONS Docket No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 February 17, 2026 The Edison Electric Institute (EEI) appreciates the opportunity to submit comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA or Agency) proposed rule, Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (Proposed Rule). 91 Fed. Reg. 2,008 (January 15, 2026). Through the Proposed Rule, EPA plans to update and clarify several substantive and procedural requirements for water quality certification under Clean Water Act (CWA or the Act) Section 401. EEI generally supports the Proposed Rule, and in these comments offers modifications to further improve the certification process and facilitate the continued buildout of infrastructure required to meet American energy needs. EEI is the leading voice and advocate for investor-owned electric companies in the United States, which serve more than 250 million customers in 50 states and the District of Columbia. EEI member companies work every day to provide reliable energy to their customers at the lowest cost possible. EEI member companies also form the backbone of our nation’s electric power industry, which is essential to advancing our economic and national security priorities, including achieving global dominance in artificial intelligence (AI) and advanced computing technologies. Over the next five years, investor-owned electric companies will invest more than $1.1 trillion to enhance the electric grid, building critically needed new generation and transmission and distribution infrastructure required to power economic prosperity. 1 CWA Section 401 grants states and Tribes that have been approved for “treatment as a state” status the authority to review activities requiring a federal license or permit that may result in a discharge into “navigable waters” to certify that any such discharge will comply with certain applicable federal, state, and tribal water quality requirements. See 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a). CWA Section 401 thus carves out an important water quality protection role for states and authorized Tribes in federal licensing and permitting processes. EEI members own, operate, build, and maintain electric power generation, transmission, and distribution facilities requiring federal licenses or permits that deliver electric power throughout the U.S. These activities may result in discharges into navigable waters. EEI members are therefore directly impacted by the CWA Section 401 certification process and have a significant interest in its implementation. An efficient, consistent, and predictable certification process that simultaneously protects water quality is critical to EEI members’ goals of continuing to provide reliable energy at the lowest cost. I. Electric Companies Are Investing to Meet Unprecedented Demand Growth. Across the country, investor-owned electric companies are investing in a broad range of technologies to deliver reliable power at the lowest reasonable cost to meet customers’ needs while also satisfying federal and state requirements. Though EEI member companies operate in differing regulatory environments and face varied customer demands, we share a clear message: to meet our nation’s needs in this moment, we must use every available tool to deploy new generation capacity and maximize the capabilities of our transmission and distribution infrastructure. 2 As EEI members drive to serve our customers at the lowest cost, customer needs are rapidly growing. The power sector is facing unprecedented levels of demand growth. Projections consistently show a significant amount of new generation capacity must come online in the years ahead, mostly due to increasing manufacturing and the rapid proliferation of AI and advanced computing technologies. This represents both a tremendous opportunity and a challenge for the power sector, after nearly two decades of minimal increases in net electricity demand and in the face of high development costs, supply chain and workforce pressures, affordability concerns for customers, and siting and permitting barriers. EEI members are well-positioned to meet this challenge and support this national priority, with plans for $1.1 trillion in grid investments during the next five years to better serve our customers and facilitate data center and major industrial development, while also achieving reliability and securing broader benefits for existing customers and communities. II. EEI Supports Finalization of EPA’s Clarification Regarding the Scope of Certification. EPA proposes to clarify that “[t]he scope of certification is limited to assuring that a discharge from a federally licensed or permitted activity will comply with applicable and appropriate water quality requirements.” 91 Fed. Reg. at 2,040 (to be codified at 40 C.F.R. § 121.3). Further, EPA proposed to define “discharge” as a “discharge from a point source into waters of the United States.” Id. (to be codified at 40 C.F.R. § 121.1). EEI supports finalizing this clarification. CWA Section 401(a)(1) states that the state licensing or permitting agency must certify that “any such discharge will comply with” applicable provisions of the CWA for any applicant for a federal license or permit to conduct any activity that “may result in any discharge into the navigable waters.” See 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1). This section refers to the 3 importance of the discharge itself for a certification multiple times. Therefore, EPA’s clarification aligns the regulatory text with both the language and the intent of the CWA. III. EEI Supports Clarification of the One-Year Certification Timeframe. CWA Section 401 states that the certifying authority must act on a request for certification “within a reasonable period of time (which shall not exceed one year).” See 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1). EEI members have experienced complications in multiple states regarding the one- year timeframe and therefore EEI supports EPA clarifying this timeframe in the Proposed Rule. Specifically, EEI supports EPA’s proposal to clarify that the timeline starts when the application is submitted; that resubmittal of additional information does not restart the timeline; and, that failure by the certifying authority to act on a request within the reasonable period of time results in a waiver. See 91 Fed. Reg. at 2,041. These clarifications provide certainty and predictability for EEI members throughout the country seeking certifications. EEI also supports EPA’s clarification that a certifying authority may not request withdrawal and resubmittal of a request for certification in order to extend the reasonable period of time. Id. (to be codified at 40 C.F.R. § 121.6(e)). EEI members have experienced the negative consequences of this approach in the past and support EPA providing clarification that authorities may no longer delay certification in this way. IV. EEI Supports the Uniformity and Certainty in the Proposed Rule Regarding the Required Elements of a Certification Request. EPA proposes to specify an enumerated list of documents and information that must be included in a request for certification for all Federal licenses or permits, and to remove regulatory text allowing states and tribes to require additional types of information in a request for certification. See 90 Fed. Reg. at 2,017 (to be codified at 40 C.F.R. § 121.5). EEI supports EPA providing uniformity and certainty across the country regarding the required elements of a 4 certification request. The certainty and predictability of a uniform request for certification will promote more efficient application processes for EEI members that operate across multiple states and tribal jurisdictions. It will allow members to file one unified application instead of multiple separate certification applications with different requirements across several jurisdictions, thereby streamlining the application process and speeding certification for projects that cross wide geographical ranges. V. EEI Supports EPA Clearly Defining a Role for Project Proponents Regarding Modifications of Certification Conditions. EPA proposes requiring the Federal agency, the certifying authority, and the applicant to all agree in writing that the certifying authority may modify a grant of certification (with or without conditions), before any modification occurs. See 90 Fed. Reg. at 2,041 (to be codified at 40 C.F.R. § 121.10). EEI supports this change for the same reason as the other proposed changes: it will provide certainty and predictability to applicants. In the past, EEI members have experienced circumstances where a certifying authority unilaterally pushes for impractical or unnecessary modifications to certification conditions. Requiring all parties to agree on modification will help ensure the modification is necessary and will lead to the best possible outcome for the application. VI. EPA Should Consider Exempting Facilities from CWA Section 401 Certification if the Facility already has a National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System Permit for the Relevant Discharges. In some instances, states have required CWA Section 401 certification where the facility in question has already established compliance with water quality requirements through an existing valid National Pollutant Discharge Elimination System (NPDES) permit issued pursuant to 5 Section 402 of the CWA by a state under delegated authority from EPA. 1 To the extent that a 401 certification is required for discharges to navigable waters or other applicable requirements included in the NPDES permitting process, EPA should clarify that a valid NPDES permit indicates compliance with water quality requirements under CWA Section 401. Indeed, a NPDES permit cannot be issued “when the imposition of conditions cannot ensure compliance with the applicable water quality requirements of all affected States,” or, for a new source, “if the discharge from its construction or operation will cause or contribute to the violation of water quality standards.” 2 This clarification will remove duplicative processes and therefore streamline compliance with the CWA. We emphasize that the limited role of Section 401 is demonstrated by the history of the provision as compared to that of Section 402, which created the CWA’s discharge permitting process. Prior to the passage of the modern CWA in 1972, Section 21(b) of the Water Quality Improvement Act contained a certification provision identical to Section 401, which applied to projects that did not proceed through a federally enforceable discharge permitting process, as that process did not yet exist. The Water Quality Improvement Act’s certification provision thus predated the development of the more comprehensive CWA permitting process under Section 402. Congress’ transference of the prior certification provision into Section 401 of the new CWA 1 These comments use “NPDES” to refer to CWA Section 402 permits issued by state authorities operating permitting programs under EPA delegated authority. Some states call these “SPDES” programs, the “S” standing for “State”, or insert the name of the state (“Texas Pollution Discharge Elimination System” or TPDES). These are state-issued permits that do not require a 401 certification. It is important to note that a CWA Section 402 permit issued by EPA itself, as for a non-delegated state, tribe, or territory, is a federally issued permit that must be supported by a 401 certification. 40 C.F.R. § 124.53(a). 2 40 C.F.R. § 122.4(d), (i). 6 in 1972, along with the creation of the NPDES process in Section 402, created two processes that should be read as not duplicative. Section 401 becomes duplicative, contrary to this statutory structure, when it is applied to projects that also must undergo Section 402 permitting. Congress’ inclusion of both Section 401 and the new Section 402 in the modern CWA creates a simple coordinated process. At its core, Section 401 is a pre-construction approval process, 3 whereas Section 402 permits are required for operations, not for construction. Section 401 also logically exists to apply pre-construction review to those projects to which the new Section 402 did not apply, just as it did prior to 1972 before the NPDES process existed— allowing the state to condition projects that otherwise will not undergo permitting. It is only rational that states would look to the Section 402 permitting process for continuing protection of water quality for projects whose operations result in a discharge. Therefore, based on the appropriate limited role of Section 401 in the overall CWA system, and given the requirement that, at least where discharges are concerned, NPDES permits must protect water quality standards, EPA should provide guidance to the states that: (1) the existence of a valid Section 402 permit provides reasonable assurance that the discharge will comply with water quality requirements, and (2) that this reasonable assurance creates a basis on which a 401 certification can be issued or, if the state prefers, waived. VII. EPA Should Revise the Pre-Filing Meeting Request Requirement. The Proposed Rule requires project proponents to request a pre-filing meeting from the certifying authority at least 30 days prior to a certification request submission. However, certifying authorities are allowed to waive or shorten this requirement. See 40 C.F.R. § 121.4. 3 See, e.g., § 401(a)(3), which explains that absent changed circumstances, permittees need not obtain a certification for operation when one has been obtained pre-construction. 7 In the experience of EEI members, the pre-filing meeting request requirement delays small projects that have minimal to no water quality impact. EEI members have noted that after a certifying authority receives a pre-filing meeting request for minor projects with minimal or no water quality impact, the certifying authority will then often wait the full 30 days before proceeding with the certification even if there are no other changes. As a result, the pre-filing requirement has become a de facto 30-day delay for small, short-lived, and generally non- controversial construction or maintenance projects. This type of delay is unnecessary, and the pre-filing meeting request process ends up slowing down certification with no concomitant benefit to the environment. EPA should instead make the pre-filing meeting request voluntary— or consider creating categorical exemptions from the requirement for smaller, less complex projects that have minimal or no water quality impacts, or need to be done on an emergency basis. At a minimum, EPA should consider allowing project proponents to participate in the certifying authority’s waiver process for determining whether the pre-filing meeting is necessary. These changes would provide flexibility and facilitate quicker certifications for the critical infrastructure projects EEI members conduct that have minimal or no water quality impacts. VIII. EPA Should Assert in Regulatory Text that Withdrawals from Navigable Waters Do Not Trigger the CWA Section 401 Process. Courts have concluded that a water withdrawal is not a discharge and therefore does not trigger the need for a water quality certification. 4 EPA appropriately clarified in the preamble to the 2023 rule that withdrawals from navigable waters neither trigger the CWA Section 401 4 See, e.g., North Carolina v. FERC, 112 F.3d 1175, 1187 (D.C. Cir. 1997) (holding that withdrawal of water from lake does not constitute discharge for CWA Section 401 purposes). 8 process, nor are they “discharges” whose impacts may be addressed by Section 401 certification conditions. 87 Fed. Reg. at 35,329. 5 Further, EPA appears to assert withdrawals from navigable waters do not trigger the CWA Section 401 process in the Proposed Rule by narrowing the scope of the certifying authority review to just an assessment of whether a facility’s point source discharges into waters of the United States comply with applicable water quality requirements, but does not explicitly exempt withdrawals from the certification process. 91 Fed. Reg. at 2,023. To provide clarity, the Agency should explicitly exempt withdrawals in the regulatory text. IX. Conclusion. EEI supports EPA’s finalization of the Proposed Rule with the modifications explained above. Questions on these comments may be directed to Patrick McGuire (202-508-5167) or Daniel Whittle (202-508-5990). 5 See also EPA, 2023 Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule Final 2023 Rule Questions & Answers (last updated Nov. 2024) at 6, https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-12/qa-for-2023-cwa-section-401-rule- implementation-112024.pdf. 9

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the docketed EPA rule on Water Quality Certification Regulations and responds to multiple specific provisions of the proposed rule.
rationales.reasoning: The arguments are generally coherent and organized by issue, with clear links between the proposed regulatory changes and EEI's stated concerns, though some claims are more asserted than deeply analyzed.
rationales.evidence: The letter cites statutory provisions, Federal Register references, EPA regulations, and a court case, and it includes some concrete industry experience, but it relies more on legal citation and advocacy than empirical data.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible impacts such as delays, duplicative processes, and uncertainty for infrastructure projects, and these effects are described in a reasonably specific way, though not quantified.
rationales.actionability: The comment offers clear requests, such as clarifying scope, making pre-filing voluntary, exempting withdrawals, and revising certification timing rules, which are feasible regulatory actions.
rationales.structure_formatting: The submission is professionally formatted, well organized with numbered sections and a clear conclusion, and it is easy to follow.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, relevant industry comment letter with clear regulatory asks and solid legal framing, but it is somewhat limited by a lack of robust empirical support and by advocacy-oriented rather than deeply developed analysis.

overall_summary: EEI provides a well-structured and highly relevant comment letter that engages directly with the proposed rule and offers concrete regulatory modifications. Its main strengths are clarity, organization, and actionable recommendations; its main weakness is that it relies more on legal interpretation and industry experience than on detailed data or quantitative evidence.

comment_text: Trucking companies can’t afford anything with the current rates. Insurance, repairs, driver pay, fuel, permits, taxes, even you have to pay for parking in almost every truck stop and etc. Brokers aquiring other brokerages for millions- billions of dollars. FMCSA have to start regulating these brokers. It’s getting out of hand

rationales.relevance: The comment is generally relevant to trucking industry economics and broker regulation, which appears related to the likely docket topic, but it does not identify the specific rule or requested issues.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is understandable and logically connected: high operating costs and broker consolidation are presented as reasons for increased regulation. However, the reasoning is brief and somewhat conclusory.
rationales.evidence: No data, citations, examples, or specific incidents are provided. The comment relies entirely on general assertions.
rationales.impact: It claims that current rates and broker consolidation are making conditions unsustainable for trucking companies, but the impacts are not quantified or explained in detail.
rationales.actionability: The only clear request is that FMCSA should start regulating brokers. This is a broad policy ask without specific recommendations or feasible steps.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is short and readable, but informal, loosely organized, and contains grammatical issues. It lacks the structure of a polished public comment.
rationales.overall: This is a concise, relevant complaint about trucking economics and broker regulation, but it is weak on evidence, specificity, and actionable detail. It reads more like a general grievance than a substantive policy comment.

overall_summary: The comment is relevant and easy to understand, but it provides little evidence or detail. It makes a broad call for broker regulation without explaining specific harms or proposing concrete regulatory changes.

comment_text:
I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

It is blatently obvious that the reason for such irresponsible/immoral practices is because so many political people have financial interests in pharmicuticals and the like and that is the only reason for their acceptance of such irrational practices to be allowed. There is no longer the Red vs. Blue, Democtrats vs. Repubicans. Last time I checked when you mix red and blue together you get purple. So from now on you will be seen, understood and shared as the purple uniparty. You people are pathetic and an embarrassment to this country that the people founded not political pin heads. 
 
Good Day.

rationales.relevance: The comment is directly about approval and labeling of a beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon, which matches the apparent topic. However, it drifts into unrelated political accusations at the end.
rationales.reasoning: The main concerns are understandable, but the argumentation is uneven and sometimes speculative. It asserts risks and labeling problems without clearly connecting them to regulatory standards or showing why the claims follow from the facts presented.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes some concrete technical references, such as Saccharomyces cerevisiae and specific genes, but provides no citations, data, or documented examples. Many claims about allergenicity, contamination, and long-term safety are asserted rather than supported.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies plausible consumer transparency and allergen concerns, but the magnitude of harm is not well quantified. The claimed impacts are possible, yet not demonstrated with specific evidence or examples.
rationales.actionability: It does offer clear requests, including stricter labeling, allergen warnings, and proteomic analysis. These are actionable in principle, though some requests are broad or not fully tied to feasible regulatory mechanisms.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter begins in a reasonably organized, numbered format with clear sections and a conclusion. That said, it becomes less professional and less coherent in the final paragraph, which weakens readability and tone.
rationales.overall: The comment is relevant and contains some actionable concerns, but its persuasive value is reduced by weak evidence, speculative reasoning, and an unprofessional political rant at the end. It reads more like a mixed-quality advocacy letter than a strong regulatory comment.

overall_summary: This is a partially relevant comment that raises legitimate labeling and safety concerns about a synthetic-biology-produced color additive, but it is weakened by limited evidence, some speculative claims, and a highly unprofessional closing section. The strongest parts are the clear requests for labeling and safety review; the weakest are the unsupported assertions and political digression.

And gives carriers and Brokers a way to combat fraud by being able to tell all parties in the load. It will foster Trust. And create stability which for too long has been lacking in our supply chain.

rationales.relevance: The comment clearly addresses broker transparency under 49 CFR 371.3 and supports a 48-hour disclosure requirement, which is directly related to the likely docket topic.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is understandable and generally coherent, but it is brief and mostly asserts benefits without developing a detailed logical case or addressing counterarguments.

rationales.impact: It identifies plausible benefits such as fairness, trust, fraud reduction, and supply chain stability, but these impacts are stated broadly and without specific examples or measurable effects.

rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is short and readable, but it has grammar and punctuation issues and lacks a clear organized structure such as separate points or paragraphs.
rationales.overall: A relevant and understandable supportive comment, but it is limited by minimal evidence, limited detail, and weak formatting. It communicates a clear position without offering much substantive support.

comment_text:
I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive and focuses on the requested issues of safety, transparency, and labeling.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with clear links between synthetic biology production, consumer expectations, and labeling concerns, though some claims are asserted more strongly than demonstrated.
rationales.evidence: The letter includes some concrete technical references, such as specific genes and the yeast host, but it provides little empirical data, citations, or independent studies to support the safety and allergenicity claims.
rationales.impact: The potential impacts are plausible, especially around consumer deception and possible allergen exposure, but the letter does not quantify risk or provide strong evidence that these harms are likely or widespread.
rationales.actionability: It offers clear requests, including stricter labeling, allergen warnings, and proteomic testing, which are feasible in principle and easy for the agency to understand.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is well organized with numbered sections, clear headings, and a professional tone, making it easy to follow and review.
rationales.overall: This is a focused and well-structured comment with clear policy asks, but it would be stronger with more evidence and more substantiated claims about safety and consumer impact.

overall_summary: A relevant and clearly organized comment that raises understandable labeling and safety concerns about synthetic-biology-produced beetroot red. Its main weakness is limited supporting evidence, which reduces the strength of its safety and allergenicity arguments.

comment_text: Christopher Phalen Vice President, Domestic Policy February 17, 2026 The Honorable Jessica Kramer Assistant Administrator Office of Water Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue, N.W. Washington, DC 20460 RE: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations, 91 Fed. Reg. 2008 (January 15, 2026); Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Dear Assistant Administrator Kramer: The National Association of Manufacturers (“NAM”) respectfully submits these comments on the proposed rulemaking issued by the Environmental Protection Agency (“EPA” or “the Agency”) entitled, Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (“the proposed rule” or “the proposal”). The NAM appreciates the opportunity to submit comments on the proposed rule and supports the EPA’s commitment to recalibrating the implementation of Clean Water Act (“CWA”) Section 401 (33 U.S.C. 1341) to realign it with the best statutory reading and congressional intent of the CWA. The NAM is the voice of the manufacturing community and the leading advocate for a policy agenda that supports and empowers the 13 million people who make things in America. As the largest manufacturing association in the U.S., the NAM’s membership includes businesses of all sizes, across all industrial sectors, and in all 50 states. Manufacturers collectively contribute $2.9 trillion to the U.S. economy—and right-sized, commonsense regulations are critical to sustaining the manufacturing strength that underpins our nation’s prosperity. Manufacturers appreciate EPA’s commitment to delivering on the President’s directive to reconsider regulatory developments that present “substantial restraint[s] on our economic growth and ability to build and innovate,” 1 by proposing to “revise several procedural and substantive aspects of the Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule...to address areas of regulatory uncertainty and implementation challenges.”2 The NAM has long called for comprehensive permitting reform, inclusive of regulatory and legislative actions, to drive the growth and competitiveness of manufacturing in the U.S. Modernizing the implementing regulations of the CWA is a key part of such a comprehensive 1 Unleashing Prosperity Through Deregulation, Exec. Order No. 14192, 90 Fed. Reg. 9065 (6 February 2025); Presidential Memorandum: Memorandum for the Heads of Executive Departments and Agencies, Directing the Repeal of Unlawful Regulations (9 April 2025); Repeal of Greenhouse Gas Emissions Standards for Fossil Fuel-Fired Electric Generating Units, 90 Fed. Reg. 25752 (17 June 2025). RIN 2060-AW55; Prevention of Significant Deterioration (PSD) and Nonattainment New Source Review (NNSR): Regulations Related to Project Emissions Accounting; Withdrawal of Proposed Rule, 90 Fed. Reg. 34206 (21 July 2025). RIN 2060-AV62. 2 Proposed rule, at 2008. 1 approach.3 And as the NAM stated in response to the Agency’s 2025 Establishment of Public Docket and Listening Sessions on Implementation Challenges Associated With Clean Water Act Section 401,4 “water quality certification under Section 401 of the CWA has posed challenges in instances when certifying authorities exceed the scope of their statutory mandate” 5 which has “impeded interstate commerce, to the detriment of energy development and manufacturing competitiveness.”6 Manufacturers in America need regulatory certainty and clarity to make capital-intensive investments in federally permitted facilities. Clear timelines and defined review criteria are critical for steel mills and raw material manufacturing and handling facilities that rely on predictable federal permitting schedules. The NAM therefore supports the Agency’s proposal and “a return to appropriate implementation of the requirements of Section 401 of the Clean Water Act, ensuring the certification process remains focused on point source discharges and not a project’s broader scope.”7 Rightsizing the Scope of Certification Reviews The NAM supports the Agency’s proposal to appropriately limit the scope of Section 401 certification reviews to “an assessment of whether a facility's point source discharges into waters of the United States will comply with specified water quality requirements.”8 This change from an activity-based scope is supported by the plain text of the CWA (as amended in 1972) and constitutes the best reading of the statute. See Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369 (2024). Focusing first on the statutory text of Section 401(a)(1), when read in its entirety, this section does not authorize a state or interstate water pollution control agency to provide a certification of an entire “activity.” 9 Rather, the term “activity” is used in a broader context wherein a state or interstate water quality certification is part of a larger “license or permit.” That certification is limited to “any discharge into the navigable waters” “at the point where the discharge originates or will originate” and ensures that “any such discharge will comply with the applicable provisions of sections 1311, 1312, 1313, 1316, and 1317 of this title.”10 The plain language of this subsection of the CWA demonstrates that the certification authority is not to be applied to an “activity” as a whole, only to point source discharges into the navigable waters (i.e., waters of the United States) that may result from any such activity. 3 America Builds: Clean Water Permitting and Project Delivery Hearing before Subcommittee on Water Resources and Environment, 119th Cong. (February 11, 2025) (statement of Noah Hanners, Executive Vice President, Nucor 4 Establishment of Public Docket and Listening Sessions on Implementation Challenges Associated With Clean Water Act Section 401. 90 Fed. Reg. 29828 (7 July 2025). Available at https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2025/07/07/2025-12564/establishment-of-public-docket-and-listening- sessions-on-implementation-challenges-associated-with 5 See NAM comments on Establishment of Public Docket and Listening Sessions on Implementation Challenges Associated With Clean Water Act Section 401. 90 Fed. Reg. 29828 (7 July 2025). Available at https://www.regulations.gov/comment/EPA-HQ-OW-2025-0272-0046 6 Ibid. 7 Ibid. 8 Proposed rule, at 2026 (emphasis in original). 9 33 U.S.C. 1341(a)(1) 10 33 U.S.C. 1341(a)(1) 2 This statutory interpretation is further supported by Loper Bright. Specifically, the Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright obviates previous Chevron deference afforded by the Court to the Agency under its holding in PUD No. 1. of Jefferson County v. Washington Department of Ecology (PUD No. 1).11 The NAM discussed this case previously in our 2019 comments during the first Trump administration’s rulemaking process to right-size the implementation of CWA Section 401.12 As the Agency correctly lays out in the proposal, the Chevron deference afforded in PUD No. 1 was based on ”regulations that predated the 1972 CWA amendments and therefore contained outdated statutory terms, most importantly “activity” rather than “discharge” in CWA Section 401(a)(1).”13 The NAM supports the Agency’s proposal to limit the scope of certification reviews to properly align with statutory authority to “assur[e] that a discharge from a federally licensed or permitted activity will comply with applicable and appropriate water quality requirements.”14 This change will ensure there is no overreach into non-jurisdictional waters and provide much needed clarity for manufacturers by preventing Section 401 from being used to regulate activities unrelated to water quality impacts from the discharge. Certification Decisions Tied to Water Quality Standards Similar to constraining certification reviews to focus exclusively on point source discharges, the NAM also supports the Agency’s proposal to require certification conditions to cite the specific applicable water quality requirement. This will significantly enhance both transparency and enforceability while supporting consistent implementation across regional jurisdictions. As the NAM has noted since 2019, it is clear from the legislative history that Congress specifically intended to address water quality from point source discharges in Section 401 (as previously discussed). 15 Proposing to require state or interstate certifying authorities to expressly disclose if they are granting, granting with conditions, denying, or waiving a certification for an applicable license or permit, with additional supporting information required16 will provide a clear linkage between conditions and regulatory standards. This will help manufacturers understand compliance obligations and avoid conflicting requirements. The NAM therefore respectfully asks the Agency to finalize the proposed changes to CFR 121.7 (c)-(f). 11 511 U.S. 700 (1994). 12 Updating Regulations on Water Quality Certification, Proposed Rule, 84 Fed. Reg. 44,080 (Aug. 22, 2019). Available at https://www.regulations.gov/comment/EPA-HQ-OW-2019-0405-0878 13 Proposed rule, at 2025. 14 Proposed rule, at 2023. 15 See 2019 NAM comment, Available at https://www.regulations.gov/comment/EPA-HQ-OW-2019-0405-0878, pp. 3- 4, Senator Edmund Muskie quotation, etc. 16 In particular, where a certification is denied, ”where certification is denied, the EPA is proposing that the written notification of denial state the reasons for denial, including the specific water quality requirements with which the discharge(s) will not comply; a statement explaining why the discharge will not comply with the identified water quality requirements; or if the denial is due to insufficient information, a description of any missing water quality-related information.” 3 Standardizing Requests for Certification Along with ensuring that the scope of certifications are updated to reflect the correct statutory interpretation, manufacturers support the Agency’s proposal to standardize the contents of a certification request. The proposed changes will improve efficiency, consistency, and predictability in the certification process. Under the current regulatory regime, the process of merely requesting a Section 401 certification is too often used to delay and de facto block critical manufacturing and energy projects—before even reaching discharge and water quality analysis. A plain reading of the statute17 and controlling court precedent18 demonstrate that “receipt” does not require a certification request be “complete.” (Further discussion of manufacturers’ position on the Agency’s proposal regarding timelines is below). The process, beginning upon receipt of the certification request, is the forum through which completeness is to be achieved. Any other reading of the statute that allows sub-federal certifying authorities to impose differing or subjective criteria of “completeness” would obviate the authority granted to the Administrator of the Agency under the CWA. 19 The Agency, in its proposed changes to CFR 121.5 eliminating state- or tribe-specific additional information requirements, provides clear guidelines as to what constitutes a “complete” application upon “receipt.” The NAM supports these proposed revisions, as they will reduce inconsistent practices across jurisdictions affecting permittees, while lowering administrative burden without diminishing water quality protection. To augment the Agency’s proposal in CFR 121.5, the NAM also respectfully requests additional clarification that the standardized list of required materials in 121.5 is intended to be sufficient for certification review and that completeness determinations may not be used to expand the scope of review or delay the start of the statutory review period, including by relying on open-ended language such as “other relevant documentation” in the proposed 121.5(c)(4). Ensuring Appropriate Timelines Returning to the issue of the timeline afforded to certifying authorities to grant, deny, or waive certification, the NAM supports the Agency’s proposal. Firm deadlines are particularly important for major projects subject to Section 401. The statute is clear that, “[i]f the State, interstate agency, or Administrator, as the case may be, fails or refuses to act on a request for certification, within a reasonable period of time (which shall not exceed one year) after receipt of such request, the certification requirements of this subsection shall be waived with respect to such Federal application.”20 The proposal appropriately reinforces the statutory one-year maximum period for action on a certification request. Additionally, removing automatic extensions tied to public notice or force majeure events promotes timely decision-making. The NAM appreciates the Agency proposing 17 33 U.S.C. 1341(a)(1) 18 New York State Department of Environmental Conservation v. Federal Energy Regulatory Commission, 884 F.3d 450 (2d Cir. 2018) 19 “The Administrator is authorized to prescribe such regulations as are necessary to carry out his functions under this chapter.” 33 U.S.C. 1361(a). 20 U.S.C. 1341(a)(1), emphasis added 4 to bar certifying authorities from requesting or compelling applicants to withdraw a request for certification to avoid exceeding the clear one-year statutory requirement. Protecting Applicant Rights in Certification Modifications Manufacturers recognize that some modifications to certifications, or modifications made in the certifying process, are necessary and provide value to projects and water quality outcomes. The current regulations implementing Section 401, however, do not include the project applicant as a formal part of any agreement to proceed with a modification or the final language of such a modification. This has too often led to a lack of trust between certifying authorities and applicants, when applicants ultimately must effectuate any certification modification. It has also proven to be a mechanism by which certifying authorities can misuse the modification process to impose unreasonable modifications on an applicant, in some cases providing de facto certification denials without an authority having to formally deny a project certification as the CWA requires. The NAM therefore supports the Agency’s proposed revisions to CFR 121.10 clarifying the procedures for modifying an issued Section 401 water quality certification. Specifically, providing a defined process for applicant involvement in certification modifications promotes transparency, fairness, and better coordination between applicants and certifying authorities. Additionally, maintaining the prohibition on unilateral modification of certifications by certifying authorities and allowing applicant participation during the modification process will improve predictability for all stakeholders. Providing Predictable Neighboring State Review and Objection Process Finally, regarding the CWA Section 401(a)(2), which prescribes how the Agency notifies “other State[s]” (currently referred to in the CFR as “neighboring jurisdictions”) of potential water quality impacts from a proposed certification in a different state, the NAM supports the proposed rule establishing a 90-day deadline for federal agencies to act on neighboring state objections. This will reduce the open-ended delay risk and ensure the predictable resolution of other states’ objections, which is vital for major projects. The NAM agrees and supports the Agency’s determination that the proposed firm deadlines preserve neighboring state participation while safeguarding timely project decisions. ******** The NAM appreciates the Agency’s consideration of these comments and the Administration’s ongoing engagement with manufacturers throughout the process of preparing this proposal to correctly implement the requirements of CWA Section 401. Manufacturers strongly support a return to appropriately scoped and timed certifications that provide certainty and predictability to manufacturers while reflecting the appropriate balance between federal and state authorities, as Congress intended. Sincerely, Christopher Phalen Vice President, Domestic Policy 5

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the EPA proposal on Water Quality Certification Regulations and responds to multiple specific issues in the docket, including scope, timelines, completeness, modifications, and neighboring state review.
rationales.reasoning: The letter is logically organized and generally coherent, with each section tying statutory interpretation to a policy position. Some arguments are advocacy-driven, but the overall reasoning is clear and consistent.
rationales.evidence: It cites the Clean Water Act, Supreme Court and circuit cases, Federal Register notices, and prior NAM comments. However, it relies more on legal citations and assertions than on empirical data or concrete project examples.
rationales.impact: The comment explains plausible impacts on manufacturing certainty, permitting timelines, and interstate commerce, but the claims are mostly qualitative and not supported by quantified analysis or detailed case studies.
rationales.actionability: The letter makes clear requests, such as finalizing specific regulatory revisions and clarifying standardized materials and timelines. The recommendations are feasible and tied to the proposed rule, though some asks are framed broadly.
rationales.structure_formatting: The submission is professionally formatted, well sectioned, and easy to follow. Headings and paragraph flow improve readability, and the tone is formal and appropriate for a public comment letter.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, highly relevant industry comment letter with clear positions and solid legal framing. Its main limitation is the lack of empirical evidence and detailed impact analysis, but it is otherwise well-structured and actionable.

overall_summary: A strong and highly relevant comment letter that directly engages the proposed rule and offers clear regulatory preferences. It is well organized and legally grounded, though it relies more on statutory and case-law arguments than on empirical evidence or quantified impact analysis.

comment_text: From: stephaniejhemphill@everyactioncustom.com To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/10/2026 11:27:08 AM Subject: Docket ID# EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 — Opposition to Proposed 2026 Changes to Circumscribe Certification Authority of States and Tribes -------------------------------------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------- [You don't often get email from stephaniejhemphill@everyactioncustom.com. Learn why this is important at https://aka.ms/LearnAboutSenderIdentification ] Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Dear Administrator Lee Zeldin, I wish to communicate my strong opposition to the EPA’s proposed 2026 rule changes that would limit certification authority used by States and Tribes to protect clean water. The Clean Water Act was adopted by Congress to restore and maintain clean water and to ensure that States and Tribes could prevent violation of the water quality requirements they have adopted. These proposed certification rules would serve the special interests of polluters at the expense of clean water, the rights of States to protect the health and welfare of their residents, and the sovereignty of Tribes. 1. ) Near me, the Fond du Lac Band of Lake Superior Chippewa has done pioneering and effective work to protect the water quality in the St. Louis River. They rely on federal support, and the federal government has a trust responsibility to help them. Therefore, I oppose the Rule’s elimination of current regulations facilitating Tribal certification authority and supporting neighboring/downstream Tribes in objecting to federal permits and preventing violations of Reservation water quality requirements. 2. ) In Minnesota, we’ve used existing certification authority to prevent degradation and protect streams, Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters, and Outstanding Resource Value waters, like the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park. Therefore, I oppose the Rule’s restrictions that would prevent any State or Tribe in the United States from setting conditions on Federal “general permits.” 3. ) It is a mistake to tighten a noose around the scope of certification. The proposed rules would prevent conditions or denial of Federal permits even if activities would violate State and Tribal standards and contaminate lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or seepage through groundwater into surface water. We have a lot of experience with this in the Minnesota mining country. 4. ) I oppose the Rule’s forced waiver of State or Tribal certification after a year even if time is needed due to public process, acts of God, or because the project proposer concealed the truth about water impacts in its draft permit application. This rule would encourage polluters to hide the facts and do a bait-and-switch after time has run out for certification. 5. ) I oppose the Rule’s creation of new power in the EPA Administrator to determine without guardrails that an entire category of discharges will never affect downstream States or Tribes and deprive them of authority to object to violations of water quality requirements. What category would this EPA remove from certification review? All pipeline discharge? All mining waste? The EPA must reject or withdraw its 2026 proposed rule to undermine State and Tribal certification. Existing rules under the Clean Water Act do a much better job of protecting us and our children from contaminated water that they can’t safely drink, swim in, or fish. Thank you for considering my comments, Sincerely, Stephanie Hemphill 3614 Prindle Rd. Duluth MN 55803 Sincerely, Stephanie Hemphill 3614 Prindle Rd Duluth, MN 55803-9340 stephaniejhemphill@outlook.com

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the docket topic: proposed changes to state and tribal water quality certification authority under the Clean Water Act. It repeatedly references the specific rule changes and requested EPA action.
rationales.reasoning: The letter is logically organized around numbered objections and generally connects each proposed change to a claimed harm. The arguments are coherent, though some points are more rhetorical than deeply developed.
rationales.evidence: It includes concrete examples such as the Fond du Lac Band, the St. Louis River, Minnesota mining country, the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness, and Voyageurs National Park. However, it provides little quantitative data, citations, or detailed factual support.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies plausible impacts like weakened tribal sovereignty, degraded water quality, and reduced ability to prevent contamination. The harms are specific enough to be credible, though not extensively substantiated.
rationales.actionability: It clearly asks EPA to reject or withdraw the proposed rule and opposes several specific provisions. The requested action is feasible and understandable, even if it does not propose alternative regulatory language.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is readable and uses a clear numbered list, which helps organization. However, it includes some repetition, informal phrasing, and duplicated signature/contact information, which reduces polish.
rationales.overall: This is a strong public comment that is highly relevant and clearly opposed to the proposal, with concrete examples and a clear request for EPA action. Its main weaknesses are limited evidentiary support and somewhat uneven formatting.

overall_summary: A relevant, coherent, and persuasive opposition comment with clear policy objections and practical examples, but limited formal evidence and only moderate polish.

I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with clear claims about consumer deception, process concerns, and allergenicity. Some assertions are broad or not fully substantiated, but the logic is easy to follow.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes a few concrete technical references, such as Saccharomyces cerevisiae and specific genes, but it provides no citations, data, or empirical studies to support the safety or allergenicity claims.

rationales.actionability: It offers specific requests, including proteomic analysis, clearer labeling standards, and allergen warnings, which are feasible in principle, though some are stated in broad terms.

rationales.overall: This is a focused and well-structured public comment with clear policy asks, but it is weakened by limited evidence and somewhat speculative safety claims.

comment_text:
I strongly support increased transparency in property broker transactions as a necessary step toward fairness and accountability in the trucking industry. For too long, motor carriers and owner-operators have operated with limited visibility into brokered transactions, often resulting in disputes over payments and a lack of trust in the system.

By requiring brokers to disclose transaction records promptly and in an accessible format, FMCSA will create a more level playing field, ensuring that carriers receive fair compensation and can make informed business decisions. Transparency will deter unethical practices, improve industry stability, and promote fair competition.

I urge FMCSA to adopt strong enforcement measures to ensure compliance with these transparency rules. Without meaningful penalties for non-compliance, brokers may continue opaque practices that disadvantage carriers.

rationales.relevance: The comment is directly about broker transaction transparency and FMCSA enforcement, which appears closely aligned with the likely docket topic. It addresses the core issue of disclosure and compliance.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is coherent and easy to follow: transparency is framed as improving fairness, accountability, and competition, with enforcement needed to make the rule effective. The logic is clear, though not deeply developed.
rationales.evidence: The comment provides no citations, data, examples, or specific incidents to support its claims. It relies entirely on general assertions.
rationales.impact: The claimed impacts are plausible, such as improved fairness and reduced disputes, but they are stated broadly without concrete detail or measurable outcomes.
rationales.actionability: It offers a clear request for stronger transparency requirements and meaningful penalties for non-compliance. However, it does not specify particular regulatory language, timelines, or enforcement mechanisms.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is concise, organized into a clear opening, supporting rationale, and concluding request. It is professional and readable, though brief.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and well-written comment with a clear policy position, but it is limited by the absence of evidence and specific implementation details. It is solid advocacy, but not especially substantive.

overall_summary: A clear, relevant comment supporting broker transparency and enforcement in trucking, but it is mostly general advocacy without supporting evidence or detailed recommendations.
