Confidentiality clauses in agreements between shippers and brokers are widespread 
and often prohibit brokers from disclosing rate information – the same information 
the Proposed Rule would mandate be disclosed upon a carrier’s request. Indeed, 
such information is so intrinsically valuable, it may well be subject to protection 
under federal and state trade secret laws , thus exposing brokers to potential 
litigation by shippers . While these agreements sometimes have exceptions 
authorizing disclosure where required by law, more often, those exceptions apply 
only to disclosures required by subpoena and are conditioned upon prior notice to 
the shipper in order to afford an opportunity for the shipper to seek a protective 
order. In other words, the Proposed Rule will require brokers to choose between a 
regulatory violation or breaching commercial contracts.  The Proposed Rule did no t 
take into account the time and resources to re -negotiate the multitude of shipper -
broker contracts currently in existence that would be necessary to address the 
impact of the Proposed Rule.  
FMCSA claims that the required disclosure of broker records is necessary for 
carriers to be able to resolve disputes with brokers over items like detention time, 
fuel surcharge or cargo loss or damage. However, these items are normally covered 
and spelled out in a broker -carrier agreement. Moreover, even though FMCSA 
increased the broker bond amount as required by the Moving Ahead for Progress in 
the 21st Century Act (“MAP -21”), it did not mandate disclosure or even mention the 
difficulty of resolving broke r-carrier disputes leading to an unfair or inefficient 
transportation system. Instead, the increased bond amount was intended to fulfill 
the contemporaneous amendment, in MAP -21, of 49 U.S.C. § 13904 to state that 
regulation of brokers shall provide for pr otection of shippers and motor carriers . 
With an increased bond, there is an increased likelihood that a carrier that provides 
services and is not paid by the broker will have recourse against the bond. If 
additional protection of carriers was needed,  e.g., making broker records disclosure 
a regulatory obligation,  MAP -21 would have been a logical vehicle to address it. As 
noted earlier, FMCSA points to no reliable evidence that some new problem has 
developed. And again, the proponents of the Proposed Rule simply seek t o increase 
rates carriers are paid, ignoring the ups and downs of the free market.  
B. FMCSA Underestimates the Burden of Compliance . 
 USDOT Docket Operations  
January 21, 2025  
Page 22 
 
While most brokers do keep more and more information in electronic format, the 
various data points that must be disclosed under the Proposed Rule are frequently 
not kept in one place. FMCSA’s estimated costs do not take into account the need to 
develop sof tware and capacity to pull the required data points from multiple 
locations and systems. There is reason to believe most carriers would request the 
information, because there is minimal cost or inconvenience to them, even if the 
information is wholly unnec essary.  Thus, it is reasonable to assume that FMCSA 
has underestimated the burden to brokers, who will need to add additional 
headcount to oversee the record request process.  
 
Further, a broker may not always be able to provide all the data points required by 
the Proposed Rule within 48 hours of a request being made by the carrier. Many 
brokers, as yet another added convenience that benefits carriers, offer quick pay 
type arrang ements where the broker pays the carrier even if the shipper has not yet 
paid the broker. Take an example where a broker says it will pay the carrier within 
seven days of proof of delivery , but the shipper does not pay the broker until thirty 
days after delivery. If the carrier requests the broker records upon payment from 
the broker, the broker will not have information on the “amount of compensation 
received by the broker” or the “date of payment.”80 The same likely holds true for 
“any penalties assessed in connection with the shipment.”81 How is the broker to 
comply? Is there a continuing duty to update and disclose records for each 
shipment? If so, those costs to brokers have not been considered by FMCSA.  
Questions Posed by FMCSA  
1. What impact, if any, would the proposed rule have on freight rates? Please 
provide support for your position.  
 
While there is some uncertainty about impact of the Proposed Rule on freight 
rates,  what is certain is that it will add costs to brokers, and these costs will 
have to be recouped somewhere.   And if the Proposed Rule undercuts brokers to 
the point there are only a handful left, experience suggests (as recognized by the 
ICC) that small carriers will have a more difficult time obtaining loads. As 
FMCSA suggests, the Proposed Rule  may allow carriers to try to negotiate 
higher rates for the carrier, and it may allow shippers to negotiate lower rates 
 
80 Proposed 49 C.F.R. § 371.3(a)(4).  
81 Proposed 49 C.F.R. § 371.3(a)( 5). USDOT Docket Operations  
January 21, 2025  
Page 23 
 
for the broker in certain instances . However, by and large, deregulation has 
meant that freight rates are shaped by the market forces of freight demand and 
freight capacity. To the extent that the Proposed Rule squeezes out brokers, 
shippers will have to take on additional costs to qualify  and manage their 
carriers  (previously handled by brokers) . The shipper’s desire to recoup those 
costs will be downward pressure on the freight rates a shipper will bear. 
Therefore, in the highly competitive trucking industry,  it is unlikely the 
Proposed Rule will lead to the increased revenues that carrier proponents desire.   
2. How common is electronic recordkeeping among household goods brokers? 
What burden, if any, would be imposed if electronic recordkeeping was 
required?  
 
Commenters are not household goods brokers and are not in a position to 
respond.  
 
3. How much time would a broker spend creating an electronic record from paper 
documents for the record mandated by § 371.3? What would be the costs for a 
broker to create an electronic record per transaction?  
 
FMCSA is correct that Commenters generally keep records electronically, but 
many records are simply images of documents. More relevant to the cost -benefit 
analysis, even if the records are kept electronically, the elements required to be 
disclosed are not generally kept in one place as a single record. Costs would vary 
by broker to compile a compliant record in response to a request by party to a 
brokered transaction under the Proposed Rule. However, the costs would involve 
multiple queries and involve huma n oversight, meaning FMCSA’s estimated 
costs are substantially underestimated.  
 
4.  Do you believe that the 48 -hour timeframe proposed for § 371.3(c) would 
create a substantial burden for brokers? Why or why not? If you disagree with 
the proposed 48 -hour timeframe, what timeframe would best balance the 
objectives of transparency while min imizing the burden on brokers?   
Yes, this would create a substantial burden for brokers. As discussed in the 
comments above, some of the elements of the required record disclosure 
regarding payment by the shipper may not even be known within 48 hours of 
the request being made. Moreover, mandating disclosure is a substantial 
burden given that FMCSA recognizes that waivers of the right to the records 
are frequently agreed to contractually.  
Although the Proposed Rule should not go forward in any form, the 48 hour 
requirement should be extended to seven business days after the request and 
should only require disclosure of those elements known at the time of the party’s USDOT Docket Operations  
January 21, 2025  
Page 24 
 
request. There should be no continuing duty on the broker to supplement 
information.  
5. If this proposal effectively reduced instances of illegal brokering, through 
carrier policing with transparency information, would the brokers engaged in 
illegal practices exit the market, resulting in the transfer of illicit profits to 
legally operating motor carriers and /or brokers?  
 
No, the proposal will do nothing to combat illegal brokering.  Commenters ’ 
experience is that parties that engage in illegal brokering often do not even hold 
broker authority.  The fraud usually involves stealing freight charges (by 
masquerading as a carrier, agreeing to haul a load, getting paid, but then 
brokering the load to a third party with no intention of paying the third party), 
or stealing freight (by masquerading as a carrier, agreeing to haul a load, 
brokering to a third party after changing the delivery location).  Parties willing 
to steal freight charges or to steal cargo will not be deterred by the threat of a 
regulatory penalty  – there will be no one to assess the penalty against.  Even if 
FMCSA were willing to investigate fraud and identify the underlying bad actors 
(something which it has not done to date), they are often based overseas, and 
again, would not usually hold broker authori ty in the first place.  
6. Should freight brokers and household goods brokers be subject to the same 
recordkeeping requirements under § 371.3? If your answer is “no,” why should 
they be subject to different requirements?  
 
Commenters are not household goods brokers and are not in a position to 
respond.  
7. Should parties requesting records under § 371.3(c) be required to submit their 
request in writing? Should parties requesting records under § 371.3(c) be 
required to submit their request electronically? Would requiring a specific 
format for submitted reques ts impose a cost on the parties or otherwise deter 
requests for transparency? Please provide support for your position.  
 
Yes, requests should be required to be in writing and electronic.  Allowing verbal 
requests for records is entirely unfair - a carrier could just allege it requested 
records with no proof and could then complain that a broker failed to respond 
within the proposed 48 hours.  Likewise, requests should have to be made 
electronically.  A handwritten piece of mail may well be illegible  and, in any 
event, would require significantly more time and expense to respond. The time 
for which the disclosure is required should not commence until after a request 
for records is received by the broker.  USDOT Docket Operations  
January 21, 2025  
Page 25 
 
 
8. Would the proposal that records be provided electronically under § 371.3(c) 
make broker transparency more likely, as compared to not specifying a method 
of provided the records? Should the Agency be more specific in requiring a 
particular format for record s provided under § 371.3(c), and if so, what 
method and/or format is preferrable? Please provide support for your position.  
 
Realistically, brokers are likely to respond electronically as it is likely to be the 
most efficient and reliable means.  FMCSA should not propose a specific format. 
However, FMCSA should clarify that the obligation to respond is with respect to 
the requir ed elements that are known to the broker at the time of the request.  
Conclusion  
For the reasons noted above, Commenters re spectfully request that FMCSA 
withdraw the Proposed Rule and cease consideration of any further economic 
regulatory intervention, unless more specifically authorized by Congress.  
 
Respectfully submitted,  
 
 /s/ Prasad Sharma      /s/ Nathaniel Saylor  
 Prasad Sharma       Nathaniel Saylor  
 
 
 
 
4905 -4116 -9935, v. 10

Its main weakness is limited empirical evidence, but overall it is a high-quality submission.

comment_text: The move toward rate transparency being a "duty" of broker means nothing unless FMCSA also says brokers cannot waive their new regulatory duty without such a waiver constituting "evasion of regulation" in furtherance of "unreasonable restraint of trade" in violation of the Sherman Antitrust Act. Without expressly prohibiting such contractual waivers, FMCSA is allowing brokers to continue to evade regulation and deregulate themselves. The status quo remains. While Congress has explicitly allowed shippers and carriers to agree to waive certain requirements, Congress has not passed a law allowing the same for brokers. FMCSA already knows this and they have determined brokers are NOT shippers. FMCSA is therefore misguided in their logic and has this backwards. There does not have to be a law specifically prohibiting brokers from waiving because there already is a general law called Evasion of Regulation. There would need to be a law allowing brokers to waive, and no such law exists.

rationales.relevance: The comment is directly about broker rate transparency, contractual waivers, and FMCSA's authority, so it is clearly on-topic for a broker regulation docket.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is understandable and generally coherent, but it relies on asserted legal conclusions without fully developing the chain of reasoning or addressing counterarguments.
rationales.evidence: It provides no citations, statutory references beyond broad mentions of the Sherman Act, or concrete examples to support the claims.
rationales.impact: The comment claims brokers can evade regulation and that the status quo will remain, but it does not quantify or concretely explain the practical effects of the waiver issue.
rationales.actionability: It implicitly asks FMCSA to prohibit contractual waivers, but it does not present a precise regulatory text change or a clearly framed request.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is a single dense paragraph with repetitive phrasing and limited organization, which reduces readability and professionalism.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant but largely unsupported and somewhat repetitive comment that raises a legal concern without strong evidence, detailed analysis, or a clear actionable proposal.

overall_summary: The comment is on-topic and makes a clear policy objection, but it is weak on evidence, specificity, and actionable recommendations. Its legal assertions are not well supported, and the presentation is dense and repetitive.

comment_text:
I strongly support increased transparency in property broker transactions as a necessary step toward fairness and accountability in the trucking industry. For too long, motor carriers and owner-operators have operated with limited visibility into brokered transactions, often resulting in disputes over payments and a lack of trust in the system.

Manbir Atwal

rationales.evidence: No facts, data, examples, citations, or concrete incidents are provided to support the claims.

rationales.overall: This is a relevant but very limited public comment: it states a clear viewpoint, yet offers little supporting detail, analysis, or actionable guidance.

overall_summary: A short, relevant comment that supports broker transparency, but it is too brief to provide strong reasoning, evidence, or concrete recommendations.

comment_text: From: Julie Bernstein <noreply@adv.actionnetwork.org> To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 1/21/2026 3:11:08 PM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 -------------------------------------------------------- Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. The Environmental Protection Agency, The proposed changes to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act would be extremely detrimental.. For 50 years, this provision has provided essential protection for our nation's waterways by giving states and authorized tribes the ability to review federally permitted projects that could affect their water quality. The proposed rule would significantly limit this authority by restricting reviews to only "direct discharges" into waterways, rather than allowing comprehensive assessments of broader environmental impacts. This change would weaken local communities' ability to protect drinking water sources, fisheries, wetlands, and cultural resources. While a fuel pipeline or an LNG port may not involve any direct discharge, they still pose a real threat if there is a leak. We know that these threats have caused huge environmental impacts when they occur so these eventualities must be part of any siting decision. tEven a manufacturer that intends to abide to limitations on the discharge of toxic pollutants can pose a serious danger to a community if for example, they are not abiding by stringent regulations to mitigate against a fire risk. It should absolutely be the business of a nearby community to ensure that before such a company is allowed to operate that they have laid out how they will mi tigate against any fire hazard and that they have in place to alert everyone in the surrounding community immediately if a fire breaks out and will then be responsible for monitoring the impact of any contamination to local waterways and for any necessary cleanup or even supply of drinking water should a reservoir or the water table impact local well water. Furthermore, the proposed strict deadlines and limited review scope would accelerate project approvals without ensuring adequate environmental protection. This comes at a particularly concerning time, following recent judicial decisions that have already reduced federal water protections. Water is a shared resource that sustains communities, ecosystems, and economies. Once contaminated, restoration can take generations. The current Section 401 authority provides essential safeguards that allow local communities to protect their water resources for current and future generations. I urge the EPA to withdraw this proposed rule and maintain the current authority that allows states and tribes to conduct meaningful water quality reviews. Our nation's waterways deserve comprehensive protection, not weakened standards that prioritize expedited project approvals over environmental stewardship. Sincerely, Julie Bernstein Julie Massachusetts

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 and focuses on Section 401 of the Clean Water Act, the scope of state and tribal review, and the proposed rule's effects.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent: it explains that narrowing review to direct discharges could miss broader risks and weaken protections. Some points are repetitive and a few sentences are awkwardly phrased, but the core logic is clear.
rationales.evidence: The comment relies mostly on general assertions and hypothetical examples rather than specific facts, data, legal citations, or concrete case studies. It mentions recent judicial decisions but does not identify them.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible harms such as threats to drinking water, fisheries, wetlands, cultural resources, and community safety, but the impacts are described broadly without much specificity or quantification.
rationales.actionability: The letter gives a clear request to withdraw the proposed rule and maintain current Section 401 authority. It is feasible and direct, though it does not offer alternative regulatory language or detailed implementation suggestions.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment has a clear opening, body, and closing, but it is a single dense paragraph with several grammatical errors, typos, and run-on sentences that reduce readability and professionalism.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and substantively on-topic comment with a clear policy ask, but it would be stronger with better organization, more precise evidence, and cleaner writing.

overall_summary: A strongly relevant public comment that clearly opposes the proposed rule and asks EPA to withdraw it. Its main weaknesses are limited evidence and somewhat disorganized, error-filled presentation, but the core reasoning and requested action are understandable and on point.

comment_text:
I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with clear links between synthetic biology production, consumer expectations, and safety concerns, though some claims are asserted more strongly than demonstrated.

comment_text:
I am writing to oppose EPA’s proposed changes to Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401. These changes would weaken a core safeguard Congress deliberately included to ensure that federally permitted projects do not degrade water quality or undermine State and Tribal water quality standards. Section 401 is essential to the CWA’s purpose: to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters (33 U.S.C. §1251(a)).

EPA states that the purpose of this proposed rule is to address “regulatory uncertainty and implementation challenges,” increase “transparency, efficiency, and predictability,” and align regulations with the scope of the Clean Water Act. I respectfully disagree that the proposed revisions will achieve these goals. Instead, the proposal appears to use the language of process improvement to justify a substantive narrowing of Section 401 authority that Congress intentionally granted to States and authorized Tribes.

EPA’s justification relies heavily on claims that stakeholders raised “questions” about the scope of certification under the 2023 Rule. However, disagreement from regulated entities about the breadth of Section 401 is not evidence of statutory ambiguity—it is often evidence that Section 401 is functioning as Congress intended: as a meaningful check to ensure federally permitted activities comply with water quality standards and other appropriate requirements of State and Tribal law. Section 401 was designed because federal permitting programs historically issued licenses and permits “without any assurance that the [water quality] standards will be met or even considered.” Narrowing certification scope in the name of “certainty” risks returning to the problem Congress sought to solve.

Furthermore, Section 401 is a preventative safeguard. The conditions and monitoring requirements that States and Tribes include are not administrative burdens; they are often the primary enforceable mechanisms ensuring compliance with water quality standards. If the proposed rule reduces the scope of conditions or narrows what can be considered, then “efficiency” is achieved only by shifting environmental risk to the public and downstream communities.

EPA states that the proposed rule will ensure States and Tribes “understand and adhere to their section 401 role.” This framing implies that States and Tribes have been acting outside their authority, when in fact Section 401 explicitly authorizes certifications to include “any effluent limitations and other limitations, and monitoring requirements necessary to assure” compliance with the Clean Water Act and “any other appropriate requirement” of State or Tribal law (33 U.S.C. §1341(d)). Congress intentionally gave States and Tribes a strong role in protecting water quality within their borders, consistent with the Clean Water Act’s cooperative federalism structure. A rule that reinterprets this authority narrowly is a policy choice to reduce State and Tribal power.

The proposed revisions target core provisions, not minor procedural issues. They go to the heart of whether Section 401 remains an effective safeguard. If the scope of certification is narrowed, then conditions and denials become harder, even when water quality standards are at risk.

EPA’s cost-benefit framing is incomplete and biased toward short-term applicant savings as EPA states that it prepared an economic analysis to evaluate “cost savings and benefits” relative to the 2023 Rule. However, the costs of weakening Section 401 are not primarily borne by applicants—they are borne by the public through degraded water quality, increased downstream treatment costs, loss of aquatic habitat, increased flooding and sedimentation impacts, and long-term remediation obligations.

In addition, weakening Section 401 may increase conflict and litigation, undermining the predictability the Agency claims it is seeking. If State and Tribal authority is curtailed, affected jurisdictions and communities will have fewer tools to resolve water quality concerns early, increasing the likelihood of delays later through disputes and court challenges.

For these reasons, I urge EPA to withdraw the proposed changes and maintain a strong, workable Section 401 framework that protects water quality and respects State and Tribal authority.

Respectfully,

A citizen from Missouri

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses EPA’s proposed changes to Clean Water Act Section 401 and repeatedly engages the stated goals of the rulemaking, including uncertainty, efficiency, and scope of authority.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is logically organized and generally coherent, with clear links between the proposed rule, statutory purpose, and potential consequences. Some claims are asserted more than demonstrated, but the overall reasoning is strong.
rationales.evidence: The comment cites statutory provisions and quotes EPA’s stated rationale, which helps ground the argument. However, it relies mostly on legal and policy assertions rather than empirical data, case examples, or detailed citations.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible impacts such as degraded water quality, increased downstream costs, habitat loss, and more litigation. These impacts are relevant and believable, though not quantified or supported with specific examples.
rationales.actionability: The request is clear: withdraw the proposed changes and maintain a strong Section 401 framework. This is feasible and directly responsive, though it does not offer alternative regulatory language or more granular recommendations.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is well organized into distinct paragraphs, uses professional tone, and is easy to follow. It reads like a polished public comment letter, though it is not heavily formatted with headings or bullet points.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, relevant public comment that clearly opposes the proposal and explains why. It is persuasive and well structured, but would be stronger with more concrete evidence and specific examples.

overall_summary: A solid and highly relevant comment letter that clearly opposes EPA’s proposed Section 401 changes, presents a coherent policy argument, and makes a direct request. Its main weakness is limited empirical evidence and specificity, but it is still a persuasive and professionally written submission.

comment_text:
This proposed FMCSA rule is important because it reinforces transparency in brokered freight transactions, ensuring carriers can access vital records about their deals. 

However, to truly protect small carriers, these records should be automatically included in rate confirmations. Without automatic sharing, brokers might retaliate by blacklisting carriers who request the information—ultimately hurting small businesses that rely on fair and open dealings like myself.

comment_text:
I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

comment_text: TO: ​ ​ United States Environmental Protection Agency FROM: ​ The Alaska Center and Cook Inletkeeper DATE: ​ February 17, 2026 RE: ​ ​ Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 The Alaska Center is a nonprofit organization working to engage, empower, and elect Alaskans to stand up for our clean air and water, healthy communities, and a strong democracy. Cook Inletkeeper is a community-based nonprofit dedicated to protecting Alaska’s Cook Inlet watershed and the life it sustains. On behalf of our members and supporters, we submit the following comments. We strongly oppose the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed update to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act. This change limits the ability of states and tribal governments to review projects and possibly block them if they threaten waterways, wetlands, and drinking water. Federal, state, and tribal cooperation is fundamental to ensuring the public’s interests are represented and reflected in future developments — both in Alaska, and across the United States. Weakening Section 401 only serves to undermine important water protections. Overview Over the course of the last year Alaska has received disproportionate attention from the Trump Administration around a single topic — resource extraction.1 The current Administration has pushed its agenda onto Alaska’s land, waters, and communities with little input or regard for public opinion. Proposed activities, from an increase in mining to drilling for oil and deforestation, all have significant implications for Alaska’s water supplies. The timing of this proposal from the EPA makes it clear that the federal government is hopeful that easing safeguards around clean water will allow industries to build faster and with fewer regulations. In practice, Alaska’s natural resources are inherently unique, and deserve due consideration whenever they are subject to federal policies made in Washington, D.C. For example, Alaska is home to more than 40 percent of the entire nation's surface water resources.2 The state is 1 https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-actions/2025/01/unleashing-alaskas-extraordinary-resource-potential/ 2 https://dnr.alaska.gov/mlw/water/#:~:text=Alaska%20has%20more%20than%2040,%25%20surface%20water% 2C%2018%25%20groundwater 1 home to three million lakes, over 12,000 rivers, thousands of streams and creeks, and approximately 100,000 glaciers, according to the State Department of Natural Resources. These figures put the scale and potential impact to Alaska from the EPA’s proposal into focus. According to Alaska’s State Constitution, water is a public resource in Alaska intended to deliver maximum benefits to the public.3 These benefits are numerous in scope, as water drives much of Alaska’s state economy. In addition to providing 98 percent of the country’s salmon supply, local waters drive our recreational industry.4 Moreover, clean water is a backbone to our state’s subsistence resources and animal life. For these reasons, among others, Alaskans have long understood that water bodies require protection from overuse and contamination. Local Expertise Alaska is no stranger to the potential fallout of pollution and contamination driven by extractive industries. These experiences have proven that small missteps can have far-ranging geographic consequences, impacting Alaskan’s health, and the well-being of our environment. For example, fallout from the Red Dog Mine in Alaska, a massive zinc-lead operation, shows the serious consequences of contamination from mining activity. In 2016, the mine released 756 million pounds of toxic chemicals into the environment.5 That same year, according to the EPA’s Toxic Releases Inventory (TRI), the mine released more toxic waste than any other facility in the U.S. The town of Kotzeube, despite being 80 miles away, was declared the most industrially polluted town in the country as streams and rivers mixed with debris.6 Contaminants released from the mine included a number of materials harmful to human health. Runoff from these types of contaminates is easily transported through waterways and evaporation. Not surprisingly, this spike in waste was linked with poor water quality in the area. This raised concerns among many in the area dependent on subsistence resources which draw from this water supply, including both plant and animal life.7 Alaskans have also seen these hazards play out much closer to population centers. In 2009, it was reported that a former North Pole Refinery, located outside of Fairbanks, caused 3 https://law.justia.com/constitution/alaska/constitution-8.html 4 https://www.alaskaseafood.org/news/for-release-alaska-wild-salmon-day-honors-our-nations-favorite-fish-and-t he-people-who-provide-it/ 5 https://www.nationalgeographic.com/science/article/most-toxic-town-us-kotzebue-alaska-red-dog-mine 6 https://theworld.org/stories/2018/06/04/most-toxic-town-america 7 https://www.sciencealert.com/most-toxic-town-in-the-united-states-epa-chemicals-pollution-kotzebue-alaska 2 extensive groundwater contamination with sulfolane and PFAS. These substances migrated into local residential water supplies. A court later found poor maintenance and spills from the refinery responsible for this fallout.8 Residents were forced to find alternative water sources once the discovery was made. While we hope these experiences, and similar instances of water pollution, will never be repeated, it is in the public’s best interest to prevent similar happenings. The lessons learned from past incidents can help keep history from repeating itself - yet these lessons largely lie with locals. Removing state and tribal expertise effectively removes important knowledge from smart decision-making processes. It is important to understand that the Trump Administration's mineral and resource agenda would spike construction and extractive activities across Alaska. This volume alone increases the likelihood of incidents, and harm to both communities and local ecosystems. Health Impacts Approximately 31 Alaska Native communities lack piped, in-home running water in Alaska. These communities, which compromise roughly 1,700 homes, rely on hauling water from natural sources or community watering points.9 When we discuss safeguards to water in Alaska, we are discussing direct threats to drinking water in some cases. Alaska is also unique in the sense that water quality directly impacts principal food sources. As previously noted, salmon is a huge source of subsistence for Alaskan communities. These fish are a staple food for many larger mammals that are also hunted for subsistence and recreation. Water contamination is in essence, contamination of local food supplies. This dependence on natural resources should highlight the seriousness with which Alaskan communities approach clean water. Any direct impact to public health deserves the weigh in of anyone being put at risk - not just federal policymakers. Recently, the EPA announced it would no longer assign a cost value to human health and life when considering the cost-benefit calculations of air pollution activities.10 This raises a number of questions around how the federal government will evaluate projects, and what level of risk we can expect from future federal proposals. 8 https://alaskabeacon.com/briefs/alaska-supreme-court-finds-former-north-pole-refinery-owner-liable-for-pollutio n/ 9 https://www.adn.com/sponsored-content/2020/09/15/in-rural-alaska-access-to-water-is-preventative-medicine/#: ~:text=SPONSORED:%20Water%20and%20sanitation%20projects,with%20water%20for%2015%20years 10 https://www.nytimes.com/2026/01/12/climate/trump-epa-air-pollution.html 3 It is particularly disheartening to see tribal participation in decision-making processes minimized at a time when the U.S. government acknowledges it is following short on its legal duties to tribal nations.11 This is in spite of the fact that Traditional Ecological Knowledge, or Indigenous management styles of land and water have been previously shown to improve ecosystems and help mitigate climate change.12 Removing tribal participation is a clear step in the wrong direction. Adaptation and Resilience Alaska and the rest of the Arctic are on the front lines of climate change, warming four times faster than the rest of the planet.13 What this means, in a practical sense, is that Alaska's landscape and resources are changing at a rapid rate. Permafrost melt and changing temperatures are literally shifting the ground beneath our feet, and planning for new developments or construction requires advanced knowledge and foresight. Just this year, the Denali Commission has proposed adding five new climate-change driven threats to Alaska’s outlook. These include landslides, tsunamis, wildfires, earthquakes, and volcanoes.14 Adapting to these new challenges is essential, and both tribal and state bodies should be able to communicate their on-the-ground expertise as environmental norms continue to change. These new threats will undoubtedly impact projects that interact with our water resources, as well as the sensitivity of our waterways. In summary, The Alaska Center and Cook Inletkeeper strongly urges the EPA to not adopt the proposed revisions to Section 401. Upholding the participation of tribes and states in decision-making is vital to protecting public water sources, and maintaining clean water standards both now, and for future generations. We look forward to the opportunity to continue our engagement in this review process. Jennifer Hyde Satchel Pondolfino Federal Infrastructure Coordinator Clean Water Lead The Alaska Center Cook Inletkeeper jennifer@akcenter.org 11 https://grist.org/indigenous/us-government-says-it-is-falling-short-on-its-legal-duties-to-tribal-nations/ 12 Ibid. 13 https://www.nature.com/articles/s43247-022-00498-3 14 https://alaskabeacon.com/briefs/new-hazards-to-be-analyzed-in-alaskas-updated-statewide-threat-assessment / 4

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 and focuses on the proposed Section 401 changes, state and tribal review authority, and water protection impacts.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized around clear themes, though some claims are broad and the causal links between the proposal and specific harms are sometimes asserted rather than fully developed.
rationales.evidence: The letter includes multiple citations and concrete examples from Alaska, including Red Dog Mine, North Pole Refinery contamination, water access statistics, and climate-related risks. Some sources are secondary or anecdotal, but the evidence base is substantial.

It is persuasive and well organized, but its recommendations are fairly general and some impact claims would benefit from tighter, more direct evidence tied to the rule change.

comment_text:
The proposed regulation raises significant concerns under the Commerce Clause of the
U.S. Constitution. Imposing these mandates interferes with free trade and imposes undue
burdens on brokers, carriers, and shippers.

The expansion of 371.3 disregards the evolution of the transportation industry. Its original intent in 1980 was to prevent freight rebating in a very di0erent marketplace. This rule fails to align with the current operational realities of brokers.

The rule may violate the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 by compelling the disclosure of sensitive pricing and business information, which could severely damage brokers’
competitive standing.

The government does not usually require industry participants to share proprietary and
trade secret information with commercial counter parties with which it will likely negotiate
in the future.
This lack of information about another party’s profit margin is not unique to brokerage,
indeed, it is not unique to transportation. Just as a shipper has no idea what a carrier’s
margin might be, a party buying a pair of shoes at Walmart has no idea how much it cost
the manufacturer to make the shoe, or how much Walmart is marking up the shoe. There is
no law that requires Walmart to disclose its mark-up between what it paid the
manufacturer and what it is selling the shoe for. There is no reason to treat a broker
di0erently. Nor is there any reason to treat a broker di0erently than a motor carrier that
might contract with a shipper directly.
The rule, as proposed, will put brokers at risk of having to choose between regulatory
compliance or breaching commercial contracts with their customers.
Both OOIDA’s and SBTC’s proposed rules run counter to the clear Congressional intent
expressed in the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (“DTSA”) and its state law
counterpart the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (“UTSA”). The UTSA has been adopted in nearly
or perhaps every state. And, the DTSA passed unanimously in the Senate and by a vote of
410-2 in the House. That is a compelling argument that Congressional intent is to protect
trade secrets.
DTSA defines “trade secrets” as “all forms and types of financial, business . . . information,
including . . . compilations, . . . formulas, methods, techniques, processes . . . if – (A) the
owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the
information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being
generally know to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another
person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information.” 18
U.S.C. § 1839(3). The type of information that the proposed rule seeks to be disclosed
clearly fall within the definition of a “trade secret.”

overall_summary: A strong, directly relevant comment that raises serious legal and commercial concerns about the proposal, supported by statutory references and practical examples. Its main weaknesses are limited specificity in requested remedies and somewhat repetitive organization.

comment_text: February 17, 2026 U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 RE: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 To Whom it May Concern: The Upper Mattaponi Tribe respectfully submits this request for an extension of the public comment period for the proposed revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 regulatory requirements. The Federal Register notice provides only 30 days for public comment. Given the scope, complexity, and potential impacts of this rulemaking, that timeframe is not sufficient to allow for meaningful Tribal engagement, technical review, intergovernmental coordination, or government-to-government consultation. Section 401 of the Clean Water Act is one of the most important tools available to states and Tribes to protect water quality from federally licensed or permitted projects. Changes to its scope, interpretation, or implementation have far-reaching consequences for water resources, Tribal sovereignty, and treaty-reserved rights. A rule of this magnitude demands careful legal and scientific analysis. Tribal governments must also evaluate how the proposal affects cultural resources, subsistence practices, and long-term environmental stewardship responsibilities. Thirty days does not allow for this level of review. We therefore request a 180-day extension of the public comment period. At an absolute minimum, no less than 90 days should be provided. This additional time is necessary for Tribes to consult with leadership, coordinate with neighboring jurisdictions, engage technical experts, and fully assess the implications of the proposal. It is also necessary to meet the federal government’s trust responsibility and ensure meaningful consultation with federally recognized Tribal Nations. EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin has publicly stated that “EPA is committed to working government to government with federally recognized Tribes” and that Tribal engagement is central to EPA’s mission of protecting human health and the environment. A 30-day comment period for a rule of this magnitude is inconsistent with that commitment. Meaningful government-to-government engagement requires time, transparency, and respect for Tribal sovereignty. Importance of Section 401 and Cooperative Federalism Congress enacted the Clean Water Act with a clear and ambitious purpose to “restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters.” The Act recognizes that states and Tribes play a critical role in achieving that purpose. Section 401 embodies this cooperative federalism framework by allowing states and authorized Tribes to review federal permits or licenses that may result in discharges to Waters of the United States and to ensure that those activities comply with water quality requirements. There is no credible evidence that the Section 401 certification process is fundamentally broken. States and Tribes across the country have used this authority responsibly for decades. In the Chesapeake Bay region, Section 401 certifications have served as an essential safeguard for one of the most ecologically and economically important estuaries in the United States. Where Tribes or states do not have authority, EPA serves as the certifying authority. This balanced system reflects the structure Congress intended. The Chesapeake Bay restoration effort provides a powerful lesson. Decades of pollution led to severe ecosystem decline, requiring sustained, coordinated federal, state, and Tribal action to begin recovery. That progress remains fragile. Weakening Section 401 authority or reducing review timelines would place that progress at risk. It is much easier to contaminate water than it is to restore it (e.g., the nearly 8,000 gallons of jet fuel that accidentally got released into the James River on February 13, 2026). Decisions made under Section 401 directly affect drinking water supplies, fisheries, wetlands, submerged aquatic vegetation, and habitat that communities across the Bay and United States. Timelines and Procedural Safeguards The current certification process appropriately balances efficiency and environmental protection. Most certifications are issued well within existing timelines. The full one-year period is typically necessary only for the most complex projects, where technical analysis and public input are essential. The 2023 Certification Improvement Rule provided additional clarity, including a six- month default timeline when parties cannot otherwise agree. Shortening state and Tribal review periods, or limiting public comment opportunities, would undermine sovereign authority and create unreasonable incentives. Applicants could submit incomplete or insufficient applications and allow the clock to run out, resulting in waivers of certification authority. This would shift power away from sovereign governments and toward project applicants. If procedural changes are considered, the burden should be placed on applicants to provide complete information within a defined timeframe. The timeline for state and Tribal review should not be shortened. Public comment periods should not be further restricted. Many Tribes operate environmental programs with limited staffing and funding. Compressing timelines disproportionately harms Tribal Nations and reduces their ability to assert sovereign authority. Water systems are complex, and the risks of contamination can be severe and long-lasting. A deliberate and thorough review process is not an obstacle to progress. It is a necessary safeguard for public health and environmental protection. Tribal Sovereignty and Treatment as a State For Tribal Nations, Section 401 authority is not simply an administrative process. It is a recognition of inherent sovereignty and a mechanism for protecting treaty-reserved rights and reserved water resources. Congress enacted Section 518(e) of the Clean Water Act to ensure that Tribes can be treated in a similar manner as states for purposes of administering water quality programs. This provision reflects the federal trust responsibility and affirms that Tribes have the authority and responsibility to protect waters within their jurisdiction. Repealing or limiting the regulatory recognition of standalone Treatment as a State authority for Sections 401 and 401(a)(2) would weaken Tribal self-governance in environmental decision making. Removing clear regulatory pathways that allow Tribes to exercise Section 401 authority on their own could create real barriers to participation. Consultation is important, but it is not the same as having regulatory authority. Without clear and accessible certification authority, Tribes could be left in advisory roles instead of having enforceable decision-making power over projects that directly affect their waters and resources. Linking Section 401 authority exclusively to Section 303 Treatment as a State status could create additional administrative barriers. Many Tribal environmental programs operate with limited staff and financial resources. Requiring additional approvals before exercising certification authority could delay participation in critical decisions or discourage Tribes from seeking authority altogether, Such barriers reduce fair access to federal environmental programs and conflict with long standing federal policy that supports Tribal self-determination. Water holds deep cultural, spiritual, and subsistence importance for Tribal Nations. It sustains fisheries, wildlife, culturally significant plants, and ceremonial practices that define community identity and continuity. Section 401 authority allows Tribes to protect these living systems for future generations. Rescinding that authority sends a troubling signal about the federal government’s commitment to Tribal sovereignty. Protection of Narrative Water Quality Standards Equally concerning is any suggestion that Section 401 certification decisions should rely solely on numeric water quality standards. Water quality standards are made up of four core parts: designated uses, numeric criteria, narrative criteria, and antidegradation policies. Each of these components plays an essential role in protecting water resources. Narrative criteria are not optional or secondary. They are federally approved, legally enforceable standards that exist because not all forms of water pollution can be measured by a single number. Many forms of water quality harm cannot be reduced to a specific pollutant concentration. Habitat destruction, streambank erosion, sediment buildup, biological impairment, nuisance algae growth, foul odors, and long-term ecosystem stress often result from a combination of factors. These impacts may develop gradually and may not immediately violate a numeric threshold. Narrative standards allow regulators to address these broader conditions by requiring that waters remain free from substances or activities that cause harm to aquatic life, wildlife, or human uses. Without narrative criteria, regulators would be forced to ignore serious ecological damage simply because it does not exceed a specific numeric value. This issue is especially serious for wetlands. Wetlands are among the most productive and valuable ecosystems in the country. They filter pollutants, reduce flooding, recharge groundwater, store carbon, and provide critical habitat for fish, birds, and other wildlife. In regions such as the Chesapeake Bay watershed, wetlands play a central role in improving overall water quality by trapping sediment and nutrients before they reach rivers and the Bay itself. Yet water quality standards for wetlands are frequently written in narrative form rather than numeric. Wetland health is often measured by vegetation condition, hydrology, soil characteristics, and biological integrity rather than a single pollutant concentration. If certification decisions were limited to numeric standards only, wetlands could effectively be excluded from meaningful protection under Section 401. This would create a major regulatory gap. Activities that drain, fill, fragment, or degrade wetlands might escape full review if they do not violate a specific numeric pollutant limit. Such an outcome would undermine decades of scientific understanding about the ecological importance of wetlands. Excluding or minimizing narrative standards artificially narrows the lawful scope of review and reduces the ability to protect interconnected water systems. For Tribal Nations, numeric-only limitations are particularly harmful. Tribal water quality standards often reflect culturally specific uses that depend on healthy ecosystems, including fisheries, wildlife habitat, culturally significant plants, and ceremonial waters. These uses require protection of biological integrity and overall ecosystem function, not just compliance with isolated pollutant numbers. Wetlands are often culturally significant landscapes that support traditional foods, medicines, and practices. A certification process that disregards narrative standards fails to account for these relationships and disregards Tribal knowledge and stewardship. A scientifically sound and legally defensible Section 401 certification process must recognize both numeric and narrative standards as equally enforceable. Water systems are complex and interconnected. Effective protection requires tools that reflect that complexity. Limiting review to numeric thresholds alone ignores ecological reality, weakens wetland protection, and undermines the Clean Water Act’s core goal of restoring and maintaining the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters. Changes to “Neighboring Jurisdiction” The proposed change replacing the long-standing term “neighboring jurisdictions” with “other States” raises serious concerns. Section 401(a)(2) of the Clean Water Act was written to ensure that downstream and adjacent sovereign governments have a meaningful opportunity to review and object to federally permitted projects that may affect their water quality. EPA has stated that “other States” includes Tribes with Treatment as a State status. However, replacing clearer language with a narrower term creates confusion and may unintentionally limit Tribal participation. If Tribes are not explicitly and consistently recognized, they could face barriers in receiving notice, raising objections, or requesting conditions to protect their waters. Even if the intent is not to exclude Tribes, the revised wording may weaken the cooperative federalism structure of the Clean Water Act and conflict with EPA’s long-standing practice of treating eligible Tribes in a similar manner as states for water quality protection. Any change that affects how Tribes are recognized in this process should be clearly explained and carefully justified to avoid undermining Tribal sovereignty and downstream water protections. Conclusion The Clean Water Act’s Section 401 program is working as Congress intended. It reflects true cooperative federalism, safeguards water quality, and respects the sovereign authority of states and Tribal Nations. There is no credible evidence that the program is broken or that it requires weakening. Where implementation challenges exist, they should be addressed in ways that strengthen water protections, improve clarity, and support sound decision-making, not in ways that accelerate approvals at the expense of environmental integrity and long-term public interest. Weakening Section 401 authority or narrowing the scope of certification review would not only threaten water quality, but it would also impose serious and measurable economic harm. The Chesapeake Bay watershed supports an economy valued at more than $100 billion annually, including commercial and recreational fisheries, tourism, agriculture, and outdoor recreation. Recreational fishing alone contributes more than $1 billion each year in economic activity. When water quality declines, these sectors are immediately affected. Harmful algal blooms, sedimentation, and habitat degradation reduce fish populations, close shellfish beds, disrupt tourism, and strain local economies. Restoration is far more expensive than prevention. Since the 1980s, federal and state partners have invested over $20 billion in Chesapeake Bay restoration, with total long-term costs projected to reach tens of billions more. Weakening preventative safeguards now risk repeating past mistakes and compounding those costs. Degraded water quality increases drinking water treatment expenses, burdens aging infrastructure, and heightens public health risks. These costs fall on Tribal governments that often operate with limited budgets and staffing. A diminished Section 401 framework would shift financial responsibility away from project applicants and onto Tribes, states, and taxpayers, who would ultimately bear the long-term costs of ecosystem decline, lost fisheries, damaged cultural resources, and water treatment upgrades. Strong certification authority is not only environmentally sound policy, but also fiscally responsible governance. For the Chesapeake Bay, for Tribal Nations, and for future generations who depend on clean and reliable water, EPA must maintain the substantive framework of the 2023 Certification Improvement Rule, preserve standalone Treatment as a State authority, reject numeric-only limitations, retain existing procedural timelines, and ensure meaningful government-to- government consultation. Protecting water quality is not an obstacle to economic progress. It is the foundation of public health, ecological resilience, stable regional economies, and Tribal sovereignty. This proposed rule does not correct a failing system. Instead, it risks creating uncertainty, weakening proven safeguards, and exposing communities to long-term environmental and economic harm. The practical course is clear: strengthen what works, uphold sovereign authority, and protect the waters that sustain us all. Sincerely, Liz Buikema Environmental Protection Manager Upper Mattaponi Tribe King William, VA

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the docket topic: proposed revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 requirements, including comment period length, Tribal authority, narrative standards, and neighboring jurisdiction language.
rationales.reasoning: The letter is generally coherent and logically organized, with clear links between Section 401, Tribal sovereignty, and water protection. Some arguments are broad or repetitive, but the overall reasoning is sound.
rationales.evidence: It includes a few concrete references, such as the 2023 Certification Improvement Rule, EPA statements, Chesapeake Bay restoration spending, and the James River jet fuel spill. However, it relies heavily on assertions without detailed citations or data sources.
rationales.impact: The letter explains plausible impacts on Tribal consultation, water quality, wetlands, sovereignty, and regional economies. The impacts are specific in theme, though some claims would be stronger with more direct supporting evidence.
rationales.actionability: It makes clear requests: extend the comment period to 180 days, or at minimum 90 days; retain existing timelines; preserve standalone TAS authority; reject numeric-only limitations; and clarify Tribal recognition in neighboring jurisdiction provisions.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is professionally formatted, with a clear salutation, headings, logical sectioning, and a strong conclusion. It is lengthy, but generally readable and well organized.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, relevant public comment letter that clearly advocates for Tribal interests and regulatory caution. Its main weakness is limited citation depth, but it is persuasive, organized, and actionable.

overall_summary: A well-targeted and professionally written comment that strongly engages the docket issues, offers clear policy requests, and presents plausible harms to Tribal sovereignty and water protection. It would be stronger with more precise citations and empirical support.

comment_text:
I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive and focuses on the requested issues of safety, transparency, and labeling.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with clear links between synthetic biology production, consumer perception, and safety concerns, though some claims are asserted more strongly than they are demonstrated.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes a few concrete technical references, such as specific genes and the yeast host, but it provides little empirical data, citations, or documented examples to support the safety and allergenicity claims.
rationales.impact: The potential impacts are plausible, especially around consumer deception and allergen concerns, but the magnitude and likelihood of harm are not well quantified or substantiated.
rationales.actionability: It offers clear requests, including proteomic analysis, labeling restrictions, and allergen warnings, which are feasible in principle and easy for the agency to understand.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is well organized with numbered sections, clear headings, and a professional tone, making it easy to follow.
rationales.overall: This is a focused and readable comment with concrete policy asks, but it would be stronger with more evidence and more precise support for its safety and labeling claims.

overall_summary: The comment is highly relevant and well structured, with clear recommendations for labeling and safety review. Its main weakness is limited supporting evidence, which makes some of the asserted risks less persuasive despite the coherent presentation.

comment_text:
To Whom It May Concern,

On behalf of Caltiger, I am writing to express our strong support for the proposed rule under Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257 (RIN 2126-AC63) regarding transparency in property broker transactions.

In an industry built on partnerships between carriers, brokers, and shippers, transparency is not just beneficial—it is essential for maintaining trust and accountability. Carriers deserve visibility into the financial aspects of the loads they haul, ensuring they are compensated fairly for their services. The current lack of transparency undermines this trust, leaving carriers vulnerable to inconsistent and often inequitable payment structures.

The FMCSA’s proposed rule strikes a necessary balance. It promotes clarity without burdening honest brokers who already operate with integrity. Transparency ensures that all stakeholders can engage in fair business practices, ultimately strengthening the entire supply chain.

Caltiger stands firmly behind this initiative. We believe it will promote healthier business relationships, encourage ethical practices, and create a more sustainable industry for all. We urge the FMCSA to move forward with this rule and appreciate your commitment to protecting the interests of carriers nationwide.

Thank you for considering our perspective on this important issue.

-CalTiger Team.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses FMCSA-2023-0257 and the topic of transparency in property broker transactions, clearly supporting the proposed rule.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is coherent and easy to follow, but it remains largely general and relies on broad assertions rather than a developed line of reasoning.
rationales.evidence: No facts, data, citations, or concrete examples are provided to support the claims about fairness, trust, or payment inequities.
rationales.impact: The comment states likely benefits such as improved trust and ethical practices, but these impacts are not quantified or explained in a specific, plausible way.
rationales.actionability: It clearly urges FMCSA to move forward with the rule, but it does not offer detailed recommendations, modifications, or implementation suggestions.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is professionally formatted, concise, and readable, with a clear opening, body, and closing, though it is somewhat generic.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and polished support letter, but it is mostly high-level advocacy with limited evidence, specificity, or actionable detail.

overall_summary: A clear, professional comment that strongly supports the proposed rule and stays on topic, but it lacks supporting evidence, detailed impact analysis, and concrete recommendations.

overall_summary: A focused and readable comment that strongly engages the labeling and safety issues for a synthetic-biology-produced color additive. It is persuasive in tone and actionable, but would be stronger with citations, data, and more specific evidence for the claimed health and consumer impacts.

comment_text: ATTORNEYS GENERAL OF THE STATES OF WASHINGTON, CALIFORNIA, NEW YORK, COLORADO, CONNECTICUT, ILLINOIS, MAINE, MARYLAND, MICHIGAN, MINNESOTA, NEW JERSEY, NEW MEXICO, OREGON, RHODE ISLAND, WISCONSIN, VERMONT, THE COMMONWEALTH OF MASSACHUSETTS, AND THE DISTRICT OF COLUMBIA February 17, 2026 By U.S. Mail, E-mail, and Electronically Attn: Environmental Protection Agency EPA Docket Center, Water Docket Mail Code 28221T 2100 Pennsylvania Ave. NW Washington, D.C. 20460 RE: Proposed Rule Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations, 91 Fed. Reg. 2008 (Jan. 15, 2026), Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Dear Administrator Zeldin and Assistant Administrator Kramer: The undersigned Attorneys General (the States) submit these comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) Proposed Rule, Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations, 91 Fed. Reg. 2008 (Jan. 15, 2026), Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 (Proposed Rule). The States, via authorized regulatory agencies, have for decades exercised authority under section 401 to issue or deny water quality certifications for activities that require a federal license or permit and may result in a discharge into waters of the United States. Throughout this time, and pursuant to the policy Congress expressed in the Clean Water Act to “recognize, preserve, and protect the primary responsibilities of States,” 33 U.S.C. § 1251(b), states have successfully implemented section 401 in a manner that is consistent with the Clean Water Act, state laws, and the states’ sovereign, proprietary, and other interests in water quality within their borders. EPA’s Proposed Rule is EPA’s second attempt to unlawfully curtail states’ rights to address the water quality impacts of federally licensed or permitted activities through the Clean Water Act’s section 401. EPA’s last attempt to accomplish this improper goal in 2020 was a failure. The 2020 Rule caused confusion and project delays around the country—the exact opposite of EPA’s purported goals—while decreasing the protection of the Nation’s waters. EPA’s revision to the section 401 rule in 2023 restored section 401 practice to that which existed prior to 2020 and that had successfully governed section 401 certifications for nearly a half-century. EPA now backtracks and repeats its most significant mistakes. First and foremost, the Proposed Rule unlawfully strips states of authority that Congress, in an abundantly clear statute and legislative record, plainly intended the states to retain. As Senator Muskie, the primary sponsor and architect of the Clean Water Act stated, the goals of section 401 are straightforward: “[a]ll we ask is that the activities that threaten to pollute the environment be subjected to the February 17, 2026 Page | 2 examination of the environmental improvement agency of the State for an evaluation and recommendation before the federal license or permit be granted.” Attach. 1, 117 Cong. Rec. 38854 (1971) (Nov. 2, 1971, House Debate on the Clean Water Act) (emphasis added).1 EPA’s Proposed Rule falls well short of this mark. EPA should abandon the Proposed Rule and should instead implement section 401 in a manner that is consistent with the Clean Water Act’s text, legislative intent, and applicable caselaw. EPA’s Attempts to Bypass the Supreme Court’s Prior Interpretations of Section 401’s Scope Render EPA’s Proposal Contrary to Law ............................................. 26 C. IF ADOPTED, EPA’S PROPOSED RULE WOULD VIOLATE THE ADMINISTRATIVE PROCEDURE ACT ...................................................................... 43 A. The Proposed Rule Is Not in Accordance with Law ................................................. 43 B. EPA Lacks Statutory Authority to Promulgate the Proposed Rule ............................ 43 C. The Proposed Rule Is Arbitrary and Capricious and an Abuse of Discretion ............ 44 1. EPA failed to consider the relevant factors related to implementing section 401 and did not provide a rational basis for the Proposed Rule .......................... 45 February 17, 2026 Page | 4 2. EPA failed to provide a reasoned explanation for the change in its position on section 401 implementation ......................................................................... 46 3. The Proposed Rule does not consider and analyze alternatives .......................... 48 4. EPA’s failure to actually evaluate environmental impacts, including impacts to children, and its use of outdated economic practices are fundamentally flawed ....................................................................................... 48 X. CONCLUSION............................................................................................................... 50 I. SECTION 401 BACKGROUND A. Section 401 is a Cornerstone of Congress’s Efforts to Preserve States’ Traditional Authority Over Water Quality The states’ traditional authority in regulating water quality long precedes the Clean Water Act and is “preserved” through the Act, which is built upon recognizing the “rights of states to prevent, reduce, and eliminate pollution.” 33 U.S.C. § 1251(b). This clear commitment to recognize state sovereignty to protect water quality has roots at least as far back as the 1948 Water Pollution Control Act. Debate on that Act noted: “[t]here is not a single step that is taken under the pending bill that is not first authorized by some [s]tate agency, either by the public- health service of the [s]tate or some agency that may be designated by the [s]tate’s public-health authority.” Attach. 2, 80 Cong.
