This failure demonstrates that the agency is acting in a manner that is arbitrary and capricious and in violation of the APA. 4. EPA’s failure to actually evaluate environmental impacts, including impacts to children, and its use of outdated economic practices are fundamentally flawed Executive Order 12866 requires executive agencies to prepare “[a]n assessment, including the underlying analysis,” of the benefits and costs associated with any proposed or final regulation that is expected to have significant economic impacts. 58 Fed. Reg. 51735, 51741 (Oct. 4, 1993). EPA’s economic analysis falls short in several key respects and should be redone. First, EPA fails to adequately account for the environmental impacts from the Proposed Rule. EPA acknowledges the potential for negative environmental impacts from drastically reducing the scope of section 401 certifications compared to the 2023 Rule and the system in place for half-a-century prior to the 2020 Rule. Economic Analysis at 28–29. Yet, EPA dismisses those impacts because it asserts that “certification decisions reflective of the narrower scope would be less likely to face legal challenges.” Id. at 29. EPA further claims that, if the 2023 Rule were overturned, “any supposed or perceived lower level of environmental quality would in fact be illusory.” Id. February 17, 2026 Page | 49 These assumptions do not support EPA’s failure to incorporate environmental impacts into its cost-benefit analysis. EPA provides nothing to justify its bald claim that section 401 certifications might be challenged more under the 2023 rule. To the States’ knowledge, that is far from the case. EPA also fails to acknowledge that certifications reflecting a narrower scope are just as—if not more—likely to be challenged by environmental groups as directly contradictory to the plain language of the Clean Water Act as interpreted by the Supreme Court. In fact, a Washington State section 401 certification made under the 2020 Rule is currently facing that very challenge. EPA’s refusal to analyze environmental impacts based on what litigants or courts might do, including the currently unknowable question of whether the Supreme Court would overturn its own decisions in PUD No. 1 and S.D. Warren is unsupportable. EPA also fails to follow its own policies with regard to impacts to children. See EPA’s Children’s Health Policy.15 With a hand-wave, EPA boldly claims no need to follow its Children’s Health Policy because “EPA does not believe the action has considerations for human health.” 91 Fed. Reg. at 2037–38. This statement—void of any analysis or support—flies in the face of logic given that EPA proposes to drastically curtail the scope of water quality impacts states can review and condition under section 401. These water quality protections are designed to protect the quality of water for recreation, fish consumption, drinking, and a host of other designated uses that are related to or crucial for protecting human health. Moreover, under the Regulatory Right-to-Know Act, the Office of Management and Budget (OMB) is required to issue guidelines to agencies in order to “standardize” calculation of these impacts. Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2001, Pub. L. No. 106-554, § 624(c)(1). OMB first issued these guidelines in 2003 in Circular A-4. The guidelines were revised and significantly updated in 2023 via a process that was subject to public comment and independent peer review. 16 On January 31, 2025, the President issued Executive Order 14192, directing OMB to rescind the 2023 revision to Circular A-4 and reinstate the 2003 version. 90 Fed. Reg. 9065 (Feb. 6, 2025). As a result, EPA’s Economic Analysis was conducted under the 2003 Circular A-4. See Economic Analysis at 1–2, 38. Executive Order 12866 states that agencies must “propose or adopt a regulation upon a reasoned determination that the benefits of the intended regulation justify its costs.” 58 Fed. Reg. at 51736. Moreover, “when an agency decides to rely on a cost-benefit analysis as part of its rulemaking, a serious flaw undermining that analysis can render the rule unreasonable.” Nat’l Ass’n of Home Builders v. EPA, 682 F.3d 1032, 1040 (D.C. Cir. 2012) (citation modified). For example, an agency that fails to “examine the relevant data” or that offers “an explanation for its decision that runs counter to the evidence” engages in arbitrary action. State Farm, 463 U.S. at 43; see also City of Portland v. EPA, 507 F.3d 706, 713 (D.C. Cir. 2007) (courts will not “tolerate rules based on arbitrary and capricious cost-benefit analyses”). EPA’s reliance on outdated and obsolete economic practices further undermines EPA’s economic analysis. 15 Available at: https://www.epa.gov/children/childrens-health-policy-and-plan. 16 ICF Int’l, Individual Peer Reviewer Comments on Proposed OMB Circular No. A-4, “Regulatory Analysis” (Aug. 3, 2023), https://perma.cc/UWV4-QR46. February 17, 2026 Page | 50 X. CONCLUSION For the foregoing reasons, EPA should abandon and withdraw the Proposed Rule. NICHOLAS W. rationales.relevance: Directly addresses the proposed rule and nearly every major issue in the docket, including scope, timing, withdrawal/resubmission, modifications, tribal TAS, and APA concerns.
rationales.reasoning: Arguments are highly coherent, legally structured, and consistently tied to statutory text, legislative history, and case law. The letter anticipates counterarguments and responds to them in detail.
rationales.evidence: Extensive use of statutory citations, Supreme Court and circuit precedent, EPA guidance, Federal Register references, legislative history, and concrete state examples/statistics strongly supports the claims.
rationales.impact: The comment explains likely effects in specific and plausible terms, such as delays, denials, litigation, reduced water quality protections, and burdens on state agencies and applicants.
rationales.actionability: It clearly requests EPA to withdraw or abandon the proposal and, in several sections, recommends retaining the 2023 rule or specific provisions. Some recommendations are broad rather than narrowly operational.
rationales.structure_formatting: Very well organized with a detailed table of contents, numbered sections, subheadings, and professional formatting typical of a formal legal comment letter.
rationales.overall: A comprehensive, highly persuasive, and well-supported legal comment that directly engages the rulemaking and offers a strong basis for EPA to reconsider or withdraw the proposal.

overall_summary: This is an exceptionally strong public comment letter: legally sophisticated, heavily documented, and tightly focused on the proposed rule’s core issues. It provides a thorough statutory and case-law critique, concrete examples of likely harms, and clear requests for EPA action, though some recommendations are framed at a high level rather than as step-by-step regulatory text.

comment_text:
Subject line: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929

California’s Central Valley, the breadbasket of America, and one of the greatest surface freshwater systems in the world, relies on wetlands, rivers, and streams to filter pollution, prevent flooding in our communities, and support fishing and recreation.  California’s agricultural businesses generate billions in tax revenue for our nation while feeding millions with the rice, nuts, fruits and vegetables grown here. The Clean Water Act has been instrumental in protecting this economic engine. 

The Central Valley Project and the various reservoirs and river systems that comprise it, all considered Waters of the United States (WOTUS), demand adequate Federal protection from upstream pollution sources. It is critical that the U.S. EPA does not degrade the Clean Water Act and weaken stakeholders’ ability to protect sources that feed WOTUS in California. 

The proposed rule states at page 2040 of the Fed Reg notice:

Section 121.3 Scope of certification. The scope of a Clean Water Act section 401 certification is limited to assuring that a discharge from a federally licensed or permitted activity will comply with applicable and appropriate water quality requirements. 

The proposed rule change to Section 401 seeks to take control away from states and Tribes, thus loosening the clean water protections that have broad appeal across all elements of our economy. This rule limits stakeholders to reviewing only the direct water-quality impacts of a project’s discharges into federally protected waters, and would no longer be able to consider broader water-quality effects caused by the project in its entirety. As such, the proposed regulation is an example of Federal overreach into local governance and control of waterways, where states and authorized tribes have the best ability to review overall impacts to water quality from local activities. 

This proposed rule change will further strip protections of wetlands, which provide significant regional economic benefits. These include:

Recreational activities in the Sacramento-San Joaquin Delta. According to the Delta Stewardship Council’s 2013 Delta Plan, recreational activities are worth an estimated $350 million annually to the region. The ability of the community to continue to benefit from this revenue-generating activity is being harmed by harmful algae blooms that result from upstream pollution. Reducing California’s ability to act on upstream pollution sources will harm this significant industry.
In Yolo County, where I live, we have had to switch our drinking water sources from groundwater to a combination of groundwater and river water from the Sacramento River. Our community is dependent now on WOTUS for our drinking water. We need the State of California and our upstream neighbor, the Yocha Dehe Wintun tribe, to be able to implement policies to ensure that the flow of water from Cache Creek is able to recharge groundwater sources as well as feed into the Sacramento River through the wetlands in Yolo County. The proposed rule would diminish their ability to enact policies that ensure clean drinking water for our communities.
Further, in Yolo County, the Cache Creek watershed has been declared an impaired waterway under the Clean Water Act due to mercury discharges from legacy mining operations. As Cache Creek does not always flow directly into the Sacramento River and connects to the Central Valley system through wetlands, this change to the Clean Water Act would remove important protections that are driving action to lower mercury exposure. The proposed rule change opens up the specter of uncontrolled mercury discharge into our local waterways, water supplies and even into the air we breathe during drought years. 
Finally, in the City of Woodland, where I live, 25% of our residents reside within the 200-year flood plain. Our community is vulnerable to flooding along Cache Creek when the Sacramento River system and its wetlands are flooded. The proposed regulation entertains a fiction that these two watersheds are somehow not connected, which impacts the ability of the State of California, County of Yolo and City of Woodland, as well as the Yocha Dehe Wintun Nation upstream, to rely on a consistent, Federally-approved framework for wetlands regulation and flood control. According to the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers Lower Cache Creek December 2019 Draft Supplemental Environmental Impact Statement, the  potential impact of flooding to our community is as much as $2.3 billion in 2019 dollars. Changing the Clean Water Act in the way proposed here will open up our community to significant financial, human and environmental losses.

I encourage the U.S. EPA to strike this proposed rule and keep in place the rules that govern WOTUS and waters that feed into WOTUS today.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 and focuses on the proposed Section 401/WOTUS-related rule change, which is clearly within the docket’s likely scope.
rationales.reasoning: The letter presents a generally coherent argument that narrowing state and tribal review would weaken protections for water quality, wetlands, drinking water, and flood control. Some claims are broad, but the overall logic is understandable and consistent.
rationales.evidence: It includes several concrete references, such as the quoted proposed rule language, the Delta Stewardship Council estimate, the impaired status of Cache Creek, and the Army Corps flood-impact figure. However, it does not provide full citations or detailed supporting documentation.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies specific local harms—drinking water vulnerability, mercury exposure, recreational losses, and flood risk—and ties them to plausible consequences of the rule. The impacts are concrete, though some causal links are asserted more than demonstrated.
rationales.actionability: The main request is clear: strike the proposed rule and retain current WOTUS protections. Beyond that, the comment does not offer more nuanced alternatives, implementation suggestions, or specific regulatory edits.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is organized into a clear progression from general context to specific local examples and ends with a direct request. It is readable and professional, though somewhat long and repetitive in places.
rationales.overall: This is a strong public comment that is highly relevant and grounded in local concerns, with decent supporting facts and a clear policy ask. Its main weaknesses are limited citation detail and relatively broad recommendations.

overall_summary: A well-targeted and persuasive comment that clearly opposes the proposed rule, uses several concrete local examples and data points, and is easy to follow. It would be stronger with fuller citations and more specific alternative recommendations.

comment_text: From: Elizabeth Toohig <noreply@myfor.ms> To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/2/2026 9:49:26 AM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 -------------------------------------------- You don't often get email from noreply@myfor.ms. Learn why this is important Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. I VEHEMENTLY oppose the EPA’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations.(As I'm writing this, I'm wondering if the EPA stands for environmental PROTECTION!!) The proposal would unlawfully and dangerously narrow the authority of states and tribes to protect their waters from harmful projects — especially large oil and gas pipelines. By limiting review to narrowly defined “discharges” and constraining timelines and conditions, the rule undermines Congress’s clear intent that states and tribes serve as frontline protectors of water quality. This rollback is especially concerning for the Great Lakes, which hold 20% of the world’s surface fresh water and supply drinking water to more than 40 million people. In Michigan, the proposal could directly affect decisions regarding Enbridge’s Line 5 oil pipeline, including a proposed tunnel and potential horizontal directional drilling beneath the Straits of Mackinac—an area of extraordinary ecological, cultural, and economic significance. Enbridge has delayed a final decision on its Clean Water Act Section 404 permit from the Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy. If EPA finalizes this rule, it could affect the outcome by restricting Michigan’s ability—and tribal nations’ treaty-protected authority—to fully evaluate and reject water quality risks. States and tribes MUST RETAIN THE POWER to: • Consider cumulative and long-term water quality impacts • Protect treaty rights, fisheries, and drinking water • Deny certification when projects threaten water quality standards The EPA should withdraw this proposal and instead strengthen, not weaken, Clean Water Act protections. The Great Lakes are too important to risk for the convenience of oil pipeline companies. EPA members: PLEASE CONSIDER EVEN JUST THE NAME OF YOUR ORGANIZATION AND RESIGN IF YOU CAN'T LIVE UP TO THAT. Think of what your grandchildren will think of you if they hear you approved provisions that would weaken the Clean Water Act. Sincerely, Elizabeth Toohig Wellesley Hills, MA

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the docket topic: proposed revisions to Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations. It also engages the key issues of state and tribal authority, discharges, timelines, and conditions.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent: it explains that narrowing certification authority would weaken protections and could affect pipeline projects and water quality. Some points are persuasive but more rhetorical than analytically developed.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes a few concrete facts, such as the Great Lakes holding 20% of the world’s surface fresh water and serving over 40 million people, plus references to Enbridge Line 5 and the Straits of Mackinac. However, it provides limited citation or detailed supporting data.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible impacts, including reduced state and tribal authority, risks to drinking water, fisheries, treaty rights, and pipeline-related harms. The impacts are specific, though not deeply quantified or fully substantiated.
rationales.actionability: The comment clearly requests that EPA withdraw the proposal and strengthen Clean Water Act protections. It also specifies that states and tribes should retain authority to consider cumulative impacts and deny certification, making the recommendations fairly actionable.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is readable and organized into a clear narrative with a short list of requested protections. However, it is somewhat informal and includes emotionally charged language that reduces professionalism.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and substantively on-topic comment with clear opposition and concrete policy requests. Its main weaknesses are limited evidentiary support and a somewhat rhetorical tone, but it still presents a credible and understandable public comment.

overall_summary: A strong, directly relevant comment that clearly opposes the proposal and asks EPA to withdraw it. It is persuasive and actionable, though it relies more on advocacy language than detailed evidence or formal analysis.

comment_text: February 17, 2026 To: Environmental Protection Agency Re: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations, 91 Fed. Reg. 2008 (Jan. 15, 2026) (EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929; FRL–6976.2–01–OW) The Institute for Policy Integrity at New York University School of Law (Policy Integrity)1 respectfully submits the following comments to the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) on its proposed rule, Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (Proposed Rule). Policy Integrity is a non-partisan think tank dedicated to improving the quality of government decisionmaking through advocacy and scholarship in the fields of administrative law, economics, and public policy. Section 401 of the Clean Water Act reflects the states’ primary, historical role in protecting their water quality.2 It requires “[a]ny applicant for a Federal license or permit to conduct any activity . . . which may result in any discharge into the navigable waters” to obtain a certification from the state where the discharge will originate.3 The Proposed Rule would narrow the scope of the state’s certification review to discharges only, exempting projects’ other impacts on applicable water-quality standards.4 EPA argues that this approach is consistent with Supreme Court caselaw including PUD No. 1 of Jefferson County v. Washington Department of Ecology, which upheld Section 401 conditions unrelated to project discharges.5 It is not. This letter makes the following five points: • PUD No. 1 held that the best reading of Section 401 is that the state’s certification review includes the entire activity’s compliance with applicable water-quality standards, and did not rely on Chevron deference. • EPA mischaracterizes Section 401’s legislative history. 1 This document does not purport to present the views, if any, of New York University School of Law. 2 See 33 U.S.C. § 1251(b) (“It is the policy of the Congress to recognize, preserve, and protect the primary responsibilities and rights of States to prevent, reduce, and eliminate pollution. . . .”). 3 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a). 4 See Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations, 91 Fed. Reg. 2008, 2023 (Jan. 15, 2026) [hereinafter Proposed Rule]. 5 See id. at 2023–26. • Even assuming that PUD No. 1 relied on Chevron and that EPA’s new reading of the statute is the best reading, PUD No. 1 remains good law and EPA must comply with the Administrative Procedure Act’s reasoned decisionmaking requirements. • The major questions doctrine does not preclude EPA’s longstanding interpretation that state certification reviews under Section 401 cover the entire activity’s compliance with applicable water-quality standards. • EPA should disclose whether it used artificial intelligence tools to develop the Proposed Rule, and provide relevant information about its use of any such tools. This letter elaborates on each of these points below. Background Section 401(a)(1) of the Clean Water Act generally requires applicants for a federal license or permit to conduct any activity that “may result in any discharge into the navigable waters” to obtain a certification from the state where the discharge will originate.6 Section 401(d) establishes the scope of the state’s certification review. It provides that “[a]ny certification provided under this section shall set forth any effluent limitations and other limitations, and monitoring requirements necessary to assure that any applicant for a Federal license or permit will comply with” the requirements set forth in other specified sections of the Clean Water Act and “with any other appropriate requirement of State law set forth in such certification.”7 Such limitations and requirements “become a condition on any Federal license or permit.”8 As the Proposed Rule explains, EPA first interpreted the scope of the state’s certification review in 1971 under the predecessor to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act.9 (Congress enacted the modern Clean Water Act the following year.10) EPA concluded that the entire activity subject to the federal permitting or licensing requirement—not just the discharges from that activity—must comply with applicable water-quality standards.11 In 1994, the Supreme Court in PUD No. 1 read Section 401(d) the same way.12 Ultimately, EPA maintained its 1971 interpretation for nearly fifty years.13 In 2020, EPA restricted the scope of the state’s certification review under Section 401(d) to cover only whether the discharges from the activity satisfy the applicable 6 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a). “[I]f appropriate,” such an applicant may alternatively obtain the certification from “the interstate water pollution control agency having jurisdiction over the navigable waters” where the discharge will originate. Id. 7 33 U.S.C. § 1341(d). 8 Id. 9 State Certification of Activities Requiring a Federal License or Permit, 36 Fed. Reg. 8563 (May 8, 1971) [hereinafter 1971 Rule]; Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2023. 10 Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2023. 11 1971 Rule, supra note 9, at 8563; Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2023. 12 PUD No. 1 of Jefferson Cnty. v. Wash. Dep’t of Ecology, 511 U.S. 700, 711–13 (1994). 13 Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2023; see also Clean Water Act Section 401 Clarification Rule, 85 Fed. Reg. 42210, 42234 (July 13, 2020) [hereinafter 2020 Rule]. 2 water-quality standards.14 But soon after, in 2023, EPA reinstated the broader 1971 interpretation.15 EPA now proposes to again narrow the scope of the state’s certification review under Section 401(d) to cover only discharges.16 EPA contends that the Supreme Court’s 2024 decision in Loper Bright v. Raimondo “presents the Agency with a compelling reason to update” its longstanding interpretation because, in EPA’s current view, that interpretation “does not reflect [the] best reading of the statute.”17 Moreover, EPA claims that Section 401’s legislative history and the Supreme Court’s 2022 decision in West Virginia v. EPA further support the narrower interpretation.18 As detailed below, in reaching this conclusion, EPA misapplies several Supreme Court precedents and mischaracterizes Section 401’s legislative history. I. PUD No. 1 held that the best reading of Section 401 is that the state’s certification review includes the entire activity’s compliance with applicable water-quality standards, and did not rely on Chevron deference. In the Proposed Rule, EPA states that the Supreme Court in PUD No. 1 “ultimately deferred to EPA’s 1971 Rule, affording it Chevron deference.”19 But EPA has PUD No. 1 backwards: In deciding a dispute between a utility district and the State of Washington, the Court held that the best reading of Section 401(d) is that state certification reviews include the entire activity’s compliance with applicable water-quality standards. While the Court briefly mentioned Chevron in highlighting that its best reading of the statute matches EPA’s 1971 interpretation, it did not apply Chevron to reach the holding. In PUD No. 1, a utility district challenged Washington’s conditioning of a permit for a hydroelectric facility “on the maintenance of specific minimum stream flows” to protect nearby fish populations.20 The petitioners argued that Section 401(d) authorizes states to impose water- quality limitations only on discharges, rather than on the entire activity subject to the permitting 14 2020 Rule, supra note 13, at 42250 (“The scope of a Clean Water Act section 401 certification is limited to assuring that a discharge from a Federally licensed or permitted activity will comply with water quality requirements.”) (emphasis in original); see also Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2023. 15 Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule, 88 Fed. Reg. 66558, 66592 (Sept. 27, 2023) (“Having now carefully reconsidered the 2020 Rule’s ‘discharge-only’ interpretation of scope of review, EPA has concluded that the best reading of the statutory text is that the scope of certification is the activity subject to the Federal license or permit, not merely its potential point source discharges.”); see also Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2023. 16 Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2023 (“The proposed rule would narrow the current regulation’s broad ‘activity’- based scope of certifying authority review to what Congress clearly intended: an assessment of whether a facility’s point source discharges into waters of the United States will comply with specified water quality requirements.”) (emphasis in original). 17 Id. 18 Id. at 2026. 19 Id. at 2025. 20 PUD No. 1, 511 U.S. at 703. 3 or licensing requirement21—the same interpretation that EPA now proposes to adopt.22 The Court rejected this argument and upheld Washington’s decision to condition the permit on the maintenance of minimum stream flows.23 A seven-justice majority of the Court held that, under Section 401(d), state certification reviews cover the entire activity’s compliance with applicable water-quality standards.24 That holding turned on the Supreme Court’s own reading of the statute—not on its deference to EPA’s interpretation. Specifically, the Court emphasized that Section 401(d) “refers to the compliance of the applicant, not the discharge”: “Section 401(d) provides that any certification shall set forth ‘any effluent limitations and other limitations . . . necessary to assure that any applicant’ will comply with various provisions of the Act and appropriate state law requirements.”25 The Court then offered its best reading of Section 401: “Section 401(a)(1) identifies the category of activities subject to certification—namely, those with discharges. And § 401(d) is most reasonably read as authorizing additional conditions and limitations on the activity as a whole once the threshold condition, the existence of a discharge, is satisfied.”26 In the Proposed Rule, EPA states that the Court in PUD No. 1 “reached its holding at Chevron ‘step two,’ finding the statutory text to be ambiguous and EPA’s interpretation embodied in the 1971 Rule to be a ‘reasonable’ interpretation.”27 But the Court did no such two-step analysis, nor did the Court’s discussion of the best reading of Section 401 imply that the Court found the provision ambiguous at step one.28 The Court’s discussion of EPA’s 1971 rule, which included a single citation to Chevron, merely pointed out that the Court’s independent view of the statute’s best reading matched EPA’s 1971 interpretation.29 This brief reference to EPA’s rule is broadly consistent with Loper Bright’s guidance that agency “interpretations issued contemporaneously with the statute at issue, and which have remained consistent over time, may be especially useful in determining the statute’s meaning.”30 Because PUD No. 1 established the Court’s best reading of the statute and did not depend on Chevron, it binds EPA following Loper Bright. And PUD No. 1 held that state certification reviews under Section 401 cover the entire activity’s, rather than only the discharge’s, 21 Id. at 711. 22 Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2008. 23 PUD No. 1, 511 U.S. at 723. 24 Id. at 711–12. 25 Id. at 711 (quoting 33 U.S.C. § 1341(d)). 26 Id. at 711–12. 27 Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2025. 28 See PUD No. 1, 511 U.S. at 711–12. 29 See id. at 712 (“Our view of the statute is consistent with EPA’s regulations implementing § 401. The regulations expressly interpret § 401 as requiring the State to find that ‘there is a reasonable assurance that the activity will be conducted in a manner which will not violate applicable water quality standards.’ EPA’s conclusion that activities— not merely discharges—must comply with state water quality standards is a reasonable interpretation of § 401, and is entitled to deference.”) (internal citations omitted) (emphasis in original). 30 Loper Bright Enters. v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. 369, 394 (2024). 4 compliance with applicable water-quality standards.31 EPA cannot lawfully ignore PUD No. 1 to adopt a narrower interpretation of Section 401. II. EPA mischaracterizes Section 401’s legislative history. EPA asserts that the 1972 Clean Water Act amendments modified the scope of the state’s certification review.32 As EPA explains, the 1970 statute required the state to certify that the permitted or licensed activity “will be conducted in a manner which will not violate applicable water quality standards.”33 EPA then claims: “Significantly, Congress modified this language in 1972, requiring a certifying authority to certify ‘that any such discharge shall comply with the applicable provisions of [the CWA].’”34 EPA contends that this “change from ‘activity’ to ‘discharge’” evinces “that Congress intended the scope of certification to change from the entire ‘activity’ subject to the Federal license or permit to the ‘discharges’ of that activity.”35 But EPA cites only Section 401(a)(1), and ignores the language in Section 401(d) that the Supreme Court found dispositive.36 Section 401(d) maintained the 1970 statute’s focus on whether the permitted or licensed activity, as opposed to the discharge alone, complies with the specified water-quality standards.37 And in any case, EPA’s limited analysis of legislative history in the Proposed Rule does not circumvent the Supreme Court’s precedential holding. III. Even assuming that PUD No. 1 relied on Chevron and that EPA’s new reading of the statute is the best reading, PUD No. 1 remains good law and EPA must comply with the Administration Procedure Act’s reasoned decisionmaking requirements. Even assuming that PUD No. 1 relied on Chevron deference, which is not the case, PUD No. 1 remains good law. Although Loper Bright overturned Chevron v. Natural Resources Defense Council, the Supreme Court clarified that Loper Bright does not “call into question prior cases 31 PUD No. 1, 511 U.S. at 711–12, 723. 32 Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2024. 33 Water Quality Improvement Act of 1970, Pub. L. No. 91-224, sec. 102, § 21(b)(1) (1970) (“Any applicant for a Federal license or permit to conduct any activity including, but not limited to, the construction or operation of facilities, which may result in any discharge into the navigable waters of the United States, shall provide the licensing or permitting agency a certification from the State in which the discharge originates or will originate, or, if appropriate, from the interstate water pollution control agency having jurisdiction over the navigable waters at the point where the discharge originates or will originate, that there is reasonable assurance, as determined by the State or interstate agency that such activity will be conducted in a manner which will not violate applicable water quality standards.”); see also Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2024. 34 Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2024. 35 Id. 36 Id. 37 33 U.S.C. § 1341(d) (“Any certification provided under this section shall set forth any effluent limitations and other limitations, and monitoring requirements necessary to assure that any applicant for a Federal license or permit will comply with any applicable effluent limitations and other limitations, under [Clean Water Act Sections 301 and 302], standard of performance under [Clean Water Act Section 306], or prohibition, effluent standard, or pretreatment standard under [Clean Water Act Section 307], and with any other appropriate requirement of State law set forth in such certification. . . .”) (emphasis added). 5 that relied on the Chevron framework.”38 Instead, the Court made clear that those cases “are still subject to statutory stare decisis despite [the Court’s] change in interpretive methodology.”39 As a result, rules previously upheld under Chevron remain good law and agencies may lawfully maintain them even if they do not reflect the best reading of the statute; Loper Bright does not compel agencies to rescind such rules.40 Given this statutory stare decisis, an agency that finds that a regulation previously upheld under Chevron is inconsistent with the best reading of the relevant statute can choose between two lawful alternatives: to maintain the current regulation or to seek to modify or rescind it.41 The agency must therefore follow all of the Administrative Procedure Act’s usual requirements for reasoned decisionmaking,42 including considering the adverse impacts of the proposed change,43 evaluating the “facts and circumstances that underlay or were engendered by the prior policy,”44 and weighing “serious reliance interests” in the policy being rescinded.45 Even if PUD No. 1 relied on Chevron and if EPA’s new reading of the statute were the best reading, Loper Bright does not compel EPA to rescind the 2023 rule. EPA therefore still needs to satisfy the Administration Procedure Act’s reasoned decisionmaking requirements to rescind the 2023 rule. To the extent that EPA does not comply with these requirements, Loper Bright does not excuse its failure to comply. But, as discussed above, instead of relying on Chevron, PUD No. 1 held that the best reading of Section 401 is that state certification reviews include the entire activity’s compliance with applicable water-quality standards.46 IV. The major questions doctrine does not preclude EPA’s longstanding interpretation that state certification reviews under Section 401 cover the entire activity’s compliance with applicable water-quality standards. To support its decision to narrow state certification reviews under Section 401(d), EPA asserts that the 2023 rule violates the major questions doctrine.47 This argument fails for at least two 38 Loper Bright, 603 U.S. at 412. 39 Id. 40 Jack Jones & Max Sarinsky, What Loper Bright and Statutory Stare Decisis Mean for Deregulation, YALE J. ON REGUL. NOTICE & COMMENT BLOG (June 30, 2025), https://perma.cc/9DJW-L5M7. The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services recently acknowledged that the holdings of cases that relied on Chevron remain good law and that Loper Bright “does not change the[ir] result” that the regulation is lawful under the statute. Medicare Program: Hospital Outpatient Prospective Payment and Ambulatory Surgical Center Payment Systems; Quality Reporting Programs; Overall Hospital Quality Star Rating; Hospital Price Transparency; and Notice of Closure of a Teaching Hospital and Opportunity To Apply for Available Slots, 90 Fed. Reg. 53448, 53881 (Nov. 25, 2025). 41 JACK JONES & MAX SARINSKY, INST. FOR POL’Y INTEGRITY, “NONDISCRETIONARY” REPEALS: HOW THE TRUMP ADMINISTRATION’S UNTESTED DEREGULATORY THEORY VIOLATES THE ADMINISTRATIVE PROCEDURE ACT 7–8 (2025), https://policyintegrity.org/publications/detail/nondiscretionary-repeals. 42 Id. 43 See Michigan v. Env’t Prot. Agency, 576 U.S. 743, 752 (2015) (recognizing that agencies must normally consider costs when promulgating a rule, including “harms that regulation might do to human health or the environment”). 44 Fed. Commc’ns. Comm’n v. Fox Television Stations, Inc., 556 U.S. 502, 516 (2009). 45 Food & Drug Admin. v. Wages & White Lion Invs., L.L.C., 604 U.S. 542, 570 (2025). 46 See supra Part I. 47 Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2026. 6 reasons. First, EPA’s 2023 rule was neither unheralded nor transformative—and, even if it were, the statute provides the requisite clear authorization. Second, the emergence of the major questions doctrine did not undermine the stare decisis effect of PUD No. 1. Under the major questions doctrine, an agency needs “clear congressional authorization” to act when “the ‘history and the breadth of the authority that [the agency] has asserted,’ and the ‘economic and political significance’ of that assertion, provide a ‘reason to hesitate before concluding that Congress’ meant to confer such authority.”48 In particular, the Court in West Virginia v. EPA focused on whether EPA had “‘claim[ed] to discover in a long-extant statute an unheralded power’ representing a ‘transformative expansion in [its] regulatory authority.’”49 Biden v. Nebraska further emphasized the importance of “the ‘history and the breadth of the authority that [the agency] ha[d] asserted,’” alongside the action’s economic and political significance.50 But in the Proposed Rule, EPA focuses exclusively on the 2023 rule’s economic and political significance and ignores the relevant regulatory history and the nature of the authority that EPA asserted in the 2023 rule.51 Even by the Proposed Rule’s own account, the 2023 rule was neither unheralded nor transformative. It simply restored EPA’s 1971 interpretation of the scope of state certification reviews after the 2020 rule’s brief reversal.52 In any event, that the PUD No. 1 holding turned on the best reading of Section 401 implies that Section 401 provides the requisite “clear congressional authorization.”53 In addition, the Supreme Court has recognized that, even after a “change in interpretive methodology,”54 “stare decisis carries enhanced force” in statutory interpretation.55 Although the major questions doctrine may have modified the Court’s approach to statutory interpretation in specific contexts, PUD No. 1 remains binding on EPA. State certification reviews under Section 401(d) of the Clean Water Act thus cover the entire permitted or licensed activity’s compliance with applicable water-quality standards.56 V. EPA should disclose whether it used artificial intelligence tools to develop the Proposed Rule, and provide relevant information about its use of any such tools. Artificial intelligence (A.I.) has the potential to bring greater efficiency to the complex world of federal rulemaking, but it also raises concerns about transparency and integrity in agencies’ 48 West Virginia v. Env’t Prot. Agency, 597 U.S. 697, 721–23 (2022) (quoting Food & Drug Admin. v. Brown & Williamson Tobacco Corp., 529 U.S. 120, 159–60 (2000) & Utility Air Reg. Grp. v. Env’t Prot. Agency, 573 U.S. 302, 324 (2014)). 49 See id. at 724 (quoting Utility Air, 573 U.S. at 324). 50 Biden v. Nebraska, 600 U.S. 477, 501 (2023) (quoting West Virginia, 597 U.S. at 721). 51 See Proposed Rule, supra note 4, at 2026. 52 See id. at 2023. 53 See supra Part I; see also West Virginia, 597 U.S. at 723 (quoting Utility Air, 573 U.S. at 324). 54 See Loper Bright, 603 U.S. at 412. 55 Kimble v. Marvel Ent., LLC, 576 U.S. 446, 456 (2015); see also Loper Bright, 603 U.S. at 412. 56 PUD No. 1, 511 U.S. at 711–12, 723. 7 decisionmaking processes. To address these concerns and to develop best practices for using A.I. in federal rulemaking, agencies should make efforts to inform the public about what role A.I. plays in their decisions. By disclosing when and how they use A.I. in the regulatory process, agencies can offer an opportunity for public input on the design and application of A.I. tools that will lead to better-informed and more transparent decisions by government, and facilitate knowledge-sharing that can inform future rulemakings across agencies. In accordance with Executive Order 13,960, issued during President Trump’s first term, federal agencies should seek to “foster public trust and confidence” in their use of A.I. tools.57 To achieve this goal, agencies must ensure that the “operations and outcomes” of their use of A.I. are “sufficiently understandable by subject matter experts, users, and others.”58 Agencies should ensure that A.I. use is “well documented and traceable,” including the “design, development, acquisition, and use of AI, as well as relevant inputs and outputs of particular AI applications.”59 And agencies must be “transparent in disclosing relevant information regarding their use of AI to appropriate stakeholders, including the Congress and the public.”60 Per the Office of Management and Budget (OMB), agencies must “implement minimum risk management practices” for any A.I. use that could have “significant impacts when deployed,” and must further “prioritize the use of AI that is safe, secure, and resilient.”61 Where “proper risk mitigation is not possible, agencies must cease the use of the AI.”62 And OMB directs agencies to “provide an option for end users and the public to submit feedback on the use case” for A.I. tools, including the “design, development, and use of the AI,” and to “use such feedback to inform agency decision-making regarding the AI.”63 Accordingly, EPA should disclose whether it used A.I. tools in the development of the Proposed Rule. If EPA did use A.I. tools, it should identify: • (1) what task(s) the agency used A.I. tool(s) for (e.g., conducting research, reviewing public comments, or assisting with regulatory design); • (2) the A.I. domain(s) used (e.g., machine learning, reinforcement learning, transfer learning, generative A.I.); • (3) the entity responsible for the tool(s) (e.g., developer, provider, or vendor), and the exact model and its corresponding version; 57 Exec. Order No. 13,960, 85 Fed. Reg. 78939, 78940 (Dec. 8, 2020). 58 Id. 59 Id. 60 Id. 61 Off. of Mgmt. & Budget, Memorandum M-25-21: Accelerating Federal Use of AI through Innovation, Governance, and Public Trust 3 (Apr. 3, 2025), https://perma.cc/M69E-SCXR. 62 Id. 63 Id. at 17. 8 • (4) the steps taken to ensure that data inputs on which the A.I. tool(s) relied were accurate and traceable, including the results of any A.I. impact or risk assessments; • (5) the steps taken to review the output of the A.I. tool(s) for hallucination, bias, or otherwise inaccurate or misleading representations; • (6) the specific prompts, system instructions, and/or guardrails provided to the A.I. tool(s); and • (7) if A.I. was used for a “high-impact” use,64 how the agency implemented each of the minimum risk management practices for “high-impact” A.I. use required by OMB.65 Sincerely, Max Sarinsky, Legal Director Erin Shortell, Legal Fellow erin.shortell@nyu.edu 64 See id. at 3. 65 Id. at 15–17. 9

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the proposed EPA rule on Water Quality Certification Regulations and responds to the agency’s stated legal justifications, including PUD No. 1, legislative history, Loper Bright, and the major questions doctrine.
rationales.reasoning: The arguments are logically organized and consistently developed, with clear rebuttals to EPA’s positions and careful distinctions between statutory interpretation, precedent, and administrative law requirements.
rationales.evidence: The letter is heavily supported by statutory citations, Federal Register references, Supreme Court cases, and specific quotations, giving it strong legal and factual grounding.
rationales.impact: It explains the likely legal and regulatory consequences of EPA’s proposal, but some impact claims are more doctrinal than empirical and do not quantify real-world effects on water quality or permitting outcomes.
rationales.actionability: The letter makes clear requests, especially that EPA disclose AI use and provide specific information if AI was used, though its main legal recommendation is more of a challenge to the proposal than an operational alternative.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is well structured with a clear introduction, background, numbered sections, and bullet points, and it uses professional formatting and citation style throughout.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, polished legal comment letter with excellent relevance, rigorous legal reasoning, and substantial citation support. Its main limitation is that it is more persuasive and doctrinal than empirically detailed, but overall it is high quality.

overall_summary: A highly relevant and professionally written comment letter that directly engages the proposed rule’s legal foundations. It is especially strong on legal reasoning and citation support, with clear structure and concrete requests, though its impact analysis is somewhat less empirical than its doctrinal analysis.

comment_text:
Good evening,
We need not only transparency but we also need an investigation on why is american freight being brokers by foreign entities. I contacted a broker and they were over seas, how does this happen. I've been trucking since 1992 and there has been more and more foreign brokers and american companies can't compete with there low rates. Yes we need transparency but we also need american brokers.

Thank you for letting me vent. Hope we can get the trucking industry back to an american standard.

rationales.relevance: The comment is generally on-topic for trucking/brokerage transparency and foreign involvement, but it does not clearly tie to a specific docket issue or request beyond broad concerns.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is understandable but loosely organized and relies on assertions rather than a clear chain of reasoning. It does not explain how foreign brokers affect the market beyond stating that they offer low rates.
rationales.evidence: No concrete evidence, data, citations, or specific examples are provided beyond one personal anecdote about contacting an overseas broker.
rationales.impact: The comment claims harm to American companies and the trucking industry, but the impacts are stated in general terms without specifics, magnitude, or substantiation.
rationales.actionability: It asks for transparency and an investigation, which are identifiable requests, but they are broad and not framed as specific, feasible policy actions.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is readable and polite, but informal, repetitive, and not well structured. It reads more like a vent than a formal public comment.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant but weakly developed comment: it expresses a clear concern about foreign brokerage and transparency, yet provides little evidence, limited reasoning, and only broad policy asks.

overall_summary: The comment is relevant to trucking brokerage transparency and foreign participation, but it is informal and largely unsupported. It raises a clear concern and requests an investigation, yet lacks evidence, detailed reasoning, and specific policy recommendations.

comment_text:
Brokers and major trucking companies that rely on smaller carriers to move freight should offer fair pricing and implement reasonable in-house policies. For instance, imposing fines ranging from $150 to $500 for not using tracking apps is unacceptable. There is no federal requirement from the DOT mandating live tracking of drivers or their personal cell phones. Instead, companies should provide their own tracking devices for the freight.

Additionally, brokers should take a fair and reasonable percentage from the freight charges. For example, if a load pays $5,000, brokers should retain only 10-15% rather than the excessive 40-50% they currently take. The economic landscape has changed significantly since COVID-19, yet freight rates have remained stagnant. Meanwhile, operating costs, such as mechanic service rates, have increased, but drivers continue to receive the same per-mile pay.

rationales.relevance: The comment is clearly about trucking industry practices, broker fees, tracking requirements, and carrier compensation, which are likely relevant to a freight or trucking-related docket. It directly addresses requested issues around fair pricing and operational policies.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent: it links tracking fines and broker margins to fairness concerns and then connects stagnant freight rates with rising operating costs. However, some claims are asserted broadly without much development or nuance.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes a few concrete figures, such as $150-$500 fines, a $5,000 load example, and a suggested 10-15% broker margin. But it provides no citations, data sources, or documented examples to support the claims about current industry practices or federal requirements.
rationales.impact: The potential impacts are plausible, especially regarding driver costs, broker compensation, and the burden of tracking policies. Still, the comment does not quantify how these practices affect safety, competition, or carrier viability beyond general assertions.
rationales.actionability: It offers clear, feasible requests: prohibit or limit fines for app use, require brokers to charge a fair percentage, and have companies provide their own tracking devices. These are understandable policy suggestions, though they are not framed as formal regulatory language.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is concise, organized into two main points, and easy to read. It uses examples effectively and maintains a professional tone, though it is brief and somewhat repetitive.
rationales.overall: This is a focused and understandable public comment that raises concrete concerns about broker pricing and tracking requirements in trucking. It is strongest on relevance and actionability, but weaker on evidence and detailed support.

overall_summary: A relevant and reasonably well-structured comment that clearly identifies trucking industry concerns and proposes practical changes, but it would be stronger with supporting evidence, citations, and more detailed justification for its claims.

comment_text: Andy Beshear ENERGY AND ENVIRONMENT CABINET Rebecca Goodman GOVERNOR SECRETARY DEPARTMENT FOR ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION 300 Sower Boulevard Anthony R. Hatton Frankfort, Kentucky 40601 COMMISSIONER Phone: (502) 564-2150 Fax: 502-564-4245 February 05, 2026 Submitted via Regulations.gov Lee Zeldin, Administrator U.S. Environmental Protection Agency EPA Docket Center EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929; FRL-6976.2-01-OW, Mail Code 28221T 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue, NW Washington, DC 20460 Re: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s (“EPA”) Proposed Rule – “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations”; 91 Fed. Reg. 2,008 (January 15, 2026) – Docket EPA ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929; FRL-6976.2-01-OW Dear Administrator Zeldin: The Kentucky Division of Water (Division) respectfully submits the attached comments on the EPA’s proposed rulemaking for the “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations” published on January 15, 2026 (91 Fed. Reg. 2,008). The Division requests an extension of the comment period to at least 60 days. This proposal interplays with EPA’s recent waters of the United States (WOTUS) proposed rulemaking and provides for numerous procedural changes. The Division seeks additional time for full consideration and review. In the event this extension request is not granted, the Division submits the attached comments for consideration. Thank you for the opportunity to provide comment, for further clarification, please feel free to contact me at Sarah.Marshall@ky.gov or 502-782-3279, Sincerely, Sarah Marshall, Director Kentucky Division of Water Comments on U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s (“EPA”) Proposed Rule – “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations”; 91 Fed. Reg. 2,008 (January 15, 2026) Cooperative Federalism and WOTUS The recent waters of the United States (WOTUS) proposed rulemaking1 promotes cooperative federalism by recognizing and preserving the primary responsibilities and rights of States to prevent pollution within their borders and to plan the development and use of their land and water resources. See 33 U.S.C. 1251(b). As stated in the WOTUS comments submitted by the Commonwealth of Kentucky (Commonwealth) on December 30, 20252, the Commonwealth supports continued cooperative federalism as contemplated by the Clean Water Act (CWA), and agrees that the States are in the best position to implement responsibilities over their own waters, and to carry out Congress' overall objectives to restore and maintain the integrity of the Nation's waters in a manner that preserves the traditional sovereignty of States over their own land and water resources.3 However, the newly proposed “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations” rulemaking contradicts this premise as it significantly limits state authority to protect, maintain, and restore waters within the Commonwealth. The proposed rule, by its change to 40 C.F.R. 121.1(c), would narrow the current regulation’s broad “activity”-based scope of certifying authority review to an assessment of whether a facility’s point source discharges into waters of the United States (WOTUS) will comply with specified water quality requirements.4 Accordingly, under the EPA’s proposal, certifying authorities cannot consider water quality impacts to waters beyond WOTUS, or impacts from outside the discharge itself. This would be a departure from the current regulations, which allow for consideration of State waters that are not WOTUS in certain circumstances. Specifically, under the current regulations, certifying authorities may consider waters beyond WOTUS when certifying compliance with requirements of State law that otherwise apply to waters of the State beyond WOTUS. (88 Fed. Reg. 66,604). This interplay would invoke further issues to the Commonwealth as outlined in the Commonwealth’s recent comment letter on WOTUS. Furthermore, without a final WOTUS rulemaking, the Commonwealth cannot fully evaluate the impacts this secondary rule has on its waters, regulated entities, and the public. “Water Quality Requirements” EPA is requesting comment on whether it should limit “water quality requirements” to only numeric water quality criteria, excluding narrative water quality criteria. “Water quality requirements” should not be limited to only numeric water quality criteria. The CWA delegates to States the authority to develop and maintain a comprehensive Water Pollution Control Program with the 1 See “Updated Definition of `Waters of the United States' ” 90 Fed. Reg. 52,498 (November 20, 2025). 2 https://www.regulations.gov/comment/EPA-HQ-OW-2025-0322-0340. 3 Currently, the Commonwealth’s statutory definition of “Water” or “Waters of the Commonwealth,” found in KRS 224.1-010(32), includes all waters designated as navigable waters, as defined in 33 U.S.C. sec. 1362, and other waters with special characteristics that are meant to protect groundwater. The Commonwealth’s definition of its state waters is tied directly to the federal definition of WOTUS. 4 Section 401 certification is required for Federal licenses or permits that authorize any activity which may result in any discharge from a point source into waters of the United States. EPA and the Corps recently published a proposed rule that would define the scope of “waters of the United States.” See “Updated Definition of `Waters of the United States' ” 90 Fed. Reg. 52,498 (November 20, 2025). Any changes in which waters qualify as waters of the United States will impact the waters in which federally licensed or permitted activities must seek section 401 certification. 2 express intent of abating and controlling existing and future sources of pollution to protect the physical, chemical, and biological integrity of waters. Central to this framework, both numeric and narrative water quality criteria collectively establish the minimum thresholds necessary for designated uses to be fully supported. Limiting “Water Quality Requirements” solely to numeric criteria would significantly constrain the Commonwealth’s regulatory capacity and undermine its ability to ensure that waters continue to support essential functions, including the propagation of fish, shellfish, and wildlife, as well as recreational uses. Content of a Request for Certification – 40 CFR§ 121.5 The EPA’s proposed replacement of the term “project proponent” with “applicant”, when combined with the revised discharge only scope, raises significant concern that determinations regarding whether 401 water quality certifications apply will increasingly be made by federal permitting agencies rather than the certifying authorities. This shift risks creating procedural loopholes for general permits5 and other federal actions, particularly where no clearly identifiable applicant exists. If the federal agency is permitted to make categorical or project-level determinations that no point source discharge is present, and consequently no certification request is required, States could lose the opportunity to issue 401 water quality certifications for Nationwide Permits or other general permits further undermining cooperative federalism. Scope of Certification - 40 CFR § 121.3 The proposed requirement that a certification request include “[a]ny readily available water quality-related materials... that informed the development of the application or draft license or permit” is ambiguous, unenforceable, and problematic for certifying authorities. This language allows applicants to exclude information necessary for the certifying authority to evaluate compliance with water quality standards simply by asserting that the information was not considered, not in their possession, or not considered during project development. This shifts control of the completeness of certification requests from certifying authorities to applicants, and when combined with strict review timelines, risks forcing States to act on incomplete information or deny 401 water quality certification requests. The EPA should revise this provision to require submission of all information necessary for the certifying authority to evaluate compliance, rather than limiting required materials to those selectively provided by the applicant. Withdrawal and Resubmittal - 40 CFR § 121.6 The proposed rule removes the ability of certifying authorities, such as the Commonwealth, to administratively withdraw or reject incomplete certification requests once the minimum federal requirements for a request are met and provides no mechanism for State-initiated withdrawal procedures. This change would override long-standing and effective state practices used to ensure certification decisions are based on sufficient information. In the Commonwealth, withdrawal has been used only when an application was incomplete and the requested 5 The CWA, 33 U.S.C. Sec. 1341, supports extending the CWA section 401 water quality certification requirement to general permits, even in the absence of an 'applicant.' This is a long-established interpretation by EPA, and the proposed rulemaking does not support a clear justification to depart from this interpretation. 3 information was not provided by the applicant, allowing deficiencies to be resolved without denial. Removing this authority deprives States of a critical tool for managing incomplete applications. The proposal expressly allows applicants to withdraw and later resubmit certification requests, thereby codifying applicant-driven reset mechanisms, while denying States’ comparable tools to manage incomplete applications. This encourages strategic withdrawals by applicants to avoid denial, while forcing States to proceed to denial when information deficiencies remain unresolved. 4

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the proposed water quality certification regulations and repeatedly ties concerns to specific sections of the rule and the docket topic.
rationales.reasoning: The arguments are generally coherent and logically developed, with clear links between the proposed changes and the claimed effects on state authority and implementation.
rationales.evidence: The letter cites the Federal Register, Clean Water Act provisions, CFR sections, and state statutory references, but it relies more on legal assertions than on empirical data or concrete case examples.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible impacts such as reduced state authority, procedural loopholes, and pressure to act on incomplete applications, though the magnitude of those impacts is not quantified.
rationales.actionability: The comment makes clear requests, including extending the comment period and revising the definition of water quality requirements and the information-submission provisions.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is professionally formatted, organized by topic headings, and easy to follow, though it includes some dense legal discussion and footnote clutter.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, policy-relevant comment letter with clear legal and procedural objections, solid organization, and actionable requests, though it would be stronger with more empirical support.

overall_summary: A well-targeted and professionally written comment that directly engages the proposed rule’s legal and procedural changes. It is strongest on relevance, organization, and actionable recommendations, while its main weakness is limited factual or empirical evidence beyond legal citations.

comment_text:
I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive and focuses on the requested issues of safety, transparency, and labeling.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with clear links between synthetic biology production, consumer perception, and safety concerns, though some claims are asserted more strongly than they are demonstrated.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes a few concrete technical references, such as specific genes and the yeast host, but it provides little empirical data, citations, or documented examples to support the safety and allergenicity claims.
rationales.impact: The potential impacts are plausible, especially around consumer deception and allergen exposure, but the magnitude and likelihood of harm are not well quantified or substantiated.
rationales.actionability: It offers clear requests, including proteomic analysis, labeling restrictions, and allergen warnings, which are feasible in principle, though some are stated broadly rather than as precise regulatory proposals.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is well organized with numbered sections, clear headings, and a professional tone, making it easy to follow.
rationales.overall: This is a focused and readable comment with clear policy asks, but it relies more on concern and inference than on strong evidence, which limits its persuasive strength.

overall_summary: A relevant and well-structured comment that clearly opposes the current labeling approach and asks for stricter safety and disclosure requirements. Its main weakness is the limited use of supporting evidence, which makes the more serious safety and allergenicity claims less convincing.

comment_text:
The FMCSA attempt to require market participants to reveal private business information is a broad overreach and a misinterpretation of the breadth of FMCSA’s authority.

Some of the statutes that FMCSA cites as the basis for its authority likely do not authorize required disclosure to private third parties. And while there may be a broad grant of authority, the ICC and FMCSA have not regulated broker charges and have repeatedly recognized that brokers benefit both shippers and carriers. 

Additionally, Congress deregulated the trucking industry with an intent to rely on market forces, which makes it peculiar for FMCSA to now find it has authority to interfere with a broker’s commercial relations

rationales.relevance: The comment clearly addresses FMCSA authority and broker disclosure requirements, which appears directly related to the likely docket topic. It engages the core legal question, though it does not reference specific requested issues in detail.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent: it claims FMCSA lacks authority, cites deregulation, and notes prior agency practice. However, the logic is asserted more than developed, with limited explanation of how the cited statutes fail to support the rule.
rationales.evidence: The comment mentions statutes, the ICC, FMCSA, and congressional deregulation, but provides no specific citations, quotations, data, or concrete examples. The claims are largely conclusory.
rationales.impact: It identifies a plausible impact—interference with private business information and commercial relations—but does not quantify or concretely describe the practical consequences for market participants.
rationales.actionability: The comment mainly objects to the proposal and argues against agency authority, but it does not offer a specific alternative approach or clear requested revision beyond rejecting the rule.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is concise and readable, with a clear central thesis. Still, it is very brief and lacks paragraph structure, headings, or a more polished comment-letter format.
rationales.overall: This is a focused legal objection to FMCSA’s authority, but it is brief and largely unsupported. It is relevant and understandable, yet it would be stronger with specific citations, examples, and a more concrete request.

overall_summary: A concise, relevant comment that raises a clear legal challenge to FMCSA authority, but it relies mostly on broad assertions rather than detailed evidence or specific recommendations.

comment_text: August 6, 2025 Pe y Browne Acting Assistant Administrator Ofﬁce of Water U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 Subject: Comments on Section 401 State Water Quality Certiﬁcation Open Docket; Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-0272 Dear Ms. Browne, The undersigned organizations write to express strong support for continued improvements to the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 certiﬁcation process— ensuring that the possible changes to the regulation provide clarity, certainty, and regulatory durability. Permitting reform is essential to advancing national economic growth and modernizing critical infrastructure, while maintaining the environmental protections that are central to EPA’s mission. Efﬁcient, transparent permitting processes help accelerate the deployment of energy, transportation, and water infrastructure— driving job creation and resilience in communities across our nation. Clean Water Act Section 401 state certiﬁcations play a foundational role in this framework, ensuring that states and tribes have a meaningful voice in reviewing that federally permitted projects comply with water quality standards. Strengthening coordination and certainty around Section 401 certiﬁcations can enhance both environmental outcomes and permitting efﬁciency, supporting EPA’s goals of protecting water resources, while enabling timely infrastructure investment. Predictable permitting timelines are vital to investment and efﬁcient use of public funds. Permitting delays can cascade across project planning, and investment. Any uncertainty adds complexity—not just cost, but unpredictability that discourages bids, invites litigation, and limits the pool of infrastructure partners. These issues present real economic risks, demonstrating how regulatory predictability can unlock greater investment in critical sectors like broadband, clean water, data centers, energy, and transportation. We support the strict adherence to the statutory one-year decision requirement for certiﬁcations and urge EPA to restore safeguards from the 2020 rule that would prevent states from creating loopholes to extend this deadline. 1 A clear and enforceable one-year deadline for state action on requests for Section 401 certiﬁcations is crucial to providing the clarity developers and other stakeholders require to make informed investments in infrastructure projects needed to unleash American energy and promote American innovation. For instance, the recent experience of Rover Pipeline, LLC (“Rover”) is instructive. We understand the following: In November 2015, Rover submitted a request to Ohio for a Section 401 certiﬁcation in connection with its proposed interstate natural gas pipeline from Michigan to West Virginia. Ohio did not act on Rover’s request within the one-year timeline set by Section 401(a). Instead, Ohio sued Rover in state court in 2017, alleging violations of state water-quality requirements that Ohio could have imposed, but did not impose, through a timely Section 401 certiﬁcation. In that litigation, Ohio argued that it had not waived its power to issue a Section 401 certiﬁcation, contending that a state’s one-year timeline only begins to run once the state deems the request to be “complete.” That theory was rejected by both the Ohio Supreme Court and EPA’s 2020 implementing rule for Section 401 (since replaced by the Biden Administration’s 2023 rule, 88 Fed. Reg. 66,558 (Sept. 27, 2023)). See State ex. rel. Yost v. Rover Pipeline, LLC, 167 Ohio St. 3d 223, 226 (Ohio), petition for cert. ﬁled, No. 24-1120 (Apr. 25, 2025); Clean Water Act Section 401 Certiﬁcation Rule, 85 Fed. Reg. 42,210, 42,223-24 (July 13, 2020). The very fact that the Ohio litigation is still pending—with the state having recently sought Supreme Court review in a case where it waived its Section 401 powers some nine years ago—only underscores the extent to which regulatory certainty and predictable permitting timelines are imperative. As a group of thirteen states recently explained to EPA in commenting on EPA’s 2022 proposed Section 401 rule (87 Fed. Reg. 35,318 (Jun. 9, 2022) (which in turn led to EPA’s 2023 ﬁnal Section 401 rule, 88 Fed. Reg. 66,558 (Sept. 27, 2023)), any lack of predictable permitting timelines under Section 401—including supposed uncertainty about whether the one-year clock begins to run when the application is received or only once it is deemed “complete”—would “dramatically hinder the permitting process,” create “arbitrary delays,” and “enable States to . . . frustrate infrastructure development” by using “bureaucratic games to effectively veto a project that has signiﬁcant economic effects across an entire region.” Comments of Louisiana et al. at 7-8, Docket No. EPA-HQ-OW-2022-0128-0123 (Aug. 8, 2022), https://perma.cc/N33H- S7CW. The undersigned organizations strongly agree. It is long past time for EPA to eliminate uncertainty in permitting processes and timelines under Section 401. 2 Section 401’s scope should remain focused on the “discharge” and water quality alone. As we stated in our comments on EPA’s 2022 proposed Section 401 rule, Section 401(a)(1) requires that states must certify that any “discharge will comply with the applicable provisions of Sections 301, 302, 303, 306, and 307 of this Act.” CWA Section 401(a)(1); see also 85 Fed. Reg. 42232 (July 13, 2020). This unambiguous focus is supported by the statutory text on a discharge to navigable waters and the dozens of pages of legal analysis that EPA presented in the 2020 Rule. By staying focused on point-source discharges and water quality impacts, EPA would reinforce administrative efﬁciency and clarity across federal, tribal, and state authorities, consistent with statutory intent. As EPA concluded in the 2020 Rule, in light of the text and structure of the Clean Water Act, as well as the history of modiﬁcations between the 1970 version of the Act and the 1972 amendments, Section 401 is best interpreted as protecting water quality from federally licensed or permitted activities that may result in point source discharges into waters of the United States. Limiting the scope to the discharge and its effect on water quality, rather than broader or indirect effects of the project as a whole, is legally appropriate and will make the permitting program more predictable for federal agencies, tribes, states, and project sponsors to navigate. Receiving a timely permitting decision, particularly for private investors, is important for predictability and cost effectiveness. Expanding engagement with neighboring jurisdictions adds unnecessary delays. We support the stronger and reinforced principles of cooperative federalism, giving states and tribes a stronger voice in protecting their water quality—even when the source of a discharge is across the border. However, any engagement should be time limited. As under the 2020 rule, once the EPA receives notiﬁcation from the federal licensing or permitting agency, it has 30 days to determine whether the discharge may affect the water quality of a neighboring jurisdiction. This 30-day window is critical—it sets the pace for whether a neighboring jurisdiction can weigh in before a federal license or permit is issued. The neighboring state or tribe then has 60 days to evaluate and object to the federal license or permit. Any broader timeframe would unnecessarily delay and potentially disrupt the statutory one-year deadline for ﬁnal action. Categorical exclusions can provide certainty for projects with limited impact. Consistent with exclusions established by federal agencies for various categories of activities demonstrated to have no more than minimal impact on the environment, EPA should authorize similar exclusions as part of the CWA Section 401 certiﬁcation 3 process, which can be implemented through categorical waivers of certiﬁcation, or categorical approvals. To support this concept, applicants should be encouraged to provide data justifying the following types of limited impact activities (not an exclusive list):  Routine maintenance activities (e.g., culvert cleaning, vegetation removal).  Minor infrastructure upgrades (like replacing existing utility lines).  Low-impact energy projects (such as small energy installations).  Certain types of dredging or ﬁll operations with well-understood impacts.  Projects with no point source discharge, such as water withdrawals, agriculture, or other land-based construction. Limits on modifying certiﬁcations enhance regulatory stability. Allowing modiﬁcations beyond the statutory period invites instability and undermines trust in the permitting process. We support restricting revisions outside the initial one-year statutory window. Outside of this review period, states should not be allowed to unilaterally impose new conditions without the prior approval of the applicant and federal agency. Project sponsors should be included in modiﬁcation discussions. We urge EPA to include project sponsors in modiﬁcation discussions in addition to the permitting agencies and certifying authorities.
