Businesses and other project developers should be able to advocate for more doable implementation and are better equipped to respond to additional requirements. Section 401 and permitting experiences. Here are a few representative examples of reports based on recent project engagement experiences, each stemming from a member or members of one or more of the undersigned associations:  EPA Overreach in Alternatives Analysis: In some cases, EPA’s comments go beyond their role as a commenting agency in the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers permitting process. For example, during a recent residential project review, EPA challenged the need for essential public services like a ﬁre station and a school—raising concerns about the scope of their alternatives analysis input.  Inconsistent U.S. Army Corps of Engineers Jurisdictional Determinations: There has been signiﬁcant disagreement between responsible agencies concerning jurisdictional determinations. On one project involving agricultural lands, the state agency determined that the site was not wetlands, but the Army Corps took a different view, despite use of federal regulatory deﬁnitions and guidance. This kind of inconsistency creates major uncertainty for developers 4 (often late in the project life) potentially increasing cost due to the additional mitigation required.  Lack of Responsiveness: Timely communication is a challenge. The member or members have experienced signiﬁcant delays in receiving responses from an area U.S. Army Corps of Engineers Ofﬁce, which slows down permitting timelines considerably. Such delays, combined with overreaching comments, create signiﬁcant impacts to schedules. A mandatory response time would be very helpful.  Improved Interactions with Out-of-State ACOE Ofﬁces: There are times when overﬂow work has been transferred to less-burdened Corps’ ofﬁces in other states, resulting in dramatic improvements in the member’s or members’ experiences. Some ofﬁces were responsive, professional, and easier to work with—demonstrating that effective permitting is achievable with the right team. Despite the positive experiences, the variations show the large inconsistences in how rules are applied based on local ofﬁce leadership and/or individual preferences of reviewers. Thank you for the opportunity to provide this feedback to the open docket. If you have any questions about these comments or if you would like to discuss further, please do not hesitate to contact us. We would welcome further engagement by the EPA concerning the proposed rule. Sincerely, American Exploration & Mining Association American Gas Association American Petroleum Institute American Road & Transportation Builders Association Associated Builders and Contractors The Associated General Contractors of America Independent Petroleum Association of America International Council of Shopping Centers Liquid Energy Pipeline Association National Asphalt Pavement Association National Association of Home Builders National Council of Farmer Cooperatives National Federation of Independent Business National Mining Association National Rural Electric Cooperative Association Treated Wood Council U.S. Chamber of Commerce 5

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses Section 401 certification, timing, scope, neighboring jurisdiction review, modifications, and categorical exclusions, all of which are highly relevant to the docket topic.
rationales.reasoning: The letter is generally coherent and organized around clear policy positions, with logical links between permitting certainty, economic effects, and regulatory design. Some arguments are advocacy-driven and occasionally repetitive, but the overall reasoning is understandable.
rationales.evidence: It includes a few concrete references, such as the Rover Pipeline example, citations to EPA rules and a state court case, and a quoted prior comment. However, much of the letter relies on assertions and generalized claims rather than data or detailed empirical support.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies plausible impacts like delays, litigation risk, higher costs, and reduced investment, and these effects are specific enough to be credible. Still, the magnitude of the impacts is not quantified and some claims remain broad.
rationales.actionability: The letter offers several clear requests, including enforcing the one-year deadline, limiting scope to discharge and water quality, time-limiting neighboring jurisdiction review, allowing categorical exclusions, restricting post-deadline modifications, and including project sponsors in modification discussions.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is professionally formatted with a formal salutation, topic-focused paragraphs, bullet points, and a closing signature block. It is readable and reasonably well organized, though it contains some typographical issues and dense sections.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, policy-relevant comment letter that clearly advocates for specific regulatory changes and supports them with some legal and practical examples. Its main weakness is limited empirical evidence and a largely industry-oriented framing, but it is still a solid public comment.

overall_summary: A well-targeted and professionally presented comment letter that strongly engages the docket issues and proposes concrete regulatory changes. It is persuasive and actionable, though it would be stronger with more data and less reliance on generalized economic and procedural claims.

comment_text: Freight Brokers should be fazed out period. They got to the point where they have weaponized the entire process against the carriers. They are major disruption to the process with very little regulations while carriers are over-regulated! They are holding the entire industry hostage and they are in bed with major players with in the industry including FMCSA. They all should be investigated. If allowed to stay and operate than broker should not take more than 3% of the load!!! It is unreasonable to dictate the price of load with simple subscription, computer and phone while carriers invest hundreds of thousands of dollars in equipment and their life. If this law is passed than it should include very concise statute stating that broker should provide and disclose full information of the full cost of the load RIGHT AWAY on the rate confirmation. Further more it should include very concise statute stating that the brokers  CAN NOT black list, refuse to work or retaliate if asked to provide full info on cost of the load. Further more FMCSA should stop allowing brokers to weaponize wed site called "Carrier411" where with a simple retaliatory comment they can put carrier out of business and destroy their life. This web site became weapon and blackmail for the trucking industry providing information often inaccurate, incomplete and unfair while disseminating information directly used from FMCSA.

rationales.relevance: The comment is directly about freight brokers, broker compensation, disclosure of load costs, retaliation, and Carrier411, which are plausibly related to trucking industry regulation and broker practices.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is understandable but highly emotional and often jumps from complaint to conclusion without clear logical support. Several claims are asserted broadly without explaining causation or distinguishing specific policy changes.
rationales.evidence: No data, citations, examples, or concrete incidents are provided. The comment relies almost entirely on unsupported assertions and general accusations.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies potential harms such as unfair pricing, retaliation, and reputational damage, but the magnitude and likelihood of these harms are not substantiated.
rationales.actionability: It does include some concrete requests, such as capping broker fees at 3%, requiring disclosure of full load costs on rate confirmations, and prohibiting retaliation. However, these requests are not fully developed or framed in a feasible regulatory way.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is readable but poorly organized, repetitive, and lacks paragraph structure. It would benefit from clearer separation of concerns and more professional tone.
rationales.overall: The comment is relevant and contains a few actionable requests, but it is weakened by unsupported allegations, weak reasoning, and minimal evidence. It reads more as a grievance than a well-supported public comment.

overall_summary: This is a relevant but largely unsupported and emotionally charged comment. It raises several concrete concerns about broker practices and transparency, but it would be much stronger with evidence, clearer logic, and more precise policy recommendations.

comment_text:
I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the approval and labeling of Phytolon’s beetroot red color additive, focusing on natural labeling, safety, and allergenicity concerns that are clearly tied to the docket topic.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with a clear progression from labeling concerns to safety and testing requests. Some claims are asserted more strongly than they are demonstrated, but the overall logic is understandable.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes a few concrete technical references, such as Saccharomyces cerevisiae and specific genes, but it provides little empirical evidence, no citations, and no data or studies to substantiate the safety or allergenicity claims.
rationales.impact: The potential impacts are plausible, especially around consumer transparency and possible allergen exposure, but the comment does not quantify risk or provide specific examples showing how likely or severe these impacts are.
rationales.actionability: It offers clear requests, including proteomic analysis, labeling restrictions, and allergen warnings. These are understandable and potentially feasible, though some are framed broadly and would benefit from more precise regulatory language.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is well organized with numbered sections, clear headings, and a concise conclusion. It reads professionally and is easy to follow, though some sections are repetitive.
rationales.overall: This is a focused and well-structured comment that is highly relevant and provides clear policy requests, but it is weakened by limited evidence and somewhat speculative safety claims.

overall_summary: A strong, directly relevant comment with clear concerns and actionable requests, but it relies more on assertion than supporting evidence and would be more persuasive with citations, data, or specific regulatory references.

comment_text:
I submit this comment in opposition to the proposed changes to the Clean Water Act framework.

I am an ecological land steward and native plant practitioner working in central Pennsylvania, primarily within riparian corridors, floodplains, and headwater systems in the Susquehanna River watershed. My work includes invasive species removal, native plant restoration, erosion control, and long-term watershed stabilization on private and semi-public lands. I work directly in the field with soils, streambanks, and stormwater flow patterns. The integrity of small tributaries and seasonal waters is not abstract to me; it is visible in real time through sediment load, bank collapse, algal growth, and downstream turbidity.

First, any rule that narrows Clean Water Act jurisdiction must be reconciled with the statutes core objective: to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nations waters (33 U.S.C. 1251(a)). In practice, water quality is cumulative and hydrologically connected. Headwater streams, ephemeral channels, wetlands, and floodplain depressions function as nutrient filters, sediment traps, and groundwater recharge zones. When those systems are weakened or excluded from protection, downstream waters degrade measurably. This is not theoretical; it is observable at the watershed scale.

Second, limiting Section 401 certification authority constrains the ability of states and Tribes to apply locally informed water quality standards to federally permitted projects. In Pennsylvania, many drinking water sources rely on intact upstream tributaries and forested riparian buffers. When review authority is narrowed, local governments lose one of the few meaningful tools available to address cumulative impacts from pipelines, fill projects, channelization, and other disturbance. Any such limitation must be clearly justified under the cooperative federalism structure embedded in the Act.

Third, under the Administrative Procedure Act, the agency must provide a reasoned explanation supported by scientific evidence demonstrating that the proposed changes will not result in increased pollutant loading, sedimentation, nutrient runoff, or impairment of downstream drinking water sources. From a field perspective, disturbance to small and intermittent waters has outsized downstream effects, particularly in agricultural and rapidly developing landscapes. A rulemaking that does not adequately analyze cumulative watershed impacts risks being arbitrary and capricious under Motor Vehicle Manufacturers Association v. State Farm.

Fourth, ecological function does not conform neatly to jurisdictional categories. Seasonal and ephemeral flows in Pennsylvania routinely carry nutrients and sediments into larger tributaries during storm events. Floodplain wetlands attenuate peak flows and reduce erosion pressure. Narrowing protection for these systems may reduce regulatory paperwork in the short term but will increase long-term remediation costs, infrastructure strain, and public health risk.

If the agency intends to depart from prior interpretations that recognized hydrological connectivity and cumulative watershed impacts, it must provide a reasoned explanation for that change and address reliance interests built around existing regulatory frameworks.

Based on direct, ongoing field experience within the Susquehanna watershed, weakening protections for upstream waters or constraining state and Tribal certification authority is inconsistent with the statutory objective of maintaining water integrity. Unless the agency can demonstrate, with peer-reviewed evidence and watershed-scale modeling, that the proposed revisions will not degrade downstream water quality, the proposal should not be finalized.

Thank you for the opportunity to submit this comment.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses Clean Water Act jurisdiction, Section 401 certification, and downstream water quality impacts, which are clearly tied to the likely docket topic.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is logically organized and generally coherent, moving from statutory purpose to state authority, APA concerns, and ecological function. Some claims are broad, but the overall reasoning is sound.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes statutory citation and a case reference, plus concrete field observations, but it lacks empirical data, studies, or specific examples with measurable results.
rationales.impact: It plausibly describes downstream effects such as sedimentation, nutrient runoff, and drinking water risks, but the magnitude and likelihood of these impacts are asserted more than demonstrated.
rationales.actionability: The comment clearly urges the agency not to finalize the proposal unless it can show no degradation, but it offers limited specific alternative regulatory language or detailed implementation requests.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is professionally written, well paragraphized, and easy to follow. It uses clear transitions and a formal tone, though it is not highly detailed in formatting.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, relevant public comment with clear legal and ecological framing. Its main weakness is the limited use of concrete evidence and the lack of highly specific policy recommendations.

overall_summary: A well-structured and highly relevant comment that persuasively argues against narrowing Clean Water Act protections, but it would be stronger with more data, citations, and more specific requested changes.

comment_text: NATIONAL WILDLIFE FEDERTION • TROUT UNLIMITED February 17, 2026 Lee Zeldin Administrator EPA Docket Center, Water Docket Mail Code 28221T U.S. Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20460 Re: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations; EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929; FRL– 6976.2–01–OW Dear Administrator Zeldin: We write in response to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) proposed rule revising regulations related to Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA or the Act), a tool used by states and Tribes (when this comment letter references states, it also intends to include Tribes where that is legally appropriate) to protect state and Tribal resources from the impacts of federally permitted or licensed projects.1 This proposed rule largely recycles a rule issued in 2020 that upended almost fifty years of strong state and Tribal authority to ensure that projects such as dams, pipelines, highways, and other projects are protective of state and Tribal waters and the important services that they provide people and wildlife. This 2020 rule was subsequently replaced in 2023 by a new rule that restored the strong proper role for states and Tribes envisioned by the Act while updating the rule to provide more certainty to applicants. This proposed rule ties the hands of states and Tribes, undermining the federalism that is central to the Act and putting important waters and aquatic habitats, such as wetlands and cold-water resources, at risk. It also contradicts existing Supreme Court precedent and results in a regulatory seesaw that creates uncertainty and chaos for water protection. The National Wildlife Federation (NWF) and Trout Unlimited (TU) oppose these proposed changes and urge EPA to keep the existing rule in place. As detailed below, our foremost concern is that it illegally limits the scope of certification from the activity as a whole to the “discharge from a point source.” This dramatically limits the ability of states and Tribes to look at the totality of impacts from a project and, for example, put protections in place that guarantee habitat protections, fish passage, adequate flow, and access to fisheries and recreation. However, we have other concerns as well with the proposal. In particular: 1 Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations, 91 Fed. Reg. 2008 (Jan. 15, 2026) 1 • It redefines “water quality requirements” to limit states’ and Tribes’ ability to protect “other water quality-related requirements of state law” by narrowing the definition of these requirements to applicable and appropriate state or Tribal water-quality related requirement for discharges. This would arguably undercut critical protections such as minimum flows needed for trout and other aquatic species. • It removes the ability of states and Tribes to ensure that they have the information they need to assess how various projects may impact their waters. This puts the ball in the court of applicants and makes it harder for state officials to assess how a project will impact state waters. • It gives the applicant an effective veto of modifications to protective conditions if a project changes. This will permit many applications to say no to needed conditions when a project is modified and may incentivize an applicant to modify a project to escape various conditions of a certification. • It does not allow for reasonable period of time extensions for events that could cause delay but are out of the certifying authority’s hands like a prolonged government shutdown or major natural disaster. • It would expand EPA authority to allow it to declare that certain kinds of discharges categorically (as opposed to discharge by discharge) would not affect water quality in other jurisdictions meaning that EPA would not need to identify discharges in such categories to other potentially affected states or Tribes and states or Tribes would not have an opportunity to lodge an objection to the permit or have a hearing based on the objection. • It violates Tribal sovereignty by removing their ability to apply for treatment as a state for Section 401 certification only, meaning that in order for Tribes to exercise their 401 authority, they will need to take on the added expenses and time required for full treatment as a state. • It removes the ability of states and Tribes to protect non-federal waters. This is a critical rollback given that, as explained below, EPA is also considering a rule that would remove 81% of previously federally protected wetlands and millions of miles of streams from federal jurisdiction.2 The proposed rule is a solution in search of a problem. Nowhere in the preamble, in the proposed rule itself, or in the economic analysis is a reasonable explanation given for why this proposed rule is necessary beyond the wishes of some in the regulated community. The basic structure of the 2023 rule has been in place – with success for states, Tribes, and the overwhelming majority of applicants, not to mention the communities, people, and wildlife who depend on clean water and healthy aquatic habitat – since 1971. To upset such a beneficial system, EPA should have a strong rationale. Instead, it states that it is making this change “to better align its regulations with the text and legislative history of the CWA, increase transparency, efficiency, and predictability for certifying authorities and the regulated community, and to ensure that States and authorized Tribes understand and adhere to their section 401 role.”3 These reasons are not compelling. Indeed, the proposed rule would achieve the opposite. 2 Regulatory Impact Analysis for the Proposed Updated Definition of t Waters of the United States Rule, U.S. Env. Protection Agency and Department of the Army, p. 46-47, Tbl. 3-1 (Nov. 2025). 3 91 Fed. Reg at 2016. 2 INTRODUCTION NWF and TU represent millions of Americans who fish, hunt, recreate, wildlife watch, and otherwise enjoy the tremendous water resources and aquatic habitats of this nation. Our organizations – including our chapters, councils and affiliated organizations across the country – have decades of experience advocating for clean waters and healthy aquatic habitats so wildlife and communities can thrive. As such, NWF and TU have developed expertise and experience with the implementation and enforcement of Section 401 as a vital tool for the protection of water resources and the wildlife and people that depend on these resources. Conserving our Nation’s wetlands, streams, and rivers for fish, wildlife, and communities is at the core of our mission and is of critical importance to our members. Healthy waters support healthy populations of fish and wildlife and help to support and sustain water-based businesses, including hunting, fishing, outdoor recreation and the recreational economy. In 2024, the Bureau of Economic Analysis estimated the outdoor recreation economy contributes $1.2 trillion dollars to the U.S. economy.4 Sportfishing alone is estimated to generate more than $230 billion annually, supporting 1.1 million jobs and contributing an estimated $70 billion in salaries and wages nationwide.5 These numbers reflect the significant and growing economic value of the outdoor recreation economy, generally, and the sportfishing industry more specifically. These economic benefits can be particularly impactful in rural areas, where jobs and money brought in during fishing and hunting seasons can be enough to keep small businesses operational for the entire year. The proposed rule would result in significant loss of protection for waters that sustain these pursuits, risking the robust economic benefits associated with these values. CWA Section 401 gives states and Tribes a critical say in ensuring that federally licensed or permitted activities that discharge into waters comply with water quality standards and applicable state or Tribal laws. In practice, Section 401 gives states and Tribes a vital role in protecting waters from impacts of projects that could have a significant effect on waters and aquatic habitat such as: dams and diversions, interstate energy and infrastructure projects, including pipelines, hydropower projects, roads and other development, and datacenters. Unless the state or Tribe issues a 401 certification or waives certification, a federal agency may not issue a permit for the proposed activity.6 As importantly, a state or Tribe may condition such certification to ensure that state waters are protected and those conditions must be followed. 4 The Bureau of Economic Analysis calculated the economic output of outdoor recreation to be $1.2 trillion in 2023. See: https://recreationroundtable.org/resources/national-recreation-data/ 5 American Sportfishing Association, Sportfishing in America (2024 report). Available at: www.asafishing.org/economic-impacts-of-recreational-fishing/ 6 The most common licenses or permits that may be subject to Section 401 certification are by far are CWA Section 404 permits for the discharge of dredged or fill material, and Rivers and Harbors Act Sections 9 and 10 permits issued by the Corps. See, e.g., U.S. ENVTL. PROT. AGENCY, ECONOMIC ANALYSIS FOR THE PROPOSED CLEAN WATER ACT SECTION 401 RULEMAKING FROM THE EPA, 6-7 (2019). Next would be Section 402 NDPES permits where EPA rather than a state or tribe issues the permit. The next most common class of projects needing certification are FERC licensed hydropower and interstate natural gas pipeline projects. 3 The power of states to reject 401 applications, or to place strong protective conditions on projects as a part of approving a 401 certification, including minimum instream flows, fish passage requirements, and sediment and temperature control conditions, has been instrumental in carrying out the goals of the Clean Water Act to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters. Among other benefits, carefully crafted certifications help ensure the maintenance of the chemical, physical and biological conditions for cold-water fish species like trout and salmon need to thrive, that projects do not cut off access to or degrade fish and wildlife habitat, and that protections are in place to eliminate, minimize, or mitigate serious risks to water quality and habitat that a project might cause, such as spills or erosion. Often, the 401 certification process is the best and only opportunity to ensure that such projects will be permitted and designed in a manner that avoids the impairment of streams and rivers, and that any impact is adequately mitigated. A strong CWA and 401 certification process does not preclude ongoing development – it simply makes sure that development is guided to prevent significant, long-term impacts on drinking water, fisheries, and other natural resources, while ensuring that developments consider potential economic impacts such as harm to fish populations or damages associated with flooding that may result from poorly thought out development activities. Indeed, the 401 certification process rarely results in denial or undue delay of projects. For example, as EPA’s Economic Analysis shows, almost all permits issued by the Corps (which make up the overwhelming majority of permits subject to Section 401) are either granted (95.5%) or waived (3.8%). Only 0.8% are denied.7 This proposal to weaken the 401 permitting process is concerning on its own, but is even more concerning in light of recent actions to reduce the extent of waters protected by the federal Clean Water Act. In 2023, the Supreme Court ruling in Sackett v. EPA8 dramatically reduced the number of wetlands covered by the Act. A recent proposal from this EPA and the U.S. Army Corps9 would curtail protections further to exclude all ephemeral waters, the vast majority of wetlands,10 and an unknown number of intermittent stream miles from the regulatory protections of the Clean Water Act. Together, these proposals would result in significant diminishment of protections for the waters that hunter, anglers, and all Americans depend on. Many projects will no longer need CWA permits, meaning that the trigger for Section 401 review for many projects will disappear, further weakening the ability of states and Tribes to protect their waters. Clean Water Act Section 401 Overview The objective of the CWA is to “restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation’s waters.”11 The Act also seeks the elimination of “discharge of pollutants into the navigable waters”12 and the achievement of “water quality which provides for the 7 Economic Analysis for the Proposed Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations, U.S. Env. Protection Agency, p. 12, tbl 2 (Jan. 2026). 8 598 U.S. 651 (2023). 9 U.S. EPA and U.S. Army Corps of Engineers Proposed Rule “Updated Definition of ‘Waters of the United States’” 90 Fed. Reg. 52,498 (November 20, 2025). 10 See Regulatory Impact Analysis for the Proposed Updated Definition of t Waters of the United States Rule, U.S. Env. Protection Agency and Department of the Army, p. 46-47, Tbl. 3-1 (Nov. 2025). 11 33 U.S.C. § 1251(a) (2019). 12 33 U.S.C. § 1251(a)(1). 4 protection and propagation of fish, shellfish, and wildlife and provides for recreation in and on the water.”13 The Act seeks to achieve these goals through a strong cooperative federalism partnership that “recognize[s], preserve[s], and protect[s] the primary responsibilities and rights of the States to prevent, reduce, and eliminate pollution” of their waters.14 Section 401 is central to this state-federal partnership and a key instrument to achieving the water protection goals of the Act. The regulatory framework under the Clean Water Act is dependent on this delegation of primary responsibility to the states.15 The Supreme Court has recognized that Section 401 is “[o]ne of the primary mechanisms through which the states may assert the broad authority” reserved to them over the stewardship of their waters.16 Courts have repeatedly recognized the state’s authority under Section 401 to be a broad one that extends beyond – rather than is constrained by – federal jurisdiction. As Justice Stevens explained: “[n]ot a single sentence, phrase, or word in the Clean Water Act purports to place any constraint on a State’s power to regulate the quality of its own waters more stringently than federal law might require. In fact, the Act explicitly recognizes States’ ability to impose stricter standards.”17 In establishing the Clean Water Act, Congress recognized that states and Tribes have broad authority to protect their waters and aquatic habitat. Until the 2020 rule, this broad authority has been reinforced through decades of administrative practice and judicial precedent. The Proposed Rule Ignores the Clean Water Act’s Cooperative Federalism Structure and Undermines Tribal Sovereignty. In order to give states and Tribes a strong role in protecting their waters, the CWA gives primary authority for certain actions to states and Tribes and preserves many existing state powers. For example, Congress expressly recognized the close relationship between CWA programs and state authority over land and water. Section 101(b) of the Act18 acknowledges and preserves state authorities over both land and water. That Congress uses the phrase “land and water resources” in Section 101(b) is an explicit acknowledgment of the deep inter-relationship between 13 33 U.S.C. § 1251(a)(2). 14 33 U.S.C. § 1251 (a)-(b). 15 Keating v. Fed. Energy Rg. Comm’n, 927 F.2d 616, 622 (D.C. Cir. 1991). 16 Id. 17 PUD No. 1 v. Wash. Dep't of Ecology, 511 U.S. 700, 723 (1994) (Stevens, J., concurring) (citing 33 U.S.C. § 1311(b)(1)(C)). 18 See 33 U.S.C. §1351(b) (2019). It is the policy of the Congress to recognize, preserve, and protect the primary responsibilities and rights of States to prevent, reduce, and eliminate pollution, to plan the development and use (including restoration, preservation, and enhancement) of land and water resources, and to consult with the Administrator in the exercise of his authority under this chapter. It is the policy of Congress that the States manage the construction grant program under this chapter and implement the permit programs under sections 1342 and 1344 of this title. It is further the policy of the Congress to support and aid research relating to the prevention, reduction, and elimination of pollution and to provide Federal technical services and financial aid to State and interstate agencies and municipalities in connection with the prevention, reduction, and elimination of pollution. 5 regulating land, including, for example, riparian areas, and regulating water to protect and restore its chemical, physical and biological integrity.19 The U.S. Supreme Court has described the CWA’s cooperative federalism in Arkansas v. Oklahoma: “The Clean Water Act anticipates a partnership between the states and the Federal Government, animated by a shared objective: ‘to restore and maintain the chemical, physical, and biological integrity of the Nation's waters.’”20As Justice Stevens later explained in PUD No. 1 of Jefferson Ctny v. Wash. Dep’t of Ecology, “[T]he Act explicitly recognizes States’ ability to impose stricter standards [than federal law].”21 The Act also includes Section 510, a parallel provision, in its enforcement section,22 which states that: Nothing in this chapter shall (1) preclude or deny the right of any state … to adopt or enforce (A) any standard or limitation respecting discharges of pollutants or (B) any requirement respecting control or abatement of pollution; except [one that] is less stringent than the effluent limitation or other limitation, effluent standard, prohibition, pretreatment or standard of performance [set] under this chapter. The theme of broad authority of states to protect their land and waters has echoed throughout CWA jurisprudence. For example, in Solid Waste Agency of Northern Cook County. v. U.S. Corps of Engineers, the U.S. Supreme Court five-justice majority overturned EPA’s and the Corps’ “Migratory Bird Rule” because it might significantly impinge on states’ traditional and primary power over land and water use.23 The Court cited Section 101(b), saying, “Rather than expressing a desire to readjust the federal-state balance in this manner, Congress chose to ‘recognize, preserve, and protect the primary responsibilities and rights of States … to plan the development and use … of land and water resources …’”24 Section 101(b) is viewed as so central to the Act that justices have argued about whose interpretation of other CWA provisions is most consistent with it. In Rapanos v. United States,25 the plurality contended that its definition of “waters of the United States” was the proposed one because it was consistent with §101(b): This statement of policy was included in the Act as enacted in 1972, [so] plainly referred to something beyond the subsequently added state administration [of a 404] program. But the expansive theory advanced by the Corps, rather than “preserv[ing] the primary rights and responsibilities of the States,” would have brought virtually all “plan[ning of] the development and use . . . of land and water resources” by the States under federal control.26 19 33 U.S.C. § 1351(a). 20 Arkansas v. Oklahoma, 503 U.S. 91, 101 (1992) (citing 33 U.S.C. 1251(a)). 21 See 511 U.S. at 723 (1994) (Stevens, J., concurring). 22 33 U.S.C. §1370 (2019). 23 Solid Waste Agency v. United States Army Corps of Eng'rs, 531 U.S. 159, 167 (2001). 24 Id. at 174. 25 Rapanos v. United States, 547 U.S. 715 (2006). 26 Id. at 737. 6 … [C]lean water is not the only purpose of the statute. So is the preservation of primary state responsibility for ordinary land-use decisions.27 Despite the centrality of cooperative federalism to the Act and the importance of a strong state and Tribal role over its waters and land, this proposed rule seeks to turn that structure on its head.28 Instead, the proposed rule would improperly constrain states and Tribes in a manner that both substantively and procedurally impairs their ability to protect their waters effectively through the Section 401 certification process. The Proposed Rule Improperly Impinges on Tribal Sovereignty In the case of Tribes, the proposed rule eliminates the ability of Tribes to exercise authority over Section 401 without the burdens of taking on other expensive and cumbersome aspects of the broader “treatment in a similar manner as a state” (TAS) status – something that many Tribes do not have the resources to do. Moreover, the proposed rule also eliminates Tribes’ ability to assume treatment as a state for the purpose of being a neighboring jurisdiction. This takes away the ability of Tribes to be notified by EPA when an activity in a neighboring state may impact Tribal waters and object to such activity and have the potential to have the permit or licensed conditioned to protect the Tribe. This is counter to the Act’s structure and Tribal sovereignty. The text of the Clean Water Act gives no indication that TAS for Section 401 should not be granted separately. Section 518 of the Act provides that Tribes be given the opportunity to obtain TAS “for purposes of title II and sections 104, 106, 303, 305, 308, 309, 314, 319, 401, 402, 404, and 406” of the Act.29 The Act makes no indication that TAS for any of these sections is dependent TAS status for any other section. Furthermore, EPA offers no rationale for this change. It is arbitrary and capricious and undermines the Act’s desire to give TAS authority to Tribes for sections where the Tribe “is reasonably expected to be capable … of carrying out the functions to be exercised.”30 The Proposed Rule Improperly Interferes with the Ability of States and Tribes to Determine the Contents of a Complete Application The proposed rule would establish a standard “single complete list” of requirements for what is included in a complete request for certification and would eliminate current regulation allowing states to establish those requirements directly. In imposing this minimal information standard, the EPA would constrain the ability of states and tribes to set these requirements and ensure that 27 33 U.S.C. § 1251(b) (2019). 28 It bears noting that EPA’s reading of both the “major questions doctrine” and the “clear statement” rule also turns these concepts on their head. They are chiefly intended to protect states from undue interference from expansive federal intrusion without clear direction from Congress. This is what the Supreme Court had in mind when it stated that it “require[s] Congress to enact exceedingly clear language if it wishes to significantly alter the balance between federal and state power . . .” Sackett v. EPA, 598 U.S. 651, 679 (2023). See 91 Fed. Reg. 2026 (Jan. 15. 2026). Here, EPA is doing the opposite – imposing heavy handed federal authority over states and Tribes. Such instances do not invoke the “major questions doctrine” or the “clear statement” rule. This is especially true when the balance between states and Tribes and the federal government has been long established and the federal government is unilaterally and without proper justification asserting itself to shift that balance in its favor. 29 33 U.S.C. § 1377(e). 30 33 U.S.C. § 1377(e)(3). 7 they have the information they need to adequately assess the impact on their waters.31 While we understand that is important for applicants to have certainty about when an application is complete, the proposed rule’s approach of setting a minimum for the contents an application deprives states and Tribes of the ability to ensure that they have the information they need for a process that is driven by state and Tribal agencies, not federal ones. As is currently allowed, states and Tribes need the ability to set clear requirements for the information they need in making determinations in what is a state or Tribal decision-making process. States and Tribes are the best judges of the information they need to protect their regionally different waters and water quality concerns. Moreover, there is no direct statutory authority in the Act to support EPA’s proposed imposition a one-size-fits-all requirement on states and Tribes as to what constitutes a “complete” application for a regulatory process that occurs completely at the state or Tribal level. Instead, the Act appears to leave this regulatory function to states or Tribes. Section 510 of the Act broadly provides that nothing in the law limits state or Tribal authority to regulate their waters more stringently than the federal government. It states that, “Except as expressly provided in this chapter, nothing in this chapter shall … be construed as impairing or in any manner affecting any right or jurisdiction of the States with respect to the waters (including boundary waters) of such States.”32 Additionally, Section 401 grants certifying authorities the power to “establish procedures for public notice in the case of all applications for certification by it and, to the extent it deems appropriate, procedures for public hearings in connection with specific applications.”33 These provisions appear to leave the procedural process for determining what constitutes a complete application triggering certification to the state or Tribe. EPA points to section 501 to justify the proposal to impose content restrictions, but that section does not support this proposal. This section only states that the Administrator may “prescribe such regulations as are necessary to carry out his functions” under the Act.34 It does not confer to EPA the ability to impose regulations on state or Tribal agencies in the carrying out their functions under the Act and it is not explained how this change is necessary to the Administrator in carrying out his functions. States and Tribes therefore should not be deprived of any ability to decide when an application is complete and what information they need to assess a project under the certification process. Additionally, since the rule will create situations where states and Tribes do not have ability to ensure that they have the information they need to protect their waters before the reasonable period of time starts to wind down, this proposed rule will either force bad decision making based on limited data or will force permit denials because states or Tribes don’t have sufficient information. Shortchanging the ability of states and Tribes to determine what constitutes a complete application fundamentally undermines concepts of shared federalism written into the Act. 31 91 Fed. Reg. at 2040 (proposed rule § 121.5). 32 33 U.S.C. § 1370; see also 40 C.F.R. § 123.1(i)(1) (EPA regulations regarding state NPDES programs; specifying that “[n]othing in this part precludes a State from … [a]dopting or enforcing requirements which are more stringent or more extensive than those required under this part”). 33 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1). 34 33 U.S.C. § 1361(a). 8 The Proposed Rule Impermissibly Restricts the Scope of Certification By far the most concerning and impermissible aspect of the proposed rule is its attempt to limit the scope of certification from the activity as whole to the “discharge from a point source.” In doing so, the proposed rule impermissibly restricts states’ and Tribes’ decision-making process by limiting conditions that they can place on projects to conditions that relate to the discharge and not the activity as a whole. This narrow interpretation has been rejected by multiple courts, including the Supreme Court.35 The EPA provides no reasonable explanation for breaking course on this interpretation in the proposed rule. Section 121.3 would illegally limit conditions states can place on federally licensed and permitted projects to “assuring that a discharge from a federally licensed or permitted activity” – not the activity as whole – “will comply with applicable and appropriate water quality requirements.”36 This would radically curtail long- standing (with the exception of the 2020 rule) practice – which the Supreme Court has upheld – and dramatically limit states’ power to protect their waters from federal projects that threaten water quality and aquatic habitat. It is well established that a “discharge” into waters triggers Section 401 review but does not determine the scope of certification. In fact, the Supreme Court has already decided this question. In considering the text of the Act, it ruled that states and Tribes have certification authority over the activity as a whole. In PUD No. 1 of Jefferson Cnty v. Wash. Dep’t of Ecology, the Supreme Court held that the language of Section 401(d) of the Act “contradicts petitioner’s claim that State may only impose water quality limitations specifically tied to the ‘discharge.’ The text refers to the compliance of the applicant, not the discharge. Section 401(d) thus allows the State to impose ‘other limitations’ on the project in general to assure compliance with various provisions of the Clean Water Act and with ‘any other appropriate requirement of State law.’” 37 Contrary to EPA’s claims, the PUD No. 1 decision was not a Chevron deference decision. The analysis in PUD No. 1 centered on the statutory language itself, parsing out the specific words Congress chose to include in Sections 401(a) and (d). The Court looked to the plain language of Section 401, noting clear and key differences in language between Sections 401(a) and 401(d) that make plain that Congress intended states to have certification authority over the activity as a whole, not just the discharge. The Court’s reference to Chevron was in dicta, where the Court made the point that its own reading of the statutory text was consistent with long-standing EPA interpretation, which the Court correctly noted was, at the time, subject to deference.38 Thus, this is the interpretation of the Act that Supreme Court in looking at the plain language of the Act has already decided upon. PUD No. 1 concluded that the unambiguous language of Section 401 allows states and Tribes to apply conditions to a licensed or permitted activity as a whole, rejecting the argument that states and Tribes could only apply such conditions to a discharge.39 As the seven-member majority recognized in PUD No. 1, the CWA’s use of the term “discharge of a pollutant” creates a mere “threshold condition” for state certification, not a 35 See PUD No. 1 v. Wash. Dep't of Ecology, 511 U.S. at 712. 36 91 Fed. Reg. at 2040. 37 PUD No. 1 v. Wash. Dep't of Ecology, 511 U.S. at 711. 38 Id. at 712. 39 Id. at 713-14 (1994). 9 limitation.40 Moreover, Section 401(a) does not even require an actual discharge; rather the statute triggers certification when a discharge “may result.” It simply does not follow that Congress intended to limit state authority to an action – the discharge – that Congress did not even require to be present. As the Supreme Court stated in PUD No. 1, Section 401(d) “expands the State’s authority to impose conditions on the certification of a project” that expand beyond the threshold “discharge.”41 The Court went on to conclude that Section 401(d) “authoriz[es] additional conditions and limitations on the activity as whole once the threshold question, the existence of a discharge, is satisfied.”42 No intervening case law, including the Supreme Court’s decision in Loper Bright v. Raimondo,43 changes that holding and EPA is bound to follow it.44 The proposed rule does not do that and to the extent that it does not, it is plainly unlawful. States and Tribes have placed conditions on an activity as a whole as a critical tool for protecting state and Tribal waters and lands from the impacts of federally permitted and licensed projects for decades. For instance, for Section 404 permits, such conditions reflect states’ attempts to conform permits to EPA’s Section 404(b)(1) guidelines. Examples include a 2001 Colorado certification for the Arapahoe Basin Ski Area where the applicant agreed to pay $15,000 to fund the cleanup of an abandoned mine hot spot elsewhere in the watershed. EPA also imposed a condition in a Section 402 permit for a mine in Arizona that the applicant clean up an upstream abandoned mine.45 In that case, Arizona only provided its certification after EPA added two conditions to the draft Section 402 permit, requiring the applicant: (1) to make additional groundwater discharges that would augment Pinto Creek stream flows, and (2) to remediate sources of copper loading from an upstream inactive mine. The Proposed Rule Improperly Limits Water Quality Related Considerations to those Involving the Discharge The proposed rule is flawed in limiting the scope of certification to the “discharge,” and is similarly flawed in its attempt to limit “appropriate state or tribal water quality-related regulatory requirements” to only those requirements “for discharges.”46 The current definition of “water quality requirements” at 40 C.F.R. Sec. 121.1(j) is clear. The current definition provides that, “Water quality requirements means any limitation, standard, or other requirement under sections 301, 302, 303, 306, and 307 of the Clean Water Act, any Federal and state or Tribal laws or regulations implementing those sections, and any other water quality-related requirement of state or Tribal law.” This definition makes plain that states and Tribes should focus on water quality 40 Id. at 711-12. 41 Id. at 711. 42 Id. at 712 (emphasis added). 43 603 U.S. 369 (2024). 44 It is worth noting that twelve years after PUD No. 1, the Court, in S.D. Warren Co. v. Maine Bd. Envtl. Prot., 547 U.S. 370 (2006), upheld Maine’s conditional certification of a license renewal for a hydroelectric dam, finding that section 401’s “terms have a broad reach, requiring state approval any time a federally licensed activity ‘may’ result in a discharge (‘discharge’ of course being without any qualifiers here) and its object comprehends maintaining state water quality standards….” Id. at 380 (citation omitted). The Court held that the water flowing out of the dam constituted a “discharge” triggering the certification obligation and proceeded to uphold certification conditions that considered water quality impacts beyond those associated with that discharge, including those addressing limitations in flow that could impact habitat. See id. at 385-86. 45 See Friends of Pinto Creek v USEPA 504 F3 1007 (9th Cir 2007). 46 91 Fed. Reg. at 2040 (proposed rule 121.1(f)). 10 concerns in the certification process under federal, state, or Tribal law. This is consistent with the plain meaning of Section 401(d), which allows that states and Tribes may place limitations and requirements on an activity based on “any applicable effluent limitations and other limitations, under section 301 or 302 of this title, standard of performance under section 306 of this title, or prohibition, effluent standard, or pretreatment standard under section 307 of this title, and with any other appropriate requirement of State law set forth in such certification.”47 Like the improper proposed limitation on the scope of certification, this proposed change would have a significant, detrimental impact to water resources for millions of Americans by improperly limiting state and Tribal efforts to protect habitat from detrimental impacts of development. The proposed change would significantly constrain states’ and Tribes’ ability to ensure compliance with water quality standards to protect and restore fish populations, including conditions related to fish passage, minimum flows, dissolved oxygen, and other aspects of fish habitat impacted by the project as a whole. The limitation could also bar many conditions related to sediment and erosion control from non- point source runoff, which are not related to a discharge from a point source. These are needed to protect waters from construction site non-point source runoff that may result in exceedances of turbidity or salinity (often stated as “total dissolved solids” or TDS) standards. In some cases, states have entirely separate statutory schemes for sediment and erosion control that are not part of water quality statutes. Therefore, they would not have been submitted to EPA for approval. An example is Georgia’s Erosion and Sedimentation Act, cited in EPA’s 2010 Section 401 Handbook, through which the state imposes conditions related to buffer integrity, construction and post-construction stormwater management, and the adequacy of mitigation. This change, especially when combined with the proposed change to limit the scope of certification, could eliminate many protective conditions states and Tribes rely on to protect habitat. Effects Combined with Sackett and the Proposed Sackett Rulemaking This proposed rule must be seen in relation to recent changes to the scope of Clean Water Act protections generally. Section 401 is only triggered when a federal permit or license may result in a discharge into a water of the United States. In 2023, the U.S. Supreme Court issued a decision in Sackett v. EPA that dramatically reduced the number of waters covered by the federal Clean Water Act. Subsequently, EPA and the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers are finalizing a rule that would even further limit the number of waters protected by the Act. If the proposed EPA- Corps rule governing “waters of the United States” is finalized, it would result in the loss of protections for 81% of historically federally protected wetlands,48 ephemeral waters and streams, and untold numbers of intermittently flowing or standing waters and streams. With these rollbacks, many projects will no longer need CWA permits, meaning that the trigger for Section 401 review for many projects will disappear altogether. 47 33 U.S.C. § 1341(d) (emphasis added). 48 Regulatory Impact Analysis for the Proposed Updated Definition of t Waters of the United States Rule, U.S. Env. Protection Agency and Department of the Army, p. 46-47, Tbl. 3-1 (Nov. 2025). 11 Additionally, the proposed rule would remove the ability of states to protect non-federal waters by limiting the scope of certification to just the discharge into “waters of the United States.”49 This further removes a tool for states and Tribes to protect critical waters that have lost basic protections as a result of jurisdictional rollbacks. The combined result of these two proposed rules will be far fewer opportunities for state and Tribes to protect their waters under Section 401 and far weaker options for them to do so in the limited number of times it is triggered. This will lead to more water pollution, more destruction of critical aquatic resources, more risk of flooding and other disasters, more threats to drinking water and public health, the loss of habitat, deprivation of recreational and other opportunities, and other negative consequences for our waters and the people – which is all of us – that rely on them. The Economic Analysis Shows Section 401 is Not a Hindrance to Development. As stated above, we are left wondering what problem this proposed rule is intending to address and note the lack of any reasonable rationale for these changes. Section 401 has for most of the last 55 years been an important and reliable tool for states and Tribes to protect their waters with enormous benefits for waters, people, wildlife, and habitat. The Section 401 process rarely results in denial or undue delay of projects. For example, as EPA’s Economic Analysis shows, almost all permits issued by the Corps (which make up the overwhelming number of permits subject to Section 401) are either granted (95.5%) or waived (3.8%). Only 0.8% are denied.50 Also, as stated above, with the massive reduction in Clean Water Act jurisdiction, Section 401 will impact a potentially significantly fewer number of projects moving forward. The economic analysis also fails to look at the economic impacts that could result from a much more limited Section 401 that could result in the degradation of waters and the uses they support. Water quality supports many local industries, has a direct tie to public health costs, and is a critical driver of the outdoor recreation economy, which contributes an astounding $1.2 trillion to the U.S. economy.51 The economic contributions of sportfishing alone in the United States exceeds $230 billion per year.52 This recreation economy generates millions of quality, high- paying jobs in the U.S. across a wide variety of industries and accounts for nearly 2.5% of the GDP.53 The proposed rule would result in significant loss of protection for waters that sustain these pursuits, risking the robust economic benefits and an additional $70 billion in salaries and wages. These and other economic impacts of degraded and lost waters are not properly examined by the proposed rule. 49 See 91 Fed. Reg. (2040) (Proposed Rule 121.5). 50 Econ. Analysis at 12, Tbl. 2. 51 The Bureau of Economic Analysis calculated the economic output of outdoor recreation to be $1.2 trillion in 2023. See: https://recreationroundtable.org/resources/national-recreation-data/ 52 https://asafishing.org/industry/sportfishing-economic- impact/#:~:text=Alexandria%2C%20VA%20%E2%80%93%20March%2012%2C,in%20rural%20and%20coastal% 20regions.%22 53 https://recreationroundtable.org/resources/national-recreation-data/ 12 Section 401 Should Cover General Permits EPA is asking whether it is appropriate for Section 401 to cover general permits. It is. And, as EPA itself notes, always has been. EPA again offers no justifiable rationale why it should be otherwise. As stated above, Section 401 is largely triggered by Section 404 permits, the vast majority of which are issued under Nationwide Permits. States and Tribes review these nationwide permits and condition them to ensure that they are protective of state and Tribal water quality and frequently condition these permits to protect key state waters. Removing Section 401 authority for such permits would open countless waters to potential harm and further weaken state and Tribal authority to protect waters’ uses under Section 401. EPA’s Economic Analysis for the proposed rule illustrates the severe impact of removing general permits from certification authority:54 According to EPA’s own data, general permits comprise 94% (53,341 of 56,841) of average annual federal permits Taking certification authority away from states and Tribes for general permits would hamstring their ability to protect their waters. We strongly object to any change in this regard. 54 Proposed Rule Economic Analysis at 11. 13 EPA Failed to consult with FWS & NMFS under Section 7 of the ESA. In offering this proposal, EPA has failed to comply with the requirements of the Endangered Species Act (ESA).55 Section 7(a)(2) of the ESA requires EPA to consult with the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service (USFWS) and the National Marine Fisheries Service (NMFS) (collectively the Services) to “insure that any action authorized, funded, or carried out by such agency…is not likely to jeopardize the continued existence of any endangered species or threatened species or result in the adverse modification of [critical] habitat.”56 Agency “action” is broadly defined in the ESA’s implementing regulations to include “(b) the promulgation of regulations . . .”57 Once the consultation duty is triggered, agencies must use the “best scientific and commercial data available” in completing the consultation process.58 Any agency action that may affect a listed species or its critical habitat triggers the consultation requirement. The threshold for a finding of “may affect” is extremely low: “any possible effect, whether beneficial, benign, adverse, or of an undetermined character, triggers the formal consultation requirement.”59 The potential impact on ESA listed species from the proposed rule easily clears this threshold. However, EPA has not consulted with the Services on the impacts of this proposed rule. It must do so. The proposed rule could potentially impact many species listed as threatened or endangered pursuant to the ESA. For instance, states have used Section 401 certification to condition projects, such as hydroelectric dam projects, to ensure that they accommodate the needs of sensitive listed species such as several species of salmon. Such conditions – which may not be directly related to the discharge or may be disagreed with by the federal agency – could include fish ladders, provisions to reduce erosion and sedimentation, and flow requirements (which was at issue in PUD No. 1). Restricting states’ and Tribes’ abilities to impose such protective conditions could eliminate or degrade salmon habitat, affecting listed salmon or modifying critical habitat for such species. Likewise, hindering a state’s ability to protect habitat could impact downstream species as well. Listed orca in the Puget Sound depend on healthy salmon populations for foraging. These salmon populations are dependent on the Endangered Species Act for their survival. Impeding the states’ and Tribes’ ability to protect such populations could impact the foraging success of listed orca, in turn impacting the species. Similarly, erosion, sedimentation, spill, or other concerns associated with pipeline projects could impact a host of listed species that depend on wetlands, are vulnerable to pipeline spills or leaks, or require specific conditions to thrive that could be impacted by increased turbidity, suspended solids, reduced oxygen levels, or the like. 55 16 U.S.C. § 1531 et seq. 56 16 U.S.C. § 1536(a)(2). 57 50 C.F.R. § 402.02 (emphasis added). 58 16 U.S.C. § 1536(a)(2). 59 Interagency Cooperation—Endangered Species Act of 1973, as Amended; Final Rule, 51 FR 19,926, 19,949 (June 3, 1986); U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and National Marine Fisheries Service, Endangered Species Consultation Handbook (March 1998) at xvi (defining “may affect” as “the appropriate conclusion when a proposed action may pose any effects on listed species ….”). 14 Inadequate Commenting Period Finally, a 30-day public comment period for a rule of such importance is simply inadequate. This is a complex proposal and people should be provided more time to give thoughtful feedback. Such a short comment period hampers meaningful public input. The current rule has been, in substantial form, in place for most of the last 55 years. It also bears mentioning that this 30-day comment period comes on the heels of a 45-day comment period over the Thanksgiving and end- of-year holidays for the proposed rule redefining “waters of the U.S.” Both of these proposed rules are highly consequential rulemakings involving complex legal questions and technical matters with profound implications for the public and our waters. Executive Orders 12866 and 13563 state that the minimum comment period for significant rulemaking should be at least 60 days. Public participation is a key component to transparent, democratic, and effective governance. We urge you to provide more time for the public to offer informative comments and feedback in support of the waters that sustain them. Conclusion NWF and TU again thank you for the opportunity to comment on this proposed rule. We recommend withdrawing the proposed rule and keeping the 2023 rule in place in order to best protect waters resources and aquatic habitat and the fish, wildlife, and outdoor pursuits valued by our members and sportsmen and women across the country. Sincerely, /s/ Jim Murphy Jim Murphy Sr. Director, Legal Advocacy National Wildlife Federation /s/ Kate Miller Kate Miller Director of Government Affairs Trout Unlimited 15

rationales.relevance: The letter directly addresses the proposed Section 401 water quality certification rule and responds to multiple specific issues raised in the docket, including scope of certification, application completeness, Tribal authority, general permits, and comment period length.
rationales.reasoning: Arguments are logically organized, legally grounded, and consistently tied back to the statutory text, case law, and the proposed regulatory changes. The letter clearly explains why each change is viewed as unlawful or harmful.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes extensive citations to the Clean Water Act, Supreme Court cases, EPA Federal Register notices, economic data, and examples of state certification conditions, providing strong factual and legal support.
rationales.impact: It describes concrete and plausible impacts such as reduced habitat protection, weaker fish passage and flow protections, loss of Tribal authority, more pollution, and diminished outdoor recreation and economic benefits.
rationales.actionability: The main request is clear: withdraw the proposed rule and retain the 2023 rule. It also asks for a longer comment period. Recommendations are feasible, though most of the letter is focused on opposition rather than detailed alternative regulatory text.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is professionally formatted, well sectioned with headings, and generally easy to follow despite its length. Citations and examples are integrated throughout in a formal comment-letter style.
rationales.overall: This is a highly substantive, well-supported, and directly relevant public comment letter. It combines legal analysis, policy critique, and concrete examples to make a strong case against the proposal, with clear requested action.

overall_summary: A strong, comprehensive comment letter that is highly relevant to the docket, well reasoned, heavily supported by legal and factual citations, and clear in its requested outcome. Its only minor weakness is that it offers limited affirmative alternative drafting beyond urging withdrawal and retention of the existing rule.

comment_text: Comment on Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929-0001 Comment submitted by: Institute for Energy Research 1155 15th St NW Suite 525 Washington, DC 20005 Kenneth Stein, Vice President for Policy February 17, 2026 In February of 2025, the Institute for Energy Research submitted a petition for rescission and reconsideration of the final rule entitled Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule, 88 Fed. Reg. 66,558 (Sept. 27, 2023) (“2023 Rule”), and to initiate notice-and-comment rulemaking to rescind the Rule’s preamble and amend the requirements for Section 401 certifications and related provisions at 40 C.F.R. Parts 121, 122, and 124. IER supports EPA undertaking this rulemaking to amend the 2023 rule. IER notes that several sections of the proposed rule closely align with the recommendations in our petition and we support the finalization of those proposed elements. In other respects, the proposed rule does not adopt or does not address our recommended regulatory actions. IER urges EPA to modify the proposed rulemaking as recommended in this comment. INTRODUCTION Section 401 of the Clean Water Act provides that federal agencies may not permit or license projects “which may result in any discharge into the navigable waters” until the relevant State or authorized Tribe certifies that “any such discharge will comply with” applicable water quality requirements, or waives the opportunity to certify. 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1). This certification provision helps to ensure that projects licensed by federal agencies comply with regulations established by EPA or adopted by States and approved by EPA pursuant to the CWA.1 In enacting Section 401 in the 1972 CWA amendments, Congress tailored the certification program to the statute’s overall strategy of improving water quality by regulating point-source discharges into federally regulated waters. See Train v. City of New York, 420 U.S. 35, 37 (1975). By its plain terms, the statute limits the scope of certification to determining whether a “discharge” into the navigable waters “will comply with the applicable provisions of sections 1311, 1312, 1313, 1316, and 1317” of the CWA. 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1). To enforce these provisions, the statute allows States granting certification to include conditions that become part of the federal license—but limits such conditions to “effluent limitations and other limitations, and monitoring requirements” that are “necessary” to assure compliance with the CWA. Id. § 1341(d). And to promote efficiency and avoid delays, the statute provides that States waive certification if they fail to issue a decision “within a reasonable period of time,” which cannot “exceed one year” from receipt of the request. Id. § 1341(a)(1). Congress vested EPA with authority to superintend Section 401 as part of its responsibility to “administer” the CWA. 33 U.S.C. § 1251(d). Section 501(a) authorizes EPA to “prescribe such regulations as are necessary to carry out [its] functions under this chapter,” including EPA’s functions under Section 401. Id. § 1361(a); see id. §§ 1341(a)(1) (requiring EPA to act as the certifying authority when a State cannot), 1341(a)(2) (requiring EPA to coordinate with neighboring States regarding cross-border impacts), 1341(b) (requiring EPA to provide technical assistance upon request). Moreover, Section 304(h) specifically instructs EPA to “promulgate guidelines” that “shall include the factors which must be provided in any certification pursuant to section 1341.” Id. § 1314(h). This oversight responsibility is essential to “the Act’s purpose of authorizing the EPA to create and manage a uniform system of interstate water pollution regulation.” Arkansas v. Oklahoma, 503 U.S. 91, 110 (1992). Notwithstanding the 1972 CWA’s express command to implement Section 401, EPA relied for fifty years on a 1971 regulation issued under a repealed certification provision in the Federal Water Pollution Control Act (“FWPCA”). Unlike the CWA, the FWPCA relied on a system of water quality standards that did not control the sources of 1 For ease of reference, this comment uses “State” to refer to the certifying function performed by the relevant States, Territories, and authorized Tribes. See 33 U.S.C. §§ 1362 (defining “State” to include the District of Columbia and other federal Territories), 1377(e) (providing that Tribes may be treated as States under certain conditions, including for purposes of Section 401). pollution that affect water quality. The 1971 regulation reflected the FWPCA’s text and structure by requiring States to certify that an “activity” will comply with “applicable water quality requirements.” Because EPA retained the 1971 regulation long after the statutory language on which it relied was repealed, the case law and practices developed under this regime diverged from the CWA’s distinct approach. The Supreme Court followed EPA’s lead in this respect by affording the 1971 regulation Chevron deference without wrestling with this statutory history. See PUD No. 1 of Jefferson Cnty. v. Wash. Dep’t of Ecology, 511 U.S. 700, 712 (1994) (citing Chevron U.S.A. Inc. v. NRDC, Inc., 467 U.S. 837 (1984)). EPA sought to rectify this inaction in 2020 by adopting an implementing regulation for Section 401. See Clean Water Act Section 401 Certification Rule, 85 Fed. Reg. 42,210 (July 13, 2020) (“2020 Rule”). Relying in part on Chevron deference, the 2020 Rule sought to clarify the scope of certification, the limits on certification conditions, and the timeline for certification decisions. First, the 2020 Rule confirmed that Section 401(a)(1) limits certification to water quality impacts from a point-source discharge into the navigable waters. Id. at 42,232, 42,234. Next, the 2020 Rule interpreted Section 401(d) as limiting certification conditions to restrictions that are “necessary” to assure compliance with a CWA requirement “that applies to point source discharges into waters of the United States.” Id. at 42,253, 42,256. In addition, the 2020 Rule established a number of procedural guiderails for the certification program, including a process for federal licensing agencies to define a “reasonable period of time” of less than one year, rules for the minimum content of State certification decisions, and a process for federal licensing agencies to verify State compliance with procedural requirements. Id. at 42,258, 42,267, 42,286. Following a change in administration, however, EPA reversed course by adopting a new rule that returned to practice under the 1971 regulation. See Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Improvement Rule, 88 Fed. Reg. 66,558 (Sept. 27, 2023) (“2023 Rule”). Again relying on Chevron deference, the 2023 Rule greatly expanded the scope of certification and certification conditions and largely abdicated EPA’s supervisory role. Under the 2023 Rule, States evaluate the “activity” as a whole—including water quality impacts from non-point sources and impacts on purely intrastate waters—so long as the project may involve one point-source discharge into the navigable waters, as required by Section 401(a)(1). Id. at 66,592–601. The 2023 Rule also interpreted the phrase “any other appropriate requirement of State law” in Section 401(d) as inviting States to impose any water quality-related conditions, including State requirements unrelated to the CWA. Id. at 66,602–06. Finally, the 2023 Rule removed virtually every guardrail set out in the 2020 Rule, reasoning that Section 401 is a substantive grant of authority to States that requires EPA to defer to State prerogatives. Id. at 66,616–24. Further rulemaking is required to adhere EPA’s regulations to Section 401. Both the 2020 Rule and 2023 Rule relied on Chevron deference to assert “reasonable” readings of the statute. See 88 Fed. Reg. at 66,594–96; 85 Fed. Reg. at 42,251–52. But Chevron is no longer good law. As the Supreme Court held in Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo, 144 S. Ct. 2244, 2266 (2024), statutes “have a single, best meaning” that must be given effect, and EPA has an independent obligation to conform its actions to that meaning. Read in light of “the traditional tools of statutory construction,” id., Section 401’s text, structure, and history compel reconsideration of the 2023 Rule for three principal reasons. First, Section 401(a)(1) expressly limits certification to water quality impacts from any “discharge” into the navigable waters. The provision specifies both the trigger for certification—a project that “may result in any discharge into the navigable waters”— and the scope of certification—that “such discharge will comply with the applicable provisions of sections 1311, 1312, 1313, 1316, and 1317 of [the CWA].” 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1). This interpretation is supported by the remainder of Section 401, which repeatedly links the certification program to assuring compliance with the same list of CWA provisions, id. § 1341(a)(1), (a)(3), (a)(4), (a)(5), and by the CWA’s regulatory scheme of reducing water pollution through restrictions on point-source discharges, Cnty. of Maui v. Haw. Wildlife Fund, 140 S. Ct. 1462, 1468 (2020). The 2023 Rule instead relied on the term “applicant” and the phrase “any other appropriate requirement of State law” in Section 401(d). But Section 401(d) must be read together with the express terms of Section 401(a)(1), and even on its own terms, Section 401(d) cabins the meaning of “applicant” and “State law” in important ways. 33 U.S.C. § 1341(d). Nor does the Supreme Court’s decision in PUD No. 1 require adherence to the 2023 Rule’s flawed interpretation. As the 2023 Rule acknowledged, the Court did not identify an unambiguous meaning of the relevant statutory terms and instead deferred to the 1971 regulation as “reasonable.” 88 Fed. Reg. at 66,594. Second, Section 401(d) is best read as allowing States to grant certification by including conditions necessary to enforce compliance with the CWA, including State law requirements adopted pursuant to the CWA. Section 401(d) refers to compliance with “section[s] 1311,” “1312,” “1316,” and “1317 of this title,” all of which impose federal restrictions on discharges enforced by EPA or the States. 33 U.S.C. § 1341(d).
