EPA, 573 U.S. 302, 324 (2014)). 57 Proposed Rule Economic Analysis at 11-12. 58 91 Fed. Reg. at 2026 (quoting Sackett, 598 U.S. at 679). 59 See Sackett, 598 U.S. at 679 (“Regulation of land and water use lies at the core of traditional state authority.”); see also 33 U.S.C. 1251(b) (“It is the policy of the Congress to recognize, preserve, and protect the primary responsibilities and rights of States to prevent, reduce, and eliminate pollution, to plan the development and use (including restoration, preservation, and enhancement) of land and water resources, and to consult with the Administrator in the exercise of his authority under this chapter.”). 60 91 Fed. Reg. at 2017. 61 Id. at 2021. 12 Precluding states from having more substantial public input processes or informational requirements than EPA’s list is inconsistent with the best reading of the Clean Water Act, in at least two respects. First, section 510 of the Act broadly provides that nothing in the law limits state authority to regulate their waters more stringently than the federal government, saying: “Except as expressly provided in this chapter, nothing in this chapter shall … be construed as impairing or in any manner affecting any right or jurisdiction of the States with respect to the waters (including boundary waters) of such States.” 62 Second, section 401 itself leaves it to certifying authorities to “establish procedures for public notice in the case of all applications for certification by it and, to the extent it deems appropriate, procedures for public hearings in connection with specific applications.” 63 Because the information necessary to provide meaningful public notice of projects’ impacts to waters will vary from state to state, this directive necessarily includes the authority to specify the details of what constitutes a triggering “request for certification.” EPA suggests that it may limit certifying authorities’ section 401 procedures pursuant to section 501 of the Clean Water Act, but that is far from clear. That provision authorizes EPA “to prescribe such regulations as are necessary to carry out his functions” under the Act, 64 and EPA has made no showing that these restrictions are needed for it to perform its limited duties under the law. EPA also points to a Second Circuit ruling for support, 65 but even that decision – which ruled that a state cannot delay the beginning of the “reasonable period” by deeming a request that complies with state requirements “incomplete” – does not prevent a state from specifying a list of required elements for a communication to constitute a “request for certification,” as EPA explained in 2023. In adopting the provisions that the agency now proposes to remove, EPA discussed the Second Circuit’s ruling in detail: In NYSDEC, the Second Circuit never addressed the separate question of whether EPA or certifying authorities have the underlying authority to establish—in advance of receiving a request for certification—a list of required contents for such a request. Accordingly, the court’s holding that the reasonable period of time begins after “receipt” does not preclude the Agency from establishing such a list of minimum “request for certification” requirements, or from allowing certifying authorities to add requirements to EPA’s list or develop their own lists of request requirements. Because the statute does not define the term “request for certification,” EPA and other certifying authorities may do so in a reasonable manner that establishes—in advance of receiving the request—a discernable and predictable set of requirements for a request for certification that starts the reasonable period of time. No court has considered this issue and come to the opposite conclusion. The Agency decides, consistent with principles of cooperative federalism enshrined in the Act, to continue this lawful, familiar, and time-tested practice. 66 62 33 U.S.C. § 1370; see also 40 C.F.R. § 123.1(i)(1) (EPA regulations regarding state NPDES programs; specifying that “[n]othing in this part precludes a State from … [a]dopting or enforcing requirements which are more stringent or more extensive than those required under this part”). 63 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1). 64 33 U.S.C. § 1361(a). 65 N.Y. State Dep’t of Envtl. Conservation v. FERC, 884 F.3d 450, 455–56 (2d Cir. 2018). 66 See 88 Fed. Reg. at 66,577. 13 The existing rules’ approach, therefore, aligns with the statute’s best reading and is not in conflict with the Second Circuit’s decision. Beyond violating the best reading of the statute, EPA’s proposed restrictions on certifying authorities’ ability to manage their certification processes are arbitrary and capricious. EPA fails to provide an adequate, much less a convincing, reason for limiting what certifying authorities can require as part of a “request for certification”; to the contrary, the agency freely acknowledges that certifying authorities will commonly need more than the minimal information that the agency would say constitutes a sufficient request to initiate the certification process. EPA then offers a nonsensical explanation for starting the time for certifying authorities to act based on a paucity of information: “It is important to distinguish between the amount of information appropriate to start the certifying authority’s reasonable period of time and the amount of information that may be necessary for the certifying authority to take final action on a request for certification.” 67 This argument fails because the statutory period of time can only be considered “reasonable” to the extent that a certifying authority has the capacity to take the required action in such a time, but a certifying authority lacking all necessary information cannot reasonably act. Similarly, EPA’s proposal to eliminate the existing requirement to provide information on the approximate dates on which activity and discharge will occur, ostensibly because that might not be known to the applicant, lacks an adequate explanation. The timing of work on a project can be critical to a proper assessment of its impacts on waters; ecosystems can be affected differently depending on when a discharge will occur or when work in or near waterways happens. This is a critical piece of information that certifying authorities need to have in order to make an informed decision, so it is arbitrary to adopt rules that start the clock on decision-making without such detail. V. EPA’s Proposed Limits on What Counts as a “Water Quality Requirement” Are Unlawful. In addition to limiting states’ and Tribes’ overall scope of certification, EPA proposes to narrow the kinds of water quality related requirements on which certifying authorities can base certification decisions. EPA specifically says that only those state and Tribal requirements that regulate point source discharges into waters of the United States may be used. 68 Relatedly, EPA says that “if a State law addresses nonpoint source discharges or discharges to non-Federal waters, both of which are not within the proposed scope of certification, they are still not factors the State may consider when acting on certification requests.” 69 EPA’s proposed restrictions cannot be squared with the best reading of the law. As the agency explained just a few years ago: [T]he text is not limited to certifying compliance with provisions addressing point source discharges. Section 401(d) includes the phrase “any other appropriate requirement of State law.” 33 U.S.C. 1341(d) (emphasis added). The phrase ‘‘any other appropriate” 67 91 Fed. Reg. at 2018. 68 Id. at 2026-27. 69 Id. at 2027. 14 bears examination. The word “any” is capacious in its scope, literally meaning “all” such state law requirements and not just a limited subset such as point source-related requirements. See Ali v. Federal Bureau of Prisons, 552 U.S. 214 (2008); Harrison v. PPG Industries, 446 U.S. 578 (1980). The word “other” refers to requirements aside from the statutory provisions listed in the preceding list in section 401(d) (CWA section 301, etc.). While the word “appropriate” provides a limiting principle with respect to which requirements may be considered and applied, the word “appropriate” is to be interpreted broadly in light of the statute’s text and purpose. Michigan v. EPA, 576 U.S. 743, 752 (2015) (stating that “appropriate” is a broad and all-encompassing term that naturally and traditionally includes consideration of all the relevant factors). In this context, the phrase “any other appropriate” is best understood as allowing certifying authorities to consider state or Tribal laws regarding water quality that are not part of the enumerated list of CWA sections and that address water quality protections that are different from those covered by the enumerated list. See also PUD No. 1, 511 U.S. at 713 (declining to speculate on the scope of state laws that would be included in the phrase “any other appropriate requirement of state law” but finding that, “at a minimum, limitations imposed pursuant to state water quality standards adopted pursuant to § 303 are ‘appropriate’ requirements of state law”). 70 Similarly, EPA has previously rejected a reading of the statute relying on the interpretive canon ejusdem generis – as the agency proposes to in this rulemaking – to limit what state and Tribal requirements certifying authorities may invoke to make certification decisions. EPA claims now that the phrase “any other appropriate requirement of state law” follows a list of Clean Water Act provisions that regulate point source discharges, so it must also be limited to such requirements, but the agency itself explained why that is not the best reading of the Act: Application of the maxim ejusdem generis (“of the same kind”) to limit “appropriate requirement of State law” to only those state law provisions that impose discharge-related or point source-related restrictions is misplaced. The list of CWA provisions referenced in sections 401(a)(1) and 401(d) includes section 303, which is not limited to regulating point-source discharges. Section 303 concerns establishment of water quality standards, identification of waters that do not meet those standards, and establishment of daily maximum pollutant loads for such waters, all of which go well beyond regulation of point source discharges. Considering the breadth of section 303, using ejusdem generis to interpret “any other appropriate requirement of State law” to only apply to point sources is not consistent with congressional intent as expressed through the statutory text. 71 An example helps illustrate the unlawfulness of EPA’s proposed restriction. If a state’s water quality standards specify that a stream’s designated uses include fishing, that state may wish to condition its certification of a dam or other obstruction on the project proponent ensuring fish passage as part of the project’s design. EPA’s proposal likely would deem such a condition to be beyond the state’s authority, but the Act would clearly permit it (as would the existing regulations), because that condition would directly implement the state’s designated use. 70 88 Fed. Reg. at 66,603. 71 Id. (footnotes omitted). 15 EPA’s proposed approach is also arbitrary and capricious. As with much of the rest of its proposal, EPA does not offer a reasonable policy justification for curtailing state and Tribal authority in this respect. Worse, the proposal is objectively bad policy that is inconsistent with the Act’s objective. For instance, by prohibiting certifying authorities from continuing to evaluate a project’s impacts on water bodies that are not considered “waters of the United States,” EPA would take away a critical tool that states and Tribes have for mitigating the harm to wetlands, streams, and other waters that will occur because of pollution and destruction of non-federally regulated waters. These harms will become worse in the wake of the Supreme Court’s Sackett decision and the administration’s planned (and unlawful) additional rollbacks of the federal regulations because of pollution and destruction of non-federally regulated waters. EPA does not provide any reason why it would deny states and Tribes the ability to address the predictable degradation such projects would cause or why it would walk away from the federal government’s post-Sackett policy of using available tools to protect aquatic resources without regard to their legal status. 72 Finally, EPA seeks comment on two additional restrictions on what may be considered “any other appropriate requirement of State law,” namely limiting “water quality requirements” to just numeric water criteria and concluding that section 401(d)’s use of the term “other appropriate requirement of State law” is limited to monitoring requirements. Both of these ideas directly contradict the plain language, and thus the best reading, of the Act. As EPA previously acknowledged (and as discussed above), at a bare minimum, state and Tribal water quality standards under section 303 of the Act can be the basis of certification decisions and state water quality standards include much more than either numeric criteria or monitoring requirements. They include designated uses, narrative criteria, and antidegradation policies, to name a few. VI. EPA May Not Declare That Certain Kinds of Discharges Categorically Would Not Affect Water Quality in Other Jurisdictions. EPA proposes to authorize itself to determine that certain categories of discharges would not trigger the agency’s obligation to notify other states and Tribes of a pending project, on the grounds that there are categories for which no discharge “may affect … the quality of the waters of any other State….” 73 EPA does not propose specific categories that it would choose to ignore, nor commit to specifying such categories by regulation at a later date; to the contrary, “the 72 See, e.g., The America the Beautiful Freshwater Challenge: A Partnership to Conserve and Restore America’s Rivers, Lakes, Streams, and Wetlands (April 2024) (“Participants in the America the Beautiful Freshwater Challenge agree to lead by example and use all of the tools at their disposal to protect freshwater resources,” including, among other things, “[c]onsidering wetlands, waterways, and aquatic habitats scientifically as features with physical and biological characteristics that merit protection, conservation, and restoration of function, service, and integrity regardless of their location, legal status, or political jurisdiction”), https://bidenwhitehouse.archives.gov/wp- content/uploads/2024/04/America-the-Beautiful-Freshwater-Challenge.pdf; Memorandum from Michael L. Connor, Assistant Sec’y Army (Civil Works) to Commanding General, U.S. Army Corps Eng’rs, at (Mar. 22, 2024) (“All our Nation’s waters and wetlands are valuable resources, regardless of jurisdictional status under the Act. *** As a result, this memorandum directs the Corps, in carrying out its Civil Works missions, to use applicable authorities and available resources to engage in specific actions to protect, restore, and enhance our Nation’s waters and wetlands that are now more vulnerable, in addition to implementing the Regulatory Program in compliance with all laws and regulations.”), https://api.army.mil/e2/c/downloads/2024/03/21/a3846e04/memo-on-cw-actions-on-waters-and- wetlands-22-march-2024.pdf. 73 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(2). 16 Agency is not proposing to codify specific categorical determinations but rather merely proposing to acknowledge the development of categorical determinations in regulatory text.” 74 For starters, the idea of categorical treatment seems to be a solution in search of a problem. Aside from a single example (projects in the U.S. Virgin Islands), EPA does not identify any category of activity that it can say with confidence will not affect water quality in another state or authorized Tribe’s territory. Moreover, the agency all but admits that categorical treatment is impossible, because it would need to undertake case-by-case review in some instances: “The Agency emphasizes that projects may not always be subject to the categorical review process, even in instances where they meet the criteria for that category. In keeping with the Agency’s sole discretion to determine factors for a may affect analysis, the Agency may determine that other factors or considerations require closer analysis.” 75 Even assuming EPA has a good reason to pursue categorical treatment, however, the agency’s proposed approach of not including the categories in its regulations is procedurally invalid. Categorical treatment is rulemaking, and the Administrative Procedure Act requires that rulemaking must follow prescribed steps, including public notice and an opportunity for comment. 76 Otherwise, EPA would be able to create ad hoc categories without demonstrating that covered activities have no plausible impact on other jurisdictions’ waters, and evade its statutory duties. VII. EPA Lacks Authority to Deny Tribes the Ability to Gain Treatment as a State Status for Section 401 Alone. EPA proposes to delete the existing regulations’ provisions setting out the process for obtaining “treatment in a similar manner as a state” (TAS) status for section 401, which would force Tribes that are interested in acting as certifying authorities to obtain TAS for the section 303(c) water quality standards program. Additionally, the agency proposes to eliminate the ability of Tribes to get TAS for the limited purpose of acting as an affected state under section 401(a)(2). The proposal is unlawful. First, the best reading of the Clean Water Act demonstrates that EPA may not erect unnecessary burdens to Tribes obtaining TAS for selected sections of the Act. Congress directed in section 518 of the Act that Tribes be given the opportunity to obtain TAS “for purposes of title II and sections 104, 106, 303, 305, 308, 309, 314, 319, 401, 402, 404, and 406” of the Act. 77 By enumerating these provisions individually, without any suggestion that authority to administer one depends on the authority to administer another, Congress made clear that each program should be considered separately. The Act also specifies that a Tribe seeking TAS must show that it “is reasonably expected to be capable, in the Administrator’s judgment, of carrying out the 74 91 Fed. Reg. at 2034; see also id. at 2041 (proposed 40 C.F.R. § 121.12(a), providing: “Within 30 days after the Administrator receives notice in accordance with § 121.11(a), the Administrator shall determine either categorically or on a case-by-case basis whether a discharge from the project may affect water quality in another State.”). 75 Id. at 2034 (emphasis added). 76 See 5 U.S.C. 551(4) (defining “rule” as “the whole or part of an agency statement of general or particular applicability and future effect designed to implement, interpret, or prescribe law or policy”); id. 553 (specifying required processes for rulemaking). 77 33 U.S.C. § 1377(e). 17 functions to be exercised” to be approved, 78 which shows that Congress intended the demonstration to be function-specific. EPA tellingly does not claim that it is necessary to make the TAS demonstration for section 303(c) in order to gain TAS for section 401 alone – only that it is sufficient to do so. Because administering an entire water quality standards program could be significantly more resource-intensive than administering a section 401 certification program, EPA cannot require Tribes to demonstrate the ability to do the former in order to be authorized to do the latter. Additionally, EPA’s justification for its revisions to Tribal rights under the regulations is arbitrary and capricious. EPA does not explain how requiring Tribes to show the capacity to run a section 303(c) water quality standards program is a better policy than authorizing the section 401-only TAS option. In particular, the agency does not now address the numerous reasons that EPA and commenters identified and expressed support for adopting the section 401-only approach: These commenters supported the inclusion of section 401 TAS provisions for various reasons including interest in supporting Tribal agency, increasing Tribal participation in Federal licensing and permitting processes, providing Tribes a tool for protecting water quality and treaty rights, recognizing the vast knowledge of Tribal communities and their sovereignty, respecting the role waters play in Tribal cultures, and affording Tribes more options regarding administration of CWA programs. 79 Similarly, EPA provides no explanation why it believes it is good policy to change its position on Tribes receiving TAS for purposes of section 401(a)(2) and eliminate that stand-alone authority. Rather, the primary reason the agency gives for this element of the proposal is that it now disagrees with its prior conclusion that the roles of neighboring jurisdictions and certifying authorities are different, thus making different TAS requirements appropriate. 80 But this naked assertion fails because the functions are obviously different; most importantly, a certifying authority has direct regulatory power to impose limitations on a project to protect water quality, whereas a neighboring jurisdiction only has the authority to recommend such limitations. VIII. Section 401 Certification Applies to General Permits. EPA solicits comment on a radical additional revision of the section 401 regulations, namely interpreting the Act to deny states and Tribes the authority to review the requirements of, and make certification decisions on, general permits. The only basis for this possible change, according to EPA, is an argument that general permits do not have – when they are developed and issued – identified “applicants,” and section 401 requires any “applicant” for a federal permit to obtain certification. 81 This interpretation is flawed; while this language certainly puts the onus 78 Id. § 1377(e)(3) (emphasis added). 79 88 Fed. Reg. at 66,653. 80 EPA also suggests that its proposal is bolstered by the fact that section 518(e) does not specifically provide authority for TAS for subsection (a)(2), but refers to section 401 as a whole. 91 Fed. Reg. at 2037, n. 54. This argument fails. EPA evidently already understands that the inclusion of individual sections of the Act in section 518(e) does not preclude separate TAS requirements for subsections of listed sections, because it has separate TAS regulations for subsection 303(c) and subsection 303(d). See 40 C.F.R. §§ 131.8 (regarding subsection 303(c)) & 130.16 (regarding subsection 303(d)). 81 91 Fed. Reg. at 2020-21. 18 on an applicant for an individual permit to obtain certification, it does not preclude certification for general permits. Moreover, as EPA obliquely acknowledges, this specific interpretation was rejected as contrary to the plain language of the statute by the U.S. Court of Appeals for the First Circuit in U.S. v Marathon Development Corp., in which the court said: The last sentence of section 401(a)(1) provides, “No license or permit shall be granted if certification has been denied by the State....” 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1) (emphasis added). *** At oral argument defendants' counsel said that he read the sentence as still referring to “applicants.” But if that were the case, the sentence would be pure surplusage, adding nothing to the meaning of the section. Instead, we interpret the sentence as meaning exactly what it says: that no license or permit—whether individual or general—shall be granted if the state has denied certification. 82 Accordingly, the best reading of the statute is that general permits require certification. Exempting general permits from section 401 would also be arbitrary and capricious. EPA identifies no policy reason, much less a good one, that would justify denying states and Tribes the ability to protect their waters from activities covered by general permits. And doing so would have far-reaching consequences, as the vast majority of federally permitted activities now subject to section 401 are authorized by general permits. EPA’s Economic Analysis for the proposal illustrates this dramatically with the following figure: 83 Although EPA states that these data may not exactly reflect the number of certification decisions that occur annually, it certainly illustrates that exempting general permits – which represent a 82 867 F2d 96, 101 (1st Cir. 1989); see also Keating v. FERC, 927 F.2d 616, 619 (D.C. Cir. 1991) (describing availability of Army Corps’ individual and general permits and stating, “[r]egardless of which route is followed, however, the Corps cannot issue a permit under section 404 without first obtaining state certification pursuant to section 401 from the state in which the activity is to take place”). 83 Proposed Rule Economic Analysis at 11. 19 whopping 94% (53,341 of 56,841) of average annual federal permits – would disempower states and Tribes enormously. EPA also seeks comment on whether there are reliance interests at stake if it were to exempt general permits from certification. Of course there are. Just considering the Army Corps’ section 404 nationwide permits, the certification process is how many states and Tribes ensure that specific general permits will not cause undue harm to their waters. Moreover, the Army Corps itself relies on certifying authorities’ review as part of its analysis of whether a given nationwide permit complies with the section 404(b)(1) Guidelines. 84 In sum, EPA must not upend decades of properly requiring general permits to be subject to certification. IX. Conclusion EPA’s proposed weakening of state and Tribal authority under section 401 is illegal and thoroughly poor public policy. It should abandon its dangerous direction and withdraw this proposal. 84 See, e.g., U.S. Army Corps of Eng’rs, Decision Document: Nationwide Permit 12, at 119 (Jan. 2026) (“Water quality certification is required for discharges of dredged or fill material into waters of the United States authorized by this NWP, which will help ensure that the discharge complies with applicable water quality requirements.”), https://usace.contentdm.oclc.org/utils/getfile/collection/p16021coll9/id/3161. 20

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the proposed section 401 water quality certification rule and responds to many of the specific issues raised in the proposal, including scope of certification, request requirements, water quality requirements, categorical determinations, Tribal TAS, and general permits.
rationales.reasoning: The arguments are highly coherent and logically developed, with clear section-by-section analysis, statutory interpretation, and APA/arbitrary-and-capricious reasoning. The letter consistently ties claims back to legal text and prior agency positions.
rationales.evidence: The comment is heavily supported by citations to the Clean Water Act, Supreme Court and appellate cases, Federal Register notices, EPA guidance, prior EPA pleadings, and concrete examples. It uses substantial legal and factual support throughout.
rationales.impact: The letter explains likely impacts in specific and plausible terms, such as weakened state and Tribal authority, reduced protection for wetlands and non-federally regulated waters, and major consequences for general permits. Some impact claims are broad, but they are well grounded.
rationales.actionability: It clearly requests that EPA abandon or withdraw the proposal and identifies why specific provisions should not be finalized. However, it is more focused on opposing the rule than on offering alternative regulatory language or detailed replacement provisions.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is professionally formatted, well organized with numbered sections and subheadings, and generally easy to follow despite its length. Citations and examples are integrated in a disciplined way.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, comprehensive, and legally sophisticated public comment that directly engages the proposal and supports its objections with extensive authority. Its main limitation is that it is primarily oppositional rather than solution-oriented, but overall quality is excellent.

overall_summary: A highly relevant and persuasive comment letter with extensive legal citations, strong internal logic, and clear engagement with the proposal’s major issues. It is exceptionally well structured and evidence-rich, though it offers limited affirmative regulatory alternatives beyond urging EPA to withdraw the rule.

comment_text:
I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive and focuses on the requested issues of safety, transparency, and labeling.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with clear claims about consumer deception, process concerns, and allergenicity. Some assertions are broad or not fully substantiated, but the logic is understandable.
rationales.evidence: The letter includes a few concrete technical references, such as Saccharomyces cerevisiae and specific genes, but provides little factual support, data, or citations for the safety and allergenicity claims.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies plausible consumer and allergen concerns, but the magnitude of the risks is not quantified and the claimed harms remain somewhat speculative.
rationales.actionability: It offers specific requests, including proteomic analysis, clearer labeling standards, and allergen warnings, which are feasible in principle and easy for an agency to understand.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is well organized with numbered sections, clear headings, and a professional tone, making it easy to follow.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and well-structured comment with clear policy asks, but it is weakened by limited evidence and somewhat speculative safety claims.

overall_summary: A focused and readable comment that strongly engages the docket topic and proposes concrete labeling and testing requirements. Its main weakness is the lack of supporting evidence or citations for the safety and allergenicity concerns.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive and focuses on the requested issues of safety, transparency, and labeling.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with clear claims about natural labeling, synthetic biology, and allergenicity, though some assertions are stated more strongly than they are demonstrated.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes a few concrete technical references, such as Saccharomyces cerevisiae and specific genes, but provides no citations, data, or empirical studies to support the safety and allergenicity claims.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible consumer and allergen concerns, but the magnitude and likelihood of the harms are not quantified or supported with specific examples or evidence.
rationales.actionability: The letter offers clear requests, including proteomic analysis, labeling standards, and allergen warnings, which are feasible in principle and easy for the agency to understand.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is well organized with numbered sections, clear headings, and a formal tone, making it readable and professional.
rationales.overall: This is a focused and well-structured comment with clear policy asks, but it would be stronger with citations, data, and more substantiated claims about safety and consumer harm.

overall_summary: A relevant and organized comment that clearly opposes synthetic-biology-produced beetroot red on transparency and safety grounds. Its main weakness is limited evidentiary support for the more serious claims, though it does provide actionable recommendations.

comment_text:
The current Freight Broker/Carrier marketplace is a highly competitive and transparent marketplace which is vastly different from the marketplace and exiting practices from over 40 years ago when 371.3 was implemented.  

Expansion of 371.3 is based on an outdated, burdensome and government overreach. 
Formalized in 1980 when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, not how the market currently operates. 
The intent was to have financial transparency between all parties to a brokered load to ensure the illegal practice of freight rebating was not occurring, where a broker and shipper were common owners. 
The current marketplace involves two separate business transactions between the shipper and broker and the broker and carrier. The market is highly competitive, transparent, and benefits all parties, including shippers and end-consumers.

The transportation marketplace is already very transparent with load boards and other technology pricing tools. 
FMCSA should focus on highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic that is costing the supply-chain over $1 billion annually. 
The FMCSA already has a prohibition of coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers. 

When a consumer walks into a retail shop, the price displayed on the price tag is the price at which the consumer can purchase that product.  Nowhere does it show the price that the retail store purchased the product from the supplier. NOWHERE.  If the consumer does not like the price, they are free to seek a cheaper price or a different product of their choosing.  

This ruling defies capitalism. You should not have to see my margins to determine if I am the right business partner. Carriers have the freedom to not use brokers or move on to different brokers until they feel the rate is desirable.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses broker/carrier transparency and appears responsive to a rule about broker compensation or disclosure under 371.3, but it does not precisely engage with specific docket questions or regulatory text.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally understandable and follows a clear position, but it relies on broad assertions and analogies rather than a tightly developed legal or policy analysis.
rationales.evidence: It includes very few concrete facts and no citations. The only specific quantitative claim is the reference to freight fraud costing the supply chain over $1 billion annually, but this is unsupported in the comment.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies potential burdens on brokers and suggests market transparency already exists, but the claimed harms are stated in general terms and are not substantiated with detailed examples or measurable impacts.
rationales.actionability: It implicitly asks FMCSA not to expand 371.3 and to focus on safety and fraud instead, but it does not provide a precise alternative rule language or a detailed requested action.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is readable and mostly organized into short paragraphs, but it is somewhat repetitive, uses emphatic capitalization, and lacks the polish of a more formal submission.
rationales.overall: This is a clear opposition comment with a relevant policy stance, but it is stronger on advocacy than on evidence, specificity, or actionable regulatory recommendations.

overall_summary: The comment is relevant and easy to follow, but it is largely argumentative and rhetorical, with limited evidence, modest policy specificity, and only general requests to the agency.

comment_text: From: mcgladdery3@everyactioncustom.com To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/13/2026 4:36:56 PM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 ------------------------------------------ [You don't often get email from mcgladdery3@everyactioncustom.com. Learn why this is important at https://aka.ms/LearnAboutSenderIdentification ] Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Dear Administrator Lee Zeldin, - Waterways and wetlands will be at risk due to this proposed rule. - Changing this part of the Clean Water Act betrays the idea of federal and state cooperation, which is foundational to America’s system of government. - This limits states’ and Tribes’ opportunities to prioritize their specific needs to protect their water. - Weakening Section 401 undermines the will of Americans who want clean water protections. - Shortening project reviews does not allow states and Tribes adequate time to thoroughly assess projects that may increase pollution. - Stop this plan to harm the Great Lakes! The Great Lakes, the greatest system of surface freshwater in the world, rely on wetlands, rivers, and streams to filter pollution, prevent flooding in our communities, and support fishing and recreation. It is critical that the U.S. EPA sticks to its mission to enforce environmental laws and defend, not degrade, the Clean Water Act. The proposed rule change to Section 401 seeks to take control away from states and Tribes, thus loosening the clean water protections strongly supported by most Americans regardless of political affiliation. I am especially concerned that further weakening of clean water protections will harm waters in the Great Lakes, a vital resource that tens of millions of residents depend upon for drinking water and our economy. This proposed rule change will further strip protections of wetlands, which provide significant regional economic benefits, such as: - Supporting recreational activities that bring in $50 billion annually to the region - Filtering out pollution to keep Great Lakes water clean for drinking and recreation - Controlling flooding, reducing damage and costs to communities and homes For the reasons of popular national support, regional economic implications, the official mission of the U.S. EPA, and in support of states and Tribal rights, I urge you to fully enforce the Clean Water Act and maintain the authority of states and Tribes to protect clean water. Sincerely, Martin and Sharon Farmington Hills, MI

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 and focuses on Section 401, state and Tribal authority, wetlands, and Great Lakes water protections, which are clearly tied to the likely docket topic.
rationales.reasoning: The letter presents a generally coherent argument that weakening Section 401 would reduce state and Tribal review time and protections, but the logic is mostly asserted rather than developed in depth.
rationales.evidence: It includes one concrete economic figure ($50 billion annually) and some specific references to wetlands and Great Lakes functions, but provides no citations, sources, or detailed supporting data.
rationales.impact: The claimed impacts are plausible and specific in broad terms, such as increased pollution risk, flooding, and reduced protections for drinking water and recreation, though the magnitude is not well substantiated.
rationales.actionability: The comment clearly asks EPA to stop the plan, fully enforce the Clean Water Act, and maintain state and Tribal authority, but it does not offer more detailed or alternative policy recommendations.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is readable and organized as a short advocacy comment with bullet points and a closing request, though it is somewhat repetitive and not especially polished.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and understandable public comment that strongly opposes the proposed rule, but it relies more on advocacy language than detailed evidence or nuanced analysis.

overall_summary: A directly relevant, clearly opposed comment with decent policy focus and some concrete impact claims, but limited evidence and only moderate analytical depth.

comment_text: S TATE OF M ICHIGAN DEPARTMENT OF ENVIRONMENT, GREAT LAKES, AND ENERGY W ATER R ESOURCES D IVISION GRETCHEN WHITMER PHILLIP D. ROOS GOVERNOR DIRECTOR February 17, 2026 VIA E-MAIL Lee Zeldin, Administrator U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Office of the Administrator OW-Docket@epa.gov Dear Lee Zeldin: Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929: Comments on the Proposed Rule Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations The Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy (EGLE), Water Resources Division, submits the following comments regarding the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s (USEPA) proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 rule published in the Federal Register on January 15, 2026. EGLE is the certifying authority for the State of Michigan under CWA Section 401. Clean water is a critical issue to the State of Michigan. Michigan’s public health and economy, including tourism, agriculture, and major industries, are dependent on the Great Lakes and clean water. EGLE supports the provisions of CWA Section 401 as the basis for building a collaborative state and federal partnership in Michigan and other states to protect and improve water quality and support the designated uses of Michigan’s Great Lakes and surface waters. The current activity-based implementing regulations found at 40 CFR Part 121 allow the State of Michigan and federal agencies to realize the positive potential of this partnership under the principles of cooperative federalism and exercise the state authorities instilled by Congress to ensure that discharges authorized by federal agencies comply with state law. EGLE does not support the proposed update of the rule. Confining the scope of certification, limiting the type of conditions included in the certification, and other proposed changes are unnecessary and will have negative consequences for the health of the nation’s waters. Moreover, EGLE believes the 30-day comment period is inadequate. The current CWA Section 401 rule is effective and works well in Michigan. EGLE has not experienced problems in the implementation of the activity-based scope of certification since the current regulations took effect, and maintains there is not a compelling reason to revise the current rule. The opening sentence in CWA Section 401(b) says “Nothing in this section shall be construed to limit the authority of any department or agency pursuant to any other provision of law to require compliance with any applicable water quality requirements.” Section 401(d) follows that broad statement DEBORAH A. STABENOW BUILDING • 525 WEST ALLEGAN STREET • P.O. BOX 30458 • LANSING, MICHIGAN 48909-7958 Michigan.gov/EGLE • 800-662-9278 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Page 2 February 17, 2026 by mandating, in part, “Any certification provided under this section shall set forth any effluent limitations and other limitations, . . . and with any other appropriate requirement of State law set forth in such certification, and shall become a condition on any Federal license or permit subject to the provisions of this section.” Such clear language indicates that certification conditions may be based on appropriate state and tribal water quality requirements applicable to any water quality impacts from an activity within the breadth of the CWA’s subject matter. Narrowing the scope of certification and leaving other sources of water pollution from an activity uncontrolled will result in harm to the quality of the nation’s waters and is contrary to the purpose of the CWA. EGLE also does not support many of the other proposed changes, for example, replacing the term “project proponent” with the term “applicant” and limiting state and tribal water quality requirements to numeric standards or monitoring requirements. As implied in the Preamble, implementing such changes could be argued to exclude federal agency projects and the issuance of federal general licenses and permits from the 401 rule. A final rule that requires federal general license and permit applicants to seek individual certification would subject thousands of Michigan residents, small businesses, and others to new delays and regulatory burdens annually. The consequences of these and other proposed changes are significant and substantial and would harm the current, successful state and federal cooperative approach toward protecting water quality under the CWA framework. The activity-based CWA Section 401 rule embodies a sincere and effective application of the principles of cooperative federalism. It has been in effect for more than two years without significant implementation challenges. Any present or future implementation problems should be addressed by USEPA through the provision of technical assistance to federal agencies, certifying authorities, or applicants pursuant to CWA Section 401(b), as opposed to finalizing the proposed rule. If you have questions regarding this matter, please contact me at 517-331-7966; LoundsA@Michigan.gov; or EGLE, P.O. Box 30458, Lansing, Michigan 48909-7958. Sincerely, Amy Lounds, Assistant Division Director Water Resources Division cc: Anne Garwood, EGLE Lee Schoen, EGLE Matt Smar, EGLE Doug Bridges, EGLE

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the proposed CWA Section 401 rule revisions in the cited docket and responds to several specific changes EPA proposed.
rationales.reasoning: The letter presents a coherent argument that the current rule works well, that the proposed narrowing is inconsistent with the statute, and that implementation issues should be handled through technical assistance instead of rule revision.
rationales.evidence: It cites statutory language from CWA Section 401(b) and (d) and references Michigan's experience, but it provides limited empirical data, case examples, or external citations.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies plausible harms such as increased delays, regulatory burdens, and reduced water quality protection, though the impacts are described more qualitatively than quantitatively.
rationales.actionability: It clearly urges EPA not to finalize the proposed rule and suggests technical assistance as an alternative, but it does not offer detailed alternative regulatory text or step-by-step revisions.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is professionally formatted, clearly identifies the docket, states the position early, and follows a logical progression, though it is somewhat dense and repetitive in places.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, policy-relevant comment from an affected state agency with clear opposition, solid statutory grounding, and credible concerns, but it would be stronger with more concrete evidence and more detailed recommendations.

overall_summary: A well-targeted and professionally written agency comment that strongly opposes the proposed rule and explains why, but it relies more on legal interpretation and general assertions than on detailed evidence or specific alternative language.

comment_text:
I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive and focuses on the requested issues of safety, transparency, and labeling.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with clear claims about natural labeling, synthetic biology, and allergenicity. Some assertions are broad or speculative, but the overall logic is understandable.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes a few concrete technical references, such as Saccharomyces cerevisiae and specific genes, but it provides little factual support, no data, and no citations to studies or regulatory findings.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies plausible concerns about consumer deception and allergen exposure, but the magnitude and likelihood of harm are not well substantiated with specific examples or quantified impacts.
rationales.actionability: It offers clear requests, including proteomic analysis, labeling restrictions, and allergen warnings. These are specific enough to understand, though some are somewhat broad or difficult to implement as written.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is well organized with numbered sections, clear headings, and a professional tone. It is readable and easy to follow.
rationales.overall: This is a focused and well-structured comment with clear policy asks, but it relies more on assertion than evidence. It is strong on relevance and actionability, moderate on reasoning, and weaker on substantiation.

overall_summary: A relevant and organized comment that clearly opposes the proposed labeling approach and asks for stricter safety and disclosure requirements. Its main weakness is limited evidence and support for the more technical safety claims.

comment_text:
THE NATIONAL OWNER OPERATORS
ASSOCIATION6143 186th St, Fresh
Meadows, NY 11365
(646) 271-8288
info@tnooa.com
Tnooa.com
January 21st, 2025  
Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration  
U.S. Department of Transportation  
1200 New Jersey Avenue, SE  
Washington, DC 20590  
Subject: FMCSA-2023-0257-0001
To Whom It May Concern,  
     The National Owner Operators Association currently represents its members of over 40,000. We have direct experience in
broker-carrier relationships, and we are writing to express our full support for the enforcement and strengthening of 49 CFR
371.3. This regulation is critical for promoting fairness, transparency, and accountability within the transportation industry,
particularly in brokered general freight transactions. 
     The general freight brokerage sector currently suffers from systemic practices that disadvantage motor carriers, especially small
owner operators. Transparency under CFR 371.3 would combat exploitative schemes such as "salami slicing," where brokers
withhold small amounts across numerous transactions. While each withheld amount may seem minor, the aggregate impact
significantly harms carriers' financial stability.
     The current broker-carrier relationship is fundamentally imbalanced, with brokers controlling access to freight and
transactional details. Transparency requirements would address this imbalance by ensuring carriers have the information
necessary to negotiate fairly and protect their interests. Many carriers currently fear blacklisting or retaliatory actions when
requesting transaction records, and automatic disclosure would eliminate this risk. Do you notice within the comments
submitted how many were made anonymously? This is no coincidence. 
     This letter outlines the benefits of enforcing CFR 371.3, provides counterarguments to opposition raised by brokers, and
rebuts claims made by industry analysts and logistics companies.
     Opponents argue that the proposed rule serves no economic purpose, claiming it will not directly impact freight rates.
However, this argument misses the broader intent of fostering trust, fairness, and accountability in broker-carrier relationships.
The rule would empower carriers to verify transactions, identify discrepancies, and ensure equitable treatment.
Critics also suggest the rule imposes excessive administrative burdens. However, brokers are already required to maintain
transaction records under existing regulations. Modern transportation management systems can and do streamline
recordkeeping and automate disclosure, minimizing compliance costs. Brokers already interact with carriers on every load
electronically, as they do shippers as well.      A widespread issue we witness daily is when brokers engage in practices where small amounts of money—
detention fees, accessorial charges, tracking fees, lumper fees, fuel surcharges, quickpay fees, appointment fees,
layovers, tonu, chargebacks, or other compensations—are withheld from carriers. This practice, often referred to
as the "salami slicing" scheme, disproportionately impacts small carriers operating on thin margins. 
     By withholding funds this reduces carriers' ability to maintain equipment, ensure safety compliance, and pay
drivers competitive wages. Over time, this compromises their operational viability and safety. It is prevalent in
the industry right now. Carriers often face prolonged disputes over withheld payments. Mandatory transparency
ensures that documentation is readily available, expediting the resolution process.
      Brokers exploit this lack of oversight, leveraging systemic opacity to retain funds that rightfully belong to
carriers. While each instance might seem trivial or not worth the effort to dispute, the aggregate impact across
hundreds of thousands of transactions daily amounts to significant financial harm to carriers., the cumulative
effect across thousands of loads can be financially devastating to carriers. Brokers benefit significantly because
they exploit the lack of regulatory enforcement and carriers' limited resources to pursue small claims.
     Enforcing CFR 371.3 and mandating the automatic disclosure of transaction records would expose these
practices, empowering carriers to verify payments and seek rightful compensation.
    The existing power imbalance between brokers and carriers allows brokers to manipulate transactional terms
to their advantage. Transparency requirements are necessary to correct this imbalance.Brokers wield significant
control over access to freight, which leaves carriers with limited bargaining ability. This imbalance is
exacerbated by practices such as retaliatory blacklisting of carriers who request transparency. 
      Many carriers hesitate to request transaction records due to fear of retaliation, such as being blacklisted from
accessing future loads. Automatic disclosure would remove this risk, ensuring equitable treatment for all
carriers. Access to transactional data, including the shipper's payment to the broker, enables carriers to
negotiate more effectively, ensuring fair compensation for their services.
     Transparency is a cornerstone of accountability. Mandating disclosure of transaction records within 48 hours
ensures that brokers adhere to ethical practices and regulatory requirements. However, mandating automatic
disclosure of transaction records eliminates the need for carriers to request them, thereby removing the risk of
retaliation and ensuring equitable treatment.
    Requiring brokers to disclose records discourages fraudulent practices such as unauthorized deductions or
misrepresentation of accessorial charges. Without robust enforcement of CFR 371.3, unethical practices such as
inflated broker margins, fraudulent deductions, and unauthorized offsets will persist unchecked.
    Carriers would no longer face prolonged disputes over withheld payments, as they would have immediate
access to supporting documentation. As it stands currently, carriers are held liable for losses without knowing
the financial stakes or details of the shipper’s invoice.
Power Imbalance: Brokers control access to critical information while demanding carriers accept terms that
increase their financial exposure.1.
Lack of Recourse: In the event of disputes or claims, carriers are left to navigate liability under opaque terms,
without access to the very records that determine their obligations.2.      Some argue that brokers and carriers are price takers in a commoditized market. While freight pricing can resemble a
commodity market, brokers often exert significant control over pricing through their relationships with shippers and
carriers.
     Brokers provide services like logistics coordination, customer service, and technology integration, which allow them to
influence rates more than a typical price taker in a commodity market. Disclosing transactional details does not detract
from the value brokers bring; it ensures equitable compensation for carriers who bear the operational risks.
    Some highlight the variability in broker margins, arguing that transparency is unnecessary because brokers sometimes
lose money on loads.
    Margin variability underscores the importance of transparency to identify patterns of exploitation or excessive markups.
The dismissal of outliers, such as margins exceeding 40%, as "anomalies" further supports the need for regulatory
oversight.
   Claims that transparency will lead to deflationary pressure on carriers' rates are unfounded. As transparency does not
deflate rates but promotes fairer revenue sharing. Carriers with access to broker margins can negotiate better terms,
creating a more balanced market dynamic.
   We’ve seen suggestions that compliance will lead to market consolidation by increasing costs for brokers, these
suggestions are overstated.
      Modern technology, including TMS platforms, can streamline compliance at a reasonable cost. Transparent practices
could foster innovation and competition rather than consolidation.
    Brokers are already required to maintain transaction records under 49 CFR 371.3. The proposed rule merely ensures
these records are accessible upon request. We also know of programs existing that accommodate providing in a single
click, compliance with the cfr. 
   Concerns about data security are valid but not unique to broker transparency.
   Our industry already shares sensitive information, such as Bills of Lading (BOLs) and Rate Confirmations (Rate Cons), via
email. Transparency simply extends this framework to include shipper payment information. 
   The rule provides an opportunity to enhance data management practices industry-wide, ensuring confidentiality and
security while promoting fairness.
    Opponents claim the proposed rule serves no economic purpose because it will not directly impact freight rates. This
argument misses the broader intent of the rule—ensuring transparency and accountability.
    Transparency fosters trust and fairness in broker-carrier relationships, empowering carriers to verify transactions,
identify discrepancies, and ensure equitable treatment. While some brokers highlight market complexities and aggregation
challenges, they fail to acknowledge that transparency levels the playing field for smaller carriers lacking access to robust
data aggregation tools.
    Critics argue that brokers bear disproportionate financial risks, such as shipper bankruptcies and contractual liabilities.
While these risks are real, they are inherent to the brokerage business model and do not justify withholding financial
information from carriers.
    Carriers also contend with delayed payments, unexpected deductions, and chargebacks, making transparency vital for
ensuring they are not subjected to arbitrary or opaque practices.
The rule focuses on individual transactions, not overall broker profitability, enabling carriers to verify fair treatment and
ensure regulatory compliance.      Opposition claims that the rule contradicts Congressional intent to deregulate transportation. However, The Motor
Carrier Act of 1980 sought to remove unnecessary restrictions while maintaining protections for industry stakeholders.
Transparency requirements uphold these protections without stifling competition. Allowing brokers to use waivers
undermines the purpose of transparency and perpetuates inequities. Prohibiting waivers ensures compliance and
accountability.
      Opponents often dismiss the rule's potential to address illegal brokering. While fraudulent activities by imposters
require separate enforcement measures, the proposed rule addresses unethical practices by licensed brokers, such as
unauthorized deductions and hidden fees. 
     Requiring disclosure of transaction records helps carriers verify compliance, discouraging exploitative practices and
complementing broader enforcement efforts.
To maximize the benefits of CFR 371.3, May we recommend the following:
1. Mandatory Automatic Disclosure: Require brokers to provide transaction records automatically within 48 hours of load
completion, eliminating the need for carriers to request them.
2. Penalties for Non-Compliance: Establish strict penalties for brokers who fail to comply, deterring unethical practices.
3. Waiver Prohibition: Explicitly prohibit waivers of transparency requirements to prevent brokers from circumventing
regulations.
4. Protections Against Retaliation: Safeguard carriers from blacklisting or other retaliatory actions when exercising their
rights under CFR 371.3.
      The enforcement of CFR 371.3 is essential for addressing systemic inequities in the freight industry. Transparency will
deter unethical practices, foster accountability, and ensure fair compensation for carriers who play a critical role in the
supply chain. Opposition to these measures often reflects resistance to change rather than genuine concerns about
feasibility or economic impact.
      We urge the FMCSA to prioritize the enforcement of CFR 371.3 and adopt measures that strengthen its implementation.
Transparency is not just a regulatory obligation; it is a moral imperative to protect the backbone of the U.S. economy—the
trucking industry.
      Please find attached two documents pertaining to this critical issue. Interesting the TIA acknowledges freight fraud and
one of the solutions is requiring transparency. 
 One demonstrates the eloquent tactic of dissuasion if records are requested by a carrier. 
      The other we will point to page 7 : Safeguarding Shipments: “Freight fraud has crippled profitability for many freight
brokers and carriers, and if left unchecked, it could become a far more serious issue for shippers. Prevention is the goal.
Thoroughly vetting and auditing brokers and carriers, requiring transparency, building strategic partnerships, and leveraging
technology solutions will continue to bolster shipper defenses and benefit the transportation and logistics industry. With
proper precautions, shippers can limit their exposure and actively combat freight crime by implementing secure processes
and partnering with brokers and carriers with the same priorities. With collective vigilance, fraud and theft can be reduced.” 
     Thank you for your consideration. We are available to provide further insights or participate in discussions to support this
important initiative.
Sincerely,  
The National Owner Operators Association
Michael Boston - CEO      We are adding to further address the Transportation Intermediaries Association (TIA)’s opposition to the
proposed amendments to 49 CFR 371.3, as outlined in Docket FMCSA-2023-0257. While the TIA has
articulated concerns regarding potential economic inefficiencies and regulatory overreach, a detailed analysis
reveals that many of their assertions are either flawed or based on speculative scenarios that do not reflect the
realities faced by motor carriers, particularly small businesses and owner-operators. Some of these items are
referenced above, but they are worth reiteration. 
     The TIA argues that transparency undermines competition and market efficiency, claiming that disclosure
requirements would expose confidential business strategies. However, transparency fosters fairness and
accountability in broker-carrier relationships by mitigating information asymmetry. It is not an impediment to
market efficiency but a safeguard against unethical practices such as hidden fees, unauthorized deductions, and
systemic noncompliance with rate agreements. In their own document attached they openly suggest
transparency is needed to fight fraud, 
     Small motor carriers, which constitute the majority of the trucking industry, are often at a significant
disadvantage due to opaque brokerage practices. Current regulations under 49 CFR 371.3 already mandate
brokers to maintain transaction records, but the lack of enforcement has allowed practices such as salami slicing
—small, systematic underpayments that collectively amount to significant financial losses—to proliferate
unchecked. Transparency would empower carriers to verify that they are compensated fairly and would hold
brokers accountable for discrepancies, leveling the playing field.
     TIA’s concern that transparency will destabilize the market and lead to increased costs is unfounded. Similar
transparency measures have been implemented in other industries with net positive outcomes. For example, in
real estate transactions, both parties have access to pricing information, fostering trust and equitable
negotiations. Brokers in the freight industry often claim that they operate with narrow margins, yet resist
transparency measures that would validate these claims. If brokers’ assertions of fair margins and competitive
practices are accurate, transparency should pose no threat.
     TIA’s invocation of the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (DTSA) is a misapplication of the law. The financial
terms of individual transactions between brokers, shippers, and carriers do not meet the DTSA’s criteria for trade
secrets. The proposed regulation requires disclosure of transactional details only to the parties directly involved,
not public dissemination. The confidentiality of these records can be maintained through contractual agreements,
as is common in other industries. Furthermore, the argument that waivers should be permissible undermines the
very purpose of the regulation, creating loopholes that would perpetuate current inequities.
     TIA’s assertion that brokers bear disproportionate financial risks is misleading. While brokers may face
delayed shipper payments, carriers routinely encounter similar or greater risks, including unpaid invoices,
detention without compensation, and unexpected deductions. Brokers often mitigate these risks by charging
carriers quick-pay fees or utilizing factoring arrangements. By contrast, carriers have limited recourse when
brokers fail to honor contractual terms. Carriers have the option to factor their loads instead of using relying
brokers to pay them directly. This affords a broker to pay a factoring company at 30 days. The proposed rule
would provide carriers with access to transaction records, enabling them to validate charges and mitigate
financial uncertainties.
     TIA’s claim that the FMCSA should prioritize addressing freight fraud over implementing transparency
measures is a false dichotomy. Transparency and fraud prevention are complementary objectives. Enhanced
access to transaction records would deter unethical practices such as double brokering, unauthorized deductions,
and misrepresentation of rates. Furthermore, automatic record-sharing requirements within 48 hours of delivery
would streamline dispute resolution and reduce the administrative burden on carriers seeking to substantiate
claims.
        TIA’s cost projections for implementing electronic data-sharing systems are exaggerated. Many brokers
already utilize Transportation Management Systems (TMS) that can be adapted for compliance with minimal
additional investment. The assertion that compliance would cost brokers millions of dollars ignores
technological advancements and scalable solutions that can reduce implementation costs for both brokers and
carriers.
      The TIA’s argument that the proposed rule contradicts Congressional intent to deregulate transportation is
flawed. The Motor Carrier Act of 1980 and subsequent deregulation efforts aimed to eliminate unnecessary
restrictions while preserving essential protections for industry stakeholders. Transparency measures align
with these protections, ensuring fair practices without imposing burdensome constraints. The FMCSA’s
authority to enforce 49 CFR 371.3 derives from its mandate to protect carriers and shippers from unfair
practices, a mandate that remains relevant in today’s market.
      TIA’s commissioned report warns of potential economic disruptions but fails to account for the broader
benefits of transparency. The trucking industry’s ecosystem relies on trust and collaboration between brokers,
carriers, and shippers. Lack of transparency erodes this trust, leading to inefficiencies, disputes, and financial
losses that disproportionately affect small carriers. By enforcing transparency, the FMCSA would promote
equitable treatment, enhance market confidence, and ultimately contribute to a more resilient supply chain.
This report also states “It is true, however, that an effective regulation would provide more detailed ex-post
factor disclosure. Such disclosure might make resolving difficult-to-quantify disputes between carriers,
brokers and the many other actors in this play easier. Also, such disclosure would help expose those brokers
who practice fraud”
      The proposed amendments to 49 CFR 371.3 are not only justified but necessary to address longstanding
inequities in the freight industry. Transparency is a cornerstone of fair and efficient markets, and its
implementation would benefit all stakeholders by fostering accountability, reducing disputes, and enhancing
trust. The FMCSA must reject the TIA’s reasons for opposition and prioritize the interests of the broader
trucking community, particularly small carriers and owner-operators who form the backbone of the industry. 
     Once again, thank you for considering this perspective. we on behalf of over 40,000 members along with
other industry associations urge the FMCSA to move forward with the proposed amendments to CFR 371.3
and to enforce these regulations in a manner that ensures transparency, fairness, and accountability within the
transportation industry for ALL.

rationales.relevance: The comment is highly on-topic, directly addressing FMCSA-2023-0257 and the proposed enforcement/strengthening of 49 CFR 371.3, with repeated discussion of broker-carrier transparency and record disclosure.
rationales.reasoning: The letter presents a generally coherent position and responds to opposing arguments, but it is repetitive, occasionally circular, and sometimes makes broad assertions without tightly developed logic.
rationales.evidence: It includes some concrete references, such as 49 CFR 371.3, the Motor Carrier Act of 1980, DTSA, and an attached TIA document, but it lacks specific data, empirical studies, or detailed citations supporting its claims.
rationales.impact: The comment clearly describes plausible harms to small carriers, such as withheld fees, retaliation, and cash-flow strain, but the magnitude and frequency of these impacts are mostly asserted rather than demonstrated.
rationales.actionability: It offers clear recommendations, including automatic disclosure within 48 hours, penalties for noncompliance, waiver prohibition, and anti-retaliation protections, which are feasible and specific.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter has a recognizable opening, body, and recommendation section, but it is poorly edited, highly repetitive, and contains formatting issues, run-on passages, and some grammatical errors.
rationales.overall: This is a strongly relevant advocacy comment with clear policy asks, but its persuasive strength is limited by repetition, weak evidentiary support, and uneven organization.

overall_summary: A highly relevant and forceful comment letter advocating for stronger broker transparency under 49 CFR 371.3. It is most effective in its clear policy recommendations and direct engagement with the docket topic, but it would be stronger with better editing, more precise evidence, and less repetition.

comment_text: February 17, 2026 Jessica Kramer, Assistant Administrator Oﬃce of Water Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 VIA Federal eRulemaking Portal: https://www.regulations.gov. Re: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 Comments of Chelan County Public Utility District on the Proposal to Update the Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certiﬁcation Regulations Dear Assistant Administrator Kramer: Chelan County Public Utility District (Chelan PUD) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) Proposed Rule to update its Clean Water Act (CWA) Section 401 water quality certiﬁcation regulations. As a hydropower owner and operator, Chelan PUD strongly supports the Proposed Rule because it would revise EPA’s 2023 rule to properly limit the scope of certiﬁcations to discharges in accordance with the statute. If implemented as envisioned, the Proposed Rule would help restore congressional intent under the CWA and appropriately recognize the exclusive authority of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC) to issue licenses for the purpose of constructing, operating, and maintaining non-federal hydroelectric dams under the Federal Power Act (FPA). In addition to our own comments, Chelan PUD is a member of both the National Hydropower Association (NHA) and Northwest Hydroelectric Association (NWHA) and support their comments in this Docket. Background Chelan PUD is a Washington State public utility district formed in 1936 by local voters who wanted affordable power for both rural and urban residents. We own and operate two hydroelectric dams on the Columbia River (the Rocky Reach and Rock Island Projects) and one on Lake Chelan licensed by FERC. These three hydropower projects total about 2,000 megawatts of capacity and deliver approximately 1100 average megawatts of clean, renewable energy.
