For decades, the region has relied on our hydropower system, which generates surplus power used throughout the Northwest after serving our 50,000 local customers. We received new FERC licenses supported by Section 401 water quality COMMISSIONERS: Kelly Allen, Garry Arseneault, Carnan Bergren, Steve McKenna, Randy Smith GENERAL MANAGER: Kirk Hudson Chelan County PUD comments cont. certifications for the Rocky Reach Project in 2009 and the Lake Chelan Project in 2006. The Rock Island Project is seeking a new federal operating license from FERC because its existing license will expire in 2028. As part of that process, Chelan PUD will request a Section 401 water quality certification from the Washington State Department of Ecology. In navigating compliance obligations under the CWA and FPA, two issues stand out. First, the scope of Section 401 influences how agencies and tribes participate in hydropower licensing under other aspects of federal law – particularly FPA Sections 10(a) and 10(j). Second, the interpretation of what constitutes a discharge that is subject to certification for compliance with Sections 301, 302, 303, 306 and 307 of the CWA presents unique challenges in the context of hydropower projects, whose discharges primarily pass water through the project from upstream to downstream, rather than add wastewater or other water to a waterbody. The FPA gives FERC the exclusive authority to issue licenses for the purpose of constructing, operating, and maintaining non-federal hydroelectric dams. Under Section 10(a) of the FPA, FERC is required to assure that a hydropower project is “best adapted to a comprehensive plan of development” of the aﬀected river basin for beneﬁcial uses such as energy generation, water supply, ﬂood control, recreation, and ﬁsh and wildlife. 1 In 1986, the Electric Consumers Protection Act (ECPA) was enacted. It broadened FERC’s responsibilities in the licensing process by requiring it to give “equal consideration to the purposes of energy conservation, the protection, mitigation of damage to, and enhancement of, ﬁsh and wildlife (including related spawning grounds and habitat), the protection of recreational opportunities, and the preservation of other aspects of environmental quality.” 2 However, federal and state agencies each play a deﬁned role in imposing mandatory conditions and prescriptions and making ﬁsh and wildlife recommendations. • Sections 4(e) of the FPA authorizes the Secretaries of the Interior and Agriculture to impose mandatory conditions for projects on federal reservations. FERC must accept these conditions and incorporate them into a new license for the project, without modiﬁcation. 3 • Section 18 of the FPA authorizes the Secretaries of Commerce and Interior to impose mandatory ﬁshway prescriptions on projects. FERC must accept these conditions and incorporate them into a new license without modiﬁcation. 4 • Section 10(j) of the FPA 5 provides federal and state ﬁsh and wildlife agencies the opportunity to recommend to FERC terms and conditions related to the protection, mitigation, and enhancement of ﬁsh and wildlife resources aﬀected by the project. FERC is required to accept section 10(j) recommendations unless it determines they are inconsistent with the purposes and requirements of the FPA. 1 16 U.S.C. § 803(a)(1). 2 16 U.S.C. § 797(e). 3 16 U.S.C § 797(e). 4 16 U.S.C. § 811. 5 16 U.S.C. § 803(j) 2 Chelan County PUD comments cont. • Section 10(a) of the FPA 6 provides federal and state agencies and tribes the opportunity to recommend to FERC license conditions for the comprehensive use of the waterway, such as public recreation, navigation, water supply and other public uses. FERC has discretion to accept, modify, or reject recommendations submitted under Section 10(a). Through the FPA, therefore, federal and state ﬁsh and wildlife agencies have signiﬁcant authorities to provide input into the hydropower licensing process regarding land use and ﬁsh and wildlife. FERC, in turn, is responsible for balancing competing uses by exercising its discretion to accept or reject these recommendations. When Section 401 is interpreted as allowing certifying authorities to impose broad conditions untethered to discharges, it becomes a vehicle to convert discretionary recommendations made through FPA Sections 10(a) and 10(j) into mandatory Section 401 certiﬁcation conditions. That outcome denies FERC its exclusive role and discretion under the FPA and expands Section 401 beyond its discharge-centered statutory function. In practice, this has resulted in some Section 401 certiﬁcations that include requirements such as ﬁsh passage and ﬂow mandates, recreation measures, payment arrangements for oﬀsite programs, oﬀsite mitigation or studies, and other obligations not directly tied to a point source discharge from the project. The practical consequence is that a state certifying agency often ﬁnds itself in the role, whether it wants to or not, of being pressured to fulﬁll all the desires of other parties without mandatory conditioning authority by dumping various recommendations and suggestions into the Section 401 certiﬁcation. This approach can result in Section 401 certiﬁcations with conditions that are duplicative of other license conditions or conﬂict with other requirements or project purposes. Besides bypassing FERC’s role, this catch-all, backstop method of utilizing the Section 401 certiﬁcation also inhibits the ability of parties to negotiate in good faith through a license settlement process. At a time of rapidly growing electricity demand and pressing reinvestment needs to maintain dam infrastructure and generation units, the licensing process needs clear statutory lines: Section 401 should focus on discharges; broader programmatic measures should be considered and balanced through the FERC licensing process. Chelan PUD Comments on the Proposed Regulations 1. For consistency with Section 401, EPA should limit the scope of Section 401 decisions and conditions to assuring that discharges from federally licensed or permitted activities comply with CWA Sections 301, 302, 303, 306, and 307 and state water quality-related regulations for point source discharges. 6 16 U.S.C. § 803(a). 3 Chelan County PUD comments cont. Chelan PUD supports the proposed regulations, which describe the scope of the Section 401 certiﬁcation requirements as “limited to assuring that a discharge from a federal licensed or permitted activity will comply with applicable and appropriate water quality requirements.” 7 Further, it deﬁnes “discharge” as a “discharge from a point source into waters of the United States.” 8 The proposed scope of certiﬁcation properly aligns EPA’s rules with the CWA, which requires certiﬁcation only when an activity “may result in any discharge into the navigable waters,” and requires certiﬁcation only that “any such discharge will comply” with speciﬁed provisions of the CWA. Any applicant for a Federal license or permit to conduct any activity including, but not limited to, the construction or operation of facilities, which may result in any discharge into the navigable waters, shall provide the licensing or permitting agency a certiﬁcation from the State in which the discharge originates or will originate, or, if appropriate, from the interstate water pollution control agency having jurisdiction over the navigable waters at the point where the discharge originates or will originate, that any such discharge will comply with the applicable provisions of sections 1311, 1312, 1313, 1316, and 1317 of this title. 9 Section 401 does not require or authorize state water quality agencies to certify compliance for the entire activity being licensed or permitted under federal law—just the discharge that triggers the need for certiﬁcation. This distinction matters in practice because some state certifying authorities have used Section 401 to impose broad, programmatic, and multi- decade obligations on hydropower projects that are not tied to a point source discharge, and that overlap with or displace FERC’s role under the FPA. As EPA points out in the preamble to the Proposed Rule, there is a signiﬁcant role for states and tribes under the CWA, but there are also limits: “Section 401 envisions a robust State and Tribal role in the Federal licensing or permitting proceedings, including those in which local authority may otherwise be preempted by Federal law. Section 401 also places important limitations on how that role may be implemented to maintain an eﬃcient process, consistent with the overall cooperative federalism construct established by the CWA.” 10 Subsection (d) of CWA Section 401 authorizes the certifying authority to place conditions on any certiﬁcation, which become conditions on the federal license or permit: 7 91 Fed. Reg. 2023 (Jan. 16, 2026). 8 Ibid. 9 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1). 10 91 Fed. Reg. 2010 (Jan. 15, 2026). 4 Chelan County PUD comments cont. Any certiﬁcation provided under this section shall set forth any eﬄuent limitations and other limitations, and monitoring requirements necessary to assure that any applicant for a Federal license or permit will comply with any applicable eﬄuent limitations and other limitations, under section 1311 or 1312 of this title [CWA Sections 301 and 302], standard of performance under section 1316 of this title [CWA Section 306], or prohibition, eﬄuent standard, or pretreatment standard under section 1317 of this title [CWA Section 307], and with any other appropriate requirement of State law set forth in such certiﬁcation, and shall become a condition on any Federal license or permit subject to the provisions of this section. 11 In implementing Section 401(d), some states have interpreted the phrase “any other appropriate requirement of State law” as authority to include wide-ranging conditions in WQCs so long as they relate – even loosely - to water, water management or resources that use water. EPA’s 2023 rule reinforced this expansive approach by stating it “defers to the relevant State and Tribe to deﬁne which of their state or Tribal provisions qualify as appropriate ‘state law’ or Tribal law for purposes of implementing section 401.” 12 EPA points out that the “best reading of the text of CWA section 401 limits the scope of certiﬁcation to “discharges” and not to the “activity.” Further EPA opines on the CWA’s relationships to discharges under Section 401(a)(2), where references to discharge are “clear indications that the subject of the entire CWA section 401 process – from certiﬁcation pursuant to CWA section 401(a)(1) to the neighboring jurisdiction process pursuant to CWA section 401(a)(2)—is focused on discharges, not the broader activity.” 13 EPA further highlights how amendments to the CWA in 1972 support EPA’s interpretation, speciﬁcally that Congress modiﬁed language from “activity” to discharge” when discussing the role of a certifying authority in determining compliance.14 The 1972 amendments to Section 401(a)(1) are as follows: Any applicant for a Federal license or permit to conduct any activity . . . which may result in any discharge into the navigable waters of the United States, shall provide the licensing or permitting agency a certiﬁcation from the State in which the discharge originates or will originate . . . that there is reasonable assurance, as determined by the State . . . that such activity will be conducted in a manner which will not violate applicable water quality standards that any such discharge will comply with the applicable provisions of sections 1311, 1312, 1313, 1316, and 1317 of this title. Congress clearly focused on discharges in its 1972 amendments. Chelan PUD agrees with and supports EPA’s Proposed Rule, which addresses the relationship of Section 401 11 33 U.S.C. § 1341(d) (emphasis added). 12 88 Fed. Reg. 66,604 (Sept. 27, 2023). 13 91 Fed. Reg. 2024 (Jan. 16, 2026). 14 Ibid. 5 Chelan County PUD comments cont. certiﬁcation to discharges as deﬁned in the 1972 amendments and makes clear that conditions must remain tethered to assuring compliance with applicable CWA provisions for the discharges that trigger certiﬁcation. 2. EPA should further clarify the scope of certiﬁcation in the context of hydropower projects. A discharge-focused 401 certiﬁcation matters for hydropower licensing. Many hydropower projects – particularly run-of-river facilities – do not “originate” 15 water quality conditions; they pass river water through turbines, ﬁshways, and or spillways and return it downstream. Water entering a reservoir or powerhouse may already fail to meet standards due to upstream agricultural runoﬀ, sediment, temperature, or other non-project sources. When Section 401 is treated as a broad waterbody restoration mandate, FERC license applicants face pressure to mitigate upstream or non-attributable conditions through operational constraints, oﬀsite projects, or ﬁnancial contributions. Those measures are beyond the statutory logic of certifying “any such discharge” from the federally licensed activity. EPA’s Proposed Rule should clarify the scope of certiﬁcation in the ﬁnal rule to prevent scenarios where hydropower projects must mitigate pollutants (such as heat, sediments and nitrogen) from upstream sources that are beyond their control. Further, it should be made clear that a certiﬁcation condition for a hydropower project cannot go beyond the scope of certiﬁcation by regulating the activity as a whole – for example, requiring ﬁsh passage facilities or minimum ﬂow requirements. Such requirements are addressed through the comprehensive FERC licensing process through the input of multiple federal and state agencies and balanced by FERC. Chelan PUD supports the deﬁnition proposed by NHA in its comment letter, speciﬁcally, the proposed regulations at Section 121.3 should be revised to state: The scope of a Clean Water Act section 401 certiﬁcation is limited to assuring that a discharge from a federally licensed or permitted activity will comply with applicable and appropriate water quality requirements. Activities or eﬀects that are not within the scope of certiﬁcation include, but are not limited to: (a) Eﬀects caused by the presence of pollutants in a discharge that are not attributable to the discharge from the Federally licensed or permitted activity; (b) Eﬀects attributable to features of the Federally licensed or permitted activity other than the discharge; and (c) Eﬀects caused by the absence or reduction of a discharge, including eﬀects attributable to the diversion or impoundment of water. 3. EPA should clarify enforcement and conﬁrm FERC’s authority to address out-of-scope conditions and waiver. 15 33 U.S.C. § 1341(a)(1) 6 Chelan County PUD comments cont. Section 401(d) provides that certiﬁcation conditions “shall become a condition on any Federal license or permit” subject to Section 401. 16 The CWA does not confer authority on other entities to enforce certiﬁcation conditions outside the federal license or permit. Instead, it states conditions “shall become a condition on any Federal. license or permit.” Conditions are enforceable after they become part of the federal license or permit and are tied to the federal license or permit. However, sometimes certifying authorities and third parties attempt to enforce certiﬁcation conditions themselves. To address this tactic, EPA should expressly provide in the ﬁnal rule that certiﬁcation conditions are enforceable only after incorporation into the federal license or permit, and then only by the federal licensing or permitting agency in the same manner and to the same extent as other license or permit conditions. Further, Chelan PUD supports EPA explicitly recognizing that a federal permitting or licensing authority like FERC can reject or decline to include certiﬁcation conditions that are outside the lawful scope of Section 401. A proactive recognition of this authority by EPA would be an important factor in supporting the relative roles of participants in the hydropower licensing process and facilitate any settlement negotiations. Finally, Chelan PUD supports NHA’s comments regarding FERC’s ability to determine that certiﬁcation has been waived where a certifying authority fails or refuses to act within the statutory timeframe, or where a denial rests on reasons outside the scope of certiﬁcation. Conclusion As EPA ﬁnalizes the rule, we urge the Agency to be explicit in ﬁnal regulatory text and guidance that 1) the discharge-based scope constraint applies to certiﬁcation conditions as well as certiﬁcation decisions; and 2) certifying authorities may not treat Section 401 as a “catch-all” backstop for broad, multi-decade settlement terms or non-discharge programmatic obligations that are better considered and balanced through the FERC licensing process under the FPA. Thank you for the opportunity to provide input. Sincerely, Alene Underwood Director, Natural Resources Chelan County PUD 16 33 U.S.C. § 1341(d). 7

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the docket topic: EPA's proposed update to Clean Water Act Section 401 regulations. It focuses on the scope of certification, hydropower licensing, and the relationship to FERC authority, all highly pertinent to the rulemaking.
rationales.reasoning: The letter presents a coherent legal and policy argument, moving from statutory background to the claimed problem and then to requested regulatory changes. The logic is clear and internally consistent, especially in tying discharge-based certification to the statutory text.
rationales.evidence: It includes multiple statutory citations, references to EPA Federal Register notices, and concrete hydropower project examples from Chelan PUD. While it is more legal than empirical, it uses solid documentary support and specific factual context.
rationales.impact: The comment explains plausible impacts on hydropower licensing, settlement negotiations, and project operations, and gives concrete examples such as fish passage, minimum flows, and upstream pollutant mitigation. The impacts are specific and credible, though not quantified.
rationales.actionability: The letter makes clear requests: limit Section 401 scope to discharges, clarify hydropower-specific limits, specify enforcement boundaries, and recognize FERC's authority regarding out-of-scope conditions and waiver. These are feasible and directly tied to the rule text.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is well organized with a formal letter format, headings, numbered sections, and bullet points. Readability is strong, though the length and dense legal citations make it somewhat cumbersome.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, highly relevant public comment that is legally grounded, well reasoned, and actionable. Its main strength is detailed statutory argumentation; its main limitation is that it relies more on legal interpretation than on empirical evidence.

overall_summary: A strong and highly relevant comment letter focused on narrowing Section 401 to discharge-related issues in hydropower licensing. It is persuasive, well supported by legal citations and concrete examples, and offers clear regulatory requests, though it is more doctrinal than data-driven.

comment_text: Gaá Čhíng Ziíbi Daáwaá Aníšhinaábek Little River Band of Ottawa Indians Natural Resources Department 2608 Government Center Dr. Manistee, MI 49660 Telephone: (231)723-1594 Fax: (231)723-8873 February 17, 2026 Submitted via: https://www.regulations.gov/ U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Office of Water 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929: Comments on Clean Water Act section 401 proposed revisions. Dear Administrator and Members of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency, I am writing on behalf of the Little River Band of Ottawa Indians (LRBOI) to comment on the proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 Water Quality Certification Regulations, published in the Federal Register on January 15, 2026. LRBOI strongly opposes these changes, which threaten our rights and responsibilities to protect our waters. As a federally recognized Tribe with deep ties to our territories in Michigan, the health of our water resources is essential to our culture and well-being. Our treaty rights to hunt, fish, and gather are directly linked to water quality within our ceded lands, making any threat to these waters a threat to our way of life. Section 401 has been a vital tool for Tribes and states to ensure that projects comply with water quality standards. The proposed revisions would narrow certification definitions, impose rigid procedures, and remove necessary flexibility, undermining our ability to protect our resources and creating uncertainty in the certification process. We believe the 2023 Clean Water Act Section 401 Improvement Rule provided a sound framework for consultation and public participation, respecting Tribal sovereignty. We urge the EPA to uphold these principles and reconsider or withdraw the proposed changes completely. LRBOI calls on the EPA to listen to the voices of the Tribes as we request more meetings with Tribal leaders for genuine consultation. The recent public hearing did not feel sufficient. In summary, the LRBOI opposes the EPA's proposed revisions. Our Treaty and Reserved Rights, which are essential for protecting our waters, depend on the effective implementation of Section 401. We are committed to safeguarding our natural resources and urge collaboration to protect our waters, lands, and wildlife. Thank you for your attention to this important matter. LRBOI welcomes any questions or further discussion. Respectfully, Alycia Peterson Great Lakes Policy Specialist Little River Band of Ottawa Indians - Natural Resources Department

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the EPA docket on proposed Clean Water Act section 401 revisions and focuses on Tribal rights, consultation, and water quality certification, which are clearly on-topic.
rationales.reasoning: The letter presents a coherent position opposing the rule and explains the connection between water quality, treaty rights, and Tribal sovereignty, but the argument is fairly general and does not deeply analyze specific provisions of the proposal.
rationales.evidence: It relies mainly on broad assertions about treaty rights, Tribal responsibilities, and the importance of Section 401, but provides little concrete evidence, data, or specific examples of harm.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies plausible impacts such as reduced Tribal ability to protect waters and increased uncertainty in certification, but the impacts are described in general terms rather than with detailed, specific consequences.
rationales.actionability: It makes a clear request to reconsider or withdraw the proposed changes and asks for more meetings with Tribal leaders, but it does not offer detailed alternative regulatory language or specific procedural fixes.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is professionally formatted and readable, with a clear opening, body, and closing, though it is brief and somewhat repetitive in its main points.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and credible Tribal comment letter that clearly opposes the proposal and emphasizes sovereignty and water protection, but it would be stronger with more specific evidence, detailed analysis, and more concrete recommendations.

overall_summary: A focused and professionally written Tribal comment that strongly opposes the proposed Section 401 revisions and clearly ties them to treaty rights and water protection. Its main weakness is the lack of detailed evidence and specific, actionable critique of the rule text.

comment_text: February 17, 2026 U.S. Environmental Protection Agency EPA Docket Center, Water Docket (Mail Code 28221T) 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW Washington, DC 20460 Min Waban Dan Submitted via Regulations.gov Administrative Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 (EPA, 2026a; EPA, 2026b; Office Regulations.gov, 2026) 523 Ashmun Street RE: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (40 CFR Part 121) – Proposed Rule Sault Ste. Marie To Whom it May Concern: Michigan On behalf of the Sault Ste. Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians, we submit the 49783 following comments regarding the EPA’s proposed rule, “Updating the Water Phone Quality Certification Regulations” (the “Proposed Rule”). The Sault Ste. Marie 906.635.6050 Tribe of Chippewa Indians (Sault Tribe) is a sovereign government with inherent authority and federal trust protections. Sault Tribe citizens exercise treaty- Fax reserved rights throughout the 1836 Ceded Territory, including rights directly tied 906-632-6696 to and dependent upon clean water and healthy aquatic ecosystems. The Government degradation of waters that support these treaty resources undermines both our Services rights and the federal government’s trust responsibility. Membership Section 401 of the Clean Water Act (CWA) is a crucial safeguard established by Services Congress that allows states and authorized tribes to ensure that federally licensed Economic or permitted activities comply with applicable water quality requirements. When Development properly implemented, Section 401 protects not only chemical criteria but also the Commission physical and biological integrity of waters. This integrity is essential for our cultural practices, subsistence, and intergenerational stewardship. EPA’s stated goal of improving clarity and efficiency in Section 401 implementation is understandable. However, as proposed, several provisions would narrow how certifications function in practice and reduce the flexibility that Tribes need to build a complete record, protect designated uses, and uphold treaty responsibilities, especially in complex, place-specific settings like the 1836 Treaty Ceded Territory. In short, a more predictable process is not a win if it becomes predictably under-protective. The Sault Ste. Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians offers the following recommendations to ensure the final rule supports effective water quality protection, respects tribal sovereignty, and maintains Section 401 as a meaningful safeguard rather than a procedural hurdle. Our comments can be broken down into six categories, including: (1) ensuring the scope of Section 401 certification does not create regulatory gaps for indirect and non-point source impacts; (2) preserving the ability of certifying authorities to require site-specific information necessary to assure compliance; (3) aligning the reasonable period of time with consultation timelines and practical data needs; (4) ensuring certification decision requirements do not marginalize Tribal authorities or Indigenous Knowledge systems; (5) providing sufficient time under Section 401(a)(2) for meaningful review of downstream impacts; and (6) preserving Tribal sovereignty by upholding standalone Section 401 pathways and resisting the erosion of Tribal rights. Issue 1. Scope of Certification: Avoid a Regulatory Gap for Indirect and Non‑Point Impacts EPA is proposing to narrow Section 401 review from evaluating whether the overall project will comply with water quality requirements to evaluating whether a point source discharge will comply. The Tribe is concerned that this change could create a larger regulatory gap for common nonpoint and indirect impacts from federally permitted activities, including altered flows, sediment mobilization, temperature changes, and habitat disturbance. Even when there isn’t a clear “pipe discharge” driving the impact, these effects can still degrade the physical and biological integrity of waters and impair their designated uses. Within the 1836 Ceded Territory, these impacts can degrade waters that support treaty resources and interfere with the Tribe’s treaty-reserved rights, regardless of whether they are from a point or indirect source. Requested change: In addition to point source discharges, EPA should be explicit that water quality certifications may include conditions addressing indirect and nonpoint impacts caused by the federally licensed or permitted activity when needed to ensure compliance with applicable water quality requirements, including protection of designated uses and antidegradation. Issue 2. Requests for Certification: Preserve the Ability to Require Site‑Specific Information Needed to Assure Compliance EPA proposes a single defined list of required components for certification requests and would prohibit certifying authorities from requiring additional components. While predictability matters, a fixed checklist can limit the Tribe’s ability to request site‑specific information and data needed to evaluate compliance, particularly for complex projects with localized hydrology, sensitive cultural resources, or treaty rights-related impacts. Requested change: Allow certifying authorities to require additional, water quality‑related information that is (a) published in advance or triggered by objective project categories/locations, (b) tied to water quality requirements and designated uses, and (c) proportional to project risk. Any changes should preserve regulatory authority for certifying authorities to require additional information when necessary to assess whether the proposed activity will meet water quality requirements and protect Tribal waters and resources. Issue 3. Reasonable Period of Time: Align the Rule with Consultation Timelines and Practical Data Needs Section 401 requires certifying authorities to make a decision within a set “reasonable period of time” (no longer than one year); otherwise, the certification is treated as waived. EPA’s proposal would remove certain automatic extensions and would prevent certifying authorities from asking applicants to withdraw and resubmit a request. The Tribe is concerned these changes could force premature approvals or denials when meaningful consultation timelines, seasonal field constraints, or unresolved data gaps make it impossible to develop a complete administrative record, an impact that falls especially hard on tribes with limited staffing compared to larger state agencies and well-resourced applicants. While forward progress is a reasonable expectation, decisions that have the potential to impact treaty resources via impacts of water quality should be given the amount of time necessary to come to sound science-based decisions and should not be based on arbitrary timelines. Requested change: Restore limited, clearly defined options to pause or extend the decision period for specific circumstances (such as major disruptive events, required public notice steps, or documented consultation needs), and allow withdrawal/resubmission when initiated by the applicant or when both parties agree it is necessary to resolve identified information gaps. Issue 4. Contents of Certification Decisions: Ensure Requirements Do Not Marginalize Tribal Authorities or Indigenous Knowledge EPA proposes to define the required contents of certification decisions in regulatory text. The Tribe supports transparency but is concerned that overly rigid templates could be used to challenge tribal decisions that incorporate tribal legal authorities, treaty‑reserved rights, or Indigenous Knowledge in a manner appropriate to the Tribe’s governance and water protection responsibilities. Requested change: Treat decision‑content elements as minimums, allow incorporation by reference of supporting findings and technical documentation, and confirm that certifying authorities may include additional findings and conditions grounded in applicable tribal authority where relevant to assuring compliance with water quality requirements. Issue 5. Section 401(a)(2): Provide Sufficient Time for Meaningful Review of Downstream Impacts EPA proposes a 90‑day timeline for federal agencies to hold a public hearing and make a determination on a notified state’s objection under Section 401(a)(2). The Tribe is concerned that a rigid 90‑day timeframe may be insufficient for meaningful evaluation of downstream and interjurisdictional impacts affecting treaty resources. Requested change: Provide flexibility to extend the 90‑day period where needed to evaluate complex impacts, including impacts to treaty resources and downstream designated uses. The rule should be explicit in authorizing federal agencies to extend hearing and determination timelines at the request of affected Tribes or other governments (e.g., states) when additional time is needed for consultation. The 90-day timeframe should be viewed as the default timeframe, not a maximum. Issue 6. Preserving Tribal Sovereignty: Uphold Standalone Section 401 Pathways and Resist Erosion of Tribal Rights. EPA’s proposal would eliminate the stand-alone regulatory pathway for tribes to obtain Treatment in a Similar Manner as a State (TAS) for Clean Water Act Section 401 and instead require tribes to pursue TAS through the water quality standards process (Section 303(c)). Sault Tribe is concerned that this creates a significant barrier for Tribes that have strong water protection programs and treaty responsibilities but lack the staffing, time, or funding to complete a full water quality standards approval process simply to exercise Section 401 authority. Requested change: Retain a stand-alone Section 401 TAS pathway (including for the neighboring jurisdiction process) or establish a streamlined alternative that allows tribes to obtain and use Section 401 authority without requiring full water quality standards approval as a prerequisite. In closing, Section 401 is a crucial safeguard in the Clean Water Act, designed to prevent federally permitted activities from harming water resources and ecosystems. For the Sault Ste. Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians, this includes protecting treaty resources within the 1836 Treaty Ceded Territory that have been memorialized in the 2007 Inland Consent Decree and the 2023 Great Lakes Decree. The Tribe urges the EPA to revise the final rule to avoid restricting certifications to a discharge-only focus and to eliminate the one-size-fits- all checklist for certifying authorities. It’s important to retain necessary tools for creating a defensible record and to ensure decision-content requirements are minimum standards that respect tribal authority and Indigenous Knowledge. The process should allow for adjustments to certifications based on new information and must facilitate tribal exercise of Section 401 authority without imposing resource-intensive prerequisites. These changes would ensure that Section 401 remains protective, supports timely permitting, and safeguards waters and treaty resources. EPA should finalize a rule that enables Tribes to act as co-regulators and respects Tribal sovereignty and governing authority. Respectfully Submitted, Austin Lowes, Chairman Sault Ste. Marie Tribe of Chippewa Indians

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the EPA proposed rule on Water Quality Certification Regulations and responds to multiple specific issues in the rulemaking.
rationales.reasoning: The arguments are generally coherent and logically connected, with clear explanations of why the proposed changes could weaken tribal water protection, though some points are more asserted than deeply analyzed.
rationales.evidence: The letter includes concrete references to treaty territory, consultation constraints, and specific legal provisions, but it provides little empirical data, case law, or technical citations beyond general references.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible harms such as regulatory gaps, rushed decisions, and reduced tribal authority, and explains why these could affect treaty resources and water quality, though the magnitude is not quantified.
rationales.actionability: The letter offers clear, feasible requests for EPA to revise the rule, including preserving authority to request additional information, allowing extensions, and retaining a standalone TAS pathway.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is well organized into numbered issues with requested changes and a clear conclusion, though the formatting appears somewhat cluttered in places due to docket/header text.
rationales.overall: This is a strong, policy-relevant tribal comment letter with clear recommendations and solid issue framing, but it would be stronger with more supporting evidence and tighter formatting.

overall_summary: A substantive and highly relevant comment that clearly engages the proposed rule and offers specific revisions from a tribal sovereignty and water protection perspective. Its main weaknesses are limited empirical support and somewhat uneven presentation, but overall it is a strong public comment.

comment_text:
Brokers act as middlemen, facilitating the negotiation of freight rates between shippers and carriers. There is little visibility into how brokers set their fees or how much they are marking up freight costs. This lack of transparency leaves carriers, particularly smaller or independent operators, at a disadvantage when negotiating rates.

Carriers often find themselves in the dark about how brokers arrive at the rates they offer. In some cases, brokers are able to charge inflated fees while offering the carrier a much lower payout for the same load, effectively squeezing the profit margins of the trucker. The widespread use of tracking apps and ELDs has given brokers access to real-time data on routes, traffic, and load availability, but this has also led to some brokers using this information to pressure carriers into accepting lower-paying loads.

The absence of clear breakdowns for how freight rates are determined or how brokers set their commission has made the industry ripe for exploitation. Without regulatory oversight, brokers have the power to set terms that may not reflect the true value of the service, leading to potential abuses.

The trucking industry has long been criticized for its fragmented structure, with brokers often controlling much of the pricing power. This power imbalance has made it difficult for smaller carriers and independent owner-operators to negotiate fair terms. In the absence of proper regulation, some brokers have been known to engage in predatory pricing practices, using their market position to extract excessive profits at the expense of truckers.

Regulation is needed to ensure brokers operate fairly and transparently. Key areas where regulation could help include:

Brokers should be required to clearly disclose their commission structure, allowing both shippers and carriers to understand the true cost of the transaction. This could prevent brokers from inflating prices without offering value or transparent services.

Regulators could enforce guidelines to prevent brokers from setting rates that are too low, ensuring that drivers are compensated fairly for their work. This would protect smaller carriers from being pushed out of the market by large brokers who leverage their size to dictate terms.

Without adequate oversight, brokers have been known to engage in fraudulent practices, such as failing to pay carriers for services rendered or misrepresenting load availability. Clear regulations would help ensure that brokers fulfill their contractual obligations and maintain integrity in their dealings with carriers.

Brokers have a direct impact on the income of drivers. When brokers negotiate freight rates, they ultimately decide how much drivers get paid for their services. In many cases, brokers have the leverage to push down rates, sometimes to the point where drivers earn insufficient compensation for the work involved. This has contributed to a growing dissatisfaction among truckers and an increase in turnover rates in the industry.

With the increasing use of tracking apps and ELDs, brokers now have unprecedented access to data that gives them an advantage in price negotiations. However, this information is often used to the detriment of carriers, who may feel pressured into accepting lower-paying loads because brokers can easily compare rates and negotiate based on real-time data. The digital tools intended to increase efficiency are, in some cases, further consolidating power in the hands of brokers, leaving little room for small carriers to thrive.

Similar to how carriers are regulated by the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA), brokers should be subject to similar oversight. Requiring brokers to obtain licenses and adhere to specific industry standards would promote greater accountability.

Brokers should be required to provide detailed reports on their fees, rates, and payment timelines, making it easier for carriers to spot unfair practices and for regulators to intervene when necessary.

By standardizing the contracts between brokers, shippers, and carriers, there could be more consistency and fairness in how freight agreements are structured, ensuring that carriers are not subjected to predatory contract terms.

The trucking industry, vital to the movement of goods across the country, is at a crossroads. To foster a more fair and equitable environment for all stakeholders, there must be greater transparency and regulation of freight brokers. While technology has the potential to streamline operations and enhance safety, it must not come at the cost of fair pricing, driver welfare, or ethical practices. Only through better regulation and a commitment to transparency can we ensure the long-term stability and integrity of the trucking industry.

rationales.relevance: The comment is clearly about freight brokers, rate transparency, and regulation, which are likely relevant to a docket on trucking or broker practices. However, it does not reference any specific requested issues or docket questions.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and consistently argues that brokers have too much pricing power and that regulation is needed. Still, it relies on broad assertions and repeats similar points without developing a tightly reasoned case.
rationales.evidence: The comment provides almost no concrete evidence, data, citations, or specific examples. Claims about predatory pricing, fraud, and turnover are asserted rather than supported.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible harms to small carriers, owner-operators, and driver income, but the impacts are described in general terms. The magnitude and frequency of these harms are not demonstrated.
rationales.actionability: The comment offers several policy directions, such as disclosure of commission structures, licensing, reporting requirements, and standardized contracts. These are actionable in concept, though they remain somewhat broad and not fully specified.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is readable and organized into paragraphs with a clear overall theme, but it is repetitive and lacks a polished letter structure with headings, concise issue framing, or prioritized recommendations.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and understandable advocacy comment that clearly calls for broker transparency and regulation, but it is weakened by a lack of evidence, limited specificity, and repetitive generalizations.

overall_summary: A relevant but largely unsupported comment that makes understandable policy demands about broker transparency and regulation, yet would be stronger with concrete evidence, sharper reasoning, and more specific recommendations.

comment_text:
I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive and focuses on the requested issues of safety, transparency, and labeling.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with clear links between synthetic biology production, consumer perception, and safety concerns, though some claims are asserted more strongly than they are demonstrated.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes a few concrete technical references, such as Saccharomyces cerevisiae and specific genes, but provides no citations, data, or independent studies to substantiate the safety and allergenicity claims.
rationales.impact: The potential impacts are plausible in general terms, especially around consumer deception and allergen concerns, but the magnitude and likelihood of harm are not well quantified or supported.
rationales.actionability: It offers specific requests, including proteomic analysis, clearer labeling standards, and allergen warnings, which are feasible and directly tied to the concerns raised.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is well organized with numbered sections, clear headings, and a professional tone, making it easy to follow.
rationales.overall: This is a focused and well-structured comment with clear policy asks, but it would be stronger with more evidence and more precise support for its safety and labeling claims.

overall_summary: A relevant and organized comment that clearly opposes the proposed labeling approach and asks for specific regulatory actions. Its main weakness is limited evidentiary support for the safety and allergenicity concerns it raises.

comment_text:
“I am a one truck owner and have deal with brokers for ALL of my work. Transparency in most of the information on loads is very important and FAIR. Brokers currently do not use point to point distance in order to pay on less mileage, they use zip code to zip code … why? They do not describe the product on occasions where they have something that drivers do not care to deal with. The load boards are FULL with very underpriced loads, it is disgusting.

Brokers make a living by sitting at their desks and making profit by underpaying the people who are doing the actual work! The working stiffs should be allowed to know what is going on with each load before we bid on it. What is it? What business is it going to? We need transparency!!!!” 

Broker to over 45 days to pay carrier One truck operator like have to hire Factoring Companies to keep cash flow to manage expenses & they charge over 3% Plus Prime rate So carriers losing money to pay their Fee & Prime because broker took longer than 30 day & Broker didn’t pay us anything extra to take extra days to make payment.
Broker Carrier package are totally against Small feet companies & owner operator when we ask them questions they Blacklisted us or comments on Freight guard & That Put us out off business. 
They are posting one load at several time even after booked to shop around when they book same load on cheaper Rate they just send email that load is canceled or Product not ready etc. if carrier cancel load for some reason they add comment on freight guard. Some shipper/Receiver charge lumper fee & broker made carrier to pay even they reimbursed still carrier lossing 3to 4% as factoring companies charge. They have all information about carrier now they require to Connect ELD when we call them to Confirm shippment they didn’t answer our call & made us to stay on line for over hour. Brokers are too strick for appointments if carrier didn’t make pickup/Drop on time they charge us. But when shipper/Receiver take while to load/offload they don’t pay us anything even we get their on time. They pay $150 for layover its nothing as compared to our Expense still carrier losing money. Some broker Discrimination according to State & Race . Need to fix minimum pay rate pr mile broker paying under $1 in some state & cities State like California have high tax & brower paying $1 to $1.50 how can we staying in business when broker keeping all the money to sit in their office. FMCA requires carriers to maintain all records & they don’t even audit brokers & they take advantage of this. All rule/law everything on Carrier & Drivers nothing on brokers. Need to balance this out. 

I want Broker transparency.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses broker transparency, load information, payment timing, detention/layover pay, and broker-carrier practices, which appear closely tied to the likely docket topic.
rationales.reasoning: The main concerns are understandable and generally coherent, but the comment jumps between issues and sometimes mixes grievances without clearly connecting them into a structured argument.
rationales.evidence: It includes a few concrete examples such as payment delays over 45 days, factoring fees, under-$1 per mile rates, and layover pay, but provides no citations, data, or specific cases to support the broader claims.
rationales.impact: The comment explains plausible harms to small carriers and owner-operators, including cash-flow strain, added financing costs, and business risk, though the magnitude is mostly asserted rather than demonstrated.
rationales.actionability: It clearly asks for broker transparency and a more balanced regulatory framework, but the requested changes are broad and not very specific or operationalized.
rationales.structure_formatting: The submission is readable enough to follow, but it is a long, run-on paragraph with repeated points, informal language, and limited organization, which reduces professionalism and clarity.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and earnest public comment that raises important carrier concerns, but it would be stronger with clearer organization, more precise recommendations, and better-supported examples.

overall_summary: The comment is highly relevant and raises credible concerns about broker transparency and carrier treatment, especially for small operators. However, it is loosely organized, light on evidence, and offers only broad policy requests rather than specific, actionable proposals.

comment_text: To whomever it may concern, As a current public health graduate student; this proposal is important to me as I am highly interested in environmental health and its impact to all species of life. The concise focus on how water quality directly affects community health outcomes, highlights the nature of how important this proposal is and its revisions that are offered. In relation to clean water being the foundation for disease prevention and overalll population health, my interests in how federal policy influences real-world environmental protections play hand in hand. From my understanding, the proposal in general wants to update the Clean Water Act 401 water quality certification rules and regulations. The U.S. Environmental Protection Agency suggests that this new proposal on certification regulations improves numeorus goals set forth in different aspects such as clarity, efficiency and predictability in certification processing. Although the Clean Water Act covers all aspects associated with rules and regulations pertaining to the importance of keeping our water safe and protected from harmful impacts, the emphasis on reviewing discharges into waters of the United States is just as important; therefore we can see why making sure the process of receiving a water quality certificate are concise and accurate is equally important. The reason I support this proposal is because of the clear processes and actions taken to help states or tribes make timely descisions that affect the health of our waters are both important. The introduction of over emphasizing efficient the elimination of environmental safeguards. The revisions that are being proposed for implementation to me seems to hold predicatliby that benefits the health of the public by ensuring consistent enforcement of water quality standards. Industrial pollution, air pollution, and water pollution have all caused reoccurring illnesses and health conditions among the population. Directly focusing on water pollution, numeorus diseases such as but not limited to; gastrointestinal illness, chronic illnesses, and other environmental exposure risks are all related to the health of our waters. Environmental health will continue to be a major concern and contributor to our planets health and health of humanity. Regulations pertaining to environmental health matters to me because these priorities can and will help health protection, not just administrative efficiency. In accordance with this proposal, I want to suggest more advocation towards water pollution and the safety of the public. However, with the encouragement from EPA when it comes to maintaining a health centered approach when it comes to finalizing this rule will help push forward this proposal. With that being said, I want to extend my great appreciation for the opportunity to comment on the proposed rule and utilize my voice. All in all, I hope that the final say in this proposed rule will help continue the priority of clean water as a public health necessity and not dim the concerns that are proposed.

rationales.relevance: The comment is clearly about the Clean Water Act 401 water quality certification proposal and repeatedly ties clean water to public health, which matches the docket topic well.
rationales.reasoning: The letter generally follows a coherent pro-rule argument, but the logic is repetitive and sometimes unclear, with several awkward transitions and broad assertions that are not fully developed.
rationales.evidence: It offers very few concrete facts, data, or citations. Most claims about health effects and EPA goals are general and unsupported by specific examples or references.
rationales.impact: The comment identifies plausible public health impacts from water pollution and improved water protections, but the impacts are described in broad terms rather than with specific, measurable consequences.
rationales.actionability: It expresses support for maintaining a health-centered approach and stronger attention to water pollution, but it does not provide detailed, feasible recommendations or specific requested changes to the rule.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment has a basic letter format and a clear overall position, but it contains grammar issues, repetition, typos, and visible HTML artifacts that reduce readability and professionalism.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and earnest public comment that supports the proposal on public health grounds, but it is limited by weak evidence, limited specificity, and modest clarity in its recommendations and presentation.

overall_summary: A relevant but fairly general supportive comment that connects clean water policy to public health, yet lacks strong evidence, detailed recommendations, and polished formatting.

comment_text: From: kimsaunt@everyactioncustom.com To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/13/2026 2:19:57 PM Subject: Docket ID# EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 — Opposition to Proposed 2026 Changes to Circumscribe Certification Authority of States and Tribes -------------------------------------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------- [You don't often get email from kimsaunt@everyactioncustom.com. Learn why this is important at https://aka.ms/LearnAboutSenderIdentification ] Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Dear Administrator Lee Zeldin, I appreciate the opportunity to communicate my disgust with the EPA’s proposed 2026 rule changes that would circumscribe certification authority used by States and Tribes to protect clean water. The Clean Water Act was adopted by Congress to restore and maintain clean water and to ensure that States and Tribes could prevent violation of the water quality requirements they have adopted. The EPA’s proposed certification rules would serve the special interests of polluters at the expense of clean water, the rights of States to protect the health and welfare of their residents, and the sovereignty of Tribes. The EPA’s proposed 2026 certification rules are unlawful, unreasonable, and just plain wrong. Specifically: 1. ) I oppose the Rule’s elimination of current regulations facilitating Tribal certification authority and supporting neighboring/downstream Tribes in objecting to federal permits and preventing violations of Reservation water quality requirements. Shame on you for erasing Tribes from these regulations. 2. ) I oppose the Rule’s restrictions that would prevent any State or Tribe in the United States from setting conditions on Federal “general permits.” In Minnesota, we’ve used existing certification authority to prevent degradation and protect streams, Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters, and Outstanding Resource Value waters, like the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park. 3. ) I oppose all the Rule’s changes that tighten a noose around the scope of certification. They would prevent conditions or denial of Federal permits even if activities would violate State and Tribal standards and contaminate lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or seepage through groundwater into surface water. 4. ) I oppose the Rule’s forced waiver of State or Tribal certification after a year even if time is needed due to public process, acts of God, or because the project proposer concealed the truth about water impacts in its draft permit application. This rule would encourage polluters to hide the facts and do a bait-and-switch after time has run out for certification. 5. ) I oppose the Rule’s creation of new power in the EPA Administrator to determine without guardrails that an entire category of discharges will never affect downstream States or Tribes and deprive them of authority to object to violations of water quality requirements. What category would this EPA remove from certification review? All pipeline discharge? All mining waste? The EPA must reject or withdraw its 2026 proposed rule to undermine State and Tribal certification. Existing rules under the Clean Water Act do a much better job of protecting us and our children from contaminated water that they can’t safely drink, swim in, or fish. Thank you for considering my comments, Sincerely, Mrs. Lindaq Hall 9462 Lime Ave Fontana, CA 92335-5356 kimsaunt@gmail.com

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the docket topic: proposed changes to Clean Water Act certification authority for States and Tribes. It responds to multiple specific rule provisions and stays tightly focused on the issue.
rationales.reasoning: The letter is logically organized around numbered objections and generally explains why the commenter believes the rule is harmful. The arguments are clear, though some points are more rhetorical than deeply developed.
rationales.evidence: The comment includes a few concrete examples, such as Minnesota protections for streams, Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters, and the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park. However, it provides little factual support, data, or citations beyond these examples.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible harms, including weakened Tribal and State authority, increased pollution risk, and reduced protection for wetlands and downstream waters. The impacts are specific enough to be credible, though not quantified.
rationales.actionability: The commenter clearly requests that EPA reject or withdraw the proposed rule and preserve existing certification rules. The recommendations are straightforward and feasible, even if not highly detailed.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is readable and professionally formatted overall, with a clear introduction, numbered list of concerns, and a concise conclusion. Some phrasing is emotional and informal, but the structure is strong.
rationales.overall: This is a strong public comment letter because it is highly relevant, organized, and clearly opposed to the proposed rule. Its main weakness is limited evidentiary support, but it still offers specific concerns and a clear request for EPA action.

overall_summary: A focused and well-structured opposition comment that directly addresses the proposed rule and explains several concrete concerns. It is persuasive in tone and action-oriented, but would be stronger with more citations, data, or legal references.

comment_text:
I am writing to formally submit my concerns regarding the regulatory approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive produced by Phytolon. While the resulting pigment may be chemically similar to that found in traditional beets, the method of production and the proposed marketing of this ingredient as "natural" raise significant safety, transparency, and allergenicity concerns that have not been sufficiently addressed.

1. Misleading "Natural" Characterization
The characterization of this additive as a "natural" color is fundamentally misleading to the consumer. This dye is not "sourced from nature" in the traditional sense; it is a product of Synthetic Biology (SynBio), synthesized in industrial fermentation vats using genetically engineered Saccharomyces cerevisiae.

Consumer Deception: Consumers associate "beetroot red" with a whole-food source. Labeling a vat-grown, bio-engineered pigment as "natural" or "beetroot-derived" obscures the reality of its synthetic origin.

2. Concerns Regarding Synthetic Biology and Process Integrity
The use of multi-gene biosynthetic pathways (including genes like BvDODA1 and BvCYP76AD1) inserted into a yeast host represents a high degree of processing. This is a radical departure from simple extraction.

The stability of these engineered pathways over long-term industrial cycles and the potential for unintended metabolic byproducts must be scrutinized more heavily than traditional food colors.

3. Unaddressed Allergenicity and Residual Contamination
The safety profile of this additive is inextricably linked to its production host. Using Saccharomyces cerevisiae introduces specific risks that are not present in soil-grown beets:

Residual Host Cell Proteins (HCPs): Even with high-level purification, there is a risk of residual yeast proteins remaining in the final dye. For individuals with yeast allergies or sensitivities, this poses a direct health risk.

Novel Protein Exposure: The presence of biosynthetic enzymes (such as the glucosyltransferases used to stabilize the dye) has not been sufficiently evaluated for potential allergenicity in the human gut or the impact on the microbiome.

Lack of Long-Term Data: There is a dearth of independent, peer-reviewed, long-term feeding studies that confirm the safety of these specific fermented betalains compared to their traditionally extracted counterparts.

4. Call for Rigorous Safety Testing
Before final approval is granted, the following must be mandated:

1. Proteomic Analysis: Rigorous testing to prove the absolute absence of residual yeast DNA and allergenic proteins.

2. Clear Labeling Standards: Strict enforcement of labeling that prevents the use of "natural" or "no artificial colors" for any ingredient produced via Synthetic Biology.

3. Allergen Warnings: Mandatory disclosure if the product was manufactured using a yeast-based fermentation process, to protect sensitive populations.

Conclusion: The push for "sustainable" colors should not come at the expense of consumer transparency and safety. The agency must prioritize the "Right to Know" for consumers who wish to avoid Synthetic Biology and ensure that the allergen screening for these novel additives is as rigorous as those for pharmaceutical-grade biologics. I urge the agency to require clear labeling that distinguishes between plant-extracted pigments and those produced via Synthetic Biology.

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses the approval and labeling of the beetroot red color additive and focuses on the requested issues of safety, transparency, and labeling.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent and organized, with clear claims about natural labeling, synthetic biology, and allergenicity. Some assertions are broad or speculative, but the overall logic is understandable.
rationales.evidence: The comment cites specific genes and production methods, but provides little concrete data, regulatory citation, or empirical evidence to support the safety and allergenicity concerns.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible consumer transparency and allergenicity concerns, but the magnitude of harm is not quantified and some claimed impacts are asserted rather than demonstrated.
rationales.actionability: The letter includes clear requests, such as proteomic analysis, labeling standards, and allergen warnings, which are feasible in principle and directly tied to the concerns raised.
rationales.structure_formatting: The comment is well organized with numbered sections, clear headings, and a professional tone, making it easy to follow.
rationales.overall: A focused and well-structured comment that is highly relevant and actionable, but it would be stronger with more evidence and more specific support for the safety claims.

overall_summary: This is a strong public comment on the labeling and safety of a synthetic-biology-produced color additive. It is directly on topic, clearly organized, and offers concrete requests, but it relies more on concern and inference than on data or citations.

comment_text:
To Whom It May Concern,

As the owner of Trans Sohal Logistics, a trucking company with a dedicated fleet and over 20 years of experience in the transportation industry, I am writing to express my strong support for the proposed rule under Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257 (RIN 2126-AC63) regarding transparency in property broker transactions.

Transparency is essential for maintaining fairness, trust, and sustainability within the logistics industry. For far too long, carriers have operated without access to critical transaction details, creating an imbalance of power that disproportionately benefits brokers while leaving carriers at a disadvantage. This rule is a necessary step toward restoring equity and ensuring that carriers are compensated fairly for the vital role they play in keeping goods moving across the nation.

Without visibility into broker margins, carriers are unable to assess whether they are receiving a fair share of the freight charges. This lack of transparency not only undermines carriers' negotiating power but also threatens the viability of small and independent trucking businesses—the backbone of the American supply chain.

Honest brokers should welcome this rule. Those operating with integrity have nothing to lose from transparency and everything to gain in fostering trust with their carrier partners. This rule will not punish ethical business practices but will instead shine a light on unfair dealings that have persisted in the shadows for too long.

We appreciate the FMCSA’s leadership in addressing this critical issue and urge the agency to move forward with the implementation of this rule without delay. By standing with carriers, the FMCSA can help create a more balanced, competitive, and sustainable freight industry for all stakeholders.

Thank you for your attention to this matter and for considering the perspectives of those on the front lines of transportation.

Raj Sohal
Owner & Operator
Trans Sohal Logistics

rationales.relevance: The comment directly addresses FMCSA-2023-0257 and the topic of transparency in property broker transactions, clearly expressing support for the proposed rule.
rationales.reasoning: The argument is generally coherent: it links transparency to fairness, carrier bargaining power, and industry sustainability. However, it relies on broad assertions and does not fully develop the causal chain.
rationales.evidence: The letter provides almost no concrete evidence, data, or citations. It relies mainly on the author's experience and general claims about industry conditions.
rationales.impact: It identifies plausible impacts such as improved fairness and support for small carriers, but the claims are broad and not quantified or substantiated with specific examples.
rationales.actionability: The request to move forward with implementation is clear, but it does not offer detailed, feasible recommendations or specific regulatory changes beyond general support.
rationales.structure_formatting: The letter is well organized, professional, and easy to read, with a clear opening, body, and closing. It follows a standard comment-letter format.
rationales.overall: This is a relevant and professionally written support letter, but it is limited by a lack of evidence and specificity. It is persuasive at a high level, yet not especially substantive or actionable.

overall_summary: A clear, relevant, and well-formatted supportive comment, but it is mostly general advocacy with limited evidence, specificity, or detailed recommendations.
